```mermaid
    C5["REPOSITIONED PRODUCTS"]
    C6["REFORMULATIONS"]
    C7["NEW PACKAGING ONLY"]

    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C1 --> C4
    C1 --> C5
    C1 --> C6
    C1 --> C7
```

### 4.3 Cross-Functional Team Architecture for NPD

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1["NPD STEERING COMMITTEE"]
    D2["R AND D DEPARTMENT"]
    D3["MARKETING DEPARTMENT"]
    D4["QUALITY ASSURANCE"]
    D5["SUPPLY CHAIN TEAM"]
    D6["FINANCE DEPARTMENT"]
    D7["REGULATORY AFFAIRS"]
    D8["NPD PROJECT MANAGER"]

    D1 --> D8
    D8 --> D2
    D8 --> D3
    D8 --> D4
    D8 --> D5
    D8 --> D6
    D8 --> D7
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1.** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]** Define a new food product and list the six categories of new products according to Booth's classification.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A new food product is any food offering that is perceived by the consumer as significantly different from existing alternatives in terms of ingredients, processing, packaging, or positioning, and is introduced through a structured Stage-Gate development process. Booth's classification (1984) categorizes new food products into six types: **(1)** New Formulations, **(2)** New Product Lines, **(3)** Line Extensions, **(4)** Repositioned Products, **(5)** Reformulations, and **(6)** New Packaging. The framework helps firms match innovation intensity with risk tolerance and resource allocation.

**Question 2.** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]** State any three reasons for new product development in the food industry.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**(1)** Market Expansion — firms enter adjacent categories to grow revenue and diversify risk. **(2)** Consumer Trend Alignment — companies respond to evolving preferences such as plant-based diets, clean-label demand, and functional foods. **(3)** Margin Enhancement — premiumization of legacy SKUs lifts average selling price and gross margin. Additional acceptable reasons include competitive response, regulatory compliance, and technological leverage.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> In Part B answers on new product development, students commonly lose marks by: **(a)** omitting the Stage-Gate framework when describing the process, **(b)** confusing Booth's classification with other taxonomies without attributing the source, **(c)** failing to cite the failure rate statistics with proper numbers, and **(d)** writing generic answers without domain-specific food industry examples such as dairy, bakery, or beverage segments. Always anchor your answer in a concrete food category to score full marks.

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question]** Discuss in detail the Stage-Gate process for new food product development. Mention the stages, gate outputs, and the role of the cross-functional team.

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the seven stages of the Stage-Gate process with gate outputs.

**Model Answer:**
The Stage-Gate process divides new food product development into seven discrete stages separated by five decision gates.

**Stage 1 — Idea Generation:** Multiple sources such as consumer research, competitor monitoring, trade fairs, and internal brainstorming produce an idea bank of 50–200 raw concepts. **Gate 1 Output:** Screened idea shortlist of 10–20 viable concepts.

**Stage 2 — Concept Screening:** Concepts are evaluated against strategic fit, technical feasibility, and consumer appeal criteria. **Gate 2 Output:** 3–5 winning concepts approved for detailed development.

**Stage 3 — Concept Development:** Winning concepts are translated into product prototypes and subjected to preliminary sensory evaluation with consumer panels. **Gate 3 Output:** Approved product concept with validated sensory profile.

**Stage 4 — Business Analysis:** Detailed financial modeling is performed including ROI, NPV, and break-even calculations. **Gate 4 Output:** Approved business case with projected financials.

**Stage 5 — Product Development:** Pilot-scale batches are produced in plant trials, and shelf-life, packaging, and regulatory studies are initiated. **Gate 5 Output:** Market-ready product with documented specifications.

**Stage 6 — Market Testing:** Limited geographic launches or in-store trials measure trial rate and repeat purchase behavior. **Gate 6 Output:** Validated market acceptance data.

**Stage 7 — Commercialization:** Full-scale production and multi-channel launch are executed with marketing and supply chain alignment. **Final Gate Output:** Launch readiness confirmation.

This staged structure reduces risk by committing progressively larger resources only at justified decision points.

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]:** Describe the cross-functional team structure and explain why it is essential.

**Model Answer:**
New food product development requires expertise spanning multiple organizational functions because the decisions at each gate are multidimensional.

**Core Cross-Functional Members:**

**(1) R&D Department:** Owns formulation, process design, and prototype preparation. They ensure technical feasibility at industrial scale.

**(2) Marketing Department:** Owns consumer insights, positioning, pricing, and launch communication. They validate commercial demand.

**(3) Quality Assurance:** Owns food safety protocols, regulatory compliance (FSSAI in India, FDA in the United States), and shelf-life validation.

**(4) Supply Chain Team:** Owns raw material sourcing, packaging procurement, and distribution planning for nationwide rollout.

**(5) Finance Department:** Owns ROI, NPV, and pricing models. They sign off on commercial viability.

**(6) Regulatory Affairs:** Owns label compliance, nutritional panel accuracy, and claims substantiation.

**(7) NPD Project Manager:** Coordinates all functions, maintains stage deliverables, and enforces timelines.

**Why it is essential:** A single-function perspective creates blind spots. For example, R&D may approve a technically excellent formulation that Marketing rejects on price, or QA may reject a prototype due to a regulatory mismatch that early cross-functional review could have prevented. Cross-functional integration ensures balanced decisions, faster time-to-market, and reduced risk of late-stage failure.

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question]** "New product failure in the food industry is alarmingly high." Critically analyze the reasons for new product failure and suggest strategies to minimize it.

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]:** Analyze the major reasons for new food product failure with statistics.

**Model Answer:**
Industry studies consistently report that approximately **70% to 80%** of newly launched food products are withdrawn from retail within three years of launch. This figure is one of the highest failure rates across consumer goods categories. The reasons can be categorized into five distinct failure modes.

**1. Concept Failure:** Many new food products are launched based on internal enthusiasm rather than validated consumer needs. Surveys indicate that nearly **30%** of food product failures originate from the absence of genuine unmet consumer demand.

**2. Technical Failure:** Some products succeed in the laboratory but fail at industrial scale due to inconsistent texture, color drift, or shelf-life degradation. Approximately **15%** of new food product failures are attributed to technical issues.

**3. Commercial Failure:** A technically excellent product may be priced above the consumer's willingness-to-pay threshold, resulting in poor trial and repeat purchase. This represents the largest single failure category at approximately **35%** of total failures.

**4. Timing Failure:** Launching too early before consumer awareness exists, or too late after a trend has peaked, leads to missed opportunity. Approximately **10%** of failures fall into this category.

**5. Execution Failure:** Even strong products fail due to inadequate distribution, weak in-store merchandising, or supply chain disruptions. This accounts for the remaining **10%** of failures.

The combined effect of these failure modes justifies the industry's investment in structured Stage-Gate processes and consumer-led validation methods.

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]:** Suggest strategies to minimize new product failure.

**Model Answer:**

**Strategy 1 — Robust Consumer Insight Generation:** Invest in ethnographic research, focus groups, and conjoint analysis before committing development resources. Validated unmet need is the single strongest predictor of launch success.

**Strategy 2 — Stage-Gate Resource Commitment:** Avoid committing large capital expenditure until each gate is passed. This ensures that weak concepts are killed early when sunk costs are minimal.

**Strategy 3 — Pilot Plant Validation:** Always validate formulations and processes at pilot scale before full commercialization to surface technical risks.

**Strategy 4 — Test Marketing Discipline:** Conduct controlled geographic launches or simulated test markets to measure trial rate and repeat purchase before national rollout.

**Strategy 5 — Cross-Functional Governance:** Establish a permanent NPD steering committee with representation from R&D, Marketing, QA, Supply Chain, Finance, and Regulatory Affairs to ensure balanced decision-making.

**Strategy 6 — Post-Launch Tracking:** Use retail audit data, consumer complaint analytics, and social media sentiment tracking to identify underperformance signals within 90 days of launch and trigger corrective action.

When these six strategies are deployed together, food industry benchmarks indicate that the new product success rate can be lifted from the baseline **20%–30%** to **45%–55%**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Introduction to New Products**

- **Definition:** A new food product is any offering perceived by consumers as significantly different in formulation, processing, packaging, or positioning.

- **Booth's Six Categories:** New Formulations, New Product Lines, Line Extensions, Repositioned Products, Reformulations, New Packaging — this is the highest-frequency Part A topic.

- **Strategic Drivers:** Market expansion, trend alignment, competitive response, margin enhancement, regulatory compliance, technological leverage.

- **Five Success Pillars:** Strategic alignment, consumer insight, technical feasibility, commercial viability, organizational readiness.

- **Five Failure Modes:** Concept, technical, commercial, timing, execution — with commercial failure being the largest at ~35%.

- **Industry Benchmark Failure Rate:** Approximately **70%–80%** of new food products are withdrawn within three years.

- **Stage-Gate Process:** Seven stages and five gates — memorize the sequence and the gate outputs.

- **Cross-Functional Team:** R&D, Marketing, QA, Supply Chain, Finance, Regulatory, Project Manager.

- **Key Financial Formulas:** ROI, Break-Even, NPV, Market Penetration, Trial Rate, Repeat Purchase — each is examinable.

- **Time-to-Market:** 18–36 months for novel food categories.

- **R&D Spend Ratio:** 1.5%–3% of revenue for established food firms.

- **KTU Exam Tip:** Always illustrate your answer with a concrete food category example (dairy, bakery, beverage, snacks) to score full marks in 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Customers and Consumers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT834 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Customers and Consumers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining the Two Pillars of Food Markets

In the discipline of **Food Product Design and Development**, understanding the precise difference between a **Customer** and a **Consumer** is the foundational step toward commercial success. Although often used interchangeably in casual conversation, they represent two structurally different roles within a transaction chain, and conflating them is one of the most common causes of new food product failure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition — Customer:**
> A *customer* is the individual or organization that **purchases** a food product. The customer is the entity that exchanges monetary value (or an equivalent) for the right to own or use the product. The purchasing decision may or may not be based on personal consumption.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition — Consumer:**
> A *consumer* is the individual who **actually uses, consumes, or derives utility** from the food product. The consumer is the one who evaluates the product based on sensory experience, nutritional benefit, and personal satisfaction.

### 1.2 The Structural Difference — A Simple Analogy

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a mother in a household buying a jar of organic honey from a supermarket.
- **The Customer** = The mother (she pays ₹450, scans the QR code, signs the digital wallet).
- **The Consumer** = Her 6-year-old child (who actually eats the honey on toast and decides whether it "tastes good").

The mother as customer cares about: price, brand trust, packaging seal, label claims (FSSAI, organic certification), shelf life, and value for money.
The child as consumer cares about: sweetness, color, viscosity, aftertaste, and how it feels on bread.

If the food product designer designs for **only the customer** but ignores the **consumer**, the product fails in repeat purchase. If the designer designs for **only the consumer** but ignores the **customer**, the product never makes it into the shopping basket. **Food Product Design must satisfy both simultaneously.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** In KTU ESE questions, the standard benchmark expected is that students articulate *both* the purchasing role (customer) and the consuming role (consumer) and explain why food product development must address the expectations of **both** stakeholders in the value chain.

### 1.3 Categories of Customers and Consumers

**Customers can be classified into:**
1. **Individual / Household Customers** — end retail buyers (B2C — Business to Consumer)
2. **Institutional Customers** — hotels, airlines, hospitals, canteens (B2B — Business to Business)
3. **Industrial Customers** — food processors buying ingredients in bulk (B2B)

**Consumers can be classified into:**
1. **Primary Consumers** — directly eat/drink the product
2. **Secondary Consumers** — derive utility indirectly (e.g., a person fed by a caregiver)
3. **Tertiary Consumers** — influencers of consumption (e.g., a pediatrician recommending baby food)

### 1.4 Key Metrics in Food Industry Context

| Metric | Unit | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) | ₹ per customer | Marketing efficiency |
| Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) | ₹ per customer | Long-term profitability |
| Consumer Satisfaction Index (CSI) | Score 0–100 | Product acceptance |
| Net Promoter Score (NPS) | Score −100 to +100 | Brand loyalty |
| Repeat Purchase Rate (RPR) | **%** | Customer retention |

The standard benchmark for a healthy food product in the Indian FMCG sector is:
- **Repeat Purchase Rate (RPR) ≥ 30%** within the first 6 months.
- **NPS ≥ 40** is considered strong category leadership.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Customer–Consumer Disconnect Model

In modern food product marketing, the **Customer–Consumer Disconnect** refers to the gap between what the *purchaser* demands and what the *user* desires. This disconnect is the single most important analytical concept in Module 1.

### The Four Quadrants of Buyer–User Identity:

| Quadrant | Customer | Consumer | Example in Food Industry |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Q1: Same Person** | Yes | Yes | A working professional buying and eating a protein bar |
| **Q2: Different People** | Yes | No | Father buying breakfast cereal for his teenage son |
| **Q3: Same Person** | No | Yes | Office canteen staff eating food they didn't buy |
| **Q4: Same Person** | No | No | A farmer's produce sold to a processor (intermediate B2B trade) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Note:** Module 1 explicitly requires students to identify *which* quadrant a chosen food product belongs to during product conceptualization, because the marketing strategy, packaging design, sensory testing, and pricing strategy all differ depending on whether customer and consumer are the same entity.

## 2.2 Theoretical Frameworks Governing Consumer Behavior

### A. The Engel–Blackwell–Miniard (EBM) Model

This is the most widely taught consumer decision-making model in food product design.

**The 5 sequential stages:**
1. **Need Recognition** — Hunger, health awareness, or social occasion triggers the need.
2. **Information Search** — Consumer scans labels, reviews, advertisements, peer opinions.
3. **Evaluation of Alternatives** — Compares brands on attributes: taste, price, nutrition, packaging.
4. **Purchase Decision** — The actual act of buying (note: here, the *customer* takes over).
5. **Post-Purchase Behavior** — Satisfaction, repeat purchase, word-of-mouth, or dissonance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Food companies like Britannia and Nestlé apply the EBM model to design advertisements that trigger need recognition (e.g., hunger pangs in a Maggi commercial) and follow up with coupons and offers to close the purchase decision stage.

### B. The Howard–Sheth Model of Buyer Behavior

This model emphasizes **learning, attitude, and confidence** in repeat purchases. It is especially relevant for established food categories where brand loyalty is high (e.g., Indian tea brands like Tata Tea or Brooke Bond).

### C. Maslow's Hierarchy Applied to Food Consumption

Although Maslow's model is a general motivation theory, in food context it translates as:
- **Physiological needs** → Hunger, basic nutrition (bread, rice, dal)
- **Safety needs** → Food safety, hygiene, FSSAI compliance (packaged pasteurized milk)
- **Social/Belongingness needs** → Sharing food, festive sweets, branded restaurants
- **Esteem needs** → Premium chocolates, organic imports, gourmet cheeses
- **Self-actualization** → Vegan, keto, sustainable, ethically sourced foods

## 2.3 Customer Satisfaction Theory — The Expectancy–Disconfirmation Model

The most cited formula in customer satisfaction literature is the **Expectancy–Disconfirmation Paradigm** formulated by Richard Oliver (1980):

$$
S = P - E
$$

Where:
- $S$ = **Satisfaction** (subjective score)
- $P$ = **Perceived Performance** (actual experience after consumption)
- $E$ = **Expectation** (pre-purchase anticipated performance)

**Three possible outcomes:**
1. **Positive Disconfirmation** → $P > E$ → Customer delighted → loyalty + word-of-mouth
2. **Simple Confirmation** → $P = E$ → Customer neutral → repeat purchase only
3. **Negative Disconfirmation** → $P < E$ → Customer dissatisfied → complaints, returns, brand switching

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Formula | Variables & Units | Application in Food Industry |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Satisfaction | $S = P - E$ | $S$: score, $P$: perceived performance, $E$: expectation | Product reformulation decisions |
| 2 | Repeat Purchase Rate | $RPR = \left(\dfrac{N_r}{N_t}\right) \times 100$ | $N_r$: repeat buyers, $N_t$: total buyers | Loyalty tracking |
| 3 | Customer Lifetime Value | $CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{R_t}{(1+d)^t} - CAC$ | $R_t$: revenue at period $t$, $d$: discount rate, $CAC$: acquisition cost | Long-term marketing ROI |
| 4 | Net Promoter Score | $NPS = \%P - \%D$ | $\%P$: promoters, $\%D$: detractors | Brand health metric |
| 5 | Market Penetration | $MP = \left(\dfrac{Q_s}{Q_t}\right) \times 100$ | $Q_s$: units sold, $Q_t$: total addressable market | Launch success |
| 6 | Break-Even Volume | $BEV = \dfrac{F}{P - V}$ | $F$: fixed cost (₹), $P$: price/unit (₹), $V$: variable cost/unit (₹) | Pricing strategy |
| 7 | Price Elasticity of Demand | $E_p = \dfrac{\%\Delta Q}{\%\Delta P}$ | $\%\Delta Q$: % change in quantity demanded, $\%\Delta P$: % change in price | Premium vs mass-market food positioning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Reminder:** In the Break-Even formula $BEV = \dfrac{F}{P - V}$, the denominator $(P - V)$ is called the **Contribution Margin**. In food industry projects, students often forget that packaging cost, distribution cost, and GST must be included in $V$. Always show the *unit* of the final answer (e.g., "1,250 packets").

### 2.5 Why This Theory Matters in Real Engineering Practice

In **Food Process Engineering**, these formulas feed directly into:
- **Process Capacity Decisions** — How many units per shift to produce (depends on BEV).
- **Packaging Line Investment** — Capex justified only if projected CLV exceeds installation cost within 3 years.
- **Sensory Lab Operations** — Expectation is calibrated via focus groups; perceived performance is measured via trained panels and consumer tests (9-point hedonic scale).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation of the Customer Satisfaction Score (Oliver's Paradigm)

We will formally derive the relationship between satisfaction, perceived performance, and expectation as it would appear in a KTU 14-mark answer.

### Step 1 — Define the baseline assumption
At the moment of purchase, the *customer* (or *consumer*) holds a **pre-purchase expectation** about how the food product will perform. Let this expectation be a quantifiable variable.

Let $E$ denote the expectation score, measured on a standardized scale (commonly 1 to 9 in food sensory science).

### Step 2 — Define post-purchase perception
After consumption, the same individual forms a **post-purchase perception** of how the food actually performed. Let this be $P$ on the same scale.

### Step 3 — Define satisfaction as a difference
Satisfaction, by definition in Oliver's 1980 framework, is the **algebraic difference** between perception and expectation. This is because satisfaction is fundamentally a *gap-measured* construct — humans judge experiences relative to their pre-existing standards.

$$
S = P - E
$$

### Step 4 — Classify the three outcomes
We now classify the satisfaction states by analyzing the sign of $S$.

**Case (i) — Positive Disconfirmation:**

$$
P > E \implies S > 0
$$

This represents a state of satisfaction higher than mere confirmation — the consumer is *delighted*. In food terms: the product *exceeded* sensory, nutritional, or value expectations. This drives loyalty.

**Case (ii) — Simple Confirmation:**

$$
P = E \implies S = 0
$$

The product met expectations exactly. The consumer is *neutral*. No strong loyalty is built, but no damage is done. In food industry terms, this is the typical outcome of a "good enough" product.

**Case (iii) — Negative Disconfirmation:**

$$
P < E \implies S < 0
$$

The product failed to meet expectations. The consumer is *dissatisfied*. This drives complaints, returns, negative reviews, and brand switching.

### Step 5 — Aggregate to a population score
For KTU-level analysis, satisfaction is rarely computed for a single person. We extend to a sample of $n$ consumers:

$$
S_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_i - E_i)
$$

### Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A food company launches a new mango drink. 5 consumers were surveyed before and after tasting. The expectation scores and perception scores are:

| Consumer ($i$) | Expectation ($E_i$) | Perception ($P_i$) | $P_i - E_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 7 | 8 | +1 |
| 2 | 6 | 5 | −1 |
| 3 | 8 | 9 | +1 |
| 4 | 5 | 7 | +2 |
| 5 | 9 | 6 | −3 |

Compute the average satisfaction score.

**Solution:**

$$
S_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_i - E_i)
$$

$$
S_{avg} = \dfrac{(+1) + (-1) + (+1) + (+2) + (-3)}{5}
$$

$$
S_{avg} = \dfrac{0}{5} = 0
$$

**Interpretation:** The product meets expectations *on average*, but the high variance (ranging from −3 to +2) signals **inconsistent quality** — a red flag for food product designers. The reformulation must target the *underperforming* sub-segment of consumers whose perception scores fell far below their expectations.

## 3.2 Derivation of Break-Even Volume for a New Food Product

This is a **classic KTU numerical** often asked in the 14-mark section.

### Step 1 — Define the cost identity
Total Cost (TC) of producing $Q$ units is the sum of fixed costs and variable costs:

$$
TC = F + V \cdot Q
$$

Where:
- $F$ = Fixed Cost (machinery, plant rent, R&D amortization) in ₹
- $V$ = Variable Cost per unit (raw materials, packaging, labor) in ₹/unit
- $Q$ = Quantity produced (units)

### Step 2 — Define total revenue
Total Revenue (TR) from selling $Q$ units at price $P$ per unit is:

$$
TR = P \cdot Q
$$

### Step 3 — Define break-even condition
At the break-even point, the company neither makes profit nor loss:

$$
TR = TC
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute and solve for $Q$

$$
P \cdot Q = F + V \cdot Q
$$

$$
P \cdot Q - V \cdot Q = F
$$

$$
Q \cdot (P - V) = F
$$

$$
Q_{BE} = \dfrac{F}{P - V}
$$

The term $(P - V)$ is the **contribution margin** — the amount each unit contributes toward covering fixed costs.

### Step 5 — Worked Example

A food startup plans to launch a millet-based snack. The fixed cost (kitchen setup, initial marketing, FSSAI licensing, packaging design) is **₹2,00,000**. The variable cost per packet is **₹15**. The selling price is **₹40** per packet. Compute the break-even volume.

$$
Q_{BE} = \dfrac{F}{P - V}
$$

$$
Q_{BE} = \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{40 - 15}
$$

$$
Q_{BE} = \dfrac{2{,}00{,}000}{25}
$$

$$
Q_{BE} = 8{,}000 \text{ packets}
$$

**Interpretation:** The startup must sell **8,000 packets** before it begins generating profit. This number is critical for the food product designer — if the addressable market size in the launch city cannot absorb 8,000 packets within the product's shelf life (typically 4–6 months for snacks), the launch is commercially unviable.

## 3.3 The 3×3 Analytical Matrix — Customer vs Consumer Attributes

This matrix is a high-yield KTU analytical tool. Food product designers must score any concept on the following 3×3 grid:

| Attribute | Customer Cares About | Consumer Cares About | Design Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Price** | Yes — primary decision factor | Indirectly | Cost optimization in formulation |
| **Taste** | Indirectly | Yes — primary decision factor | Sensory panel optimization |
| **Packaging** | Yes — shelf appeal, info | Indirectly | Dual-design strategy |
| **Nutrition** | Yes — label claim driven | Sometimes — personal need | Fortification strategy |
| **Convenience** | Sometimes | Yes — daily usage | Single-serve vs family pack |
| **Brand Trust** | Yes — purchase risk reduction | Yes — repeat purchase | Quality consistency |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Customer–Consumer Interaction Flow in Food Product Design

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the complete interaction between customer expectations, consumer experience, and the design feedback loop.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Need Recognition - Hunger or Health Trigger] --> B[Information Search - Labels, Ads, Peers]
    B --> C[Customer Purchases Food Product]
    C --> D[Consumer Uses or Consumes Product]
    D --> E{Expectation vs Perception Check}
    E -->|P greater than E| F[Positive Disconfirmation - Delight]
    E -->|P equals E| G[Simple Confirmation - Neutral]
    E -->|P less than E| H[Negative Disconfirmation - Dissatisfaction]
    F --> I[Repeat Purchase - Loyalty Builds]
    G --> J[May or May Not Repeat]
    H --> K[Returns, Complaints, Brand Switch]
    I --> L[Feedback to Food Product Designer]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Reformulation or Repositioning]
    M --> N[New Product Launch Cycle]
    N --> A
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Customer vs Consumer Decision Channels

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CUST[Customer Side - Purchasing Decision]
        C1[Price Sensitivity]
        C2[Brand Trust]
        C3[Packaging Appeal]
        C4[Label Claims and Certifications]
        C5[Promotional Offers]
    end

    subgraph PROD[Food Product Itself]
        P1[Formulation]
        P2[Process Parameters]
        P3[Packaging Material]
        P4[Shelf Life]
    end

    subgraph CONS[Consumer Side - Consumption Decision]
        K1[Taste and Flavor]
        K2[Texture and Mouthfeel]
        K3[Visual Appearance]
        K4[Convenience of Use]
        K5[Health Impact]
    end

    C1 --> P1
    C2 --> P3
    C3 --> P3
    C4 --> P3
    C5 --> P4

    P1 --> K1
    P2 --> K2
    P3 --> K3
    P4 --> K4
    P1 --> K5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The left block represents *what drives the customer to buy*; the right block represents *what the consumer evaluates during use*. The central product block (PROD) is where the **food product designer must integrate both demands simultaneously**. A formulation that is cheap (good for customer) but tastes bad (bad for consumer) is a commercial failure.

## 4.3 Sequential Map of the EBM Consumer Decision Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Stage 1: Need Recognition] --> S2[Stage 2: Information Search]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3: Evaluation of Alternatives]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4: Purchase Decision - Customer Acts]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5: Post Purchase Evaluation - Consumer Acts]
    S5 --> S6{Outcome}
    S6 -->|Satisfied| S7[Repeat Purchase and Loyalty]
    S6 -->|Dissatisfied| S8[Complaint or Brand Switch]
    S7 --> S9[Word of Mouth Marketing]
    S8 --> S10[Negative Publicity]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
Differentiate between a *customer* and a *consumer* in the context of food product development. Give one example where these two roles are played by different individuals.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *customer* is the entity that purchases a food product by paying money, while a *consumer* is the entity that actually consumes or uses the product. The customer evaluates price, brand, packaging, and label claims; the consumer evaluates taste, texture, nutrition, and satisfaction. 

**Example:** A mother purchasing a jar of Cerelac (customer) for her 8-month-old infant who actually consumes it (consumer). Here, the food product designer must satisfy both — the mother's need for nutrition assurance and the infant's acceptance of taste and texture.

*(Award 1 mark for definition of customer, 1 mark for definition of consumer, 1 mark for example.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
State and explain the Expectancy–Disconfirmation Model of customer satisfaction. What are the three possible outcomes?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Expectancy–Disconfirmation Model, given by Oliver (1980), expresses satisfaction as the difference between perceived performance ($P$) and pre-purchase expectation ($E$):

$$
S = P - E
$$

The three outcomes are:
1. **Positive Disconfirmation** ($P > E$) — product exceeds expectations; consumer is delighted.
2. **Simple Confirmation** ($P = E$) — product meets expectations; consumer is neutral.
3. **Negative Disconfirmation** ($P < E$) — product falls short; consumer is dissatisfied.

*(Award 1 mark for equation, 2 marks for the three outcomes.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the Engel–Blackwell–Miniard (EBM) model of consumer decision-making. Illustrate each stage with a relevant food industry example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A food company introduces a new probiotic yogurt. Fixed cost of launch is ₹3,00,000. Variable cost per cup is ₹18. Selling price per cup is ₹45.
  (i) Compute the break-even volume.
  (ii) If the company sells 12,000 cups in the first quarter, what is the profit?
  (iii) Comment on the commercial viability of this product. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) EBM Model Explanation (7 marks):**

The Engel–Blackwell–Miniard model is a five-stage sequential framework used by food product designers and marketers to understand how consumers reach a purchase and repurchase decision.

**Stage 1 — Need Recognition:** The process begins when the consumer perceives a gap between their current state and a desired state. In food terms, this could be hunger, a health concern (high cholesterol), or a social occasion (a house party).

*Food industry example:* A consumer feeling heavy after a festival meal sees an advertisement for a probiotic curd drink like Yakult and recognizes a digestive health need.

**Stage 2 — Information Search:** The consumer actively or passively gathers information about available alternatives. Sources include internal memory (past experience), peer recommendations, online reviews, advertising, and in-store displays.

*Food industry example:* Before buying oats, the consumer reads nutrition labels, compares prices on BigBasket vs Amazon Fresh, and watches YouTube reviews.

**Stage 3 — Evaluation of Alternatives:** The consumer compares alternatives on a set of evaluative criteria — taste, price, nutrition, brand, packaging, shelf life, convenience.

*Food industry example:* Choosing between Saffola vs Quaker oats based on price, fiber content, and flavor options.

**Stage 4 — Purchase Decision:** The actual transaction occurs. Here, the **customer** acts. Two factors can intervene between intention and action: (i) attitude of others (a family member disapproves), (ii) situational factors (the preferred brand is out of stock).

*Food industry example:* Going to the supermarket and selecting the 1 kg pack over the 500 g pack because of a ₹30 discount.

**Stage 5 — Post-Purchase Behavior:** After consumption, the consumer evaluates the product against expectations. If satisfied, repeat purchase follows; if dissatisfied, cognitive dissonance and brand switching may occur.

*Food industry example:* The consumer enjoys the oats, repeats purchase next month, and recommends the brand to a colleague — completing the loyalty loop.

*(Award 1 mark per stage for the concept, 0.5–1 mark for the food industry example, total 7 marks.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Break-Even Volume:**

Given:
- Fixed Cost $F$ = ₹3,00,000
- Variable Cost per unit $V$ = ₹18
- Selling Price per unit $P$ = ₹45

Using the formula derived in Section 3.2:

$$
Q_{BE} = \dfrac{F}{P - V}
$$

$$
Q_{BE} = \dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{45 - 18}
$$

$$
Q_{BE} = \dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{27}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{BE} = 11{,}111.11 \approx 11{,}112 \text{ cups}}
$$

**(ii) Profit when 12,000 cups are sold:**

Total Revenue:
$$
TR = P \times Q = 45 \times 12{,}000 = 5{,}40{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

Total Cost:
$$
TC = F + V \times Q = 3{,}00{,}000 + 18 \times 12{,}000 = 3{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}16{,}000 = 5{,}16{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

Profit:
$$
\pi = TR - TC = 5{,}40{,}000 - 5{,}16{,}000 = 24{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
\boxed{\pi = ₹24{,}000}
$$

**(iii) Commercial Viability Comment:**

The profit of ₹24,000 on a turnover of ₹5,40,000 gives a profit margin of approximately **4.4%**, which is below the Indian FMCG industry average of 8–12%. However, since the company has crossed the break-even threshold of 11,112 cups in the first quarter, the product is operationally viable. The food product designer should recommend cost reduction strategies (e.g., bulk procurement of milk, automated cup-filling) and price optimization to improve the margin before scaling up.

*(Award 2 marks for BEV, 3 marks for profit calculation, 2 marks for the commercial viability comment.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Discuss the Customer–Consumer Disconnect Model. Using the four-quadrant framework, classify each of the following scenarios and explain the design implications: **(7 marks)**
  1. A college student buying and eating a Maggi cup pack himself.
  2. A father buying Bournvita for his daughter.
  3. A hospital kitchen using bulk dal they did not purchase themselves.

**(b)** Explain Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs as applied to food consumption. For each level, identify one Indian food product example and the design strategy a food product designer should adopt. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Customer–Consumer Disconnect Model (7 marks):**

The Customer–Consumer Disconnect Model recognizes that the person paying for a food product (customer) and the person using it (consumer) are often different entities, and their priorities may diverge or conflict.

**Scenario 1: College student buying Maggi and eating it himself.**
- Customer = Consumer = College Student
- **Quadrant 1** (Same person, same role).
- **Design implication:** The designer can optimize for a single user. Focus on taste, affordability, single-serve convenience, and quick preparation time — directly serving both roles with one design philosophy.

**Scenario 2: Father buying Bournvita for his daughter.**
- Customer = Father
- Consumer = Daughter
- **Quadrant 2** (Different people).
- **Design implication:** The father (customer) cares about brand trust, nutritional claims, FSSAI labeling, price per kg, and family pack economy. The daughter (consumer) cares about chocolate taste, mixability in milk, and visual color. The designer must create packaging that signals health and nutrition to the father (e.g., "growth", "immunity") while ensuring the milk-chocolate taste that the daughter enjoys.

**Scenario 3: Hospital kitchen using bulk dal they did not purchase.**
- Customer = Procurement officer
- Consumer = Hospital kitchen staff (and ultimately patients)
- **Quadrant 2** with a triple-layer chain (Procurement → Kitchen → Patient).
- **Design implication:** The 25 kg dal sack must satisfy the procurement officer (price, FSSAI certification, supplier reliability), the kitchen staff (ease of storage, ease of opening, consistency in cooking), and the patient (taste, digestibility, hygiene). A failure at any layer can cause rejection.

*(Award 1 mark per scenario for correct quadrant identification, 1 mark per scenario for design implication, totaling 6 marks. Award 1 mark for an introductory sentence defining the model.)*

**(b) Maslow's Hierarchy Applied to Food (7 marks):**

**Level 1 — Physiological Needs:**
Food as fuel for survival. Hunger and basic nutrition.
*Indian example:* Basic atta, rice, dal, salt.
*Design strategy:* Maximize caloric density at minimum cost. Focus on bulk supply, basic fortification (iodized salt, fortified atta), and long shelf life. Affordability is the dominant design constraint.

**Level 2 — Safety Needs:**
Food safety, hygiene, absence of contamination.
*Indian example:* Pasteurized Amul Taaza milk in sealed pouches, FSSAI-certified packaged bread.
*Design strategy:* Implement HACCP, ISO 22000, and FSSAI compliance. Packaging must signal safety (tamper-evident seals, expiry dates, batch codes). Cold chain integrity is a critical design parameter.

**Level 3 — Social / Belongingness Needs:**
Food as a vehicle for bonding, sharing, cultural identity.
*Indian example:* Festival sweets like Mysore Pak, family-pack biryani kits, branded restaurant chains.
*Design strategy:* Packaging design should evoke warmth and sharing (family packs, gift boxes). Marketing should focus on occasions (Diwali, weddings, cricket matches).

**Level 4 — Esteem Needs:**
Food as a status symbol, premiumization.
*Indian example:* Belgian chocolate bars, imported cheeses, single-origin coffee.
*Design strategy:* Premium packaging materials (rigid boxes, foil stamping, embossed logos), small batch production, gourmet positioning, higher price points justified by provenance and craftsmanship.

**Level 5 — Self-Actualization:**
Food aligned with personal values, ethics, sustainability.
*Indian example:* Organic millets, vegan dairy alternatives, fair-trade coffee.
*Design strategy:* Transparent sourcing stories, certifications (India Organic, USDA Organic, Fairtrade), eco-friendly packaging, minimal processing, clean labels. Marketing must educate and inspire, not just sell.

*(Award 1 mark per level for example, 1 mark per level for design strategy = 10 slots for 7 marks; award full credit for 5 well-answered levels.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic:**
> 
> 1. **Conflating customer and consumer** — Many students write as if the two are identical. Examiners will deduct up to **2 marks** if you do not clearly distinguish the *purchasing* role from the *consuming* role.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — When computing Break-Even Volume, students often write "11111.11" without the unit "cups" or "packets". This loses **0.5 to 1 mark** automatically.
> 
> 3. **Skipping the commercial interpretation** — After a numerical answer, examiners expect a 2–3 sentence comment on whether the result is commercially viable. Skipping this loses **1 to 2 marks**.
> 
> 4. **Listing Maslow's levels without food context** — Simply stating "Physiological, Safety, Love, Esteem, Self-Actualization" without giving a *food product example* for each level will fetch only partial credit (at most **50% marks** for that sub-question).
> 
> 5. **Ignoring the Customer–Consumer Disconnect in design questions** — If the question asks about a specific food product (e.g., baby food, pet food, hospital meals) and your answer treats customer and consumer as one person, you will lose significant marks because the question implicitly tests your understanding of the disconnect.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Customer ≠ Consumer.** Customer is the *purchaser*; consumer is the *user*. This distinction is the foundation of Module 1.
- **Customer–Consumer Disconnect** is the gap between what the buyer demands and what the user desires; ignoring it causes product failure.
- The **EBM Model** (5 stages) — Need Recognition → Information Search → Evaluation → Purchase Decision → Post-Purchase Behavior — is the most frequently tested framework in KTU exams.
- **Oliver's Expectancy–Disconfirmation Model** is expressed as $S = P - E$. The three outcomes are positive disconfirmation ($S > 0$), simple confirmation ($S = 0$), and negative disconfirmation ($S < 0$).
- **Maslow's Hierarchy** applied to food: Physiological → Safety → Social → Esteem → Self-Actualization. Each level requires a different food design strategy.
- **Break-Even Volume** formula $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{F}{P - V}$ is a high-yield numerical; always show units in the final answer.
- **Repeat Purchase Rate (RPR ≥ 30%)** and **Net Promoter Score (NPS ≥ 40)** are industry benchmarks for new food product success.
- The **four-quadrant framework** (Same/Different × Customer/Consumer) helps classify any food product scenario and tailor the design.
- In **B2B food markets** (institutional, industrial), the customer is rarely the consumer; in **B2C markets**, the customer and consumer are usually the same.
- Always end numerical answers with a **commercial viability comment** — this is what distinguishes a KTU full-marks answer from a partial-credit answer.
- Always cite the **food industry example** when explaining theoretical frameworks — abstract definitions without context will lose marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Value Addition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT834 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Value Addition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Value Addition** in the context of food product design and development is the process of enhancing the economic, nutritional, functional, sensory, or convenience value of a raw agricultural commodity or primary food material through the application of scientific, technological, and managerial interventions, thereby increasing its market worth and consumer desirability.

In formal KTU terminology aligned with the 2024 Scheme syllabus:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Value Addition (KTU Definition):** A systematic transformation of a primary agricultural produce into a processed, semi-processed, or refined food product that possesses enhanced shelf-life, improved nutritional profile, superior sensory attributes, better packaging, and greater consumer convenience, leading to a measurable increase in monetary worth at every node of the food value chain.

Mathematically, the concept can be represented as:

$$V_{added} = V_{final} - V_{raw} - C_{processing}$$

Where:
- $V_{added}$ = Value Added (monetary units/kg)
- $V_{final}$ = Market price of finished product (₹/kg)
- $V_{raw}$ = Price of raw commodity (₹/kg)
- $C_{processing}$ = Total cost of processing, packaging, and labor (₹/kg)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you buy a **raw mango** from a local farmer for ₹20 per kilogram. The mango is perishable, dirty, seasonal, and cannot be consumed immediately. Now, if you wash, peel, slice, dry, and pack that same mango as dried mango chips in attractive food-grade pouches, you can sell the finished product for ₹400 per kilogram.

The mango is **chemically the same fruit**, but its **perceived value, shelf-life, usability, and market price have multiplied several times**. This transformation is the essence of value addition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** A lump of clay is worth ₹10. A skilled artisan shapes the same clay into a beautiful decorative idol worth ₹500. The raw material cost is identical — it is the *artistry, design, and finishing* that adds value. Food value addition operates on the exact same principle — the raw ingredient is constant, but processing, formulation, packaging, and branding add layers of worth.

## 1.3 Why Value Addition is Critical in Food Science

The Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) estimates that approximately **30–40%** of food produced globally is lost or wasted due to inadequate post-harvest handling and lack of value addition infrastructure. Value addition directly addresses three major challenges:

1. **Post-harvest losses** — processed products have longer shelf life.
2. **Farmer income** — primary producers earn 3× to 10× more from processed goods.
3. **Consumer convenience** — ready-to-eat, ready-to-cook formats fit modern lifestyles.

The standard benchmark metric used by food economists is the **Value Addition Ratio (VAR)**:

$$VAR = \frac{V_{final}}{V_{raw}}$$

A VAR greater than **2.0** is generally considered commercially viable for small-scale food enterprises.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** Examiners frequently test the difference between *primary processing* (cleaning, grading) and *secondary/tertiary processing* (formulation, packaging). Remember that **all forms of value addition are not equal** — tertiary processing with branding yields the highest VAR.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Value Addition

The value addition process in food product development follows a structured sequential flow:

- **Step 1 — Raw Material Selection**
  Identify the primary agricultural commodity. The choice determines the entire downstream processing pathway. Why? Because the intrinsic composition (moisture, sugar, fiber, fat) dictates what transformations are physically and chemically feasible.

- **Step 2 — Primary Processing**
  Cleaning, sorting, grading, peeling, trimming, washing. This removes impurities and standardizes the input. How? Mechanical separation, air classification, water flotation.

- **Step 3 — Secondary Processing**
  Conversion into intermediate or finished products via thermal, mechanical, or biotechnological means — e.g., drying, milling, fermentation, pasteurization, extrusion.

- **Step 4 — Tertiary Processing / Formulation**
  Blending with ingredients, fortification, flavoring, enrichment, and standardization of recipe to meet nutritional and sensory targets.

- **Step 5 — Packaging and Labeling**
  Primary (product contact), secondary (display), and tertiary (transport) packaging. Labeling must comply with **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)** regulations.

- **Step 6 — Branding and Marketing**
  Establishing product identity, pricing strategy, distribution channels, and consumer positioning.

- **Step 7 — Quality Assurance and Shelf-Life Validation**
  Microbial, chemical, and sensory testing to ensure regulatory compliance and consumer safety.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Value Added (VA) | $V_{added} = V_{final} - V_{raw} - C_{processing}$ | Net monetary gain per unit mass | ₹/kg |
| Value Addition Ratio (VAR) | $VAR = \dfrac{V_{final}}{V_{raw}}$ | Multiplier on raw material price | Dimensionless; VAR > 2.0 is viable |
| Gross Profit Margin | $GPM = \dfrac{V_{added}}{V_{final}} \times 100$ | Percentage profit over final price | Expressed as % |
| Processing Cost Ratio | $PCR = \dfrac{C_{processing}}{V_{final}} \times 100$ | Share of processing cost in final price | Expressed as % |
| Shelf-Life Extension Factor | $SLEF = \dfrac{S_{processed}}{S_{raw}}$ | Ratio of processed to raw shelf life | Dimensionless |
| Energy Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{useful}}{E_{input}} \times 100$ | Energy retained in final product | Expressed as % |
| Cost of Value Addition | $C_{va} = C_l + C_e + C_m + C_p + C_d$ | Sum of labor, energy, material, packaging, distribution | ₹/kg |

Where:
- $C_l$ = Labor cost
- $C_e$ = Energy cost
- $C_m$ = Material (ingredient) cost
- $C_p$ = Packaging cost
- $C_d$ = Distribution cost

## 2.3 Classification of Value Addition

Value addition is classified into **four hierarchical levels**, each progressively delivering higher economic returns.

| Level | Type | Examples | Typical VAR Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Level I | Primary Processing | Cleaning, grading, sorting, packaging of fresh produce | 1.2 – 1.5 |
| Level II | Secondary Processing | Milling, drying, freezing, pasteurization | 1.5 – 3.0 |
| Level III | Tertiary Processing | Formulation, fortification, ready-to-eat products | 3.0 – 6.0 |
| Level IV | Quaternary Processing | Branding, certification, organic labeling, export-grade finishing | 6.0 – 15.0 |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In food engineering, the value addition concept directly informs decisions in:

- **Process line design** — selecting equipment (driers, extruders, fermenters) that maximize VAR.
- **Supply chain optimization** — minimizing post-harvest losses through cold chain integration.
- **Product development strategy** — identifying "value-adding" features (e.g., gluten-free, high-protein, low-sugar) that justify premium pricing.
- **Regulatory compliance** — FSSAI labeling mandates nutritional and shelf-life declarations, which are themselves value-adding informational layers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In KTU examinations, students often confuse *value addition* with *value creation*. Value addition transforms an existing product, while value creation invents an entirely new product category. The former is the focus of Module 1.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem — Value Addition in Mango Pulp Processing

To cement the theoretical understanding, let us work through a complete KTU-style numerical problem covering the full value addition chain.

**Problem Statement:**
A small-scale food processing unit purchases raw mangoes at **₹30/kg**. The unit processes the mangoes into mango pulp and subsequently into a packaged mango drink. The following data is provided:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Raw mango purchase price | ₹30/kg |
| Mass yield of pulp from raw mango | 60% |
| Mass yield of mango drink from pulp | 80% |
| Cost of primary processing (peeling, pulping) | ₹5/kg raw mango |
| Cost of secondary processing (pasteurization, sugar addition) | ₹15/kg pulp |
| Cost of packaging (per kg of finished drink) | ₹20/kg |
| Selling price of finished mango drink | ₹250/kg |

**Step 1 — Calculate the yield chain (mass balance).**

Starting with 1000 kg of raw mango, we trace the mass through each processing stage.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{pulp} &= M_{raw} \times Y_{raw \to pulp} \\
M_{pulp} &= 1000 \times 0.60 \\
M_{pulp} &= 600 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Explanation: A 1000 kg batch of raw mango yields 600 kg of pulp at 60% extraction efficiency.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{drink} &= M_{pulp} \times Y_{pulp \to drink} \\
M_{drink} &= 600 \times 0.80 \\
M_{drink} &= 480 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Explanation: 600 kg of pulp produces 480 kg of finished mango drink at 80% conversion efficiency.

**Step 2 — Calculate the cost of raw material per kg of finished drink.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{raw,per\_kg\_drink} &= \frac{M_{raw}}{M_{drink}} \times P_{raw} \\
C_{raw,per\_kg\_drink} &= \frac{1000}{480} \times 30 \\
C_{raw,per\_kg\_drink} &= 62.50 \text{ ₹/kg drink}
\end{aligned}
$$

Explanation: To produce 1 kg of finished mango drink, 2.083 kg of raw mango is consumed, costing ₹62.50.

**Step 3 — Calculate the cost of primary processing per kg of finished drink.**

Primary processing cost is incurred on raw mango, so it must also be scaled by the same ratio.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{primary,per\_kg\_drink} &= \frac{M_{raw}}{M_{drink}} \times C_{primary} \\
C_{primary,per\_kg\_drink} &= \frac{1000}{480} \times 5 \\
C_{primary,per\_kg\_drink} &= 10.42 \text{ ₹/kg drink}
\end{aligned}
$$

Explanation: For each kg of finished drink, ₹10.42 of primary processing is attributable.

**Step 4 — Calculate the cost of secondary processing per kg of finished drink.**

Secondary processing is applied to pulp, so we use the pulp-to-drink ratio.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{secondary,per\_kg\_drink} &= \frac{M_{pulp}}{M_{drink}} \times C_{secondary} \\
C_{secondary,per\_kg\_drink} &= \frac{600}{480} \times 15 \\
C_{secondary,per\_kg\_drink} &= 18.75 \text{ ₹/kg drink}
\end{aligned}
$$

Explanation: Secondary processing on pulp accumulates to ₹18.75 per kg of finished drink.

**Step 5 — Calculate total cost per kg of finished drink.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{total} &= C_{raw} + C_{primary} + C_{secondary} + C_{packaging} \\
C_{total} &= 62.50 + 10.42 + 18.75 + 20.00 \\
C_{total} &= 111.67 \text{ ₹/kg drink}
\end{aligned}
$$

Explanation: The total cost stack for producing 1 kg of mango drink is ₹111.67.

**Step 6 — Calculate Value Added (VA) per kg of finished drink.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{added} &= V_{final} - V_{raw,equivalent} - C_{processing} \\
V_{added} &= 250 - 62.50 - (10.42 + 18.75 + 20.00) \\
V_{added} &= 250 - 62.50 - 49.17 \\
V_{added} &= 138.33 \text{ ₹/kg drink}
\end{aligned}
$$

Explanation: The net value added per kg of finished drink is ₹138.33.

**Step 7 — Calculate Value Addition Ratio (VAR).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
VAR &= \frac{V_{final}}{V_{raw,per\_kg\_drink}} \\
VAR &= \frac{250}{62.50} \\
VAR &= 4.0
\end{aligned}
$$

Explanation: The finished product sells for 4 times the raw material cost on an equivalent basis. Since VAR > 2.0, this venture is **commercially viable**.

**Step 8 — Calculate Gross Profit Margin (GPM).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GPM &= \frac{V_{added}}{V_{final}} \times 100 \\
GPM &= \frac{138.33}{250} \times 100 \\
GPM &= 55.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Explanation: 55.33% of the final selling price is contributed by value addition activities.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Value Addition Decision Framework

The following matrix helps in deciding whether a given transformation qualifies as a meaningful value addition:

| Decision Criterion | Low Value Addition | High Value Addition |
|---|---|---|
| Value Addition Ratio (VAR) | < 1.5 | > 3.0 |
| Shelf-Life Extension | < 2× | > 5× |
| Nutritional Enhancement | None | Fortified, enriched |
| Market Reach | Local | National / Export |
| Consumer Convenience | Bulk, raw | Ready-to-eat, single-serve |
| Branding / Certification | Unbranded | FSSAI, AGMARK, Organic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** When asked to "evaluate the level of value addition," do not answer in a single sentence. Always present the calculation of VAR and GPM, then map the product to the Level I–IV classification table. This three-step answer structure scores full marks in KTU board evaluations.

## 3.3 Mass Balance — The Foundation of Value Addition Mathematics

Every value addition calculation rests on accurate mass balance. The general chain is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{final} &= M_{raw} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i \\
C_{raw,per\_kg\_final} &= \frac{P_{raw}}{M_{final}/M_{raw}} = \frac{P_{raw}}{\prod Y_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $Y_i$ is the yield of the $i^{th}$ processing stage. The cumulative cost of processing on raw material is:

$$
C_{proc,raw-equivalent} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left( C_j \times \frac{M_{input,j}}{M_{final}} \right)$$

This formula is the **cornerstone of food process economics** and is tested repeatedly in KTU university examinations.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Value Addition Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The complete value addition chain from raw commodity to finished consumer product is illustrated below.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Agricultural Commodity] --> B[Primary Processing]
    B --> B1[Cleaning]
    B --> B2[Sorting]
    B --> B3[Grading]
    B --> B4[Packaging of Fresh Produce]
    B1 --> C[Secondary Processing]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> C1[Drying]
    C --> C2[Milling]
    C --> C3[Fermentation]
    C --> C4[Thermal Processing]
    C1 --> D[Tertiary Processing]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> D1[Formulation]
    D --> D2[Fortification]
    D --> D3[Flavor Engineering]
    D1 --> E[Quaternary Processing]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> E1[Branding]
    E --> E2[Certification]
    E --> E3[Labeling and Packaging]
    E1 --> F[Finished Value-Added Product]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Market Distribution and Consumer]
    G --> H[Value Realization]
    H --> I[Feedback Loop for Product Improvement]
    I --> A
```

## 4.2 Value Chain Economics Architecture

This block diagram maps the economic flow and cost accumulation across the value chain, showing how raw material cost is progressively transformed into finished product price.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Raw Material Cost: V_raw] --> P1[Primary Processing Cost: C_p1]
    P1 --> P2[Secondary Processing Cost: C_p2]
    P2 --> P3[Formulation Cost: C_f]
    P3 --> P4[Packaging Cost: C_pkg]
    P4 --> P5[Distribution and Marketing: C_dist]
    P5 --> F[Final Market Price: V_final]
    F --> VA[Value Added: V_final minus V_raw minus C_total]
    VA --> PROF[Profit and Commercial Viability Check: VAR greater than 2.0]
```

## 4.3 Classification Hierarchy of Value Addition

This hierarchical diagram represents the four-tier classification system used in food product development economics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Level 1: Primary Processing] --> L1A[Cleaning, Sorting, Grading]
    L1 --> L1B[VAR Range: 1.2 to 1.5]
    L2[Level 2: Secondary Processing] --> L2A[Drying, Milling, Fermentation]
    L2 --> L2B[VAR Range: 1.5 to 3.0]
    L3[Level 3: Tertiary Processing] --> L3A[Formulation, Fortification, RTE Products]
    L3 --> L3B[VAR Range: 3.0 to 6.0]
    L4[Level 4: Quaternary Processing] --> L4A[Branding, Certification, Export Finishing]
    L4 --> L4B[VAR Range: 6.0 to 15.0]
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** In the Mermaid block above, the four levels are stacked in ascending order of economic value. Each arrow represents the cumulative value addition achieved at that stage. The VAR ranges provide a quick quantitative benchmark for KTU numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define the term **value addition** in the context of food product development. List any **four** benefits of value addition to the primary producer.

**Model Answer:**

Value addition is the process of enhancing the economic, nutritional, functional, or sensory worth of a raw agricultural commodity through processing, packaging, formulation, and branding interventions. The processed product commands a higher market price than the raw commodity, thereby generating surplus value.

**Four benefits to the primary producer:**

1. **Higher income realization** — processed goods sell at 3× to 10× the raw commodity price.
2. **Reduction of post-harvest losses** — value-added products have extended shelf life, reducing spoilage.
3. **Market diversification** — processed products access urban, retail, and export markets that fresh produce cannot penetrate.
4. **Employment generation** — processing units create rural employment, especially for women and small entrepreneurs.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for benefits)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **primary processing** and **secondary processing** of foods with one suitable example for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Primary Processing | Secondary Processing |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Minimal handling to clean, sort, and grade raw produce | Conversion into intermediate or finished products through thermal, mechanical, or biological means |
| Objective | Standardization and loss reduction | Transformation of form and extension of shelf life |
| Examples | Washing and grading of fresh tomatoes; sorting of rice grains | Drying of grapes into raisins; pasteurization of milk |
| Value Addition Level | Level I (VAR 1.2 – 1.5) | Level II (VAR 1.5 – 3.0) |

*(3 marks: 1 for differentiation table, 1 each for examples)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Module 1 Choice Question]**

**(a)** Explain the **four levels of value addition** in food processing with suitable examples for each level. Compute the **Value Addition Ratio (VAR)** for a product where the raw material costs ₹40/kg and the finished product sells at ₹180/kg. Comment on its commercial viability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A food processing unit converts raw tomatoes into tomato ketchup. Given the data below, calculate the **Value Added per kg of ketchup** and the **Gross Profit Margin (GPM)**. **(7 marks)**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Raw tomato price | ₹20/kg |
| Yield of pulp from raw tomato | 75% |
| Yield of ketchup from pulp | 65% |
| Primary processing cost (per kg raw tomato) | ₹4 |
| Secondary processing cost (per kg pulp) | ₹12 |
| Packaging cost (per kg ketchup) | ₹18 |
| Selling price of ketchup | ₹220/kg |

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Four Levels of Value Addition**

The four hierarchical levels of value addition are:

- **Level I — Primary Processing:** Involves cleaning, washing, sorting, grading, and minimal packaging of fresh produce. *Example:* Sorting and grading of apples for retail sale. VAR typically ranges from 1.2 to 1.5.

- **Level II — Secondary Processing:** Involves physical, chemical, or biological conversion of raw materials into shelf-stable intermediates. *Example:* Conversion of milk into cheese or milk powder. VAR ranges from 1.5 to 3.0.

- **Level III — Tertiary Processing:** Involves formulation, fortification, and creation of ready-to-eat or ready-to-cook products. *Example:* Manufacture of fortified breakfast cereals or instant noodles. VAR ranges from 3.0 to 6.0.

- **Level IV — Quaternary Processing:** Involves branding, certification (organic, GI tag, FSSAI), export-grade packaging, and market positioning. *Example:* Premium export-grade Darjeeling tea with certified packaging. VAR ranges from 6.0 to 15.0.

**VAR Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
VAR &= \frac{V_{final}}{V_{raw}} \\
VAR &= \frac{180}{40} \\
VAR &= 4.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment on Commercial Viability:** Since VAR = 4.5, which is significantly greater than the commercial viability threshold of 2.0, the product is **highly commercially viable** and falls in the Level III (tertiary) category of value addition.

*(7 marks: 4 for levels with examples, 2 for VAR calculation, 1 for viability comment)*

---

**Part (b) — Value Added and GPM Calculation for Tomato Ketchup**

**Step 1 — Mass Balance for 1000 kg of raw tomato.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{pulp} &= 1000 \times 0.75 = 750 \text{ kg} \\
M_{ketchup} &= 750 \times 0.65 = 487.5 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Cost of raw material per kg of ketchup.**

$$
C_{raw,per\_kg} = \frac{1000}{487.5} \times 20 = 41.03 \text{ ₹/kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Primary processing cost per kg of ketchup.**

$$
C_{primary,per\_kg} = \frac{1000}{487.5} \times 4 = 8.21 \text{ ₹/kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Secondary processing cost per kg of ketchup.**

$$
C_{secondary,per\_kg} = \frac{750}{487.5} \times 12 = 18.46 \text{ ₹/kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Total cost per kg of ketchup.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{total} &= 41.03 + 8.21 + 18.46 + 18.00 \\
C_{total} &= 85.70 \text{ ₹/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Value Added per kg.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{added} &= V_{final} - C_{total} \\
V_{added} &= 220 - 85.70 \\
V_{added} &= 134.30 \text{ ₹/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Gross Profit Margin (GPM).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GPM &= \frac{134.30}{220} \times 100 \\
GPM &= 61.05\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The value added is ₹134.30 per kg of ketchup, and the gross profit margin is **61.05%**, indicating a highly profitable value addition venture.

*(7 marks: 2 for mass balance, 3 for cost calculations, 2 for VA and GPM)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Module 1 Choice Question]**

**(a)** Discuss the **role of packaging and branding** as value addition tools in food product development. Support your answer with **three examples** of branded value-added food products available in the Indian market. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dairy unit processes raw milk into ghee. Given the following data, calculate the **Value Addition Ratio (VAR)**, **Value Added (VA) per kg of ghee**, and the **Processing Cost Ratio (PCR)**. **(7 marks)**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Raw milk price | ₹50/litre |
| Density of milk | 1.03 kg/litre |
| Yield of ghee from milk | 6.25% |
| Processing cost (per kg of ghee) | ₹120 |
| Selling price of ghee | ₹800/kg |

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Role of Packaging and Branding**

Packaging and branding are powerful **tertiary and quaternary** value addition tools. They do not alter the chemical composition of the food, but they fundamentally transform the **perceived value** and **market accessibility** of the product.

- **Functional role of packaging:** Protection from physical, chemical, microbial, and environmental damage; extension of shelf life; provision of nutritional and regulatory information; convenience features like resealable zippers, single-serve pouches, and microwave-safe containers.

- **Commercial role of branding:** Establishes consumer trust, enables premium pricing, supports product differentiation in crowded markets, and creates brand equity that can be leveraged across product lines.

- **Three examples of branded Indian value-added products:**
  1. **Amul Butter** — branded dairy product commanding premium over loose white butter.
  2. **MTR Ready-to-Eat Meals** — branded convenience foods with extended shelf life.
  3. **Tata Sampann** — branded, FSSAI-certified pulses and spices with traceability assurance.

*(7 marks: 3 for packaging role, 2 for branding role, 2 for examples)*

---

**Part (b) — VAR, VA, and PCR for Ghee**

**Step 1 — Convert raw milk price to per kg basis.**

$$
P_{milk,kg} = \frac{50}{1.03} = 48.54 \text{ ₹/kg}
$$

Explanation: Since milk density is 1.03 kg/L, 1 L of milk weighs 1.03 kg, so the price per kg of milk is ₹50 divided by 1.03.

**Step 2 — Calculate cost of raw milk per kg of ghee.**

$$
C_{raw,per\_kg\_ghee} = \frac{P_{milk,kg}}{Y_{ghee}} = \frac{48.54}{0.0625} = 776.70 \text{ ₹/kg}
$$

Explanation: To produce 1 kg of ghee, 16 kg of milk is required (since yield is 6.25%). At ₹48.54 per kg, the raw material cost per kg of ghee is ₹776.70.

**Step 3 — Calculate Value Addition Ratio (VAR).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
VAR &= \frac{V_{final}}{V_{raw,per\_kg}} \\
VAR &= \frac{800}{776.70} \\
VAR &= 1.03
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Value Added (VA) per kg of ghee.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{added} &= V_{final} - V_{raw,per\_kg} - C_{processing} \\
V_{added} &= 800 - 776.70 - 120 \\
V_{added} &= -96.70 \text{ ₹/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate Processing Cost Ratio (PCR).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PCR &= \frac{C_{processing}}{V_{final}} \times 100 \\
PCR &= \frac{120}{800} \times 100 \\
PCR &= 15.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The negative value added (–₹96.70/kg) and VAR of 1.03 indicate that the ghee processing unit is currently **operating at a loss**. The high raw material cost of milk (97% of the final price) is the primary constraint. The unit can restore profitability by either negotiating lower milk prices, improving ghee yield through better technology, or commanding a higher selling price for branded, certified organic ghee.

*(7 marks: 1 for density conversion, 2 for raw cost per kg ghee, 2 for VA and VAR, 2 for PCR and interpretation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Loss Points:**
>
> 1. **Density conversion error (–2 marks):** Students often forget to convert milk price from ₹/L to ₹/kg using the density. Always check the unit consistency before any calculation.
>
> 2. **Missing mass balance scaling (–2 marks):** When processing costs are incurred at different stages, students forget to scale them by the appropriate mass ratio. Primary processing on raw material must be scaled by $M_{raw}/M_{final}$, secondary processing on intermediate by $M_{intermediate}/M_{final}$.
>
> 3. **Not interpreting the result (–1 mark):** A numerical answer without a concluding statement on commercial viability (VAR vs threshold of 2.0) is considered incomplete by KTU board examiners.
>
> 4. **Confusing VA and GPM (–1 mark):** Value Added (VA) is in ₹/kg (absolute), while GPM is in %. Mixing units or interchanging formulas results in mark deduction.
>
> 5. **Ignoring negative VA interpretation (–2 marks in Question B):** When VA is negative, students often stop at the calculation. KTU examiners expect a brief explanation of *why* the value addition failed and *how* it can be improved.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Value addition = enhancing economic, nutritional, sensory, or convenience worth of a raw commodity through processing and presentation.
- **Core Formula:** $V_{added} = V_{final} - V_{raw} - C_{processing}$.
- **Commercial Benchmark:** VAR = $V_{final} / V_{raw}$ must exceed **2.0** for commercial viability.
- **Four Levels:** Primary (cleaning, grading) → Secondary (drying, milling) → Tertiary (formulation, RTE) → Quaternary (branding, certification).
- **VAR Ranges by Level:** Level I: 1.2–1.5, Level II: 1.5–3.0, Level III: 3.0–6.0, Level IV: 6.0–15.0.
- **Mass Balance First:** Always establish the mass flow $M_{raw} \to M_{final}$ before any cost calculation. The yield chain governs all downstream economics.
- **Cost Scaling Rule:** Every cost incurred at stage $j$ on mass $M_j$ must be scaled by $M_j / M_{final}$ to be expressed per kg of finished product.
- **GPM Interpretation:** A GPM above **40%** is considered strong; above **60%** is excellent for food products.
- **Negative VA:** A negative value added signals that raw material cost plus processing cost exceeds the selling price — a loss-making venture requiring intervention.
- **Regulatory Anchor:** All value-added food products in India must comply with **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India)** labeling and safety regulations (2011, as amended).
- **Unit Consistency:** Always check whether prices are quoted per kg, per litre, or per piece. Use density (e.g., milk: 1.03 kg/L) for unit conversion before calculation.
- **KTU-Favorite Topics for Module 1:** Definition, four levels, examples at each level, VAR/GPM/PCR calculations, and interpretation of commercial viability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Marketing Characteristics of New Products, Product Life Cycle and Profit Picture, Corporate Avenues for Growth and Profitability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a "New Product" in Food Marketing?

In the context of **Food Product Design and Development**, the marketing definition of a "new product" is far broader than simply inventing a never-before-seen item. According to KTU-aligned marketing theory (rooted in Kotler's framework adopted by Indian FMCG majors like ITC, Britannia, and Nestlé India), any product that a **potential customer perceives as new** qualifies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (New Product):**
> A new product is any good, service, or idea that is perceived by a target customer as being new in any of its dimensions — packaging, formulation, positioning, brand, or usage occasion. *Newness is in the eye of the consumer, not the laboratory.*

### The 6 Degrees of "Newness" (Booz, Allen & Hamilton Classification)

Food companies classify new products along a continuum of novelty:

1. **New-to-the-World Products (Discontinuous Innovations):** Genuinely novel (e.g., the world's first plant-based "egg" from Beyond Meat entering the Indian market).
2. **New Product Lines:** A firm enters an established category for the first time (e.g., Parle launching a premium cookie line when it was known for biscuits).
3. **Additions to Existing Product Lines:** Line extensions (e.g., Maggi adding Whole Wheat variants).
4. **Improvements/Revisions of Existing Products:** Reformulation or quality upgrades (e.g., reducing sodium content in chips).
5. **Repositioning:** Targeting a new market segment with the same product (e.g., marketing oats as a weight-loss food rather than a breakfast cereal).
6. **Cost Reductions:** New products offering equivalent performance at lower cost (economy packs of established food brands).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Question papers often test whether a student understands that even a change in *packaging* (e.g., tetra-pack milk replacing glass bottles) can be marketed as a "new product."

## 1.2 Marketing Characteristics of New Products — Rogers' Five Attributes

When launching any new food product, its rate of **adoption** by consumers (the diffusion of innovation) depends on five perceived characteristics, originally proposed by **Everett M. Rogers (1962)** and highly applicable to FMCG/Food marketing:

| # | Characteristic | Meaning in Food Context | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Relative Advantage** | Perceived superiority over existing alternatives | Probiotic curd claiming better gut health than regular curd |
| 2 | **Compatibility** | Fit with consumer lifestyle, culture, and habits | Ready-to-eat idli batter for working urbanites |
| 3 | **Complexity** | Difficulty of understanding or using the product | QR-code traced organic honey — initially confusing |
| 4 | **Trialability** | Ease of trying the product in small quantities | Single-serve sachets of kheer mix |
| 5 | **Observability** | Visibility of benefits to others | Visible whey separation in paneer (negative observability) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The *first three* (Advantage, Compatibility, Complexity) typically account for **~70% of adoption variance** in food categories. Lower complexity and higher compatibility accelerate adoption dramatically.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The "Dosa Tiffin" Story

Imagine **Amma's Dosa Tiffin** in Chennai has been selling traditional dosa for 50 years (an "established product"). Now imagine the owner, Arun, decides to sell **"Ragi Dosa Premix"** — a new product.

- Arun's customers (busy IT professionals) perceive **convenience** (Relative Advantage) ✓
- It **fits** their South Indian identity (Compatibility) ✓
- Just add water and make (Low Complexity) ✓
- Small trial packs available (Trialability) ✓
- Health benefits visible in 2 weeks — family notices (Observability) ✓

**Result:** Rapid adoption. Arun's PLC accelerates through Introduction → Growth in 9 months.

This mirrors every successful Indian food launch — from **Slim Dosa** to **Too Yumm!** snacks.
