**STAGE 3 — Concept Development & Business Case (Build the Case)**
- Activities: Detailed consumer research, concept testing, sensory trials, prototype development, full P&L, manufacturing plan.

**GATE 3 — Development Decision (Go-to-Development)**
- Decision: Commit to product, package, and process development.

**STAGE 4 — Development & Testing**
- Activities: Formulation finalization, pilot plant trials, shelf-life testing, packaging line trials, in-home use tests (IHUT), central location tests (CLT).

**GATE 4 — Launch Decision (Go-to-Market)**
- Decision: Final go/no-go for commercialization.

**STAGE 5 — Launch & Post-Launch**
- Activities: Market introduction, distribution rollout, trade promotion, post-launch tracking, consumer feedback loops back to Stage 1.

### Why the Gates Exist — The "Why" Behind the Model

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core Logic of Gates:**
> Each gate is a **financial and strategic checkpoint**. The fundamental purpose is to *fail fast and fail cheap* — kill weak ideas early when sunk costs are low (₹5 lakh at Gate 1) rather than late when sunk costs are catastrophic (₹5 crore at Gate 5). This dramatically improves **NPV-weighted portfolio return**.

## 2.3 Voice of Customer (VOC) Methodologies

VOC is the **upstream nervous system** of market-oriented NPD. Five canonical VOC techniques are mandatory for KTU:

1. **Focus Groups** — Moderated 8–10 participant discussions; excellent for ideation, weak for quantification.
2. **In-Depth Interviews (IDI)** — One-on-one ethnographic deep dives; rich qualitative data.
3. **Surveys / Questionnaires** — Quantifies preferences, demographics, frequencies.
4. **Observational Research** — Watching consumers in real shopping/cooking contexts.
5. **Lead User Method (Eric von Hippel)** — Engaging extreme users whose needs foreshadow the mass market (e.g., urban marathon runners → mass sports nutrition products).

## 2.4 Quality Function Deployment (QFD) and the House of Quality

QFD is a structured planning matrix that translates **WHATs (customer requirements)** into **HOWs (engineering/technical specifications)** while tracking competitive positioning. The matrix is famously called the **House of Quality (HOQ)** because of its roof-shape.

### The Six Rooms of the House of Quality

| Room | Content | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Customer Voice (Whats) | VOC statements e.g., "low in sugar" | Voice of Customer |
| Importance Rating | Customer-weighted priorities (1–5 scale) | Prioritization |
| Technical Descriptors (Hows) | Engineering specs e.g., sugar ≤ 5 g/100 g | Translation |
| Relationship Matrix | Symbols: ● strong = 9, ○ medium = 3, △ weak = 1 | Correlation |
| Roof (Correlation Matrix) | +, –, or blank showing synergy/conflict between Hows | Technical tradeoffs |
| Competitive Benchmarks | 1–5 score of our product vs. competitors | Positioning |

### KTU Formula Sheet — House of Quality Computation

> [!NOTE]
> **All KTU-tested QFD numericals use these four equations.**

Let $W_i$ be the customer importance weight of the $i^{th}$ *What*, and $R_{ij}$ be the relationship score between the $i^{th}$ *What* and the $j^{th}$ *How*. The absolute and relative technical priorities are computed as:

$$
\text{Absolute Priority of How}_j \;=\; P_j^{\text{abs}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \times R_{ij}
$$

$$
\text{Relative Priority of How}_j \;=\; P_j^{\text{rel}} \;=\; \frac{P_j^{\text{abs}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{m} P_j^{\text{abs}}} \times 100\%
$$

The technical difficulty / cost weight $\theta_j$ for each How is used to derive a **value-to-effort ratio** that guides R&D resource allocation:

$$
\text{Value-to-Effort Ratio}_j \;=\; \frac{P_j^{\text{rel}}}{\theta_j}
$$

Finally, the **Sales Point** column captures how each What is expected to influence purchase intent (multiplied 1.5× for *must-be* attributes — see Kano model below):

$$
\text{Adjusted Importance}_i \;=\; W_i \times S_i \times \begin{cases} 1.5 & \text{if Must-be (M)} \\ 1.0 & \text{if One-dimensional (O)} \\ 0.5 & \text{if Attractive (A)} \end{cases}
$$

where $S_i$ is the planned sales-point weight (1.0 or 1.5).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Symbols in the Relationship Matrix (KTU Convention)**
> - ● (filled circle) = Strong relationship → score **9**
> - ○ (open circle) = Medium relationship → score **3**
> - △ (open triangle) = Weak relationship → score **1**
> - Blank = No relationship → score **0**

## 2.5 Kano Model of Customer Satisfaction

The Kano Model (Professor Noriaki Kano, 1984) classifies product attributes into five categories based on how their presence/absence affects satisfaction:

| Kano Category | More Feature → | Less Feature → | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Must-Be (M)** | Satisfaction does NOT increase | Satisfaction drops sharply | *Threshold attributes* — must be present, but investing more yields no competitive return |
| **One-Dimensional (O)** | Satisfaction rises linearly | Satisfaction falls linearly | *Performance attributes* — direct ROI on improvement |
| **Attractive (A)** | Satisfaction rises sharply | Satisfaction does NOT fall | *Differentiators* — biggest source of delight |
| **Indifferent (I)** | No effect | No effect | Ignore |
| **Reverse (R)** | Dissatisfaction rises | Satisfaction rises | Customer dislikes the feature |

## 2.6 Conjoint Analysis (Mathematical Foundation)

Conjoint analysis is the **most quantitative** market-oriented methodology. It decomposes a consumer's *overall preference* for a product into *part-worth utilities* for each attribute level.

$$
U_{jk} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \beta_{ij} \cdot x_{ijk} \;+\; \varepsilon_{jk}
$$

where:
- $U_{jk}$ = utility of respondent $j$ for product profile $k$
- $\beta_{ij}$ = part-worth utility of level $i$ for respondent $j$
- $x_{ijk}$ = 1 if attribute level $i$ is present in profile $k$, else 0
- $\varepsilon_{jk}$ = random error term

The predicted market share under a *first-choice* (Max-Utility) simulation rule is:

$$
\text{Predicted Share}_{\text{profile } k} \;=\; \frac{1}{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}\!\left[\,U_{jk} \;=\; \max_{p}\, U_{jp}\,\right]
$$

where $\mathbb{1}[\cdot]$ is the indicator function and $N$ is the number of simulated respondents.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Food Industry Use Case**
> Conjoint analysis is routinely used by brands like **Maggi, Cadbury, Amul** to price and formulate variants. A typical study reveals: *"Consumers will pay ₹4 more for a 20% increase in protein content, but will not pay for a 10% reduction in sugar."* This guides formulation and pricing teams.

## 2.7 Blue Ocean Strategy (Kim & Mauborgne)

Blue Ocean Strategy argues that companies should avoid competing in *red oceans* (existing, bloody markets) and instead create *blue oceans* (uncontested market space). Tools include the **Strategy Canvas** and the **Four Actions Framework** (Eliminate–Reduce–Raise–Create grid).

The **Value Innovation** equation that summarizes the philosophy:

$$
\text{Value Innovation} \;=\; \text{Utility} \uparrow \;+\; \text{Price} \downarrow \;+\; \text{Cost} \downarrow
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Food Industry Blue Ocean Examples (memorize for KTU):**
> - **Honest Tea** vs. sugary sodas → eliminated artificial sweeteners, raised organic content.
> - **ITC Sunfeast Farmlite** → eliminated maida-based claim, raised whole-grain content.
> - **Epigamia Greek Yogurt** in India → created the *Greek yogurt* category where none existed, bypassing established dahi brands.

## 2.8 Market Testing & Commercialization Methods

After development, market-oriented NPD uses these testing layers:

- **In-Home Use Test (IHUT)** — Product sent home for 1–2 weeks of usage feedback.
- **Central Location Test (CLT)** — Consumers evaluate at a controlled venue (mall, lab).
- **Test Marketing** — Limited geographic launch (e.g., one city) before national rollout.
- **Simulated Test Marketing (STM)** — Computer-simulated purchase behavior in lab settings.
- **Sales Wave Test** — Repeated exposure with measured repeat-purchase rates.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example — House of Quality Computation (14-Mark Style Problem)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A food company is developing a new ready-to-drink (RTD) protein shake. The House of Quality has **3 Whats** and **3 Hows**. The customer importance weights, relationship scores, and technical difficulties are given. Compute the absolute priorities, relative priorities, and rank the Hows.

**Given Data Table:**

| What (Customer Requirement) | Weight $W_i$ | How₁ (Protein ≥ 20 g) | How₂ (Sugar ≤ 5 g) | How₃ (Price ≤ ₹50) | Technical Difficulty $\theta_j$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| W₁: High protein | 5 | 9 | 1 | 0 | 0.6 |
| W₂: Low sugar | 4 | 1 | 9 | 1 | 0.5 |
| W₃: Affordable | 3 | 1 | 1 | 9 | 0.4 |

**Step 1 — Compute Absolute Priority for How₁ (Protein)**

$$
P_1^{\text{abs}} = (W_1 \times R_{1,1}) + (W_2 \times R_{2,1}) + (W_3 \times R_{3,1})
$$

$$
P_1^{\text{abs}} = (5 \times 9) + (4 \times 1) + (3 \times 1) = 45 + 4 + 3 = 52
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Absolute Priority for How₂ (Low Sugar)**

$$
P_2^{\text{abs}} = (W_1 \times R_{1,2}) + (W_2 \times R_{2,2}) + (W_3 \times R_{3,2})
$$

$$
P_2^{\text{abs}} = (5 \times 1) + (4 \times 9) + (3 \times 1) = 5 + 36 + 3 = 44
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Absolute Priority for How₃ (Affordable Price)**

$$
P_3^{\text{abs}} = (W_1 \times R_{1,3}) + (W_2 \times R_{2,3}) + (W_3 \times R_{3,3})
$$

$$
P_3^{\text{abs}} = (5 \times 0) + (4 \times 1) + (3 \times 9) = 0 + 4 + 27 = 31
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Sum of Absolute Priorities**

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{3} P_j^{\text{abs}} = 52 + 44 + 31 = 127
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Relative Priorities (in %)**

$$
P_1^{\text{rel}} = \frac{52}{127} \times 100\% = 40.94\%
$$

$$
P_2^{\text{rel}} = \frac{44}{127} \times 100\% = 34.65\%
$$

$$
P_3^{\text{rel}} = \frac{31}{127} \times 100\% = 24.41\%
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Value-to-Effort Ratios**

$$
\text{V/E}_1 = \frac{40.94}{0.6} = 68.23
$$

$$
\text{V/E}_2 = \frac{34.65}{0.5} = 69.30
$$

$$
\text{V/E}_3 = \frac{24.41}{0.4} = 61.02
$$

**Step 7 — Final Engineering Ranking**

| Rank | How | $P^{\text{rel}}$ (%) | V/E Ratio | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | How₂ (Sugar ≤ 5 g) | 34.65 | **69.30** | Best bang-per-rupee — invest first |
| **2** | How₁ (Protein ≥ 20 g) | 40.94 | 68.23 | Highest priority, slightly harder |
| **3** | How₃ (Price ≤ ₹50) | 24.41 | 61.02 | Important but costlier |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Decision:** Despite How₁ having the highest relative priority, How₂ delivers marginally higher value-per-effort. Smart R&D teams would *first* lock in the sugar-reduction tech (lower technical risk), then scale protein, then optimize cost.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Conjoint Analysis with Part-Worths (10-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A sensory study on flavored yogurt used a 3-attribute conjoint design: Flavor (Strawberry/Mango/Coconut), Sweetness (Low/Medium/High), and Price (₹20/₹30/₹40). The part-worth utilities from 100 respondents are tabulated below. Predict the market share of *Strawberry-Low-₹20* vs. *Mango-Medium-₹30*.

**Given Part-Worth Utilities:**

| Attribute | Level | Utility $\beta$ |
|---|---|---|
| Flavor | Strawberry | +2.0 |
| Flavor | Mango | +1.0 |
| Flavor | Coconut | −1.5 |
| Sweetness | Low | +0.5 |
| Sweetness | Medium | +0.8 |
| Sweetness | High | −1.0 |
| Price | ₹20 | +1.5 |
| Price | ₹30 | +0.5 |
| Price | ₹40 | −2.0 |

**Step 1 — Compute Total Utility of Profile A (Strawberry-Low-₹20)**

$$
U_A = \beta_{\text{Strawberry}} + \beta_{\text{Low}} + \beta_{₹20}
$$

$$
U_A = 2.0 + 0.5 + 1.5 = 4.0
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Total Utility of Profile B (Mango-Medium-₹30)**

$$
U_B = \beta_{\text{Mango}} + \beta_{\text{Medium}} + \beta_{₹30}
$$

$$
U_B = 1.0 + 0.8 + 0.5 = 2.3
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Utility of All Other Profiles (excerpt)**

$$
U_C \;(\text{Coconut-Low-₹30}) = -1.5 + 0.5 + 0.5 = -0.5
$$

$$
U_D \;(\text{Strawberry-Medium-₹40}) = 2.0 + 0.8 - 2.0 = 0.8
$$

$$
U_E \;(\text{Mango-High-₹20}) = 1.0 - 1.0 + 1.5 = 1.5
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the First-Choice (Max-Utility) Rule Across Simulated Respondents**

In a homothetic simulation (where every respondent has identical part-worths, the calculation reduces to deterministic ranking). Profile A has the **highest utility** (4.0), so under the max-utility rule:

$$
\text{Predicted Share of Profile A} \;=\; \frac{N_A^{\text{max}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \;=\; \frac{100}{100} \;=\; 100\%
$$

**Step 5 — Heterogeneous Simulation (realistic extension)**

In reality, each of the 100 respondents has a slightly different $\beta$ vector (drawn from a multivariate normal distribution). The simulated share is computed by counting how many respondents select each profile as their maximum-utility choice. Suppose from a Monte-Carlo run:

- Profile A wins for 42 respondents
- Profile E wins for 28 respondents
- Profile B wins for 18 respondents
- Profile D wins for 8 respondents
- Profile C wins for 4 respondents

Then:

$$
\text{Predicted Share of A} = \frac{42}{100} = 42\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The *maximum utility* rule is aggressive. Many firms use the **Bradley-Terry / Logit choice rule** instead:
>
> $$ P(\text{choose } A) \;=\; \frac{e^{U_A}}{\sum_{p} e^{U_p}} $$
>
> which produces smoother, more realistic shares even for non-winning profiles.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Five Market-Oriented NPD Methodologies

| Methodology | Originator | Primary Output | Strength | Limitation | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Stage-Gate®** | Robert G. Cooper (1986) | Go/Kill decisions | Disciplined, ROI-positive | Rigid, can kill radical ideas | Established categories, FMCG |
| **QFD / House of Quality** | Yoji Akao (1966) | Cross-functional design matrix | Aligns customer & engineering voice | Time-intensive to build | Complex technical products |
| **Kano Model** | Noriaki Kano (1984) | Attribute satisfaction category | Identifies delighters | Survey must be statistically valid | Portfolio prioritization |
| **Conjoint Analysis** | Paul Green & V. Rao (1971) | Part-worth utilities & shares | Quantifies tradeoffs | Requires large samples (n ≥ 100) | Pricing & formulation |
| **Lead User Method** | Eric von Hippel (1986) | Breakthrough concepts | Identifies future trends | Lead users are rare & non-representative | Radical innovation |
| **Blue Ocean Strategy** | Kim & Mauborgne (2005) | Value curve reconstruction | Creates new markets | Requires courage to exit core | Strategic repositioning |

---

## 3.4 Decision Tree — Selecting the Right Methodology

```
START
  │
  ├─ Is the problem a RADICAL innovation?
  │     YES → Lead User Method + Blue Ocean
  │     NO  ↓
  │
  ├─ Do you need to OPTIMIZE formulation/price?
  │     YES → Conjoint Analysis
  │     NO  ↓
  │
  ├─ Do you need to PRIORITIZE which features to invest in?
  │     YES → Kano Model → drive attribute investment
  │     NO  ↓
  │
  ├─ Do you need to TRANSLATE voice-of-customer into specs?
  │     YES → QFD / House of Quality
  │     NO  ↓
  │
  └─ Do you need to GOVERN the end-to-end pipeline?
        YES → Stage-Gate® (default for any FMCG/ food firm)
```
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Stage-Gate® Process for Food NPD

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Discovery and Ideation] -->|VOC, Trends, Lead Users| B(Gate 1: Idea Screen)
    B -->|GO| C[Stage 2: Scoping]
    B -->|HOLD| H1[Recycle into next cycle]
    B -->|KILL| K1[Archive with learnings]
    C --> D(Gate 2: Second Screen)
    D -->|GO| E[Stage 3: Concept and Business Case]
    D -->|KILL| K1
    E --> F(Gate 3: Development Decision)
    F -->|GO| G[Stage 4: Development and Testing]
    G --> H(Gate 4: Launch Decision)
    H -->|GO| I[Stage 5: Launch and Post-Launch]
    I -->|Feedback Loop| A
    G -.->|IHUT, CLT, Shelf Life| G
    E -.->|Sensory Trials, Concept Test| E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for Students:** Solid arrows denote forward progression; dotted self-loops denote intra-stage iteration. The feedback arrow from Stage 5 → Stage 1 captures the *closed-loop learning* principle that distinguishes market-oriented NPD from linear models.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — House of Quality (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ROOF[Roof - Correlation Matrix]
        R1[How1 vs How2]
        R2[How2 vs How3]
        R3[How1 vs How3]
    end

    subgraph WHATS[Customer Whats - Left Wall]
        W1[W1: High Protein]
        W2[W2: Low Sugar]
        W3[W3: Affordable]
        W4[W4: Good Taste]
    end

    subgraph WEIGHTS[Importance Weights]
        IM1[Weight 5]
        IM2[Weight 4]
        IM3[Weight 3]
        IM4[Weight 5]
    end

    subgraph REL[Relationship Matrix - Center]
        S1[Strong 9]
        S2[Medium 3]
        S3[Weak 1]
    end

    subgraph HOWS[Technical Hows - Floor]
        H1[How1: Protein 20g]
        H2[How2: Sugar 5g]
        H3[How3: Price 50 INR]
    end

    subgraph TECH[Technical Difficulty]
        T1[Diff 0.6]
        T2[Diff 0.5]
        T3[Diff 0.4]
    end

    subgraph COMP[Competitive Benchmarks - Right Wall]
        C1[Our Brand]
        C2[Competitor A]
        C3[Competitor B]
    end

    WHATS --> REL
    WEIGHTS --> REL
    REL --> HOWS
    HOWS --> TECH
    REL --> COMP
    HOWS --> ROOF
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — NPD Methodology Selection Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Market-Oriented NPD Trigger]) --> Q1{Innovation Type?}
    Q1 -->|Incremental| SG[Stage-Gate]
    Q1 -->|Platform| QFD[QFD and House of Quality]
    Q1 -->|Disruptive| LU[Lead User Method]
    Q1 -->|Repositioning| BOS[Blue Ocean Strategy]

    SG --> S1{Pricing or Formulation?}
    QFD --> S1
    LU --> S1
    BOS --> S1

    S1 -->|YES| CJ[Conjoint Analysis]
    S1 -->|NO| S2{Feature Prioritization?}
    S2 -->|YES| KN[Kano Model]
    S2 -->|NO| S3{Customer to Spec Translation?}
    S3 -->|YES| QFD
    S3 -->|NO| SG

    CJ --> OUT([Optimized Product Concept])
    KN --> OUT
    SG --> OUT
    LU --> OUT
    BOS --> OUT
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Kano Model Satisfaction Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feature Absent] --> B[Feature Present]
    subgraph KANO[Kano Curve Quadrants]
        K1[Must Be: Satisfaction flat then collapses]:::mustbe
        K2[One Dimensional: Linear rise and fall]:::one
        K3[Attractive: Sharp rise, no fall]:::attract
        K4[Indifferent: Flat line]:::indiff
    end
    classDef mustbe fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    classDef one fill:#d6e6ff,stroke:#003399,color:#000
    classDef attract fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef indiff fill:#eeeeee,stroke:#666666,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** *Must-be* attributes (e.g., food safety, basic shelf-life) are non-negotiable. Investing more yields no customer delight — but their absence causes catastrophic dissatisfaction. *Attractive* attributes are the source of WOW moments (e.g., a self-heating ready-meal pack). KTU questions often ask *"Classify the following attribute as M, O, A, or I"* — students must justify with the satisfaction-vs-investment curve logic.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Market-Oriented NPD. List any four differences between Market-Pull and Technology-Push NPD.**

**Model Answer (10 key lines):**

Market-Oriented New Product Development (NPD) is a structured innovation framework in which *consumer needs, market intelligence, and competitor signals* drive every stage from ideation to commercialization, in contrast to technology-driven models that begin with internal R&D capability.

| # | Market-Pull NPD | Technology-Push NPD |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Initiated by unmet customer need | Initiated by new R&D breakthrough |
| 2 | Lower commercial failure risk | Higher commercial failure risk |
| 3 | VOC and trend data are primary inputs | Patent and lab data are primary inputs |
| 4 | Marketing has leadership voice | R&D has leadership voice |
| 5 | Examples: Amul Pro, Tata Sampann | Examples: Tang (orig.), 3D-printed food pilots |

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the House of Quality (HOQ) in Quality Function Deployment? Name its six rooms.**

**Model Answer (8 key lines):**

The **House of Quality (HOQ)** is the central planning matrix of QFD that systematically translates *customer requirements* (Whats) into *technical specifications* (Hows), enabling cross-functional teams to design products that meet quantified market needs.

**Six Rooms of HOQ:**
1. **Customer Voice (Whats)** — VOC statements on the left wall.
2. **Importance Weights** — Customer-prioritized ratings.
3. **Relationship Matrix** — Correlation symbols (●, ○, △) at the centre.
4. **Technical Descriptors (Hows)** — Engineering specs on the floor.
5. **Roof (Correlation Matrix)** — Trade-offs among Hows.
6. **Competitive Benchmarks** — Our product vs. competitors on the right wall.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice is Mandatory.** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** *Explain in detail the Stage-Gate® process of NPD as proposed by Robert G. Cooper. List all five stages and five gates with their respective decision criteria.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *Discuss Voice of Customer (VOC) techniques. Explain with food-industry examples how focus groups, in-depth interviews, and lead user analysis are used to generate NPD ideas.* **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) Stage-Gate® Process

The Stage-Gate® process, developed by **Dr. Robert G. Cooper** in the 1980s, is the most widely adopted market-oriented NPD framework in the FMCG and food industries. It divides the entire NPD journey into **five stages** separated by **five gates**, where each gate acts as a *go/kill/hold/recycle decision checkpoint*.

**The Five Stages:**
1. **Stage 1 — Discovery:** Systematic scanning of markets, customers, and technology. Output is a funnel of 20–100 raw ideas sourced via VOC, trend reports, lead-user workshops, and competitor teardowns.
2. **Stage 2 — Scoping:** Quick preliminary assessment of each surviving idea — light market research, technical feasibility, and a rough financial estimate.
3. **Stage 3 — Concept Development & Business Case:** The idea is fleshed out into a product concept, tested with consumers, formulated into a prototype, and packaged into a full P&L business case.
4. **Stage 4 — Development & Testing:** Pilot-plant trials, packaging-line validation, shelf-life studies, sensory panels, IHUT, and CLT.
5. **Stage 5 — Launch & Post-Launch:** Commercial rollout with trade marketing, distribution, and a closed-loop feedback system.

**The Five Gates & Their Criteria:**

| Gate | Decision Criteria |
|---|---|
| Gate 1 — Idea Screen | Strategic fit, market size, technical do-ability, rough ROI |
| Gate 2 — Second Screen | Project attractiveness, competitive intensity, capability fit |
| Gate 3 — Development Decision | Full business case approval, manufacturability confirmed, regulatory clearance |
| Gate 4 — Launch Decision | Final go-to-market readiness, distribution locked, marketing plan approved |
| Gate 5 — Post-Launch Review | Sales vs. forecast, consumer satisfaction, ROI vs. plan — feeds back into Stage 1 |

**Why It Works:** The model enforces *disciplined capital allocation* — weak ideas are killed early when sunk costs are low, dramatically improving the portfolio NPV. *(End of 7-mark answer.)*

#### Model Solution — (b) VOC Techniques with Food Examples

Voice of Customer (VOC) is the systematic capture of stated, unstated, and latent customer needs. Three canonical VOC techniques are:

**(i) Focus Groups (FG):** A moderator guides 8–10 carefully screened participants through a 90–120 minute structured discussion. *Food example:* Britannia conducted focus groups with mothers to develop *Tiger Biscuits'* positioning around *energy + iron*. The group dynamic surfaces latent needs through associative thinking.

**(ii) In-Depth Interviews (IDI):** A trained interviewer conducts a 45–60 minute one-on-one session in the consumer's natural environment (kitchen, retail store). *Food example:* ITC Foods deployed IDIs in South Indian homes to understand *chai-making rituals* before reformulating Sunfeast YiPPee! Mood Masala.

**(iii) Lead User Analysis:** Pioneered by **Eric von Hippel (MIT)**, this method identifies *extreme users* whose current needs forecast mass-market needs 6–24 months ahead. *Food example:* For a high-protein sports nutrition launch, lead users would be *competitive marathon runners and CrossFit athletes* — their needs (clean-label protein, fast recovery) trickle down to weekend gym-goers within 18 months.

> The triad of FG (breadth), IDI (depth), and Lead User (foresight) gives a balanced VOC pipeline. *(End of 7-mark answer.)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** *Explain the Kano Model of customer satisfaction. Classify the following food-product attributes as Must-be (M), One-dimensional (O), Attractive (A), or Indifferent (I), with justification: (i) Microbial safety, (ii) Low-sugar claim, (iii) AR-enabled cooking instructions on the pack, (iv) Price.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *Explain Quality Function Deployment (QFD). For the data given below, compute the absolute priority, relative priority, and value-to-effort ratio for each technical requirement (How), and rank them accordingly.* **(7 Marks)**

**Given Data:**

| What | Weight | How₁: Protein ≥ 20 g | How₂: Sugar ≤ 5 g | How₃: Cost ≤ ₹40 | Tech. Difficulty $\theta$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| W₁: Muscle building | 5 | 9 | 1 | 0 | 0.7 |
| W₂: Diabetic-friendly | 4 | 1 | 9 | 1 | 0.4 |
| W₃: Affordable | 3 | 1 | 1 | 9 | 0.5 |

#### Model Solution — (a) Kano Model

The **Kano Model** (Prof. Noriaki Kano, Tokyo RIKA University, 1984) classifies product attributes into five categories based on the *asymmetric relationship* between feature investment and customer satisfaction.

**(i) Microbial safety → Must-be (M):** Absence causes catastrophic dissatisfaction (food poisoning, recalls), but additional investment in safety beyond regulatory thresholds does *not* increase satisfaction. It is a **threshold attribute**.

**(ii) Low-sugar claim → One-dimensional (O):** More low-sugar performance → linearly higher satisfaction among health-conscious consumers. Each unit of sugar reduction is rewarded proportionally in purchase intent.

**(iii) AR-enabled cooking instructions → Attractive (A):** Its *absence* does not dissatisfy (consumers tolerate text/PDF instructions), but its *presence* generates delight and viral social-media moments. This is a classic **delighter**.

**(iv) Price → One-dimensional (O):** Lower price → higher satisfaction in the relevant price band. However, in premium segments, price can flip to Attractive ("expensive = trustworthy"). KTU answers should note this contextual caveat.

#### Model Solution — (b) QFD Computation

**Step 1 — Absolute Priorities:**

$$
P_1^{\text{abs}} = (5 \times 9) + (4 \times 1) + (3 \times 1) = 45 + 4 + 3 = 52
$$

$$
P_2^{\text{abs}} = (5 \times 1) + (4 \times 9) + (3 \times 1) = 5 + 36 + 3 = 44
$$

$$
P_3^{\text{abs}} = (5 \times 0) + (4 \times 1) + (3 \times 9) = 0 + 4 + 27 = 31
$$

**Step 2 — Sum:**

$$
\sum P_j^{\text{abs}} = 52 + 44 + 31 = 127
$$

**Step 3 — Relative Priorities (%):**

$$
P_1^{\text{rel}} = \frac{52}{127} \times 100\% = 40.94\%
$$

$$
P_2^{\text{rel}} = \frac{44}{127} \times 100\% = 34.65\%
$$

$$
P_3^{\text{rel}} = \frac{31}{127} \times 100\% = 24.41\%
$$

**Step 4 — Value-to-Effort Ratios:**

$$
\text{V/E}_1 = \frac{40.94}{0.7} = 58.49
$$

$$
\text{V/E}_2 = \frac{34.65}{0.4} = 86.62
$$

$$
\text{V/E}_3 = \frac{24.41}{0.5} = 48.82
$$

**Step 5 — Final Ranking:**

| Rank | How | Relative Priority (%) | V/E Ratio |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | How₂ (Sugar ≤ 5 g) | 34.65 | **86.62** |
| **2** | How₁ (Protein ≥ 20 g) | 40.94 | 58.49 |
| **3** | How₃ (Cost ≤ ₹40) | 24.41 | 48.82 |

**Engineering Decision:** R&D should *first* invest in sugar-reduction technology (highest V/E and lowest risk), then scale protein formulation, then optimize cost.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
> - **QFD Pitfall #1:** Students forget to convert relative priorities to percentages (out of 100) — they lose 1 mark.
> - **QFD Pitfall #2:** Using **0 instead of 1** for blank cells in the relationship matrix — the standard convention is **blank = 0**, weak △ = **1**, medium ○ = **3**, strong ● = **9**. Memorize this.
> - **Kano Pitfall:** Classifying *price* as Indifferent — it is almost always One-Dimensional in value segments and Attractive in premium segments. Always justify with context.
> - **Stage-Gate Pitfall:** Missing the *feedback loop* from Stage 5 back to Stage 1. Examiners explicitly test "closed-loop learning" awareness.
> - **Conjoint Pitfall:** Confusing *part-worth utilities* with *preference shares*. They are linearly related only under specific choice models.
> - **General Pitfall:** Writing *answers without food-industry examples*. KTU valuators explicitly reward industry contextualization — abstract MBA answers score lower.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- **Market-Oriented NPD** = consumer-need-anchored innovation framework; opposite of *technology-push*.
- **Four Pillars:** Customer focus, competitor intelligence, cross-functional integration, profitability orientation.
- **Three Horizons:** H1 (extensions), H2 (new categories), H3 (transformational bets).
- **Stage-Gate® (Cooper, 1986):** 5 stages × 5 gates; *fail fast, fail cheap* philosophy; closed-loop learning.
- **VOC Techniques:** Focus Groups (breadth), IDIs (depth), Surveys (quant), Observation (context), Lead User (foresight).
- **QFD / HOQ:** 6 rooms — Whats, Weights, Relationship Matrix (●9, ○3, △1, blank0), Hows, Roof, Competitive.
- **QFD Formulas:**
  - $P_j^{\text{abs}} = \sum_i W_i \cdot R_{ij}$
  - $P_j^{\text{rel}} = P_j^{\text{abs}} / \sum P^{\text{abs}} \times 100\%$
  - $\text{V/E}_j = P_j^{\text{rel}} / \theta_j$
- **Kano Categories:** Must-be (M), One-dimensional (O), Attractive (A), Indifferent (I), Reverse (R).
- **Kano Adjusted Importance:** M × 1.5, O × 1.0, A × 0.5.
- **Conjoint Utility:** $U_{jk} = \sum_i \beta_{ij} x_{ijk} + \varepsilon_{jk}$.
- **Conjoint Share (Max-Utility):** count respondents whose $U$ is highest for the profile.
- **Conjoint Share (Logit / Bradley-Terry):** $P(\text{choose } A) = e^{U_A} / \sum_p e^{U_p}$.
- **Blue Ocean Equation:** Value Innovation = Utility ↑ + Price ↓ + Cost ↓.
- **Lead User Method (von Hippel, MIT):** Use extreme users to forecast mass-market needs.
- **Market Testing Methods:** IHUT, CLT, Test Marketing, Simulated Test Marketing, Sales Wave Test.
- **Fifth Generation NPD:** Networked, Agile, Open-Innovation, co-creation with consumers.
- **Industry Anchor Examples:** Maggi, Cadbury, Amul, ITC Sunfeast, Britannia, Honest Tea, Epigamia.
- **Always remember:** Indian food-industry examples fetch extra valuation marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Organization for Successful NPD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT834 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The Organization for Successful NPD

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**New Product Development (NPD) Organization** refers to the deliberate structuring of people, resources, processes, authority, and cultural mechanisms within a firm to consistently generate, evaluate, and commercialize innovative food products. According to the KTU 2024 scheme framework (aligning with Cooper's NPD principles and the Product Development & Management Association - PDMA guidelines), the organization for successful NPD is **not a single department** but a **cross-functional socio-technical system** that integrates R&D, marketing, operations, quality, regulatory, and supply chain functions under a unified innovation strategy.

The three foundational pillars of an NPD organization are:

1. **Structural Pillar** – The formal reporting and authority framework (functional, project, matrix, or venture structures).
2. **Cultural Pillar** – Shared values, risk tolerance, and innovation climate.
3. **Process Pillar** – Stage-Gate or Agile workflows governing idea-to-launch movement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In food product design, the "organization" element explicitly includes *cross-functional teams, top-management commitment, NPD strategy fit, and NPD portfolio management*. Examiners frequently test the ability to **distinguish between organizational structures** and to **identify NPD success/failure drivers**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen as an NPD Organization**

Imagine a Michelin-star kitchen designing a new menu item (the "new product"):
- The **Head Chef (NPD Director)** sets the vision and approves the "gate" decisions (tasting approval before serving).
- The **Sous Chef (Project Manager)** coordinates execution and timing.
- The **Pastry Chef, Saucier, and Line Cooks** represent R&D, Marketing, and Operations — each brings specialized expertise, but the dish fails if the saucier never talks to the pastry chef.
- The **Kitchen Brigade System** = the **NPD organizational structure** (who reports to whom, who has authority).
- The **Recipe Development Process** (brainstorm → prototype → test → plate) = the **Stage-Gate Process**.
- The **Restaurant Owner (Top Management)** allocates budget and removes obstacles.

Just as a kitchen needs a clear brigade system **and** a tested recipe workflow to launch a successful dish, a food company needs both an **organizational structure** and an **NPD process** to launch a successful product.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Definitions)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **NPD Strategy** | A company's declared approach to innovation (e.g., proactive, reactive, platform, derivative). |
| **Cross-Functional Team (CFT)** | A team with members from R&D, Marketing, Operations, Quality, Finance working jointly on a project. |
| **Stage-Gate Process** | A disciplined innovation management framework dividing NPD into stages separated by decision gates. |
| **NPD Champion** | A senior executive who defends and promotes an NPD project internally. |
| **Product Innovation Charter (PIC)** | A strategic document defining the innovation arena, goals, and guidelines for NPD. |
| **Gating / Killing** | The act of discontinuing a weak project at a decision gate to free resources. |
| **Core Team vs. Extended Team** | Core = full-time dedicated members; Extended = part-time contributors (sensory panelists, regulatory, etc.). |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of a Successful NPD Organization

According to KTU-aligned NPD theory (based on Cooper, 2017 and PDMA Best Practices), an organization structured for successful NPD must excel in five interlinked dimensions:

**Pillar 1 — NPD Strategy**
A clearly articulated Product Innovation Charter (PIC) that answers:
- *Where will we play?* (product categories, technologies, markets)
- *What are our goals?* (growth %, market share, sustainability)
- *What are our guidelines?* (budget, timeline, risk tolerance)

**Pillar 2 — Portfolio Management**
Allocating resources across a balanced mix of:
- **Platform projects** (long-term, foundational — e.g., developing a new plant-protein base).
- **Derivative projects** (incremental — e.g., a new flavor variant of an existing snack).
- **Breakthrough projects** (high-risk/high-reward — e.g., 3D-printed food).

**Pillar 3 — Process Discipline**
Adopting a formal Stage-Gate® (Cooper) or Agile-Stage-Gate hybrid process. The 4–6 stages typically include: Discovery → Scoping → Business Case → Development → Testing & Validation → Launch.

**Pillar 4 — Organizational Structure & Culture**
Selecting the right structural form (functional, cross-functional matrix, dedicated NPD unit, or venture team) and fostering a culture of psychological safety, customer obsession, and intrapreneurship.

**Pillar 5 — Top Management Commitment**
Visible, sustained support from senior leadership in the form of budget allocation, gate-keeping authority, removing organizational obstacles, and celebrating both successes and "intelligent failures."

### 2.2 Organizational Structures for NPD — A Comparative Breakdown

The KTU syllabus expects students to differentiate between four classical organizational structures used in food NPD:

**Structure A — Functional Organization**
- Each function (R&D, Marketing, Production) works on its own slice of the project.
- Project Manager is a *coordinator* with limited authority.
- *Strength:* Simple, efficient use of specialists.
- *Weakness:* Slow, fragmented communication, "not-my-problem" silos.
- *Best for:* Small companies with few, low-complexity projects.

**Structure B — Cross-Functional Project Matrix**
- Functional managers retain control of resources; Project Manager coordinates across functions.
- Dual authority: project manager has *influence*, functional managers have *command*.
- *Strength:* Flexible, good information flow.
- *Weakness:* Power conflicts, dual-boss syndrome.
- *Best for:* Mid-sized food companies running multiple NPD projects simultaneously.

**Structure C — Dedicated NPD / New Venture Team**
- A standalone unit physically and organizationally separated from the parent company.
- Team reports to a senior VP of Innovation or a Venture Board.
- *Strength:* Speed, focus, entrepreneurial culture.
- *Weakness:* Risk of "not-invented-here" syndrome when integrating back into the parent.
- *Best for:* Breakthrough, platform, or strategic initiatives (e.g., launching a novel functional beverage line).

**Structure D — Hybrid (Stage-Gate Tailored) Structure**
- Different structures for different stages (functional in Discovery → matrix in Development → venture in Launch).
- *Strength:* Tailored to project needs; matches Cooper's recommendation.
- *Weakness:* Complex to manage; requires mature PMO capability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in food industry:** A company like Nestlé uses a **hybrid structure** — central R&D for platforms, brand teams for derivatives, and venture units (e.g., the WHealthpreneurs accelerator) for breakthrough innovations.

### 2.3 Roles Within the NPD Organization (KTU High-Yield)

| Role | Primary Responsibility | Typical Background |
|---|---|---|
| **NPD Director / VP Innovation** | Strategic vision, portfolio balance, gate decisions | Senior management |
| **Project Manager / Team Leader** | Day-to-day coordination, timeline, gate deliverables | Mid-management (often R&D or marketing) |
| **NPD Champion** | Internal lobbying, resource acquisition, removing roadblocks | Respected senior figure, often outside the team |
| **Sponsor** | Final authority on major funding and continuation decisions | C-suite or Business Unit Head |
| **Gatekeeper / Steering Committee** | Independent evaluation at each gate | Cross-functional senior panel |
| **Core Team Members** | Functional deliverables (R&D, marketing, supply, QA, regulatory) | Functional experts |
| **Extended Team / Stakeholders** | Sensory panels, consumer insights, legal, external partners | Ad-hoc contributors |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Key Quantitative Relationships

Although NPD organization is largely qualitative, certain metrics and ratios are *testable* under KTU 2024 analytics-based questions.

| Metric / Concept | Equation / Boundary Condition | Units / Notes |
|---|---:|---|
| **NPD Success Rate (Industry Benchmark)** | $SR_{NPD} = \dfrac{\text{Products launched AND profitable after 2 yrs}}{\text{Total products launched}} \times 100\%$ | Industry average $\approx$ **20–25 %**; food industry $\approx$ **15–20 %**. |
| **R&D-to-Sales Intensity** | $I_{RD} = \dfrac{\text{Annual R\&D Spend}}{\text{Annual Net Sales}} \times 100\%$ | Food industry benchmark $\approx$ **1–2 %**; high-tech $>$ **8 %**. |
| **NPD Project Attrition Rate** | $AR_{NPD} = \dfrac{\text{Projects killed at gates}}{\text{Projects entered at Stage 1}} \times 100\%$ | Healthy attrition $\approx$ **60–70 %** at early gates. |
| **Time-to-Market (TTM)** | $TTM = \sum_{i=1}^{n} D_i$ where $D_i$ = days in stage $i$ | Food industry average $\approx$ **12–24 months**. |
| **Resource Allocation Rule (Cooper's 70-20-10)** | $0.70 \times B$ for derivatives, $0.20 \times B$ for platform, $0.10 \times B$ for breakthrough | $B$ = total NPD budget. |
| **Gate Decision Logic** | $\text{Proceed iff} \;\; \sum_{j} w_j \cdot S_j \geq T_{gate}$ | $w_j$ = weight, $S_j$ = score on criterion $j$, $T_{gate}$ = gate threshold. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule for Table Cells:** When writing inequalities or absolute values inside any markdown table above, vertical bars are replaced with `\vert`. For example, write $\vert x \vert$ **not** $\vert x \vert$ if the value appears inline in text. Inside tables, prefer `\vert` or `\mid` to keep the markdown parser intact.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering / Food Industry Utility

- **Resource Optimization:** Aligning organizational structure with project portfolio minimizes duplicated R&D effort in food companies with hundreds of SKUs (e.g., sauces, snacks, beverages).
- **Speed-to-Market:** Properly empowered NPD teams reduce TTM by 30–40 %, a critical competitive advantage in perishable food categories.
- **Risk Mitigation:** Stage-gate discipline prevents large capital investments in products that fail consumer testing (e.g., a $5 M extrusion line for a snack that fails sensory panels).
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Cross-functional teams ensure early integration of FSSAI/USDA/EU regulatory inputs, avoiding costly reformulations during scale-up.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step: Building an NPD Organization (KTU Model)

The following derivation-style breakdown describes how a food firm should *construct* an NPD organization from scratch — a high-frequency KTU 14-mark question type.

**Step 1 — Define the NPD Strategy (PIC)**
- Articulate *Innovation Arena* (categories, technologies, geographies).
- Set quantitative goals: revenue from new products target ($\geq$ 15 % of total revenue by Year 3).
- Define risk-tolerance band (conservative $\to$ aggressive).
- *Logic:* Without a PIC, teams chase incoherent projects (e.g., a dairy company funding cellular-meat R&D without strategic rationale).

**Step 2 — Diagnose Current Organization**
- Audit existing structure (functional? matrix?).
- Map current portfolio: count projects by type (platform / derivative / breakthrough).
- Identify pain points: gate slippage, unclear ownership, inter-departmental conflict.

**Step 3 — Choose the Organizational Structure**
Apply the decision rule:

$$
\text{Choose Structure} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Functional}, & \text{if } N_p \leq 3 \text{ and } C_p \leq \text{low} \\
\text{Matrix}, & \text{if } 3 < N_p \leq 15 \text{ and } C_p = \text{medium} \\
\text{Dedicated NPD Unit}, & \text{if } N_p > 15 \text{ or } C_p = \text{high (breakthrough)} \\
\text{Hybrid Stage-Gate}, & \text{if multiple project types in parallel}
\end{cases}
$$

where $N_p$ = number of active NPD projects and $C_p$ = project complexity.

- *Logic explanation:* Low volume and low complexity → functional simplicity suffices. Higher volumes need matrix to share resources. Breakthrough-heavy portfolios need a venture-style dedicated unit to escape operational drag.

**Step 4 — Define Roles & RACI Matrix**
Assign RACI (Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed) for each role across stages:

$$
\text{RACI}_{i,j} \in \{R, A, C, I\}
$$

where $i$ indexes the role (Project Manager, R&D Lead, etc.) and $j$ indexes the activity (Discovery, Scoping, Development, Launch).

A typical RACI for a food NPD project:

| Activity | NPD Director | Project Mgr | R&D Lead | Marketing | Operations | QA/Regulatory |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Discovery | A | R | R | C | I | I |
| Business Case | A | R | C | R | C | C |
| Development | I | A | R | C | C | R |
| Launch | A | R | C | R | R | R |

*R=Responsible, A=Accountable, C=Consulted, I=Informed.*

**Step 5 — Allocate Resources Using the 70-20-10 Rule**
Given total NPD budget $B$, compute:

$$
B_{\text{deriv}} = 0.70 \cdot B
$$

$$
B_{\text{platform}} = 0.20 \cdot B
$$

$$
B_{\text{breakthrough}} = 0.10 \cdot B
$$

*Verification example:* If $B = \text{Rs. } 10\ \text{Crore}$, then $B_{\text{deriv}} = \text{Rs. } 7\ \text{Cr}$, $B_{\text{platform}} = \text{Rs. } 2\ \text{Cr}$, $B_{\text{breakthrough}} = \text{Rs. } 1\ \text{Cr}$.

**Step 6 — Install the Stage-Gate Process**
Define $n = 5$ stages and $n - 1 = 4$ gates. At each gate, compute the Gate Score:

$$
G_k = \sum_{j=1}^{m} w_j \cdot S_{j,k}
$$

where $k$ = gate index, $m$ = number of criteria (typically 5–7: strategic fit, market attractiveness, technical feasibility, financial return, risk), $w_j$ = weight ($\sum w_j = 1$), and $S_{j,k}$ = score on criterion $j$ at gate $k$ (scale 1–10).

Decision rule:

$$
\text{Decision}_k = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Proceed / Go}, & \text{if } G_k \geq T_k \\
\text{Recycle}, & \text{if } T_k - 1 \leq G_k < T_k \\
\text{Kill}, & \text{if } G_k < T_k - 1
\end{cases}
$$

where $T_k$ is the threshold for gate $k$ (e.g., $T_1 = 6.0$, $T_2 = 7.0$, $T_3 = 7.5$).

**Step 7 — Establish Top-Management Governance**
- Sponsor reviews portfolio quarterly.
- Gate-keeper committee meets at each gate (independent of project team).
- Post-launch review at 6 and 12 months (compare forecast vs. actual sales).

**Step 8 — Build Culture & Capability**
- Hire or train food scientists, sensory analysts, consumer insight specialists.
- Reward risk-taking *and* intelligent failure.
- Create an "innovation time" allocation (e.g., 10 % of R&D hours for free exploration).

**Step 9 — Continuous Improvement via PDCA**
Apply the Deming cycle: Plan (set PIC) $\to$ Do (execute) $\to$ Check (gate scorecard) $\to$ Act (refine process). The improvement delta after each cycle:

$$
\Delta_{\text{PDCA}} = \dfrac{G_{k,\text{new}} - G_{k,\text{prev}}}{G_{k,\text{prev}}} \times 100\%
$$

A positive $\Delta_{\text{PDCA}}$ indicates organizational learning.

**Step 10 — Sustain via Portfolio Reviews**
Quarterly re-balancing: kill under-performing projects, fund winners, seed new ideas to maintain:

$$
\sum_{\text{all projects}} \text{Risk} \cdot \text{Reward} \;\;\text{within tolerance band}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Success vs. Failure Drivers (KTU-Ready Table)

| Dimension | Success Driver (KEEP) | Failure Driver (AVOID) |
|---|---|---|
| **Strategy** | Clear PIC tied to business goals | Vague, ad-hoc project selection |
| **Structure** | Right structure for project type | One-size-fits-all structure |
| **Senior support** | Visible, sustained commitment | Lip service only |
| **Team** | Empowered cross-functional core team | Siloed functional hand-offs |
| **Process** | Disciplined Stage-Gate with go/kill discipline | Stage-Gate theatre (always Go) |
| **Culture** | Reward both success and intelligent failure | Blame culture kills innovation |
| **Resources** | Adequate, protected budget | Starved, raided by operations |
| **Speed** | Fast iteration with parallel work | Sequential, waterfall hand-offs |
| **Consumer focus** | Deep consumer immersion from Day 1 | Internal-only technical excellence |
| **Launch readiness** | Full go-to-market plan, not just product | "Build it and they will come" |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
