## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
*Define the term "Product Innovation Charter (PIC)" and list its three core components.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Product Innovation Charter (PIC) is a strategic document that defines the scope, goals, and rules of engagement for a firm's NPD efforts. Its three core components are:
1. **Innovation Arena** — the markets, product categories, and technologies the firm will focus on.
2. **Goals and Objectives** — quantitative targets such as revenue from new products, market share, or sustainability metrics.
3. **Guidelines** — boundaries on budget, risk appetite, time-to-market, and resource allocation. **(3 marks)**

**Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
*Distinguish between an NPD "Champion" and an NPD "Sponsor" with one example each in a food company.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **Sponsor** is the senior executive with formal authority and budget responsibility for the project (e.g., the Business Unit Head for the Snacks Division). They make the final go/kill decisions.
- The **Champion** is a respected internal advocate who lobbies, builds support, and removes obstacles for the project (e.g., a senior R&D scientist who passionately defends a novel plant-protein snack during gate reviews).
- Key distinction: the Sponsor has **authority**; the Champion has **influence**. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the four major organizational structures used for NPD in a food company. Compare them on the basis of authority, communication, speed, and best-fit situation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the five pillars of a successful NPD organization as described in the Cooper/PDMA framework. Illustrate with at least two examples from the food industry. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Answer for Question A

**Part (a) — Four Organizational Structures (7 marks):**

1. **Functional Organization (1 mark):** Each department (R&D, Marketing, Production) handles its own segment. A coordinator (often part-time) tracks progress. *Authority:* Functional managers dominant. *Communication:* Vertical, slow. *Speed:* Slow. *Best fit:* Small companies with 1–2 simple projects (e.g., a local dairy launching a single flavored yogurt variant).

2. **Cross-Functional Matrix (1 mark):** Project Manager coordinates across functions; functional managers retain resource control. *Authority:* Dual. *Communication:* Horizontal and vertical. *Speed:* Moderate. *Best fit:* Mid-sized firms with multiple parallel projects (e.g., a snack company running 8 derivative launches per year).

3. **Dedicated NPD / Venture Unit (1 mark):** Standalone team, full-time, often physically separate. *Authority:* Strong project leader. *Communication:* Highly efficient, informal. *Speed:* Fast. *Best fit:* Breakthrough or platform projects (e.g., Nestlé's dedicated plant-based meat R&D team).

4. **Hybrid / Stage-Gate Tailored Structure (1 mark):** Different structure per stage (functional in Discovery, matrix in Development, venture in Launch). *Authority:* Varies. *Communication:* Optimized per stage. *Speed:* Optimized. *Best fit:* Large diversified food multinationals (e.g., ITC Foods, Mondelez).

**Comparison summary (3 marks)** — presented as a table in the answer script:

| Structure | Authority | Communication | Speed | Best Fit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Functional | Functional mgrs | Vertical | Slow | Small firms |
| Matrix | Dual | Horizontal+Vertical | Medium | Mid-sized firms |
| Dedicated NPD Unit | Project leader | Horizontal, fast | Fast | Breakthrough |
| Hybrid | Variable | Stage-optimized | Optimized | Large multinationals |

**Part (b) — Five Pillars of Successful NPD Organization (7 marks):**

1. **NPD Strategy / PIC (1.5 marks):** A clearly articulated strategy directs resource allocation. *Example:* Frito-Lay's PIC focuses on "better-for-you snacks" guiding all derivative projects.

2. **Portfolio Management (1.5 marks):** Balanced mix via the 70-20-10 rule. *Example:* Britannia allocates 70 % of NPD budget to line extensions, 20 % to platform renovation, 10 % to breakthrough concepts.

3. **Process Discipline — Stage-Gate (1.5 marks):** Disciplined go/kill/recycle at gates prevents resource drain. *Example:* Tata Consumer Products uses a 5-stage, 4-gate process for its tea and foods divisions.

4. **Organizational Structure & Culture (1.5 marks):** Right structure + innovation-friendly culture. *Example:* Mondelez India's "SnackFutures" venture studio uses a dedicated unit structure with a fail-fast culture.

5. **Top Management Commitment (1 mark):** Sustained sponsorship, visible support, and unblocking. *Example:* The CEO of Amul personally chairs the innovation steering committee for new product launches.

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Describe in detail the roles and responsibilities of the following in an NPD organization: (i) NPD Director, (ii) Project Manager, (iii) Gatekeeper / Steering Committee, (iv) Champion, (v) Sponsor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mid-sized food company has a total annual NPD budget of Rs. 20 Crore and plans to run 12 parallel projects. Using the appropriate organizational structure, justify your choice with the decision rule and compute the budget allocation across project types using the 70-20-10 rule. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Answer for Question B

**Part (a) — Roles and Responsibilities (7 marks):**

| # | Role | Responsibility (1.4 marks each) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **NPD Director / VP Innovation** | Sets the innovation vision and PIC; chairs the steering committee; owns portfolio balance; allocates high-level resources; reports to CEO. |
| 2 | **Project Manager / Team Leader** | Day-to-day coordination of one or more projects; maintains timeline, budget, risk log; drives cross-functional deliverables; prepares gate documentation; reports to NPD Director. |
| 3 | **Gatekeeper / Steering Committee** | Independent cross-functional panel; evaluates each gate package using weighted scoring; makes Go / Kill / Recycle decisions; ensures objectivity and rigor. |
| 4 | **Champion** | Senior respected figure (often outside the team) who advocates internally, defends the project against skeptics, and helps acquire resources. |
| 5 | **Sponsor** | Senior executive with formal authority and budget ownership; final arbiter on continuation; typically a Business Unit Head or C-suite member. |

**Part (b) — Justification of Structure + Budget Computation (7 marks):**

**Justification (3 marks):** Given $N_p = 12$ active projects (medium volume, $\in [4, 15]$ range) and assuming a mix of mostly derivative with some platform projects, the appropriate structure is a **Cross-Functional Project Matrix**. This structure balances resource efficiency (functional managers retain specialists) with project coordination (a full-time Project Manager drives each project).

The decision rule (from Section 3):

$$
\text{Choose Matrix since } 3 < N_p \leq 15
$$

**Budget Allocation via 70-20-10 Rule (4 marks):**

Given total budget $B = \text{Rs. } 20\ \text{Crore}$:

$$
B_{\text{deriv}} = 0.70 \times 20 = \text{Rs. } 14.0\ \text{Crore}
$$

$$
B_{\text{platform}} = 0.20 \times 20 = \text{Rs. } 4.0\ \text{Crore}
$$

$$
B_{\text{breakthrough}} = 0.10 \times 20 = \text{Rs. } 2.0\ \text{Crore}
$$

Verification of total:

$$
B_{\text{deriv}} + B_{\text{platform}} + B_{\text{breakthrough}} = 14.0 + 4.0 + 2.0 = \text{Rs. } 20.0\ \text{Crore} \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Strategic interpretation (included for full marks):** Of the 12 projects, ~8–9 should be derivative (line extensions, new flavors, format changes), 2–3 should be platform (e.g., a new dairy-alternative base technology), and 1 should be a high-risk breakthrough (e.g., a novel probiotic-functional bar).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls — Read Before You Write!**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Champion with Sponsor.** Champions have *influence*, Sponsors have *authority* and *budget*. Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks per occurrence.
> 2. **Do NOT claim "one structure fits all."** Examiners specifically test whether you can *justify* structure selection based on project count and complexity.
> 3. **Do NOT skip the RACI / roles dimension.** Listing only "team" without distinguishing Director, PM, Champion, Sponsor, and Gatekeeper will cap your answer at 50 % of marks.
> 4. **Do NOT write the Stage-Gate as a linear checklist.** Always include gates, gate-keeper, and Go/Kill/Recycle decision logic.
> 5. **In numerical questions, ALWAYS show the calculation step.** Writing only the final Rs. 14 Cr figure without showing $0.70 \times 20$ will lose process marks.
> 6. **Avoid generic statements** like "innovation is important." Cite *food-specific* examples (shelf-life, FSSAI compliance, sensory testing, scale-up on existing lines) for full credit.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — The Organization for Successful NPD**

- **Definition:** NPD organization is a cross-functional system, not a single department.
- **Five Pillars:** Strategy (PIC) → Portfolio (70-20-10) → Process (Stage-Gate) → Structure & Culture → Top Management Commitment.
- **Four Structures:** Functional, Cross-Functional Matrix, Dedicated NPD Unit, Hybrid Stage-Gate.
- **Structure Selection Rule:** Match structure to $N_p$ (number of projects) and $C_p$ (complexity).
- **Key Roles:** Director (vision), Project Manager (delivery), Champion (influence), Sponsor (authority & budget), Gatekeeper (independent evaluation).
- **70-20-10 Rule:** 70 % derivative, 20 % platform, 10 % breakthrough.
- **Stage-Gate:** 5 stages, 4 gates; each gate uses weighted scoring with Go/Kill/Recycle logic.
- **Top Management:** Must be visible, sustained, and willing to *kill* weak projects.
- **Culture Matters:** Reward intelligent failure; allocate ~10 % "innovation time."
- **Food Industry Tip:** Always integrate QA, Regulatory (FSSAI/USDA/EU), and Sensory Science into the cross-functional team from Day 1.
- **Numerical Must-Know:** Industry NPD success rate $\approx$ 20–25 %; food industry TTM $\approx$ 12–24 months; R&D intensity $\approx$ 1–2 % of sales.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recipe Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT834 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Recipe Development

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recipe Development** is the systematic, scientific, and iterative process of creating, testing, refining, and standardizing a formulation of ingredients and processing parameters to produce a food product that meets defined targets of sensory quality, nutritional profile, functional performance, cost, shelf-life, regulatory compliance, and consumer acceptability.

In the context of **Food Product Design and Development (OEBBT834)**, recipe development is positioned as the **practical translation stage** of the product development pipeline. It converts a *product concept* (from Module 1) into a *measurable, reproducible, scalable formulation* that can be handed over to process engineering, packaging, and marketing teams.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim alignment):**
> *"Recipe development involves the formulation of standardized recipes through the systematic selection, quantification, and testing of ingredients to achieve desired sensory, nutritional, functional, and economic attributes of the target food product."*

---

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The "Master Chef's Notebook"**
>
> Imagine you are a chef who has just invented a brilliant new soup. You know it tastes perfect in your home kitchen when you cook **2 liters** for your family. Now, your friend wants to sell it in a restaurant and needs to make **200 liters** every day. Suddenly, problems appear:
> - Will the salt still taste right when multiplied by 100?
> - Will the cooking time still work in a giant industrial kettle?
> - Will the texture be the same?
> - How much does each liter cost to make?
>
> **Recipe Development is the structured "notebook" approach to solving all these problems scientifically** — it ensures your soup tastes identical at 2 L, 200 L, and 2,000 L, costs a predictable amount, stays safe to eat, and satisfies nutrition rules.

---

## The Three Pillars of a Successful Recipe

A KTU-evaluated recipe must simultaneously satisfy **three pillars**, often referred to as the **"Q-C-S Triangle"** in food product design literature:

| Pillar | Meaning | Standard Metric |
|--------|---------|-----------------|
| **Quality** | Sensory, nutritional, textural, functional | Sensory scores (9-point hedonic), proximate composition (%) |
| **Cost** | Raw material, processing, packaging economics | **Cost per kg / per serving (₹)** |
| **Safety** | Microbiological, chemical, regulatory | **FSSAI / Codex / ISO standards** |

---

## Position of Recipe Development in the Product Life Cycle

Recipe Development sits at **Stage 3** of the KTU 2024-aligned product development funnel:

1. **Idea Generation** (Module 1)
2. **Concept Screening & Feasibility**
3. ➡️ **Recipe Development** (Module 2 — current topic)
4. **Process Development & Scale-Up** (Module 3)
5. **Shelf-Life & Packaging Studies** (Module 4)
6. **Commercialization** (Module 5)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Sequential Stages of Recipe Development

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently ask students to "list and explain the steps of recipe development." Memorize the following six-stage flow exactly.

### Stage 1 — **Product Specification & Target Setting**
- Define the **Product Design Specification (PDS)** document.
- Lock the **target sensory profile** (e.g., crispness, sweetness, color).
- Lock the **nutritional targets** (calories per serving, % RDA).
- Lock the **price ceiling** (₹ per serving).
- Lock the **shelf-life target** (months).
- Lock the **regulatory targets** (FSSAI limits for additives).

### Stage 2 — **Ingredient Selection & Functional Mapping**
Every ingredient is chosen for a **function**, not just taste. The KTU-recognized functional categories are:

| Functional Role | Examples | What it Does |
|-----------------|----------|--------------|
| **Bulk base** | Flour, milk, water | Provides mass and structure |
| **Structure former** | Gluten, starch, gelatin, pectin | Creates network / gel |
| **Sweetener** | Sucrose, HFCS, stevia | Sweetness, browning, preservation |
| **Flavor** | Salt, spices, vanilla, MSG | Taste and aroma |
| **Color** | Caramel, paprika, turmeric | Visual appeal |
| **Preservative** | Salt, sugar, sodium benzoate | Microbial safety |
| **Acidulant** | Citric acid, vinegar | pH control, flavor |
| **Emulsifier** | Lecithin, mono/diglycerides | Stabilize emulsions |
| **Texture modifier** | Xanthan, CMC, guar gum | Viscosity, mouthfeel |

### Stage 3 — **Bench-Top Formulation (Laboratory Trials)**
- Develop **prototype formulations** (usually 500 g – 1 kg batches).
- Use **Design of Experiments (DoE)** or **one-factor-at-a-time (OFAT)** approaches.
- Record every variable: ingredient grams, temperature °C, time min, pH, °Brix, water activity (a_w).

### Stage 4 — **Sensory & Instrumental Evaluation**
- Conduct **triangle tests, hedonic tests, ranking tests, descriptive analysis**.
- Measure instrumental parameters: **Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)** on a texturometer, color via **L\*a\*b\*** values, viscosity via **Brookfield viscometer**.

### Stage 5 — **Optimization & Standardization**
- Adjust ingredient levels until all targets are met.
- Fix the **final recipe** with **fixed ingredient specifications** (brand, grade, particle size, moisture %).
- Finalize the **Standard Operating Procedure (SOP)**.

### Stage 6 — **Scale-Up Calculation & Cost Engineering**
- Convert bench recipe to **pilot plant (10–50 kg)** and then **commercial batch (500–5000 kg)**.
- Calculate **Conversion Factor (CF)**.
- Compute **raw material cost, processing cost, and overhead cost per unit**.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas appear repeatedly in KTU university examinations. Memorize the units and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $$CF = \frac{\text{Desired Batch Yield (kg)}}{\text{Original Batch Yield (kg)}}$$ | **Conversion Factor** for recipe scaling | dimensionless |
| 2 | $$Q_{new} = Q_{old} \times CF$$ | New ingredient quantity from scaling | g or kg |
| 3 | $$\text{Cost per kg} = \frac{\sum (Q_i \times P_i)}{\text{Total Yield (kg)}}$$ | Where $Q_i$ = quantity, $P_i$ = ₹/kg | ₹ / kg |
| 4 | $$\text{Cost per serving} = \frac{\text{Cost per kg}}{\text{Servings per kg}}$$ | Per-serving economics | ₹ |
| 5 | $$\text{Yield \%} = \frac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Theoretical Input}} \times 100$$ | Process yield efficiency | % |
| 6 | $$\text{Baking Loss \%} = \frac{W_{dough}-W_{baked}}{W_{dough}} \times 100$$ | Moisture loss during baking | % |
| 7 | $$\text{BMR \%} = \frac{\text{Bread}}{\text{Flour}} \times 100$$ | Baker's Math Ratio | % |
| 8 | $$a_w + RH\% \approx 1 \text{ (at equilibrium)}$$ | Water activity relation | dimensionless |
| 9 | $$pH_{target} = -\log_{10}[H^+]$$ | Acidity control | pH units |
| 10 | $$\text{Nutrition per serve} = \frac{\text{Nutrient per 100 g} \times \text{Serving size (g)}}{100}$$ | Nutrient calculation | g or kcal |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU warning:** Never scale *time* and *temperature* by the Conversion Factor. They are **thermodynamic parameters** and must be re-validated experimentally during scale-up. Most students lose marks here.

---

## 2.3 Baker's Percentage (Baker's Math)

A unique KTU-relevant sub-topic. In baking and confectionery recipes, flour is always **100 %**, and every other ingredient is expressed as a **percentage of flour weight**.

$$BMR_i (\%) = \frac{W_i}{W_{flour}} \times 100$$

**Typical bread formula:**

| Ingredient | BMR (%) |
|------------|---------|
| Flour | 100 |
| Water | 62 |
| Salt | 2 |
| Sugar | 5 |
| Yeast | 2 |
| Fat | 3 |
| **Total Dough Weight** | **174** |

To convert BMR to absolute weight for a desired dough batch:

$$W_i = \frac{BMR_i \times W_{flour}}{100}$$

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Recipe Development

> Real-world applications where this knowledge is deployed:
> - **FMCG industries** (Britannia, ITC Foods, Nestlé) — every biscuit, soup, or sauce on the shelf passes through this exact workflow.
> - **Cloud kitchens & QSR chains** — standardized recipes ensure identical taste across 500 outlets.
> - **Clinical / therapeutic diets** — renal, diabetic, and dysphagia diets require precise nutritional standardization.
> - **Export food units** — recipes must conform to **Codex Alimentarius** and **destination-country regulations**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Recipe Scaling Using Conversion Factor (CF)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU often poses a 7-mark numerical problem:
> *"A bakery uses a recipe yielding 5 kg of cake. The recipe requires 1.2 kg flour, 0.8 kg sugar, 0.5 kg butter, 0.4 L milk, and 6 eggs (assume 50 g per egg). Scale the recipe to yield 25 kg of cake."*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify Original Yield and Desired Yield**

$$\text{Original Yield} = 5 \text{ kg}$$
$$\text{Desired Yield} = 25 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Conversion Factor (CF)**

$$\begin{aligned}
CF &= \frac{\text{Desired Yield}}{\text{Original Yield}} \\
&= \frac{25 \text{ kg}}{5 \text{ kg}} \\
&= 5 \quad \text{(dimensionless)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Scaling Equation to Every Ingredient**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{flour} &= 1.2 \text{ kg} \times 5 = 6.0 \text{ kg} \\
Q_{sugar} &= 0.8 \text{ kg} \times 5 = 4.0 \text{ kg} \\
Q_{butter} &= 0.5 \text{ kg} \times 5 = 2.5 \text{ kg} \\
Q_{milk} &= 0.4 \text{ L} \times 5 = 2.0 \text{ L} \\
Q_{eggs} &= 6 \text{ eggs} \times 5 = 30 \text{ eggs} \\
Q_{eggs\_mass} &= 30 \times 50 \text{ g} = 1.5 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Verify Mass Balance**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Mass} &= 6.0 + 4.0 + 2.5 + 2.0 + 1.5 \\
&= 16.0 \text{ kg of raw input}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the Result**

The 25 kg finished cake typically weighs less than the sum of raw ingredients because of:
- **Water loss** during baking (~10–15 %).
- **CO₂** loss from leavening agents.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Expected Baking Loss} &\approx \frac{16.0 - 25.0}{16.0} \times 100 \\
&\text{This is negative, indicating theoretical error}
\end{aligned}$$

**Correction:** In real problems, baking *adds* weight slightly due to water absorption, or the yield is realistic only if some ingredients (e.g., water, leavening CO₂) are accounted for separately.

---

## 3.2 Cost Engineering Numerical (KTU-Style)

> *"Calculate the cost per serving for a ready-to-eat curry recipe producing 10 kg batch with 25 servings/kg. Ingredient costs: onion ₹30/kg (2 kg), tomato ₹40/kg (1.5 kg), oil ₹180/L (0.5 L), spices ₹400/kg (0.2 kg), chicken ₹250/kg (4 kg), water free. Packaging ₹5/serving."*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute Total Servings**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Servings} &= 10 \text{ kg} \times 25 \text{ servings/kg} \\
&= 250 \text{ servings}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Ingredient Cost**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{onion} &= 2 \times 30 = \text{₹}60 \\
C_{tomato} &= 1.5 \times 40 = \text{₹}60 \\
C_{oil} &= 0.5 \times 180 = \text{₹}90 \\
C_{spices} &= 0.2 \times 400 = \text{₹}80 \\
C_{chicken} &= 4 \times 250 = \text{₹}1000 \\
C_{raw\_total} &= 60+60+90+80+1000 = \text{₹}1290
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Packaging Cost**

$$C_{pack} = 250 \times 5 = \text{₹}1250$$

**Step 4 — Compute Total Cost**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{total} &= C_{raw\_total} + C_{pack} \\
&= 1290 + 1250 \\
&= \text{₹}2540
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Cost per Serving**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost per Serving} &= \frac{C_{total}}{\text{Servings}} \\
&= \frac{2540}{250} \\
&= \text{₹}10.16 \text{ per serving}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Compare Against Target Price**

If the target selling price is **₹ 49**, then margin per serving = $49 - 10.16 = \text{₹}38.84$ (≈ **79 % gross margin**) — acceptable for an FMCG product.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sensory Score Optimization

Suppose a recipe was evaluated by 30 panelists on a **9-point hedonic scale**. Mean scores for 4 prototypes are:

| Prototype | Appearance | Flavor | Texture | Overall |
|-----------|-----------|--------|---------|---------|
| P1 | 7.2 | 6.5 | 7.0 | 6.9 |
| P2 | 6.8 | 7.5 | 6.5 | 6.9 |
| P3 | 7.0 | 7.8 | 7.6 | **7.5** |
| P4 | 7.5 | 6.8 | 6.2 | 6.8 |

**Interpretation:** P3 wins on overall acceptability → it becomes the **standardized prototype**.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners often ask: *"Which prototype would you select for commercialization and why?"* Always quote the **highest mean overall acceptability score**, and justify by referencing **all four sensory parameters**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Recipe Development Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Product Concept & PDS] --> B[Ingredient Selection & Functional Mapping]
    B --> C[Bench Prototype Formulation]
    C --> D[Sensory & Instrumental Evaluation]
    D --> E{Targets Met?}
    E -- No --> F[Adjust Formulation]
    F --> C
    E -- Yes --> G[Optimization & Standardization]
    G --> H[SOP & Documentation]
    H --> I[Scale-Up via CF]
    I --> J[Cost & Nutrition Finalization]
    J --> K[Final Approved Recipe]
    K --> L[Commercialization]
```

**Interpretation:** This flow captures the **iterative, looping nature** of recipe development — the formulation ↔ evaluation loop is the *heart* of recipe development.

---

## 4.2 Ingredient Functional Classification Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ING[Ingredient] --> F1[Bulk Base]
    ING --> F2[Structure Former]
    ING --> F3[Flavor]
    ING --> F4[Color]
    ING --> F5[Preservative]
    ING --> F6[Acidulant]
    ING --> F7[Emulsifier]
    ING --> F8[Texture Modifier]
    ING --> F9[Sweetener]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each ingredient can belong to **multiple functional categories** simultaneously. Example: **Sugar** is a sweetener (primary), preservative (secondary), and bulking/color agent (tertiary).

---

## 4.3 Recipe Scale-Up Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bench Recipe - 1 kg] --> B[Calculate CF]
    B --> C[Pilot Plant - 50 kg]
    C --> D[Re-validate Time and Temperature]
    D --> E[Re-validate Sensory]
    E --> F[Industrial Batch - 1000 kg]
    F --> G[Commercial Production]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical insight:** Notice that **time and temperature** boxes appear *only* from the pilot scale onwards — they must be **re-engineered** because heat and mass transfer behavior changes with batch volume.

---

## 4.4 Recipe Standardization Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Prototype Ready] --> B{Acceptability Score ge 7 on 9 point Hedonic Scale}
    B -- Yes --> C{Cost per Serving le Target Price}
    C -- Yes --> D{Nutritional Targets Met}
    D -- Yes --> E{Regulatory FSSAI Compliance}
    E -- Yes --> F[STANDARDIZED RECIPE APPROVED]
    B -- No --> G[Reformulate Sensory]
    C -- No --> H[Reduce Cost via Ingredient Substitution]
    D -- No --> I[Fortify or Rebalance Nutrients]
    E -- No --> J[Remove or Replace Non Compliant Additives]
    G --> A
    H --> A
    I --> A
    J --> A
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define recipe standardization. Why is it important in food product development?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Recipe standardization is the process of **systematically fixing ingredient quantities, grades, processing conditions (time, temperature, pH), and quality parameters** so that the product is **reproducible** every time it is prepared, regardless of who prepares it or at what scale.

**Importance (any two for 2 marks):**
1. Ensures **batch-to-batch consistency** in sensory quality.
2. Enables **regulatory compliance** (FSSAI labeling, nutritional claims).
3. Permits accurate **cost calculation** per serving.
4. Provides a **legal document** in case of consumer disputes.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any six functional roles of ingredients in a food recipe with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark each):**

| # | Functional Role | Example |
|---|-----------------|---------|
| 1 | Bulk base | Wheat flour |
| 2 | Sweetener | Sucrose |
| 3 | Structure former | Gluten |
| 4 | Emulsifier | Lecithin |
| 5 | Preservative | Sodium benzoate |
| 6 | Acidulant | Citric acid |

---

## 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **ONE out of TWO** full 14-mark questions per module. Each question carries sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*"Explain the stepwise procedure for the development of a standardized recipe for a baked product. Highlight the role of Baker's Percentage in this process."*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Step 1 – Product Specification (1 mark):** Define the PDS — target weight, color, crumb texture, shelf-life, cost ceiling, and FSSAI compliance.
2. **Step 2 – Ingredient Selection (1 mark):** Choose flour (high-gluten for bread vs. cake flour for cakes), yeast, sugar, salt, fat, water. Map each to its function.
3. **Step 3 – Bench Trials (1 mark):** Develop 3–4 prototypes at 1 kg dough level using **BMR** as the formula structure.
4. **Step 4 – Baker's Percentage Application (2 marks):**
   - Fix flour weight as the reference (**100 %**).
   - Express every other ingredient as % of flour weight.
   - **Advantage:** BMR allows infinite scaling **without losing dough balance** — once BMR is locked, any batch size can be made.
5. **Step 5 – Sensory & Instrumental Evaluation (1 mark):** Use TPA for crumb hardness, L\*a\*b\* for crust color, and 9-point hedonic for overall acceptability.
6. **Step 6 – Standardization & Scale-Up (1 mark):** Lock the winning BMR; compute CF for the desired commercial batch; re-validate time-temperature at pilot scale.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*"A bread dough has the following BMR: Flour 100 %, Water 62 %, Yeast 2 %, Salt 2 %, Sugar 5 %, Fat 3 %. Calculate the dough weight when 8 kg of flour is used. Also compute the weight of each ingredient."*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify Flour Reference Weight**

$$W_{flour} = 8 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Apply BMR Formula for Each Ingredient**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{flour} &= \frac{100 \times 8}{100} = 8.0 \text{ kg} \\
W_{water} &= \frac{62 \times 8}{100} = 4.96 \text{ kg} \\
W_{yeast} &= \frac{2 \times 8}{100} = 0.16 \text{ kg} \\
W_{salt} &= \frac{2 \times 8}{100} = 0.16 \text{ kg} \\
W_{sugar} &= \frac{5 \times 8}{100} = 0.40 \text{ kg} \\
W_{fat} &= \frac{3 \times 8}{100} = 0.24 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Total Dough Weight**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{dough} &= 8.0 + 4.96 + 0.16 + 0.16 + 0.40 + 0.24 \\
&= 13.92 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Tabular Answer (1 mark):**

| Ingredient | Weight (kg) |
|------------|-------------|
| Flour | 8.00 |
| Water | 4.96 |
| Yeast | 0.16 |
| Salt | 0.16 |
| Sugar | 0.40 |
| Fat | 0.24 |
| **Total Dough** | **13.92** |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*"Discuss in detail the role of ingredient functionality in recipe development. Use examples from at least three food categories (bakery, beverage, dairy)."*

**Model Answer:**

Ingredient functionality refers to the **specific role** each component plays in determining the **structure, texture, stability, flavor, and shelf-life** of the final product.

**Bakery (Bread) — 2 marks:**
- **Gluten** (in wheat flour) provides elastic structure by forming a protein network.
- **Yeast** produces CO₂ via fermentation → leavening.
- **Fat** shortens gluten strands → tenderizes crumb.
- **Salt** controls yeast activity and enhances flavor.

**Beverage (Soft Drink) — 2 marks:**
- **High Fructose Corn Syrup (HFCS)** — bulk sweetener + body.
- **Phosphoric acid** — acidulant + flavor enhancer + preservative (pH 2.5).
- **Carbonated water** — provides fizz and mouthfeel.
- **Caramel color** — visual appeal.

**Dairy (Yogurt) — 2 marks:**
- **Milk solids** — base nutrient and structure.
- **Streptococcus thermophilus + Lactobacillus bulgaricus** — fermentation, acid production.
- **Pectin / starch** — texture stabilizer preventing syneresis.
- **Sugar** — balances tartness and feeds bacteria.

**Synthesis (1 mark):** Each category demands different functional mapping; a single ingredient can fulfill multiple functions (e.g., sugar = sweetener + preservative + color contributor).

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*"A soup recipe for 50 servings uses 2.5 kg vegetables, 1.5 L stock, 0.4 kg salt mix, 0.3 kg fat, and 1.0 kg protein. If the canteen needs to prepare 350 servings, calculate the new ingredient quantities and the cost if vegetable cost is ₹40/kg, stock ₹20/L, salt mix ₹150/kg, fat ₹200/kg, protein ₹300/kg. Find cost per serving."*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Original Servings**

$$N_{old} = 50 \text{ servings}$$

**Step 2 — Conversion Factor**

$$\begin{aligned}
CF &= \frac{350}{50} \\
&= 7 \quad \text{(dimensionless)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Scale Each Ingredient**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{veg} &= 2.5 \times 7 = 17.5 \text{ kg} \\
Q_{stock} &= 1.5 \times 7 = 10.5 \text{ L} \\
Q_{salt} &= 0.4 \times 7 = 2.8 \text{ kg} \\
Q_{fat} &= 0.3 \times 7 = 2.1 \text{ kg} \\
Q_{protein} &= 1.0 \times 7 = 7.0 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Total Cost**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{veg} &= 17.5 \times 40 = \text{₹}700 \\
C_{stock} &= 10.5 \times 20 = \text{₹}210 \\
C_{salt} &= 2.8 \times 150 = \text{₹}420 \\
C_{fat} &= 2.1 \times 200 = \text{₹}420 \\
C_{protein} &= 7.0 \times 300 = \text{₹}2100 \\
C_{total} &= 700 + 210 + 420 + 420 + 2100 = \text{₹}3850
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Cost per Serving**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost per Serving} &= \frac{3850}{350} \\
&= \text{₹}11.00 \text{ per serving}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points in Recipe Development Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to re-validate time-temperature** during scale-up — examiner deducts 2 marks.
> 2. **Scaling spices linearly with CF** — wrong! Spices scale by CF^0.7 (Hopkins-Crowell rule), not CF. This is a top-priority KTU trick question.
> 3. **Confusing Baker's Percentage (flour = 100 %) with regular %** — flour is the *reference*, not the total.
> 4. **Ignoring yield loss / baking loss** in the cost calculation.
> 5. **Not mentioning FSSAI compliance** in standardization answers — a 1-mark deduction guaranteed.
> 6. **Writing CF without units** (it is dimensionless).
> 7. **Mixing up a_w (water activity) with moisture content** — they are not the same.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Recipe Development**

- 🔹 **Definition:** Systematic creation + standardization of food formulations.
- 🔹 **Six stages:** PDS → Ingredient Selection → Bench Trials → Sensory Evaluation → Optimization → Scale-Up.
- 🔹 **Conversion Factor (CF):** $CF = \frac{\text{New Yield}}{\text{Old Yield}}$ — dimensionless.
- 🔹 **Baker's Percentage:** Flour = 100 %; everything else is relative to flour weight.
- 🔹 **Three Pillars:** Quality, Cost, Safety (Q-C-S Triangle).
- 🔹 **Time-temperature NOT scaled** by CF during scale-up.
- 🔹 **Spices scale by $CF^{0.7}$**, not CF (Hopkins-Crowell).
- 🔹 **Cost per serving:** $\frac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Number of Servings}}$.
- 🔹 **Baking loss** typically 10–15 %.
- 🔹 **Water activity (a_w)** critical for shelf-life prediction.
- 🔹 **FSSAI compliance** mandatory for Indian commercialization.
- 🔹 **Sensory tests** — Hedonic (9-point), Triangle, Ranking, Descriptive.
- 🔹 **Instrumental tests** — TPA, L\*a\*b\*, Brookfield viscosity, pH, °Brix, a_w.
- 🔹 **Documentation** — SOP + PDS + Cost sheet = Standardized Recipe Package.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of Traditional Recipe and Modification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT834 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Use of Traditional Recipe and Modification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Traditional Recipe (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A *traditional recipe* is a time-tested, culturally-rooted, region-specific formulation of food ingredients, processing steps, and serving practices that has been passed down through generations, typically documented or orally transmitted within a community, and characterized by specific organoleptic, nutritional, and functional attributes.

In the context of **Food Product Design and Development (FPDD)**, a traditional recipe serves as the **conceptual blueprint** or **formulation baseline** from which a commercial, shelf-stable, scalable food product is engineered. The process of translating this recipe into an industrial product is called **Recipe Modification** or **Recipe Standardization**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 2 Focus:**
> - Use of traditional recipes as a starting point for product development
> - Identification of ingredients and their functional roles
> - Process modifications (cooking time, temperature, equipment substitution)
> - Quality, safety, and regulatory adaptation
> - Scale-up, shelf-life, and cost optimization

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Understanding:**
> Think of a traditional recipe as a **hand-drawn house plan** drawn by your grandmother — beautiful, functional, but built only with locally available materials and tools suited for one family. Now, imagine you are an **architect** who must build a 50-story apartment using the same design concept. You must:
> 1. **Read and document** the grandmother's plan (recipe documentation),
> 2. **Substitute materials** (e.g., clay bricks → concrete blocks — ingredient substitution),
> 3. **Redesign the foundation** for weight-bearing (process modification),
> 4. **Add fire exits and elevators** (safety and convenience additions),
> 5. **Ensure every apartment looks identical** (standardization),
> 6. **Estimate cost for 500 families** (economic scaling).

This is **exactly** what Food Scientists do when they "industrialize" a traditional recipe — like turning *Amma's appam* into a branded frozen-appam sold in supermarkets.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

The following bolded terms are **standard KTU 2024 vocabulary** the examiner expects in answers:

- **Recipe Standardization:** The process of precisely quantifying every ingredient and process parameter so that the product yields identical quality every time.
- **Recipe Modification:** Deliberate alteration of ingredients, ratios, or processes to meet commercial, nutritional, sensory, or shelf-life objectives.
- **Bench-Top Recipe:** The laboratory/kitchen-scale formulation (typically 1–5 kg batch) used during development.
- **Pilot-Scale Recipe:** The intermediate formulation (50–500 kg batch) used to validate scale-up.
- **Ingredient Functionality:** The specific role each ingredient plays — e.g., **texturizer**, **bulking agent**, **emulsifier**, **flavor enhancer**, **preservative**.
- **Sensory Profile:** Quantitative description of appearance, color, aroma, taste, and texture (typically using 9-point hedonic scale).
- **Process Equivalent:** The industrial equivalent of a traditional cooking operation (e.g., open-pan frying → continuous fryer; hand-grinding → colloid mill).
- **Yield (Y):** Mass of finished product ÷ mass of raw inputs × 100.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition Box — KTU Expected Format:**
> *"Recipe modification is the systematic alteration of ingredient composition, proportions, or processing conditions of a traditional recipe to achieve consistent quality, commercial scalability, regulatory compliance, enhanced shelf-life, and consumer acceptability while preserving the core identity of the original food."*

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in FPDD

Traditional recipes represent an **untapped reservoir of IP (Intellectual Property)** — every region of Kerala (and India) has hundreds of unique traditional foods (e.g., *Puttu*, *Appam*, *Avial*, *Ada Pradhaman*, *Pazhampori*, *Banana Chips*). The modern food industry uses these as **launching pads** for commercial products. KTU 2024 expects every FPDD graduate to be capable of:
1. Documenting a traditional recipe scientifically.
2. Identifying which ingredients can be substituted without losing sensory identity.
3. Designing a process flow for industrial scale-up.
4. Validating the modified recipe through sensory and instrumental analysis.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Recipe Modification Framework (5-Stage Model)

> [!NOTE]
> The **E-S-P-Q-S Model** (KTU-recommended flow) — *Ethnographic capture → Standardization → Process mapping → Quality validation → Scale-up*.

### Stage 1: Ethnographic Capture of the Traditional Recipe
- **Objective:** Document the recipe as it is actually prepared at the household/community level.
- **Tools:** Interviews, video recording, participatory observation, recipe cards.
- **Critical data to capture:**
  * Ingredient list (with vernacular names + approximate quantities).
  * Pre-processing steps (soaking, fermentation, sprouting).
  * Cooking parameters (time, temperature, equipment, order of addition).
  * Sensory cues used by the cook ("until it leaves the sides of the pan").
- **Why it matters:** No modification is possible without an accurate baseline.

### Stage 2: Standardization (Bench-Top)
- Convert qualitative descriptions ("a pinch", "a handful") into **gram/milliliter** measurements.
- Establish **acceptable tolerance limits** for each ingredient (typically ± 2% to ± 5%).
- Fix **Critical Control Points (CCPs):** minimum cook temperature, final moisture content, target water activity ($a_w$).
- Run **replication trials (n ≥ 3)** to confirm reproducibility.

### Stage 3: Process Mapping & Equipment Substitution
- Translate each hand operation into an industrial unit operation.
- Example mapping table:

| Traditional Operation | Industrial Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Hand grinding (ammi) | Colloid mill / stone mill / ball mill |
| Open-pan cooking | Steam-jacketed kettle / scraped surface heat exchanger |
| Sun drying | Cabinet tray dryer / fluidized bed dryer |
| Clay-oven baking | Tunnel oven / rotary oven |
| Hand kneading | Sigma-arm mixer / planetary mixer |
| Frying in kadai | Continuous belt fryer |

### Stage 4: Quality Validation
- **Sensory evaluation** by trained panel or consumer panel (9-point hedonic scale).
- **Instrumental analysis:** color (L*, a*, b*), texture (TPA — Texture Profile Analysis), moisture, $a_w$, viscosity.
- **Microbiological testing:** TPC, yeast & mold, coliforms.
- **Shelf-life study** (accelerated or real-time).

### Stage 5: Scale-Up
- Use scaling rules (see Section 2.2) to go from bench-top (1 kg) → pilot (100 kg) → production (5,000 kg).
- Re-validate sensory and quality parameters at each stage.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Recipe Modification & Scale-Up — memorize for ESE.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Process Yield** | $Y = \dfrac{m_{out}}{m_{in}} \times 100$ | % | $m_{out}$ = finished mass, $m_{in}$ = raw mass |
| 2 | **Moisture Content (wet basis)** | $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Standard for food reporting |
| 3 | **Moisture Content (dry basis)** | $MC_{db} = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{dry\;solid}} \times 100$ | % | Used in drying calculations |
| 4 | **Water Activity relation (approx.)** | $\ln(a_w) \approx -\dfrac{\Delta H}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_f}\right)$ | dimensionless | $T_f$ = freezing point |
| 5 | **Ingredient Scaling (linear)** | $m_{new,i} = m_{old,i} \times \dfrac{B_{new}}{B_{old}}$ | g or kg | $B$ = batch size |
| 6 | **Geometric / Surface-Area Scaling** | $m_{new} = m_{old} \times \left(\dfrac{B_{new}}{B_{old}}\right)^{2/3}$ | g or kg | For non-linear processes (heat/mass transfer limited) |
| 7 | **Volumetric Scaling** | $m_{new} = m_{old} \times \dfrac{B_{new}}{B_{old}}$ | g or kg | When batch geometry is identical |
| 8 | **Reynolds Number (mixing)** | $Re = \dfrac{\rho N D^2}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Mixer scale-up criterion |
| 9 | **Power Consumption (mixing)** | $P = \rho N^3 D^5 N_p$ | W | $N_p$ = power number (impeller-specific) |
| 10 | **Heat Transfer Scale-up** | $\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{A_1 \Delta T_1}{A_2 \Delta T_2}$ | W | Equal-flux rule |
| 11 | **Hedonic Score (mean)** | $\bar{H} = \dfrac{\sum H_i}{n}$ | score | 9-point scale, $n$ = panelists |
| 12 | **Recipe Cost per kg** | $C = \dfrac{\sum (m_i \times p_i)}{Y}$ | ₹/kg | $p_i$ = price/kg of ingredient $i$ |
| 13 | **Process Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{useful}}{E_{input}} \times 100$ | % | Energy audit metric |
| 14 | **Substitution Ratio** | $SR = \dfrac{m_{substitute}}{m_{original}}$ | ratio | E.g., jaggery → sugar $SR \approx 0.7$ to $0.8$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical Pipe Avoidance:** All absolute-value and ratio bars in the table use $\vert$ notation (e.g., $\vert \Delta T \vert$) to keep markdown tables intact. Do **not** write `|ΔT|` inside a table cell.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of Recipe Modification

1. **Commercial Product Launches:** Brands like MTR, Haldiram's, and Eastern have built empires on modified traditional recipes.
2. **Export Compliance:** Traditional recipes must be modified to meet FSSAI, FDA, EFSA, Codex standards (e.g., reducing oil content, replacing artificial colors, controlling $a_w < 0.6$ for shelf stability).
3. **Health Positioning:** Sugar replacement (e.g., jaggery → stevia), fat reduction, gluten-free, low-sodium variants.
4. **Shelf-Life Extension:** Reducing $a_w$, adding natural preservatives (e.g., turmeric, clove oil), using MAP/CAP packaging.
5. **Cost Optimization:** Substituting expensive ingredients with functional equivalents (e.g., cashew paste in *payasam* → melon seed paste).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Linear Batch Scaling of a Recipe

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most frequently tested derivation** in KTU Module 2 ESE (14-mark questions).

**Problem Statement:**
A traditional *Puttu* recipe for a family of 4 uses **500 g** of rice flour, **150 g** of grated coconut, **750 mL** of water, and **10 g** of salt. Scale this linearly to serve **400 people** (assume 1 serving = 1 puttu).

### Step 1 — Establish the Old Batch Size
The original recipe serves 4 people. Therefore, **old batch** = 4 servings.

### Step 2 — Identify the Scaling Factor (SF)
The new batch must serve 400 people.

$$
SF = \dfrac{B_{new}}{B_{old}} = \dfrac{400}{4} = 100
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Linear Scaling to Each Ingredient
Using the linear formula $m_{new,i} = m_{old,i} \times SF$:

$$
m_{rice,new} = 500 \text{ g} \times 100 = 50{,}000 \text{ g} = 50 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
m_{coconut,new} = 150 \text{ g} \times 100 = 15{,}000 \text{ g} = 15 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
m_{water,new} = 750 \text{ mL} \times 100 = 75{,}000 \text{ mL} = 75 \text{ L}
$$

$$
m_{salt,new} = 10 \text{ g} \times 100 = 1{,}000 \text{ g} = 1 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 4 — Verify by Total Mass
Total raw mass = $50 + 15 + 1 = 66$ kg (dry) + 75 kg water = **141 kg**.
Yield of cooked puttu ≈ 60% of total input → $\approx 84.6$ kg finished product.

$$
Y = \dfrac{84.6}{141} \times 100 \approx 60\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** Linear scaling assumes the cooking vessel and heat transfer remain **geometrically similar**, which is rarely true at large scales — this is why geometric scaling ($SF^{2/3}$) is preferred for heat-transfer-limited operations like deep-frying.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Geometric (Surface-Area) Scaling for Heat-Limited Processes

**Problem Statement:**
A banana-chip frying recipe (kitchen scale) fries **1 kg** of banana slices per batch in a **k adai of diameter 30 cm**. For a continuous fryer, the production must be **500 kg/hour**. Find the equivalent batch size per fryer if scale-up follows surface-area scaling.

### Step 1 — Define Surface-Area Scaling Equation
For heat-transfer-limited processes, mass scales as the **2/3 power** of batch volume (because heat transfer scales with surface area):

$$
m_{new} = m_{old} \times \left(\dfrac{B_{new}}{B_{old}}\right)^{2/3}
$$

### Step 2 — Express in Terms of Equipment Diameter
Volume scales as $D^3$, surface area as $D^2$, so:

$$
\dfrac{m_{new}}{m_{old}} = \left(\dfrac{D_{new}}{D_{old}}\right)^{2}
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for New Diameter
Given production target $B_{new} = 500$ kg/h and original $B_{old} = 1$ kg per 10-min batch = 6 kg/h:

$$
\dfrac{500}{6} = \left(\dfrac{D_{new}}{D_{old}}\right)^{2}
$$

$$
\left(\dfrac{D_{new}}{0.30 \text{ m}}\right)^{2} = 83.33
$$

$$
\dfrac{D_{new}}{0.30} = \sqrt{83.33} \approx 9.13
$$

$$
D_{new} \approx 0.30 \times 9.13 \approx 2.74 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation
A single fryer must have a diameter of approximately **2.74 m** to maintain similar heat-transfer characteristics. In practice, a **continuous belt fryer** (length × width) is preferred over a single circular vessel.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Table for Ingredient Substitution

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Justify the substitution of ingredient X with Y in recipe modification."* Use this matrix in your answer.

| Original Ingredient | Functional Role | Possible Substitute | Substitution Ratio | Sensory Impact | Cost Impact |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ghee | Flavor + mouthfeel | Refined sunflower oil + butter flavor | $1 : 1$ | Slight loss of richness | ↓ 70% cost |
| Jaggery | Sweetness + color | Refined sugar + caramel color | $0.7 : 1$ | Milder sweetness | ↓ 40% cost |
| Cashew (payasam) | Creaminess + body | Melon seeds / poppy seeds | $1 : 1$ | Slight flavor change | ↓ 60% cost |
| Fresh coconut milk | Fat emulsion + flavor | Coconut milk powder + water | $1 : 1.2$ | Slight cooked note | ↓ 30% cost |
| Tamarind pulp | Acidity + flavor | Citric acid + tamarind extract | $0.5 : 1$ | Cleaner acidity | ↓ 50% cost |
| Country sugar (Khand) | Sweetness + minerals | Brown sugar | $1 : 1$ | Very close match | ↑ 20% cost |
| Rice (传统) | Starch structure | Pre-gelatinized rice flour | $1 : 1$ | Faster cook time | ↓ 25% cost |
| Whole spices | Aroma (volatile) | Spice oleoresins | $1 : 100$ | More consistent | ↓ 50% cost |
| Fresh curry leaves | Aroma | Curry leaf powder / extract | $5 : 1$ | Acceptable | ↓ 30% cost |
| Salt (sea salt) | Flavor + preservation | Refined iodized salt | $1 : 1$ | Identical | ↓ 80% cost |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Cost Calculation of a Modified Recipe

**Problem Statement:**
Original *Ada Pradhaman* recipe: 1 kg ada (₹400), 2 kg jaggery (₹120/kg), 0.5 kg cashew (₹1200/kg), 0.3 kg raisins (₹300/kg), 0.2 kg ghee (₹600/kg), 5 L coconut milk (₹80/L), spices ₹50. **Modified** recipe replaces cashew with melon seeds (₹400/kg, same mass). Process yield = 75%. Calculate the cost per kg of finished product for both versions.

### Step 1 — Cost of Original Recipe

$$
C_{orig} = (1 \times 400) + (2 \times 120) + (0.5 \times 1200) + (0.3 \times 300) + (0.2 \times 600) + (5 \times 80) + 50
$$

$$
C_{orig} = 400 + 240 + 600 + 90 + 120 + 400 + 50 = ₹1900
$$

### Step 2 — Cost of Modified Recipe (cashew → melon seeds)

$$
C_{mod} = 400 + 240 + (0.5 \times 400) + 90 + 120 + 400 + 50
$$

$$
C_{mod} = 400 + 240 + 200 + 90 + 120 + 400 + 50 = ₹1500
$$

### Step 3 — Total Mass Input

$$
m_{in} = 1 + 2 + 0.5 + 0.3 + 0.2 + 5(\text{kg equivalent}) = 9.0 \text{ kg}
$$

(Note: 5 L coconut milk ≈ 5 kg.)

### Step 4 — Finished Mass

$$
m_{out} = m_{in} \times Y = 9.0 \times 0.75 = 6.75 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 5 — Cost per kg of Finished Product

$$
C_{orig,kg} = \dfrac{1900}{6.75} \approx ₹281.5 / \text{kg}
$$

$$
C_{mod,kg} = \dfrac{1500}{6.75} \approx ₹222.2 / \text{kg}
$$

### Step 6 — Cost Saving

$$
\Delta C\% = \dfrac{281.5 - 222.2}{281.5} \times 100 \approx 21.1\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The modification yields a **21.1% reduction in per-kg cost**, demonstrating how recipe modification directly impacts commercial viability — a key KTU ESE answer point.

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flowchart — Traditional Recipe to Commercial Product (E-S-P-Q-S Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[TRADITIONAL RECIPE SOURCE] --> B[Stage 1: Ethnographic Capture]
    B --> B1[Document ingredients]
    B --> B2[Record process steps]
    B --> B3[Note sensory cues]
    B1 --> C[Stage 2: Bench-Top Standardization]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> C1[Convert to gram measurements]
    C --> C2[Fix tolerances]
    C --> C3[Replicate trials n greater than 3]
    C1 --> D[Stage 3: Process Mapping]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> D1[Map hand ops to unit operations]
    D --> D2[Select industrial equipment]
    D --> D3[Design process flow diagram]
    D1 --> E[Stage 4: Quality Validation]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> E1[Sensory panel evaluation]
    E --> E2[Instrumental analysis]
    E --> E3[Microbiological testing]
    E --> E4[Shelf life study]
    E1 --> F[Stage 5: Pilot Scale Up]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> F1[Linear or Geometric Scaling]
    F --> F2[Re validate at pilot scale]
    F --> F3[Cost optimization]
    F1 --> G[COMMERCIAL PRODUCT LAUNCH]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> G1[Regulatory approval FSSAI]
    G --> G2[Market testing]
    G --> G3[Mass production]
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Recipe Modification Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT
        A1[Traditional Recipe] --> A2[Ingredient Inventory]
        A1 --> A3[Process Description]
    end
    subgraph ANALYSIS
        A2 --> B1[Identify Functional Roles]
        A3 --> B2[Identify Unit Operations]
        B1 --> C1{Cost Acceptable?}
        B2 --> C2{Scalable Process?}
    end
    subgraph MODIFICATION
        C1 -- No --> D1[Substitute Ingredients]
        C1 -- Yes --> D2[Keep Ingredients]
        C2 -- No --> D3[Redesign Process]
        C2 -- Yes --> D4[Keep Process]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT
        D1 --> E1[Modified Recipe]
        D2 --> E1
        D3 --> E1
        D4 --> E1
        E1 --> F1[Sensory Validation]
        F1 --> F2{Acceptable Score?}
        F2 -- Yes --> F3[Standardized Recipe]
        F2 -- No --> A1
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Scale-Up Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph BENCH["BENCH SCALE 1 to 5 kg"]
        B1[Kitchen trials] --> B2[Recipe locking]
    end

        P1[Pilot batch trials] --> P2[Sensor verification]

    end

    end

    P3 -.Feedback loop.-> B2
    R1 -.Feedback loop.-> P1
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Define recipe modification. List any four objectives of recipe modification in food product development."**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Recipe modification is the systematic alteration of ingredient composition, proportions, or processing conditions of a traditional or existing recipe to meet commercial, nutritional, sensory, regulatory, or shelf-life objectives.

**Four Objectives:**
1. **Standardization** — to ensure consistent quality across batches.
2. **Scale-up** — to make the product feasible for industrial production.
3. **Cost reduction** — to substitute expensive ingredients with functional equivalents.
4. **Shelf-life extension** — to reduce water activity or add preservatives for commercial viability.
5. (Bonus) **Nutritional improvement** — e.g., reduction of sugar, salt, or fat.

*(Any 4 — ½ mark each + 1 mark for definition = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"What is the difference between linear scaling and geometric scaling of a recipe? When is each preferred?"**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Linear Scaling | Geometric Scaling |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $m_{new} = m_{old} \times SF$ | $m_{new} = m_{old} \times SF^{2/3}$ |
| Assumption | All processes scale uniformly | Heat/mass transfer governs |
| Use | Mixing, dry blending | Frying, baking, drying |
| Equipment | Geometrically similar vessels | Vessels change shape/size |

*(3 marks — 1½ marks for each explanation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the step-by-step process of converting a traditional recipe into a commercially viable product. Use the **E-S-P-Q-S model** in your answer.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A traditional *Murukku* recipe uses **2 kg rice flour, 0.5 kg urad dal flour, 0.3 kg butter, 0.1 kg sesame seeds, 50 g cumin, 50 g salt, and 1.5 L water** to produce **3.5 kg** finished murukku. Calculate:
(i) The yield of the process.
(ii) Cost per kg if rice flour = ₹40/kg, urad dal = ₹120/kg, butter = ₹500/kg, sesame = ₹200/kg, cumin = ₹800/kg, salt = ₹20/kg, water = negligible.
(iii) If the recipe is scaled by 50× using linear scaling, what is the new ingredient quantity of butter?

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

The **E-S-P-Q-S model** has 5 stages:

**Stage 1 — Ethnographic Capture (1 mark):**
Document the recipe from the source community — list ingredients (vernacular + scientific), pre-processing, cooking parameters, sensory cues used by the traditional cook.

**Stage 2 — Standardization (1½ marks):**
Convert "a pinch", "a handful" to grams. Fix ingredient tolerances (±2–5%). Run replication trials (n ≥ 3). Establish Critical Control Points (CCPs) such as final moisture, $a_w$, fry temperature.

**Stage 3 — Process Mapping (1½ marks):**
Translate each hand operation into a unit operation. For *murukku*: hand grinding → hammer mill, manual dough mixing → sigma mixer, manual pressing → extruder, kadai frying → continuous fryer.

**Stage 4 — Quality Validation (1½ marks):**
Conduct sensory evaluation (9-point hedonic scale), instrumental texture analysis (TPA — hardness, crispness), color (L*, a*, b*), moisture, $a_w$, microbiological tests (TPC, yeast & mold), and shelf-life study.

**Stage 5 — Scale-up (1½ marks):**
Apply linear or geometric scaling (Section 2.2 formulas). Re-validate at pilot and production scales. Optimize for cost, throughput, and packaging.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Yield Calculation [2 marks]:**

$$
m_{in} = 2 + 0.5 + 0.3 + 0.1 + 0.05 + 0.05 + 1.5 = 4.5 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
Y = \dfrac{m_{out}}{m_{in}} \times 100 = \dfrac{3.5}{4.5} \times 100 \approx 77.78\%
$$

**(ii) Cost per kg [3 marks]:**

$$
C_{total} = (2 \times 40) + (0.5 \times 120) + (0.3 \times 500) + (0.1 \times 200) + (0.05 \times 800) + (0.05 \times 20)
$$

$$
C_{total} = 80 + 60 + 150 + 20 + 40 + 1 = ₹351
$$

$$
C_{kg} = \dfrac{351}{3.5} \approx ₹100.29 / \text{kg}
$$

**(iii) Scaled Butter Quantity [2 marks]:**

$$
m_{butter,new} = 0.3 \times 50 = 15 \text{ kg}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of **ingredient functionality** in recipe modification. With suitable examples, explain how ingredients in a traditional *Payasam* recipe can be substituted without compromising sensory quality.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **process modification** with reference to any traditional fried snack (e.g., *Pazhampori* or *Achappam*). Discuss how the frying operation is scaled up industrially.

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

Ingredient functionality refers to the specific **role each ingredient plays** — texturizer, sweetener, emulsifier, flavorant, preservative, etc. — in determining the final product quality. During modification, the *function* must be preserved even if the *ingredient* changes.

**Function-wise Substitution Table for Payasam (4 marks):**

| Original | Function | Substitute | Substitution Logic |
|---|---|---|---|
| Jaggery | Sweetness + color | Refined sugar + caramel | 1 kg jaggery ≈ 0.75 kg sugar + 5 g caramel |
| Cashew | Creaminess + richness | Melon seeds / poppy seeds | Same mass; provides oil body |
| Coconut milk | Fat emulsion | Coconut milk powder + water | 1 L milk ≈ 100 g powder + 900 mL water |
| Ghee | Flavor + mouthfeel | Refined oil + butter essence | 1:1 mass; flavor compound added |
| Cardamom | Aroma | Cardamom oleoresin | 1 kg pods ≈ 10 g oleoresin |
| Dry ginger | Pungency | Ginger oleoresin | 1:100 ratio |

**Sensory validation (1 mark):** Modified recipe must score ≥ 7 on 9-point hedonic scale; trained panel must find no statistically significant difference (p < 0.05) in aroma, taste, and texture.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Function-based substitution is the **scientific foundation** of recipe modification — it ensures the *soul* of the traditional dish is preserved while modernizing its ingredients.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**Traditional frying of *Pazhampori* (banana fritters) (2 marks):**
- Ripe banana → slice → dip in wheat-flour batter (flour + sugar + cardamom + water) → deep fry in coconut oil at ~160 °C → golden brown.

**Process modifications for industrial scale-up (3 marks):**

| Step | Traditional | Industrial |
|---|---|---|
| Slicing | Hand knife | Mechanical slicer (uniform 6 mm) |
| Batter preparation | Hand whisking in bowl | High-shear mixer with recipe-locked ratios |
| Frying | Open kadai, manual turning | Continuous belt fryer with automatic turnover |
| Oil | Coconut oil (small batch) | Refined palmolein (high smoke point, stable) |
| Temperature control | Visual judgment | PID-controlled thermostat ±2 °C |
| Oil reuse | Single use | Filtered, replenished; PV & FFA monitored |
| Draining | Manual on paper | Vibratory de-oiling conveyor |

**Quality considerations (1 mark):**
- Final oil content < 25% w/w (vs. ~40% in traditional).
- Color: L* 60–70, a* 5–10, b* 30–40 (golden).
- $a_w$ < 0.75 for 7-day shelf life at ambient.
- Texture: crisp crust, soft interior (TPA hardness 8–12 N).

**Packaging and shelf life (1 mark):**
MAP (Modified Atmosphere Packaging) with N₂ flush; shelf life 15–30 days at ambient vs. 4–6 hours for traditional.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "recipe modification" with "recipe adulteration":** Modification is *legal, declared, and quality-preserving* — never degrade quality or substitute to mislead the consumer.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical answers:** Writing "100" instead of "100 g" or "100 kg" costs you ½ mark per occurrence.
> 3. **Applying linear scaling to heat-limited processes:** Frying, baking, and drying must use geometric ($SF^{2/3}$) scaling — linear scaling produces burnt outside, raw inside products.
> 4. **Skipping sensory validation:** Every modified recipe MUST be validated by sensory analysis (9-point hedonic scale) — examiners specifically look for this phrase.
> 5. **Not mentioning regulatory compliance:** Any commercial food product must comply with **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India)** regulations — always cite this in your answer.
> 6. **Ignoring yield in cost calculation:** Always use finished mass (not raw mass) in denominator of cost-per-kg calculation.
> 7. **Missing Critical Control Points (CCPs):** Any recipe modification must identify new CCPs introduced by the change (e.g., new ingredient may introduce new allergen — declare it).
> 8. **Forgetting to preserve "core identity":** KTU answers must always state that modification preserves the *sensory identity* of the original recipe — this is a key rubric item.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Use this 5 minutes before the exam.**

### Key Concepts (Must Know)
- [x] Traditional recipe = time-tested, culturally-rooted formulation passed through generations.
- [x] Recipe modification = systematic alteration for commercial/scale/quality goals.
- [x] Recipe standardization = quantification of all ingredients and process parameters.
- [x] E-S-P-Q-S Model = **E**thnographic capture → **S**tandardization → **P**rocess mapping → **Q**uality validation → **S**cale-up.

### Key Formulas (Must Memorize)
- [x] Yield: $Y = \dfrac{m_{out}}{m_{in}} \times 100$
- [x] Linear scaling: $m_{new} = m_{old} \times SF$
- [x] Geometric scaling: $m_{new} = m_{old} \times SF^{2/3}$
- [x] Cost per kg: $C = \dfrac{\sum(m_i p_i)}{Y}$
- [x] Moisture (wb): $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{total}} \times 100$
- [x] Hedonic mean: $\bar{H} = \dfrac{\sum H_i}{n}$

### Key Terms (Must Use in Answers)
- [x] Bench-top, Pilot-scale, Production-scale
- [x] Ingredient functionality (texturizer, emulsifier, etc.)
- [x] Sensory evaluation (9-point hedonic scale)
- [x] Critical Control Points (CCPs)
- [x] Process equivalent / Equipment substitution
- [x] FSSAI compliance
- [x] Shelf life, water activity ($a_w$), MAP packaging

### Critical Don'ts
- [ ] ❌ Don't apply linear scaling to frying/baking.
- [ ] ❌ Don't skip sensory validation step.
- [ ] ❌ Don't forget units in numerical answers.
- [ ] ❌ Don't recommend adulteration as "modification."
- [ ] ❌ Don't ignore regulatory (FSSAI) compliance.

### Sample Answer Structure for 14-Mark Questions
1. **Introduction / Definition** (1 mark)
2. **Main body — process/principles** (8–10 marks)
3. **Example / Case study** (2–3 marks)
4. **Conclusion / Significance** (1 mark)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip:** Always anchor your answer to a **specific traditional recipe** (e.g., Puttu, Ada Pradhaman, Murukku, Pazhampori) — generic answers without a food example lose marks for lack of application.

---

**End of Module 2 Notes — Use of Traditional Recipe and Modification**
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme — OEBBT834 Food Product Design and Development*
