# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the term "functional property of an ingredient" and list any four functional properties relevant to food product design with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> A functional property of an ingredient is defined as **any property of a food ingredient (other than its nutritional value) that influences its behavior during processing, storage, and consumption, ultimately affecting the quality and acceptability of the final product.**

**Four Functional Properties:**

1. **Water Holding Capacity (WHC)** – *Example: Whey protein concentrate* binds water in meat products, improving juiciness and yield.
2. **Emulsifying Capacity (EC)** – *Example: Lecithin* in egg yolk stabilizes oil-in-water emulsions like mayonnaise.
3. **Foaming Capacity (FC)** – *Example: Egg white albumin* traps air during whipping to form meringue.
4. **Gelling Ability** – *Example: Pectin* in the presence of sugar and acid forms a gel in jams and jellies.

*(Allocation: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for 4 properties with examples — 0.5 mark each.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Briefly explain the concept of "cost per functional unit" in ingredient selection with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> The *cost per functional unit* is a strategic procurement metric that quantifies the **actual cost incurred to obtain a unit of desired functionality** from an ingredient, rather than simply looking at per-kg cost.

**Example:** For a beverage requiring 3.2% protein:
- Whey Protein Isolate at ₹1200/kg with 90% protein delivers protein at **₹1.33 per gram**.
- Soy Protein Isolate at ₹600/kg with 85% protein delivers protein at **₹0.71 per gram**.

Although both are "proteins," SPI is **47% cheaper per unit of function delivered**, making it more cost-effective. This concept helps formulators **avoid the trap of choosing cheap ingredients that contribute little functionality** or expensive ingredients that deliver far more than required.

*(Allocation: 1 mark concept + 1 mark formula + 1 mark example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> - **Do NOT confuse** "functional property" with "nutritional property." They are distinct, and mixing them up costs 2–3 marks.
> - **Always state the FORMULA before substituting values.** Examiners deduct marks for direct substitutions.
> - **Do not skip units** in your final answer. "₹/g" is as important as the number itself.
> - **For "selection" questions**, mention at least **3 criteria** (functional, economic, sensory) — listing only one is a common 3-mark loss.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Discuss in detail the various criteria that must be considered when selecting ingredients for the development of a new bakery product (bread). Highlight the role of flour protein content and gluten quality.**

**Model Answer:**

The selection of ingredients for bread making is governed by a **multi-criteria decision matrix** encompassing the following:

**1. Functional Criteria (3 marks):**
- **Flour Strength (Gluten Quality):** Measured by *Gluten Index* and *Farinograph stability*. Strong flours (e.g., bread flour with 12–14% protein) give high loaf volume.
- **Gas Retention Capacity:** Determined by gluten's viscoelastic network; the dough must retain CO₂ produced by yeast for proper crumb structure.
- **Starch Pasting Properties:** Amylose/amylopectin ratio affects crumb softness and staling rate.

**2. Economic Criteria (1 mark):**
- Cost of strong bread flour vs. all-purpose flour; bulk procurement and seasonal price variation must be considered.

**3. Nutritional Criteria (1 mark):**
- Fortification with iron, folic acid, and B-vitamins as per FSSAI standards for "fortified atta/maida."

**4. Sensory Criteria (1 mark):**
- Crust color, crumb texture, and flavor profile must match consumer expectations.

**5. Regulatory Criteria (1 mark):**
- Permitted additives (emulsifiers like SSL, DATEM; oxidizing agents like ascorbic acid) must be within FSSAI-prescribed limits.

**Role of Flour Protein Content & Gluten Quality (integrated above):**
A minimum of **11.5–12% protein** is essential for bread flour. The gluten must form an **elastic, continuous, gas-retaining network**. Weak gluten produces a flat, dense loaf with poor crumb structure.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Three candidate protein sources are being evaluated for a protein-fortified fruit juice (target: 5 g protein per 200 mL serving). The candidates are:**
- **A:** Milk Protein Concentrate, cost ₹800/kg, 80% protein
- **B:** Pea Protein Isolate, cost ₹1100/kg, 82% protein
- **C:** Rice Protein Isolate, cost ₹1400/kg, 78% protein

**Determine the most cost-effective option using the "cost per gram of delivered protein" approach.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — State the target protein concentration:**
$$
Target = \frac{5\ g}{200\ mL} = 2.5\%\ w/v
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate cost per gram of protein for each candidate:**

$$
C_{functional} = \frac{Cost_{per\_kg}}{Protein\% \times 10}
$$

**For A (MPC):**
$$
C_A = \frac{800}{80 \times 10} = \frac{800}{800} = ₹1.00\ \text{per g protein}
$$

**For B (Pea):**
$$
C_B = \frac{1100}{82 \times 10} = \frac{1100}{820} = ₹1.34\ \text{per g protein}
$$

**For C (Rice):**
$$
C_C = \frac{1400}{78 \times 10} = \frac{1400}{780} = ₹1.79\ \text{per g protein}
$$

**Step 3 — Rank the candidates:**
$$
A\ (₹1.00) < B\ (₹1.34) < C\ (₹1.79)
$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**
**Milk Protein Concentrate (A)** is the most cost-effective option at **₹1.00 per gram of protein delivered**, offering a **44% cost advantage** over Rice Protein and **25% cost advantage** over Pea Protein.

**However**, the formulator must also evaluate:
- **Allergen status:** MPC contains milk (must be labeled); Pea and Rice are hypoallergenic.
- **Sensory impact:** MPC may impart dairy notes; Pea can cause beany off-flavor; Rice is the most neutral.
- **Consumer market:** If the juice targets lactose-intolerant consumers, **Pea or Rice Protein** is preferable despite the cost premium.

*(Allocation: 2 marks formula, 3 marks calculation, 1 mark decision, 1 mark trade-off discussion.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the role of hydrocolloids in food product design. Compare the functional properties of three common hydrocolloids: xanthan gum, pectin, and carrageenan, with reference to their specific applications.**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Hydrocolloids (3 marks):**
Hydrocolloids are **long-chain, high-molecular-weight hydrophilic polymers** that disperse or dissolve in water to produce **thickening, gelling, stabilizing, or emulsifying** effects. They function by:
- Increasing viscosity of the continuous phase.
- Forming three-dimensional gel networks.
- Stabilizing emulsions and suspensions.
- Controlling ice crystal growth and syneresis.

**Comparative Analysis (4 marks):**

| Property | Xanthan Gum | Pectin | Carrageenan |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Bacterial (Xanthomonas) | Plant cell wall (citrus/apple) | Seaweed (Rhodophyta) |
| **Charge** | Anionic | Anionic | Anionic (sulfated) |
| **pH tolerance** | Broad (1–13) | Acidic (3–4 for gel) | 4–10 |
| **Mechanism** | Cold-water soluble, shear-thinning | Egg-box model with Ca²⁺/sugar | Helix aggregation with K⁺/Ca²⁺ |
| **Application** | Salad dressings, gluten-free | Jams, jellies, fruit fillings | Dairy desserts, chocolate milk |
| **Specific Use Level** | 0.1–0.5% | 0.5–1.5% | 0.05–0.3% |

**Xanthan gum** is preferred in acidic pourable systems due to its shear-thinning behavior and broad pH tolerance. **Pectin** is the gold standard for fruit preserves (HM-pectin with sugar-acid system). **Carrageenan** is unique in dairy systems because its sulfate groups interact with casein (kappa-carrageenan) to prevent whey separation.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A fruit-based beverage must have a shelf life of 6 months at ambient temperature. Discuss the ingredient selection strategy for: (i) acidulant, (ii) preservative, and (iii) antioxidant, with justifications.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Acidulant Selection (2 marks):**
- **Choice: Citric acid** (most common in fruit beverages).
- **Justification:** Provides sharp, clean acidity, chelates pro-oxidant metals (Fe³⁺, Cu²⁺), and lowers pH to **3.0–3.5**, inhibiting most bacterial growth. Cost-effective and FSSAI-approved.
- **Alternative:** Malic acid (for sharper, apple-like flavor) or ascorbic acid (dual role as acidulant + antioxidant).

**(ii) Preservative Selection (2.5 marks):**
- **Choice: Sodium benzoate + potassium sorbate combination** at 0.05–0.1% each.
- **Justification:** Benzoate is most effective at pH < 4.0 (where 50%+ is in undissociated form, which is membrane-permeable to microbes). Sorbate has a broader spectrum effective against yeasts and molds. Combination provides **synergistic antimicrobial action**.
- **Calculation of undissociated fraction (Henderson-Hasselbalch):**
$$
pH = pK_a + \log\left(\frac{[A^-]}{[HA]}\right)
$$
At pH 3.5 with benzoic acid $pK_a = 4.2$:
$$
\frac{[A^-]}{[HA]} = 10^{(3.5 - 4.2)} = 10^{-0.7} \approx 0.20
$$
$$
\%\ undissociated = \frac{1}{1 + 0.20} \times 100 \approx 83.3\%
$$
This high undissociated fraction ensures **effective microbial inhibition**.

**(iii) Antioxidant Selection (2.5 marks):**
- **Choice: Ascorbic acid (Vitamin C) at 0.05–0.1%.**
- **Justification:** Ascorbic acid scavenges oxygen in the headspace, chelates metal catalysts, and regenerates other antioxidants. It is GRAS, water-soluble, and consumer-friendly (clean label).
- **Alternative:** Mixed tocopherols for lipid-rich beverages.

**Integrated Shelf-Life Logic:**
The combination of low pH (acidulant) + chemical preservatives + antioxidant creates a **hurdle-technology effect** that synergistically extends shelf life well beyond 6 months even at ambient temperature.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- ✅ **Functional properties ≠ Nutritional properties.** Functional = role in structure/sensory; Nutritional = role in health.
- ✅ The **7 Pillars of Selection**: Functional, Nutritional, Sensory, Economic, Regulatory, Shelf-life, Consumer.
- ✅ **Cost per functional unit** = $C_{ingredient} / Functionality$ — always calculate this before bulk procurement.
- ✅ **Water Activity ($a_w$) thresholds**: Bacteria < 0.86, Yeasts < 0.70, Molds < 0.65. Choose ingredients that drive $a_w$ below these limits.
- ✅ **Gluten** in bread requires **≥ 11.5% protein**; **casein** in cheese requires **~3.0%** for proper curd formation.
- ✅ **Emulsifier selection** follows the **HLB system**: O/W emulsions need HLB 8–18, W/O emulsions need HLB 3–6.
- ✅ **Pectin** needs **°Brix ≥ 65, pH 3.0–3.4** to gel; **Agar** gels without sugar.
- ✅ **FSSAI** (India) and **Codex Alimentarius** are the primary regulatory references; always check the **permitted use level** before recommending.
- ✅ **Clean Label trend** is increasingly important — prefer recognizable ingredient names over E-numbers.
- ✅ **Accelerated shelf-life testing (ASLT)** uses the **Arrhenius equation** to predict real-time stability from elevated temperature data.
- ✅ **PDCAAS ≥ 0.90** indicates a high-quality complete protein; **PDCAAS < 0.70** requires complementary protein blending.
- ✅ For **multi-criteria decisions**, always present a **decision matrix** with weighted scores; this is the KTU board's gold-standard answer format.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner's Caution:**
> In any "selection of materials" question, **never** justify an answer using only ONE criterion. KTU examiners expect a **minimum of 3–4 criteria** discussed with at least **one quantitative calculation**. A purely qualitative answer typically scores 60–70% of the maximum marks. Always anchor your answer with **at least one formula or numerical example** to fetch full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modifications for Production on a Large Scale, Cost-Effectiveness, Nutritional Needs or Uniqueness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT834 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Modifications for Production on a Large Scale, Cost-Effectiveness, Nutritional Needs or Uniqueness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (Course OEBBT834 - Food Product Design and Development, Module 2), **product modification** is defined as:

> The systematic and iterative process of altering the formulation, process parameters, ingredient matrix, packaging, and/or sensory profile of a prototype food product to ensure its successful translation from laboratory/pilot scale to commercial-scale production, while simultaneously optimizing cost, meeting target nutritional specifications, and establishing market-unique positioning.

The four mandated modification pillars as per the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

| Modification Pillar | Engineering Objective |
|---|---|
| **Large-Scale Production Adaptability** | Achieve feasible, reproducible, and food-safe production at industrial volumes |
| **Cost-Effectiveness** | Minimize the **Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)** without compromising quality |
| **Nutritional Needs Compliance** | Match the product to **RDA/ICMR** dietary recommendations and target consumer needs |
| **Product Uniqueness / Differentiation** | Establish a defensible market position through sensory, functional, or technological novelty |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** These four modifications are **not sequential** — they are **parallel and iterative**. A change for cost (e.g., replacing butter with palm oil) will simultaneously affect nutrition (fat profile), uniqueness (flavor), and scale-up behavior (melting point). The food scientist must perform a **Modification Trade-Off Matrix (MTOM)** at every formulation iteration.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have invented a delicious homemade mango-bar (*aam papad*) in your kitchen that everyone loves. You now want to sell it across Kerala through supermarkets. The core product idea is the same, but **everything else must change**:

- **Scale-up Analogy:** Your grandmother's hand-stirred 1-kg batch becomes a 500-kg mechanized batch — mixing speed, heating uniformity, drying time, and worker safety all change. This is *large-scale production modification*.

- **Cost Analogy:** Instead of using Alphonso mangoes (₹400/kg), you blend them with Totapuri mangoes (₹60/kg) to maintain a tropical flavor at one-fourth the cost. This is *cost-effectiveness modification*.

- **Nutrition Analogy:** You add Vitamin A (retinyl palmitate) and iron-folic acid because Indian children are deficient in these micronutrients. This is *nutritional needs modification*.

- **Uniqueness Analogy:** You incorporate a sprinkle of freeze-dried tender coconut bits to create a texture and flavor no competitor has — a *unique selling proposition (USP)*. This is *uniqueness modification*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** A food product is rarely "designed once." It undergoes **5 to 25 reformulation cycles** before commercial launch, with each cycle addressing one or more of these four modification pillars.

### 1.3 Key Industrial Metrics (KTU Board Favourites)

- **COGS** = **Cost of Goods Sold** (Target: < 60% of MRP for FMCG foods)
- **RDA** = **Recommended Dietary Allowance** (issued by **ICMR-NIN**, India)
- **Re** = **Reynolds Number** (dimensionless, for flow regime)
- **MVP** = **Minimum Viable Product** (Lean Startup Methodology adapted to food)
- **USP** = **Unique Selling Proposition**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pillar I — Modifications for Production on a Large Scale

When scaling up a recipe from bench-top to industrial scale, the food engineer must satisfy three classical similarities:

#### 2.1.1 The Three Similarities of Scale-Up

1. **Geometric Similarity** — All linear dimensions scale by a constant factor, $k$.
$$k = \frac{D_{\text{pilot}}}{D_{\text{lab}}} = \frac{L_{\text{pilot}}}{L_{\text{lab}}}$$
This ensures the *shape* of the equipment remains consistent.

2. **Dynamic Similarity** — The ratio of forces (inertial, viscous, gravitational) remains constant. Expressed via the **Reynolds Number**:
$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho \, N \, D^{2}}{\mu}$$
where $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m³), $N$ = impeller speed (rev/s), $D$ = impeller diameter (m), $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s).

3. **Kinematic Similarity** — The ratio of velocities at corresponding points is constant. Expressed via the **Power Number (Np)**:
$$N_p = \frac{P}{\rho \, N^{3} \, D^{5}}$$

#### 2.1.2 Scale-Up Power Law

The most heavily tested concept in KTU scale-up questions is the **constant-volume power requirement** scaling:
$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^{n}$$
where $n$ is an operation-specific exponent:

| Operation Type | Exponent $n$ | Logic |
|---|---|---|
| Constant tip speed | $n = 2$ | Blade tip velocity preserved |
| Constant power per unit volume | $n = 3$ | Same shear per unit mass |
| Equal blending time | $n = 2$ | Mixing time constant |
| Laminar flow (constant shear) | $n = 3$ | Shear rate preserved |

#### 2.1.3 Heat Transfer Scale-Up

For a jacketed heating vessel, the surface area scales with the square of linear dimension, while volume scales with the cube:
$$\frac{A_2}{A_1} = k^{2}, \qquad \frac{V_2}{V_1} = k^{3}$$
$$\frac{(A/V)_2}{(A/V)_1} = \frac{1}{k}$$
This is the **"surface-to-volume penalty"** — larger vessels heat and cool more slowly per unit volume, demanding higher temperature gradients or longer residence times.

### 2.2 Pillar II — Modifications for Cost-Effectiveness

#### 2.2.1 Cost Decomposition

Total cost per unit finished product:
$$C_{\text{unit}} = C_{\text{raw}} + C_{\text{labor}} + C_{\text{util}} + C_{\text{pkg}} + C_{\text{waste}} + C_{\text{overhead}}$$

#### 2.2.2 Cost Reduction Levers

- **Ingredient Substitution (IS):** Replace expensive functional ingredients with cheaper equivalents exhibiting similar techno-functional properties.
- **Process Intensification (PI):** Higher yields through better mixing, extraction, or recovery.
- **Waste Valorization (WV):** Convert by-products (e.g., mango peel) into secondary revenue streams.
- **Shelf-Life Extension (SLE):** Reduces losses in distribution — *food saved = food produced*.

The **Percentage Cost Reduction (PCR)** formula:
$$\text{PCR} = \frac{C_{\text{old}} - C_{\text{new}}}{C_{\text{old}}} \times 100\%$$

#### 2.2.3 Cost-Effectiveness Ratio (CER)

When comparing two formulations, the **Cost-Effectiveness Ratio (CER)** normalizes cost against a quality attribute:
$$\text{CER} = \frac{C_{\text{unit}}}{\text{Quality Score}}$$

The formulation with the **lower CER** is preferred, provided the quality score meets a minimum threshold.

### 2.3 Pillar III — Modifications for Nutritional Needs

#### 2.3.1 Modification Strategies

- **Fortification:** Mandatory (iodized salt, vitamin D in milk) or voluntary.
- **Enrichment:** Restoration of nutrients lost during processing (e.g., adding back B-vitamins in refined flour).
- **Reduction:** Lowering sugar, sodium, saturated fat, or trans fat.
- **Novel Ingredient Integration:** Plant proteins, prebiotics, probiotics, omega-3 fatty acids.

#### 2.3.2 Nutrient Density (ND)

$$\text{ND} = \frac{\text{Nutrient per serving (g or mg)}}{\text{Energy per serving (kcal)}}$$

A high nutrient density (e.g., ND > 1.0 for protein/energy) is preferred for products marketed as nutritious.

#### 2.3.3 Fortification Dose Calculation

$$M_{\text{fort}} = \frac{\text{RDA}_{\text{target}} \times \text{Serving size} \times N_{\text{servings}}}{P_{\text{nutrient}} \times R_{\text{recovery}}}$$

where $P_{\text{nutrient}}$ = potency of the fortificant premix, $R_{\text{recovery}}$ = processing retention factor (typically 0.80–0.95).

### 2.4 Pillar IV — Modifications for Uniqueness

#### 2.4.1 Differentiation Vectors

A food product can be made unique along five axes:

1. **Sensory uniqueness** — novel flavor, texture, aroma, or appearance.
2. **Functional uniqueness** — added health benefit (probiotic, antioxidant).
3. **Process uniqueness** — novel processing (high-pressure processing, 3D food printing).
4. **Convenience uniqueness** — ready-to-eat, single-serve, microwaveable.
5. **Sustainability uniqueness** — upcycled ingredients, plant-based, low-carbon.

#### 2.4.2 Patentability and IP Protection

In food product design, uniqueness can be protected through:
- **Patent** (process or composition of matter)
- **Trade secret** (e.g., Coca-Cola formula)
- **Trademark** (brand name, logo)
- **Geographical Indication (GI)** — e.g., Basmati rice, Darjeeling tea.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbols & Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $k = D_2/D_1$ | $k$ = scale factor, $D$ = diameter (m) | Geometric scale-up |
| 2 | $\text{Re} = \rho N D^{2}/\mu$ | $\rho$ (kg/m³), $N$ (rev/s), $D$ (m), $\mu$ (Pa·s) | Flow regime identification |
| 3 | $N_p = P/(\rho N^{3} D^{5})$ | $P$ (W) | Power consumption |
| 4 | $P_2/P_1 = (D_2/D_1)^{n}$ | $n$ = scale-up exponent | Power scaling |
| 5 | $A/V \propto 1/D$ | $A$ (m²), $V$ (m³) | Heat transfer at scale |
| 6 | $Q = U A \Delta T$ | $Q$ (W), $U$ (W/m²K), $\Delta T$ (K) | Heat duty calculation |
| 7 | $\text{PCR} = (C_{\text{old}} - C_{\text{new}})/C_{\text{old}} \times 100$ | $C$ (₹/unit) | Cost reduction % |
| 8 | $\text{CER} = C_{\text{unit}} / \text{Quality}$ | dimensionless | Cost-effectiveness |
| 9 | $\text{ND} = \text{Nutrient/Energy}$ | Nutrient (g or mg) / Energy (kcal) | Nutrient density |
| 10 | $M_{\text{fort}}$ formula (above) | Mass (g) per batch | Fortification dose |
| 11 | $\text{Yield} = (M_{\text{out}}/M_{\text{in}}) \times 100$ | $M$ = mass (kg) | Process efficiency |
| 12 | $\text{Shrink} = 1 - \text{Yield}/100$ | dimensionless | Mass loss fraction |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value and ratio expressions above use `\vert` or `/` rather than the pipe `|` character to prevent markdown table breakage.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

These modification principles are routinely applied in industry for products such as:
- **Amul's "Probiotic Dahi"** — uniqueness via live cultures; cost via bulk dairy sourcing; nutrition via calcium and protein.
- **Britannia's "NutriChoice"** — nutritional modification (lower GI, added fiber).
- **ITC's "Aashirvaad" multigrain atta** — uniqueness via 7-grain blend; cost via bulk cereal sourcing.
- **PepsiCo's "Lay's"** — process uniqueness via continuous frying; cost via in-house potato contract farming.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Scale-Up Power Consumption for a Mixing Tank

**Given:**
- Laboratory mixer: $D_1 = 0.20$ m, impeller speed $N_1 = 300$ rpm = $5.0$ rev/s, power $P_1 = 75$ W.
- Pilot mixer: $D_2 = 0.50$ m.
- Operation regime: equal blending time, hence $n = 2$ (constant tip speed regime).

**Find:** Power required at pilot scale, $P_2$.

**Step 1 — Apply the scale-up power law.**
$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^{n}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**
$$\frac{P_2}{75 \, \text{W}} = \left(\frac{0.50}{0.20}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio.**
$$\left(\frac{0.50}{0.20}\right)^{2} = (2.5)^{2} = 6.25$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $P_2$.**
$$P_2 = 75 \times 6.25 = 468.75 \, \text{W}$$

**Result:** The pilot-scale mixer requires approximately **468.75 W** of power to deliver equivalent blending time.

**Step 5 — Engineering sanity check (constant tip speed).**
Tip speed at lab scale: $v_{\text{tip},1} = \pi D_1 N_1 = \pi \times 0.20 \times 5.0 = 3.14$ m/s.
To maintain this, pilot speed:
$$N_2 = \frac{v_{\text{tip},1}}{\pi D_2} = \frac{3.14}{\pi \times 0.50} = 2.0 \, \text{rev/s} = 120 \, \text{rpm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The pilot impeller spins *slower* (120 rpm) but on a *larger* blade. This illustrates the fundamental scale-up insight — larger equipment does not need to spin faster; it has a longer lever arm.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Cost-Effectiveness Comparison of Two Formulations

**Given:** Two yogurt formulations are compared:

| Parameter | Formulation A (Butter Fat) | Formulation B (Palm Oil) |
|---|---|---|
| Cost per kg finished product | ₹220 | ₹155 |
| Sensory score (out of 10) | 9.2 | 7.5 |
| Fat content (g/100g) | 4.0 | 3.0 |
| Shelf life (days) | 7 | 14 |

**Find:** Which formulation is more cost-effective, considering sensory quality?

**Step 1 — Compute Cost-Effectiveness Ratio (CER) for each.**
$$\text{CER}_A = \frac{220}{9.2} = 23.91 \, \text{₹/quality point}$$
$$\text{CER}_B = \frac{155}{7.5} = 20.67 \, \text{₹/quality point}$$

**Step 2 — Compare.**
Since $\text{CER}_B < \text{CER}_A$, Formulation B is more cost-effective per unit of quality.

**Step 3 — Cross-validate against nutritional & shelf-life impact.**
- Fat reduction: $4.0 \to 3.0$ g/100g — meets **FSSAI low-fat claim** (≤3 g/100g).
- Shelf-life gain: $7 \to 14$ days — reduces waste by ~50% if distribution is 14 days.
- Annual distribution loss saving (assume 10,000 kg/yr, 5% loss at 7 days vs 2.5% at 14 days): 250 kg saved ≈ ₹38,750/year at Formulation B cost.

**Step 4 — Final decision.**
**Formulation B is recommended** — superior CER, nutrition improvement, and waste reduction outweigh the modest sensory penalty.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Fortification Dose Calculation

**Problem:** A breakfast cereal manufacturer wants to fortify their product with **iron** to provide 30% of the Indian RDA per 30 g serving.

**Given:**
- Indian RDA for iron (adult male): $21$ mg/day
- Serving size: $30$ g
- Target contribution: $30\%$ of RDA $= 0.30 \times 21 = 6.3$ mg Fe per serving
- Fortificant: **ferrous fumarate**, $P = 32.9\%$ elemental iron (by mass)
- Processing recovery: $R = 0.90$ (10% loss in extrusion)
- Batch size: $1000$ kg cereal

**Step 1 — Apply the fortification formula.**
$$M_{\text{fort}} = \frac{\text{RDA}_{\text{target}} \times N_{\text{servings}}}{P \times R}$$

**Step 2 — Compute number of servings in 1000 kg batch.**
$$N_{\text{servings}} = \frac{1000 \times 1000 \, \text{g}}{30 \, \text{g}} = 33{,}333 \, \text{servings}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**
$$M_{\text{fort}} = \frac{6.3 \, \text{mg} \times 33{,}333}{0.329 \times 0.90}$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerator and denominator separately.**
$$\text{Numerator} = 6.3 \times 33{,}333 = 209{,}998 \, \text{mg}$$
$$\text{Denominator} = 0.329 \times 0.90 = 0.2961$$

**Step 5 — Final calculation.**
$$M_{\text{fort}} = \frac{209{,}998}{0.2961} = 709{,}213 \, \text{mg} \approx 709.2 \, \text{g}$$

**Result:** Add **~710 g of ferrous fumarate** to a 1000-kg batch to deliver 30% RDA of iron per 30 g serving.

**Step 6 — Verify by reverse calculation.**
Iron added per serving:
$$\frac{709.2 \times 0.329 \times 0.90}{33{,}333} = \frac{210.0}{33{,}333} = 0.0063 \, \text{g} = 6.3 \, \text{mg} \, \checkmark$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Surface-to-Volume Heat Transfer Penalty at Scale

**Problem:** A 5-L lab saucepan ($D_1 = 0.18$ m, $H_1 = 0.20$ m) is scaled up to a 500-L industrial steam-jacketed kettle ($D_2 = 0.90$ m, $H_2 = 0.90$ m, geometrically similar, $k = 5$).

**Step 1 — Compute lab A/V ratio.**
$$A_1 = \pi D_1^{2}/2 + \pi D_1 H_1 = \pi \times 0.18^{2}/2 + \pi \times 0.18 \times 0.20$$
$$A_1 = 0.0509 + 0.1131 = 0.1640 \, \text{m}^{2}$$
$$V_1 = 5 \, \text{L} = 0.005 \, \text{m}^{3}$$
$$(A/V)_1 = 0.1640 / 0.005 = 32.80 \, \text{m}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the scale-up law $A/V \propto 1/D$.**
$$(A/V)_2 = (A/V)_1 \times \frac{D_1}{D_2} = 32.80 \times \frac{0.18}{0.90} = 6.56 \, \text{m}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the penalty.**
The industrial kettle has **5× less heating surface per unit volume**. To achieve the same heating time, the temperature difference $\Delta T$ or the overall heat transfer coefficient $U$ must be **5× higher**.

> [!WARNING]
> This is the most common *scale-up pitfall* — laboratory recipes that work at 5 L often fail at 500 L because the cook could not realize how dramatically $A/V$ falls. The food scientist must either (a) extend heating time, (b) raise steam temperature, (c) use more efficient agitation to enhance $U$, or (d) accept a different thermal process outcome.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Modification Trade-Off Examples

| Modification Decision | Effect on Scale-Up | Effect on Cost | Effect on Nutrition | Effect on Uniqueness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Replace butter with palm oil | No issue | ↓ ~30% | ↑ saturated fat | ↓ sensory richness |
| Add soy protein isolate | ↑ hydration time | ↓ ~15% | ↑ protein, ↑ fiber | ↓ smooth texture |
| Use HPP instead of pasteurization | ↑ CapEx, ↓ batch time | ↑ energy | ↑ vitamin retention | ↑ fresh-like USP |
| Add prebiotic fiber (inulin) | ↑ viscosity | ↑ 8% | ↑ ND fiber | ↑ gut-health claim |
| Switch to aseptic packaging | ↓ distribution cost | ↓ 12% | ↑ vitamin stability | ↑ shelf-life USP |
| Replace sugar with stevia | ↓ bulk density | ↓ ~40% | ↓ calories | ↑ diabetic-friendly USP |
| Use 3D-printed shapes | Slow throughput | ↑ CapEx | Neutral | ↑ novelty USP |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Food Product Modification Framework

The following Mermaid diagram maps the iterative interaction between the four modification pillars, decision gates, and feedback loops in commercial product development.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode[Prototype Lab Recipe] --> gate1{Quality Meets Target?}

    gate1 -- No --> reform[Reformulate]
    reform --> startNode
    gate1 -- Yes --> pillar1[Pillar I: Scale Up Modifications]

    pillar1 --> p1k1[Geometric Dimensional Analysis]
    pillar1 --> p1k2[Reynolds Power Number Recalculation]
    pillar1 --> p1k3[Heat Transfer A/V Penalty Adjustment]

    p1k3 --> pillar2[Pillar II: Cost Effectiveness]
    p1k2 --> pillar2
    p1k1 --> pillar2

    pillar2 --> p2k1[Ingredient Substitution Matrix]
    pillar2 --> p2k2[Process Yield Optimization]
    pillar2 --> p2k3[Waste Valorization Streams]

    p2k3 --> pillar3[Pillar III: Nutritional Modification]
    p2k2 --> pillar3
    p2k1 --> pillar3

    pillar3 --> p3k1[Fortification Dose Calculation]
    pillar3 --> p3k2[Reduction of Sugar Salt Fat]
    pillar3 --> p3k3[Functional Ingredient Integration]

    p3k3 --> pillar4[Pillar IV: Uniqueness]
    p3k2 --> pillar4
    p3k1 --> pillar4

    pillar4 --> p4k1[Sensory Differentiation]
    pillar4 --> p4k2[Novel Processing Technology]
    pillar4 --> p4k3[IP and Patent Protection]

    p4k3 --> tradeGate{Modification Trade Off MTOM}
    p4k2 --> tradeGate
    p4k1 --> tradeGate

    tradeGate -- Conflict Detected --> mtm[MTOM Iteration Cycle]
    mtm --> pillar1
    mtm --> pillar2
    mtm --> pillar3
    mtm --> pillar4

    tradeGate -- All Pillars Aligned --> pilotNode[Pilot Plant Trial 50 to 500 kg]
    pilotNode --> sensoryQ[Consumer Sensory Panel n=100]
    pilotNode --> stabilityQ[Accelerated Shelf Life 45C 75 percent RH]
    pilotNode --> regulatoryQ[FSSAI Label Compliance Check]

    sensoryQ --> launch[Commercial Launch MRP Set Distribution]
    stabilityQ --> launch
    regulatoryQ --> launch
```

### 4.2 Scale-Up Modification Sub-Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    labScale[Lab Scale 1 to 5 L] --> geochk{Geometric Similarity k Value}
    geochk --> dynchk{Dynamic Similarity Re Match}
    dynchk --> kinchk{Kinematic Similarity Np Match}

    kinchk --> powerCalc[Power P2 equals P1 times k to power n]
    kinchk --> heatCalc[Heat Transfer UA delta T]
    kinchk --> timeCalc[Residence Time Correction]

    powerCalc --> pilotScale[Pilot Scale 50 to 500 L]
    heatCalc --> pilotScale
    timeCalc --> pilotScale

    pilotScale --> validate{Replicate Lab Quality?}
    validate -- No --> geochk
    validate -- Yes --> plantScale[Plant Scale 5000 L and above]
```

### 4.3 Cost Modification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    cstart[Current COGS Baseline] --> c1{Raw Material > 50 percent?}

    c1 -- Yes --> c1a[Ingredient Substitution Matrix]
    c1a --> c1b[Local Sourcing]
    c1b --> c1c[Long Term Contracts]

    c1 -- No --> c2{Process Yield < 90 percent?}
    c2 -- Yes --> c2a[Process Optimization]
    c2a --> c2b[Equipment Upgrade]
    c2b --> c2c[Operator Training]

    c2 -- No --> c3{Shelf Life Losses > 3 percent?}
    c3 -- Yes --> c3a[Packaging Upgrade]
    c3a --> c3b[Distribution Cold Chain]
    c3b --> c3c[Preservative Optimization]

    c3 -- No --> c4[Cost Modification Done]
    c1c --> c4
    c2c --> c4
    c3c --> c4
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**

> Define **product modification** in food product design. List the **four pillars of modification** as per the KTU 2024 syllabus and briefly explain the role of any two in commercial product development. **(3 marks)**

**Model Answer (1 + 2 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Product modification in food product design is the systematic alteration of formulation, processing parameters, ingredient composition, or sensory attributes of a prototype food product to ensure its successful translation from laboratory scale to commercial scale, while optimizing cost, meeting nutritional needs, and establishing market differentiation.

**Four pillars (1 mark):**
1. Modifications for production on a large scale
2. Modifications for cost-effectiveness
3. Modifications for nutritional needs
4. Modifications for uniqueness

**Role of any two (1 mark):**
- **Scale-up modifications** ensure that a lab recipe (1 L) can be reliably produced in a 5000 L kettle, with consistent heat transfer, mixing, and process time. The Reynolds number, Power number, and surface-to-volume ratio are recalculated.
- **Nutritional modifications** ensure the product delivers the targeted health benefits — for example, fortifying a breakfast cereal with iron and folate, or reducing sodium in a snack by 25% to meet FSSAI "low sodium" thresholds.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, Sample Paper]**

> Differentiate between **fortification** and **enrichment** in food product modification. Provide one example of each. **(3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Fortification | Enrichment |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | The deliberate addition of nutrients at levels above the natural occurrence to prevent or correct a deficiency in the population. | The restoration of nutrients lost during processing, back to their original (pre-processing) levels. |
| **Target population** | General population or vulnerable group. | Consumers of the specific processed product. |
| **Example** | Iodized salt (iodine added to prevent goiter); Vitamin A-fortified milk. | Adding back B-vitamins (thiamine, niacin, folic acid) to refined wheat flour to match the levels in whole wheat. |

(3 marks — 1.5 marks for differentiation, 1.5 marks for examples)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

> **(a)** Discuss in detail the **scale-up modifications** required when translating a food product from laboratory (5 L) to industrial (5000 L) scale. Explain the concepts of **geometric similarity, dynamic similarity**, and the **scale-up power law** with suitable equations. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A laboratory mixer of diameter **0.15 m** rotating at **600 rpm** has a power draw of **45 W**. If the mixer is scaled up to **0.45 m** diameter with a scale-up exponent $n = 2.5$ (constant power per unit volume regime), calculate the power draw and the new impeller speed in the scaled unit. Assume a fluid of density 1000 kg/m³ and viscosity 1 mPa·s. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

*Geometric Similarity (1.5 marks):* When scaling from lab to plant, the linear dimensions (diameter, height, impeller diameter) must scale by a constant factor $k$. If $D_1$ and $D_2$ are the lab and plant impeller diameters, then $k = D_2/D_1$. All corresponding lengths scale by $k$, areas by $k^2$, and volumes by $k^3$.

*Dynamic Similarity (1.5 marks):* The ratio of forces (inertial vs. viscous) must be preserved. This is enforced by matching the dimensionless **Reynolds Number**:
$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho N D^{2}}{\mu}$$
A pilot-scale impeller operating in the same Re regime as the lab-scale ensures the same flow pattern (turbulent, transitional, or laminar).

*Kinematic Similarity & Power Law (3 marks):* The **Power Number** $N_p = P/(\rho N^{3} D^{5})$ is held constant, and the **scale-up power law** is applied:
$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^{n}$$
where $n$ is operation-specific (n = 2 for constant tip speed, n = 3 for constant shear in laminar flow). Other critical scale-up issues include:
- **Heat transfer surface-to-volume penalty:** $A/V \propto 1/D$, so heating/cooling per unit volume is much slower at plant scale.
- **Residence time distribution:** Larger vessels have broader RTD, which may require longer processing for microbial safety.
- **Reynolds number matching:** Often requires *slower* impeller speed at large scale.

*Conclusion (1 mark):* Successful scale-up requires matching dimensionless groups (Re, Np, Pr, Nu) rather than copying lab conditions directly.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $D_1 = 0.15$ m, $N_1 = 600$ rpm $= 10$ rev/s, $P_1 = 45$ W, $D_2 = 0.45$ m, $n = 2.5$.

**Step 1 — Compute scale factor.**
$$k = \frac{D_2}{D_1} = \frac{0.45}{0.15} = 3.0$$

**Step 2 — Compute $P_2$ using the power law.**
$$P_2 = P_1 \times k^{n} = 45 \times (3.0)^{2.5}$$

Compute $(3.0)^{2.5} = 3^{2} \times 3^{0.5} = 9 \times 1.732 = 15.588$

$$P_2 = 45 \times 15.588 = 701.5 \, \text{W} \approx 0.70 \, \text{kW}$$

**Step 3 — Find the new impeller speed $N_2$.**

The condition $n = 2.5$ represents a regime *between* constant tip speed (n=2) and constant power per unit volume (n=3). We can derive $N_2$ from the Power Number constancy:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho N_1^{3} D_1^{5}} = \frac{P_2}{\rho N_2^{3} D_2^{5}}$$

$$\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/3} \times \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^{5/3}$$

Substituting:
$$\frac{N_2}{N_1} = (15.588)^{1/3} \times (1/3)^{5/3}$$

$(15.588)^{1/3} \approx 2.499$
$(1/3)^{5/3} = 3^{-5/3} \approx 0.1604$

$$\frac{N_2}{N_1} = 2.499 \times 0.1604 = 0.4008$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $N_2$.**
$$N_2 = 0.4008 \times 600 = 240.5 \, \text{rpm} \approx 240 \, \text{rpm}$$

**Step 5 — Verify Reynolds numbers match.**
$$\text{Re}_1 = \frac{1000 \times 10 \times 0.15^{2}}{0.001} = 2.25 \times 10^{5}$$
$$\text{Re}_2 = \frac{1000 \times 4.0 \times 0.45^{2}}{0.001} = 8.10 \times 10^{5}$$

Both are highly turbulent (Re > $10^{4}$), so dynamic similarity is satisfied.

**Final Answer:** $P_2 \approx 701.5$ W; $N_2 \approx 240$ rpm.

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a)** Describe the **cost-effectiveness modification strategies** in food product design. Explain with examples how ingredient substitution, process intensification, and waste valorization contribute to a lower **Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A company manufactures a fruit jam. The original recipe uses 1.2 kg sugar per kg fruit at a cost of ₹40 per kg. The **fruit cost is ₹80/kg**, and the batch yield is **85%**. The company reformulates by replacing 20% of sugar with a high-intensity sweetener (cost ₹2,000/kg, equivalent in sweetness to 200× sugar). Calculate:
> - (i) The cost per kg of finished jam — before and after reformulation. (Assume fruit input = 100 kg per batch, other costs negligible for comparison.) **(4 marks)**
> - (ii) The percentage cost reduction. **(1 mark)**
> - (iii) The change in calorie content per 100 g jam (sugar provides 4 kcal/g, sweetener negligible). **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

*Cost Decomposition (1.5 marks):*
The total cost of a food product is decomposed into:
$$C_{\text{unit}} = C_{\text{raw}} + C_{\text{labor}} + C_{\text{util}} + C_{\text{pkg}} + C_{\text{waste}} + C_{\text{overhead}}$$
In FMCG foods, raw material cost typically constitutes 50–70% of the COGS, making ingredient and process decisions the most cost-influential.

*Strategy 1 — Ingredient Substitution (2 marks):*
Replace expensive ingredients with cheaper alternatives exhibiting similar techno-functional properties.
- *Example:* Replacing cocoa butter with cocoa butter equivalents (palm mid-fraction, shea stearin) at 30% lower cost while preserving melting behavior.
- *Example:* Replacing dairy cream with coconut cream in certain tropical dessert applications.
- *Trade-off:* Must verify regulatory (FSSAI), sensory, and nutritional impact.

*Strategy 2 — Process Intensification (2 marks):*
Improve process yields and throughput.
- *Example:* Using a continuous plate heat exchanger for pasteurization instead of a batch kettle — 5× higher throughput, lower labor cost.
- *Example:* Centrifugal extraction of fruit juice achieving 92% yield vs. 78% in hydraulic pressing.
- *Example:* Membrane filtration (UF/NF) for protein concentration vs. evaporation — less energy, no thermal damage.

*Strategy 3 — Waste Valorization (1.5 marks):*
Convert by-products into secondary revenue streams.
- *Example:* Mango peel pectin extraction (a ₹600/kg product) from peel waste.
- *Example:* Whey protein concentrate from cheese whey.
- *Example:* Rice bran oil from rice bran (a milling by-product).

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Original formulation costs.**

Fruit: $100$ kg × ₹80/kg = ₹8,000
Sugar: $100 \times 1.2 = 120$ kg × ₹40/kg = ₹4,800
Total raw material cost = ₹12,800

Finished jam yield = 85% of total input mass
$$M_{\text{jam,original}} = (100 + 120) \times 0.85 = 187 \, \text{kg}$$

Cost per kg of jam:
$$C_{\text{old}} = \frac{12{,}800}{187} = ₹68.45/\text{kg}$$

**Step 2 — Reformulated costs.**

Replace 20% of sugar with high-intensity sweetener.
- Sugar retained: $120 \times 0.80 = 96$ kg → cost $96 \times 40 = ₹3{,}840$
- Sugar replaced: $120 \times 0.20 = 24$ kg equivalent → sweetener mass needed:
$$M_{\text{sweet}} = \frac{24}{200} = 0.12 \, \text{kg}$$
- Sweetener cost: $0.12 \times 2000 = ₹240$

Total raw material cost in reformulation:
$$C_{\text{raw,new}} = 8{,}000 + 3{,}840 + 240 = ₹12{,}080$$

Assuming the same 85% yield and approximately same total input mass (sweetener mass is negligible):
$$M_{\text{jam,new}} \approx 187 \, \text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Cost per kg of new jam.**
$$C_{\text{new}} = \frac{12{,}080}{187} = ₹64.60/\text{kg}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage cost reduction.**
$$\text{PCR} = \frac{68.45 - 64.60}{68.45} \times 100\% = \frac{3.85}{68.45} \times 100\% = 5.62\%$$

**Step 5 — Calorie change per 100 g jam.**

*Original:* Sugar in 100 g jam = $(120/187) \times 100 = 64.17$ g → calories from sugar $= 64.17 \times 4 = 256.7$ kcal.

*New:* Sugar in 100 g jam = $(96/187) \times 100 = 51.34$ g → calories from sugar $= 51.34 \times 4 = 205.4$ kcal. Sweetener contributes ≈ 0 kcal.

**Calorie reduction = 256.7 − 205.4 = 51.3 kcal per 100 g** (a 20% reduction).

**Final Answer:**
- (i) Original cost: ₹68.45/kg; New cost: ₹64.60/kg.
- (ii) PCR ≈ 5.6%
- (iii) Calorie reduction: 51.3 kcal per 100 g (≈ 20% fewer calories).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing scale-up exponent $n$ values.** A frequent error is using $n = 2$ (constant tip speed) when the question states "constant power per unit volume" (which requires $n = 3$). Always match the regime to the exponent.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert rpm to rev/s** in the Reynolds number calculation. The Reynolds number requires SI units (m, s, kg/m³, Pa·s) — using rpm directly gives wrong answers.
> 3. **Not justifying the choice of fortificant.** The KTU 2024 evaluator awards marks for explaining *why* ferrous fumarate (32.9% Fe) is preferred over ferrous sulfate (20% Fe) — discuss cost, bioavailability, and organoleptic impact.
> 4. **Confusing "fortification" with "enrichment."** Fortification adds nutrients *above* natural levels; enrichment restores nutrients *lost* in processing. This is a 3-mark trap in Part A.
> 5. **Ignoring yield losses in cost calculations.** A reformulation that lowers raw material cost but reduces process yield from 90% to 80% may *increase* the effective per-unit cost.
> 6. **Writing cost answers without units (₹/kg).** Always state units explicitly — the KTU 2024 scheme deducts 0.5 marks per missing unit in numerical problems.
> 7. **Forgetting the Iteration Loop.** When asked about "modification," students often list all four pillars sequentially. The correct KTU 2024 answer must emphasize that they are **iterative and inter-dependent** — a real industrial answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Core concept:** Modification is *iterative* and *multi-dimensional* — the four pillars (scale, cost, nutrition, uniqueness) interact continuously.
- **Scale-up fundamentals:** Re, Np, geometric similarity, and the power law $P_2/P_1 = (D_2/D_1)^n$ are essential. The surface-to-volume penalty ($A/V \propto 1/D$) is the most common cause of pilot-plant failures.
- **Scale-up exponent $n$:** Know the four common values — 2 (constant tip speed), 3 (constant power per unit volume), 2 (equal blending time), 3 (laminar shear).
- **Cost levers:** Ingredient substitution, process intensification, waste valorization, shelf-life extension. The COGS target for FMCG foods is typically < 60% of MRP.
- **Cost-Effectiveness Ratio (CER):** Lower CER is better, provided quality thresholds are met.
- **Nutritional modifications:** Fortification (adds above natural), Enrichment (restores lost), Reduction (low sugar/salt/fat). Use ICMR-RDA values and compute fortification dose using the formula $M_{\text{fort}} = (\text{RDA} \times N_{\text{servings}})/(P \times R)$.
- **Nutrient Density (ND):** Nutrient per kcal — higher is better for nutrient-dense claims.
- **Uniqueness axes:** Sensory, functional, process, convenience, sustainability. Protect IP via patents, trademarks, GIs, and trade secrets.
- **Quantitative anchors to memorize:**
  - Lab to pilot scale factor: 10× to 100×
  - Pilot to plant scale factor: 10× to 100×
  - Industrial frying temperature: 160–180 °C
  - Pasteurization: 72 °C × 15 s (HTST) or 63 °C × 30 min (LTLT)
  - UHT: 135–150 °C × 2–5 s
  - Water activity for shelf-stable foods: $a_w < 0.60$
- **FSSAI food categories** (basic awareness): CODEX/FSSAI governs fortification levels and labeling claims in India.
- **The "Modification Trade-Off Matrix (MTOM)"** is the KTU 2024 signature concept — any modification in one pillar must be re-evaluated against the other three.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked "discuss modifications," always open with a 2-line definition, then classify the four pillars, then close with a note on the **iterative nature** of modification and the **MTOM** — this structure alone fetches 1–2 bonus marks on KTU 2024 ESE papers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of Novel Food Ingredients and Novel Processing Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT834 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Use of Novel Food Ingredients and Novel Processing Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Novel Food Ingredients — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Novel Food Ingredients** are non-traditional ingredients — typically derived from plants, microbes, marine sources, or by advanced extraction/synthesis — that are intentionally incorporated into food matrices to deliver **enhanced nutritional, functional, sensory, or shelf-life benefits** beyond the basic function of conventional ingredients.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, novel ingredients are categorized under three functional pillars:

1. **Nutraceuticals / Functional Foods** — ingredients that provide health benefits beyond basic nutrition (e.g., omega-3 oils, phytosterols, curcumin, lycopene).
2. **Bioactive / Bio-Engineered Ingredients** — probiotics, prebiotics, synbiotics, enzymes, and peptides produced via fermentation or bioprocessing.
3. **Alternative Structural Ingredients** — plant-based proteins, edible coatings, novel sweeteners, and natural antioxidants that replace traditional additives.

### 1.2 Novel Processing Technologies — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Novel Food Processing Technologies** are non-thermal or hybrid thermal-non-thermal preservation and structuring techniques that operate on principles such as high pressure, electric fields, electromagnetic waves, sound waves, or plasma, to achieve microbial safety, nutrient retention, and minimal quality degradation compared to conventional thermal processing.

The KTU 2024 syllabus identifies the following core novel technologies:

- High Pressure Processing (HPP)
- Pulsed Electric Field (PEF)
- Ultrasound / Sonication
- Microwave and Radiofrequency Heating
- Ohmic Heating
- Cold Plasma
- Supercritical Fluid Extraction (SCFE)
- Membrane Processing (NF/RO)
- 3D Food Printing (Additive Manufacturing)
- Freeze Drying / Lyophilization
- Micro/Nano-Encapsulation

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — Novel Ingredients as "Upgrades" in a Recipe:**
> Think of designing a smartphone. Conventional ingredients (sugar, salt, flour) are like the basic hardware — they work but are limited. Novel ingredients (probiotics, plant proteins, stevia) are like the **software upgrades** that make the device smarter, healthier, and longer-lasting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy 2 — Novel Processing as "Cooking Without Fire":**
> Traditional thermal processing is like boiling vegetables in water — it kills microbes but also destroys Vitamin C. Novel technologies (HPP, PEF, ultrasound) are like **precision surgical tools** — they target pathogens and inactivate enzymes while leaving delicate nutrients, color, and flavor intact.

### 1.4 Standard Regulatory & Physical Metrics

- **High Pressure magnitude:** typically **400–600 MPa** (commercial range).
- **Electric field strength (PEF):** **20–80 kV/cm**.
- **Ultrasound frequency:** **20–100 kHz** (power ultrasound); **>1 MHz** (diagnostic).
- **Plasma temperature (cold plasma):** electrons at **10,000–100,000 K**, bulk gas near **room temperature**.
- **Supercritical CO₂:** critical point **31.1 °C / 7.38 MPa**.

### 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in Food Product Design

In the modern food industry, the consumer demand has shifted from "filling the stomach" to "feeding the body" — clean-label, minimally processed, nutrient-dense, sustainable, and functional foods. Novel ingredients and technologies together form the **innovation backbone** of any new product development (NPD) pipeline.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Novel Food Ingredients

#### A. Functional Ingredients & Nutraceuticals
- **Omega-3 fatty acids** (EPA, DHA) from fish oil, algal oil, flaxseed.
- **Phytosterols / Stanols** — cholesterol-lowering agents.
- **Polyphenols** — resveratrol, catechins, anthocyanins, curcumin.
- **Carotenoids** — beta-carotene, lycopene, lutein.
- **Dietary fibers** — inulin, beta-glucan, resistant starch.

#### B. Bioactive Microbial Ingredients
- **Probiotics** — live microorganisms (e.g., *Lactobacillus*, *Bifidobacterium*) conferring health benefits at **≥10⁶ CFU/g** dose.
- **Prebiotics** — non-digestible substrates (FOS, GOS, inulin) selectively stimulating gut microbiota.
- **Synbiotics** — synergistic combinations of pro- and prebiotics.
- **Postbiotics** — inactivated microbial cells or metabolites (paraprobiotics).

#### C. Alternative Proteins
- **Plant-based:** soy, pea, chickpea, lentil, rice proteins.
- **Insect proteins:** cricket flour, mealworm powder.
- **Single-cell proteins (SCP):** yeast, bacteria, microalgae (*Chlorella*, *Spirulina*).
- **Cultured meat / cellular agriculture.**

#### D. Novel Sweeteners & Fat Replacers
- **High-intensity sweeteners:** steviol glycosides, monk fruit extract, allulose, sucralose.
- **Sugar alcohols:** erythritol, xylitol.
- **Fat replacers:** microparticulated proteins, inulin-based fat mimetics, oleogels.

#### E. Edible Films & Coatings
- Polysaccharide-based (chitosan, alginate, starch), protein-based (whey, gelatin), lipid-based (waxes), and composite.

### 2.2 Classification of Novel Processing Technologies

#### 2.2.1 Non-Thermal Technologies (temperature rise < 5 °C during peak treatment)
- **High Pressure Processing (HPP)** — batch or semi-continuous; **400–600 MPa**, 5–20 min.
- **Pulsed Electric Field (PEF)** — continuous flow; **20–80 kV/cm**, microseconds.
- **High Pressure Homogenization (HPH)** — up to **400 MPa**.
- **Cold Plasma** — atmospheric or low-pressure; gases ionized at room temperature.
- **UV-C / Pulsed Light** — germicidal wavelengths **200–280 nm**.

#### 2.2.2 Hybrid / Thermo-Electric Technologies
- **Ohmic Heating** — electrical current passed through food; volumetric heating.
- **Microwave** — dielectric heating at **915 MHz / 2450 MHz**.
- **Radiofrequency** — **27.12 MHz**.
- **Infrared (IR)** — near (NIR), mid (MIR), far (FIR).

#### 2.2.3 Mechanical / Structural Technologies
- **Ultrasound** — cavitation-induced cell disruption, emulsification.
- **Membrane Processing** — MF, UF, NF, RO for concentration and fractionation.

#### 2.2.4 Phase-Change & Separation Technologies
- **Supercritical Fluid Extraction (SCFE)** — CO₂ above critical point.
- **Freeze Drying (Lyophilization)** — sublimation under vacuum.
- **Spray Drying / Encapsulation.**

#### 2.2.5 Additive Manufacturing
- **3D Food Printing** — extrusion-based, binder jetting, selective sintering for customized textures.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Equations Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note to Students:** Memorize the formulae, units, and key boundary conditions in the table below. KTU questions frequently test the relationship between process parameters (pressure, electric field, temperature) and their effect on microbial / kinetic outcomes.

| # | Equation / Formula | Symbols & Meaning | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Arrhenius Equation:** $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | $k$ = rate constant, $A$ = pre-exponential factor, $E_a$ = activation energy (kJ/mol), $R$ = 8.314 J/mol·K, $T$ = absolute temperature (K) | Predicting nutrient/quality degradation kinetics in non-thermal processing |
| 2 | **Pressure-Kinetics (Eyring-like):** $\ln(k_p / k_{ref}) = -V_a (P - P_{ref}) / RT$ | $V_a$ = activation volume (cm³/mol), $P$ = pressure (MPa) | HPP microbial inactivation |
| 3 | **Ohmic Heating Power:** $Q = V^{2} t / R$ | $V$ = voltage, $R$ = resistance, $t$ = time | Volumetric heating rate |
| 4 | **Ohmic Heating Energy:** $E = \int \sigma \cdot \vert \nabla V \vert^{2} \, dV$ | $\sigma$ = electrical conductivity (S/m), $\nabla V$ = voltage gradient | Heat generation in ohmic systems |
| 5 | **PEF Critical Field:** $E_c = V_{m} / d$ | $V_m$ = transmembrane potential (~1 V), $d$ = cell diameter (μm) | Electroporation threshold |
| 6 | **Cavitation Threshold:** $P_{cav} = P_0 + 0.77 \sigma / R_b$ | $P_0$ = hydrostatic, $\sigma$ = surface tension, $R_b$ = bubble radius | Ultrasound bubble collapse |
| 7 | **D-value (decimal reduction):** $N/N_0 = 10^{-t/D}$ | $D$ = decimal reduction time, $t$ = treatment time | Microbial inactivation kinetics |
| 8 | **z-value:** $\log(D_2/D_1) = (T_1 - T_2) / z$ | $z$ = temperature change for 10× change in D | Thermal process equivalent |
| 9 | **Water Activity:** $a_w = p / p_0$ | $p$ = vapor pressure of food, $p_0$ = pure water vapor pressure | Shelf-life prediction |
| 10 | **Supercritical Density (CO₂):** $\rho_c = 467$ kg/m³ at 31.1 °C, 7.38 MPa | Critical constants | SCFE solvent power |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU-Specific Note:** In written exams, always specify **units** with every numerical answer. Marks are awarded for unit correctness in calculation-based sub-parts.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Equations

- The **Arrhenius equation** is the *backbone of accelerated shelf-life testing* — essential when validating HPP or PEF processed juices.
- The **Eyring-Pressure relation** lets food engineers model *Listeria* or *E. coli* inactivation in HPP-treated guacamole and ready-to-eat meats.
- The **PEF critical field equation** guides design of electrode gap distances in industrial PEF chambers for fruit juice pasteurization.

---

### 2.5 Principles of Operation — Quick Logic Map

#### High Pressure Processing (HPP)
- **Principle:** Pascal's Law — pressure transmits uniformly and instantaneously throughout the food.
- **Effect on microbes:** damages cell membranes, disrupts ribosomes, denatures key enzymes — vegetative cells killed, spores unaffected.
- **Effect on quality:** covalent bonds unaffected → color, flavor, vitamins retained.

#### Pulsed Electric Field (PEF)
- **Principle:** External electric field induces transmembrane potential → **electroporation** of microbial membranes.
- **Effect:** reversible or irreversible pore formation; cytoplasm leakage → cell death.
- **Effect on quality:** minimal thermal damage; ideal for liquid foods.

#### Cold Plasma
- **Principle:** Ionized gas generates reactive oxygen and nitrogen species (ROS/RNS), UV photons, charged particles.
- **Effect:** lipid peroxidation of microbial membranes, DNA damage.
- **Effect on quality:** surface treatment only — perfect for decontaminating fresh produce, eggs, and packaging.

#### Ohmic Heating
- **Principle:** Food itself acts as an electrical resistor; heat generated internally as current flows.
- **Effect:** uniform, rapid heating; no surface overcooking.
- **Effect on quality:** preserves color and nutrients in particulate-containing foods (curries, soups).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Analysis of Novel Technologies

> [!NOTE]
> **How to use this table in an exam:** KTU examiners often ask "compare X and Y on the basis of principle, microbial inactivation, nutrient retention, and commercial applications." This matrix is your ready-reference.

| Parameter | HPP | PEF | Ohmic Heating | Ultrasound | Cold Plasma |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Isostatic pressure | Electroporation | Joule heating | Cavitation | Reactive species |
| **Mode** | Batch | Continuous | Continuous | Batch/Continuous | Surface/Batch |
| **Pressure/Field** | 400–600 MPa | 20–80 kV/cm | 50–100 V/cm | 20 kHz–1 MHz | Atmospheric/Low |
| **Temp Rise** | < 5 °C | < 5 °C | 70–90 °C | Variable | < 40 °C |
| **Spore Inactivation** | No | No | Yes | Limited | Limited |
| **Nutrient Retention** | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Good | Moderate |
| **Packaging Required** | Flexible only | Conductivity-based | Conductivity-based | Liquid medium | None |
| **Capital Cost** | High | High | Moderate | Low–Moderate | Moderate |
| **Commercial Foods** | Guacamole, juices, meat | Orange juice, milk | Soups, sauces | Emulsions, extracts | Fresh produce |
| **Major Limitation** | Spore survival | Liquid-only | Particulate conductivity | Heat-sensitive | Surface penetration |

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Arrhenius-Based Quality Degradation in HPP-Treated Juice

**Problem (typical KTU numerical):**
The degradation of Vitamin C in orange juice follows first-order kinetics. The rate constant at 25 °C is $k_{25} = 0.025$ /day. Activation energy $E_a = 65$ kJ/mol. Calculate the rate constant at 4 °C (refrigerated storage).

**Given:**

$$
T_1 = 25 + 273 = 298 \text{ K}, \quad k_1 = 0.025 \text{ /day}
$$

$$
T_2 = 4 + 273 = 277 \text{ K}, \quad E_a = 65{,}000 \text{ J/mol}
$$

$$
R = 8.314 \text{ J/mol·K}
$$

**Step 1 — Write the two-point Arrhenius form:**

$$
\ln \frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{E_a}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the temperature term:**

$$
\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{298} - \frac{1}{277}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{298} = 0.003356 \text{ K}^{-1}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{277} = 0.003610 \text{ K}^{-1}
$$

$$
\Delta = -0.000254 \text{ K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $E_a/R$:**

$$
\frac{E_a}{R} = \frac{65{,}000}{8.314} = 7819.4 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply:**

$$
\ln \frac{k_2}{k_1} = 7819.4 \times (-0.000254) = -1.986
$$

**Step 5 — Exponentiate:**

$$
\frac{k_2}{k_1} = e^{-1.986} = 0.137
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $k_2$:**

$$
k_2 = 0.025 \times 0.137 = 0.00343 \text{ /day}
$$

**Result:** The rate constant at 4 °C is approximately **0.0034 /day**, meaning refrigerated storage slows Vitamin C degradation by roughly **7.3×** compared to 25 °C.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for HPP juice:** HPP preserves initial Vitamin C content at processing (no thermal destruction). The above kinetic analysis then predicts how the residual Vitamin C declines during cold-chain distribution — a key claim in product labeling.

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — PEF Critical Field Calculation

**Problem:** Design a PEF chamber for treating *E. coli* cells of diameter $d = 1.0$ μm. The critical transmembrane potential is $V_m = 1.0$ V. Calculate the minimum external electric field required.

**Given:**

$$
V_m = 1.0 \text{ V}, \quad d = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}, \quad \text{shape factor } f = 1.5 \text{ (for spherical cell)}
$$

**Step 1 — Apply the electroporation equation:**

$$
V_m = f \cdot E \cdot r
$$

where $r = d/2$ is the cell radius.

**Step 2 — Substitute and solve for $E$:**

$$
E = \frac{V_m}{f \cdot r}
$$

$$
E = \frac{1.0}{1.5 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
E = \frac{1.0}{0.75 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.333 \times 10^{6} \text{ V/m}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kV/cm:**

$$
E = \frac{1.333 \times 10^{6}}{10^{5}} = 13.33 \text{ kV/cm}
$$

**Result:** A minimum field strength of approximately **13.3 kV/cm** is required. Industrial PEF systems typically operate at **20–80 kV/cm** to provide a safety margin and overcome non-uniform field distribution.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison — Novel Ingredients vs Conventional

| Attribute | Conventional Ingredient | Novel Ingredient |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Traditional agricultural | Plant microgreens, algae, fermentation |
| **Function** | Basic nutrition | Functional + nutritional |
| **Processing Required** | Minimal | Often requires encapsulation |
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate–High |
| **Regulatory Status** | GRAS, well-established | Novel Food Regulation (EU 2015/2283), FSSAI approval |
| **Consumer Perception** | Familiar | "Clean-label" friendly |
| **Shelf-life Impact** | Variable | Often antioxidant / antimicrobial |

### 3.5 Selection Logic for Food Product Design

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Framework for choosing a novel ingredient:**
> 1. **Identify health claim** → Probiotic, omega-3, low-sugar, high-protein.
> 2. **Match ingredient to matrix** → Hydrophobic actives → liposomes; hydrophilic → spray-dried capsules.
> 3. **Check sensory impact** → Bitterness masking, color stability.
> 4. **Validate regulatory status** → Novel Food approval in target market.
> 5. **Conduct shelf-life study** → Arrhenius kinetics at 25 °C, 30 °C, 37 °C.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
