## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define **novel food ingredients** with two suitable examples.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Novel food ingredients are non-traditional substances intentionally incorporated into food products to provide enhanced **nutritional, functional, sensory, or health-promoting** properties beyond those of conventional ingredients. They are usually derived from plants, microbes, marine organisms, or via bioprocessing.
>
> **Examples (any two):**
> 1. **Steviol glycosides** — natural high-intensity sweetener from *Stevia rebaudiana* leaves (~200–300× sweeter than sucrose, zero calories).
> 2. **Spirulina protein** — single-cell protein from cyanobacterium *Arthrospira platensis* (60–70% protein, rich in phycocyanin and B-vitamins).
> 3. *(Alt)* **Inulin** — prebiotic chicory-root fiber selectively feeding gut bifidobacteria.
>
> *Word limit hint: ~80–100 words for full 3 marks.*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is **High Pressure Processing (HPP)**? State its operating range.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> High Pressure Processing (HPP) is a **non-thermal preservation technology** that subjects pre-packaged food to isostatic pressures of typically **400–600 MPa** for 5–20 minutes at room or mild temperatures. It inactivates vegetative microorganisms and enzymes by disrupting cell membranes, ribosomes, and protein conformations, while preserving covalent bonds — thus retaining color, flavor, and heat-sensitive nutrients like Vitamin C.
>
> *Word limit hint: ~60–80 words.*

---

### PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the classification, sources, and functional roles of **novel food ingredients** in food product design. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **principle, working, applications, and limitations** of High Pressure Processing (HPP) and Pulsed Electric Field (PEF) technologies. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (a):**
Novel food ingredients are classified into five major categories, each contributing distinct functional attributes to modern food design:

1. **Functional ingredients and nutraceuticals** — omega-3 fatty acids (EPA, DHA from fish/algal oils) for cardiovascular and brain health; phytosterols for cholesterol reduction; polyphenols (curcumin, resveratrol) as antioxidants; carotenoids (beta-carotene, lycopene) as provitamins and colorants; dietary fibers (inulin, beta-glucan) for gut health and glycemic control.

2. **Bioactive microbial ingredients** — **probiotics** (live *Lactobacillus*, *Bifidobacterium* at ≥10⁶ CFU/g) modulate gut microbiota; **prebiotics** (FOS, GOS) selectively feed beneficial bacteria; **synbiotics** combine both; **postbiotics** deliver inactivated cells and metabolites.

3. **Alternative proteins** — plant proteins (pea, soy, chickpea) addressing sustainability; insect proteins (cricket, mealworm); single-cell proteins from yeast, bacteria, and microalgae; cultured meat via cellular agriculture.

4. **Novel sweeteners and fat replacers** — high-intensity natural sweeteners (stevia, monk fruit, allulose) for calorie reduction; sugar alcohols (erythritol, xylitol); oleogels and inulin-gels for fat mimicry.

5. **Edible films and coatings** — polysaccharide, protein, lipid, and composite films extending shelf-life and reducing plastic waste.

**Functional roles** include: nutrition enhancement, texture modification, shelf-life extension, clean-label preservation, and addressing consumer demand for health-conscious, sustainable, and allergen-free products.

**Model Answer (b):**

**High Pressure Processing (HPP):**
- **Principle:** Pascal's law — pressure transmits uniformly and instantaneously throughout the food regardless of geometry.
- **Working:** Pre-packaged food in flexible pouches is loaded into a pressure vessel filled with water as the pressure-transmitting medium. An intensifier pump raises pressure to **400–600 MPa**, held for 5–20 minutes, then released.
- **Microbial inactivation mechanism:** damages cytoplasmic membranes, ribosomes, and protein conformations. Vegetative cells of *Listeria*, *Salmonella*, *E. coli* are inactivated. **Spores are resistant.**
- **Quality effects:** covalent bonds unaffected → vitamins, color, flavor retained.
- **Applications:** guacamole, fruit juices, ready-to-eat meats, seafood, dairy desserts.
- **Limitations:** batch process; spores unaffected; only flexible packaging allowed.

**Pulsed Electric Field (PEF):**
- **Principle:** External high-voltage pulses create a transmembrane potential that exceeds the critical threshold (~1 V), causing **electroporation** — irreversible pore formation in microbial membranes.
- **Working:** Liquid food flows continuously between two electrodes; pulses of **20–80 kV/cm** for microseconds are applied. Treatment time: < 1 second total.
- **Microbial inactivation:** vegetative bacteria and yeasts inactivated; **spores unaffected**.
- **Quality effects:** essentially non-thermal; minimal flavor/nutrient loss.
- **Applications:** orange juice, apple juice, milk, liquid egg, smoothies.
- **Limitations:** liquid foods only; electrical conductivity restrictions; high capital cost.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the principle, types, equipment, and food applications of **Ohmic Heating** and **Ultrasound** processing. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss **functional foods, nutraceuticals, and probiotics** with emphasis on their health claims, mechanisms, and product formats. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (a):**

**Ohmic Heating:**
- **Principle:** Food itself acts as an electrical resistor; alternating current (50 Hz) passes through it, generating **Joule heat ($Q = V^{2}t/R$)** volumetrically and uniformly.
- **Equipment:** treatment chamber with two electrodes, AC power supply, pre- and post-heat exchangers, holding tube.
- **Types:** batch (static) and continuous (flow).
- **Applications:** particulate-containing foods (curries, vegetable soups, fruit pulps), milk sterilization, liquid egg.
- **Advantages:** uniform heating, no surface overcooking, retention of color and nutrients, energy efficient.
- **Limitations:** requires homogeneous electrical conductivity; not suitable for foods with trapped air or fat globules.

**Ultrasound Processing:**
- **Principle:** High-intensity sound waves (**20–100 kHz**) generate **acoustic cavitation** — microbubbles form and collapse violently, producing localized high temperature (~5000 K) and pressure (~50 MPa), free radicals, and shear forces.
- **Types:** probe (direct immersion) and bath (indirect) sonication.
- **Applications:** emulsification, extraction (ultrasound-assisted extraction of bioactive compounds), crystallization control, microbial inactivation in juices, inactivation of enzymes (e.g., polyphenol oxidase).
- **Advantages:** low-cost, environmentally friendly, short processing time.
- **Limitations:** free radical generation may degrade sensitive compounds; scale-up challenges.

**Model Answer (b):**

**Functional Foods:** Foods that provide health benefits beyond basic nutrition. Examples: oats with beta-glucan (cholesterol lowering), fortified dairy with Vitamin D, omega-3 enriched eggs. Marketed claims must be scientifically substantiated under FSSAI/EFSA regulations.

**Nutraceuticals:** Bioactive compounds consumed as supplements or fortified foods. Categories:
- **Dietary supplements** (capsules, tablets) — fish oil, curcumin, glucosamine.
- **Functional foods** — fortified juices, probiotic yogurt.
- **Medical foods** — formulated for specific diseases under medical supervision.

**Probiotics:** Live microorganisms that, when administered in adequate amounts, confer a health benefit to the host (FAO/WHO definition, 2002). Common genera: *Lactobacillus*, *Bifidobacterium*, *Saccharomyces boulardii*. Minimum dose: **10⁶–10⁹ CFU/day**.

**Mechanisms of probiotic action:**
- Competitive exclusion of pathogens.
- Production of bacteriocins and organic acids.
- Modulation of immune system (IgA, cytokines).
- Enhancement of gut barrier integrity.

**Health claims:** Reduced diarrhea (antibiotic-associated), improved lactose digestion, lowered cholesterol, immune modulation, reduced symptoms of IBS.

**Product formats:** probiotic yogurt, kefir, fermented milk, probiotic chocolates, encapsulated sachets, synbiotic ice cream, probiotic breakfast cereals.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students LOSE marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing HPP with HPH** — HPP is **static batch** treatment at 400–600 MPa for minutes; HPH is **continuous flow** at up to 400 MPa — different equipment, different outcomes.
> 2. **Spore survival** — Always mention explicitly that **HPP and PEF do NOT inactivate bacterial spores**; otherwise the examiner deducts 1 mark under "completeness of microbial inactivation".
> 3. **Probiotic dosage** — Without specifying **CFU/g or CFU/day**, the answer on probiotics is incomplete.
> 4. **Units missing** — Pressure (MPa), field strength (kV/cm), temperature (°C/K) — every numerical must carry units.
> 5. **Confusing prebiotic with probiotic** — Probiotics are **live organisms**; prebiotics are **non-digestible food for those organisms**.
> 6. **Sketch missing in P-part answers** — A labeled diagram of PEF chamber or HPP vessel fetches at least **1–2 bonus marks** even when textual answer is partial.
> 7. **Forgetting "isostatic" in HPP** — Always emphasize *uniform, instantaneous, omnidirectional* pressure application.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision before KTU exam — read these bullets aloud:**

- **Novel Food Ingredients** = non-traditional ingredients with enhanced functional, nutritional, or health benefits. Five categories: **functional/nutraceutical, microbial bioactive, alternative protein, novel sweetener/fat replacer, edible films**.
- **Probiotic dose:** minimum **10⁶ CFU/g** at consumption. Genera: *Lactobacillus*, *Bifidobacterium*, *Saccharomyces*.
- **Prebiotic examples:** FOS, GOS, inulin (fructan-type); selectively feed *Bifidobacteria*.
- **Alternative proteins:** plant-based (pea, soy), insect (cricket, mealworm), SCP (yeast, *Spirulina*), cultured meat.
- **Novel sweeteners:** stevia (~200–300×), monk fruit (~150–300×), allulose (rare sugar, 70% sweetness of sucrose).
- **HPP:** 400–600 MPa, 5–20 min, isostatic, batch, **spores survive**, nutrient retention excellent.
- **PEF:** 20–80 kV/cm, microseconds, electroporation, **liquid foods only**, continuous.
- **Ohmic heating:** $Q = V^{2}t/R$, volumetric Joule heating, suitable for **particulate foods**.
- **Ultrasound:** 20–100 kHz, acoustic cavitation, applications in extraction & emulsification.
- **Cold plasma:** ROS/RNS + UV; **surface decontamination** only; gas temperature ~room temperature.
- **Microwave:** dielectric heating at **2450 MHz** (domestic) / **915 MHz** (industrial).
- **SCFE:** uses **supercritical CO₂** above 31.1 °C, 7.38 MPa; solvent-free extract.
- **Freeze drying:** sublimation of ice under vacuum; best nutrient retention but **highest cost**.
- **Membrane processing order (increasing tightness):** MF < UF < NF < RO.
- **3D Food Printing:** extrusion-based most common; enables customized texture and nutrition.
- **Key equations to memorize:** Arrhenius $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$, Eyring-pressure, $Q = V^{2}t/R$, $E_c = V_m/d$.
- **Regulatory:** EU Novel Food Regulation 2015/2283; FSSAI Novel Food approvals (India, 2022+).
- **Sustainability angle:** alternative proteins, edible films, and SCFE are **green processing** — frequently tested under "current trends".
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process and Equipment Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT834 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Process and Equipment Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Design** is the conceptual and mathematical framework that defines the sequence of unit operations, their operating parameters (temperature, pressure, flow rates, residence time), material/energy balances, and the selection criteria for equipment required to transform raw food materials into finished, shelf-stable, safe, and consumer-acceptable products.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equipment Design** is the engineering discipline of sizing, specifying, and configuring the physical hardware (reactors, heat exchangers, mixers, separators, dryers, packaging lines) that execute the unit operations defined during process design, ensuring hygienic construction, regulatory compliance, scalability, and economic feasibility.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework for **OEBBT834**, Process and Equipment Design is treated as the engineering backbone that bridges *product formulation* (sensory + nutritional design) with *commercial manufacturing reality*. It is the translation of laboratory bench-top recipes into robust, repeatable industrial processes.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Making it Click for First-Time Learners

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Kitchen vs. The Factory**
>
> Imagine you have perfected a **chocolate chip cookie recipe at home**. You know exactly:
> - How much butter, sugar, and flour to mix (recipe = formulation)
> - That the oven must be at **180 °C** for **12 minutes** (process parameters)
> - That you need a bowl, a whisk, a baking tray, and an oven (equipment)
>
> Now imagine you must produce **50,000 cookies per day** for a supermarket chain. Suddenly:
> - "A bowl" is no longer acceptable — you need an **industrial planetary mixer** of a specific volume.
> - "An oven" must be replaced by a **continuous tunnel oven** with controlled conveyor speed.
> - "12 minutes" must be converted into **residence time** as the tray passes through temperature zones.
> - "A pinch of salt" must become a precisely metered **mass flow rate in kg/h**.
>
> **Process Design** = Deciding the *what, how much, at what temperature, for how long*.
> **Equipment Design** = Picking and sizing the *machinery that makes it happen safely, hygienically, and economically*.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **standard engineering metrics** are repeatedly used in food process and equipment design:
>
> - **Steam table reference pressure:** **101.325 kPa** (atmospheric, used as the enthalpy datum)
> - **Specific heat capacity of water:** **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)**
> - **Latent heat of vaporization of water at 100 °C:** **2257 kJ/kg**
> - **Standard sanitary surface finish (Ra) for food contact:** **≤ 0.8 μm** (3-A Sanitary Standards)
> - **Hygienic design slope for self-draining surfaces:** **≥ 3°** (EHEDG recommendation)
> - **Hygienic design minimum bend radius for piping:** **≥ 1.5 × pipe diameter**
> - **Standard CIP (Clean-in-Place) rinse water temperature:** **75–85 °C**

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 B.Tech Curriculum

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome Alignment (CO Mapping for OEBBT834 Module 3):**
> - **CO3 — Process Engineering Competence:** Design unit operations and select appropriate equipment for food product manufacture.
> - **CO4 — Scale-Up & Commercialization:** Translate bench-scale recipes into pilot and industrial-scale processes.
> - **CO5 — Safety, Hygiene & Regulatory Compliance:** Integrate GMP, HACCP, and EHEDG principles into process and equipment design.

This module is the **engineer's bridge** between food science and industrial production. Without rigorous process and equipment design, even the most innovative product remains a lab curiosity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Process Design

> [!NOTE]
> Process design in the food industry rests on **five sequential pillars**. Mastering this hierarchy guarantees success in KTU 14-mark essay questions.

### Pillar 1 — Mass Balance (Material Balance)
The **law of conservation of mass**: matter is neither created nor destroyed in a non-nuclear process.

**Generic steady-state mass balance equation:**

$$\frac{dm_{sys}}{dt} = \sum \dot{m}_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out} + \sum \dot{m}_{generated} - \sum \dot{m}_{consumed}$$

For a food process with **no chemical reaction** (purely physical operations like mixing, heating, packaging), this simplifies to:

$$\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out} \quad \text{(Total mass in = Total mass out)}$$

For individual components (component mass balance):

$$\dot{m}_{A,in} + \dot{m}_{A,generated} = \dot{m}_{A,out} + \dot{m}_{A,consumed} + \dot{m}_{A,accumulated}$$

### Pillar 2 — Energy Balance
Energy entering = Energy leaving + Energy accumulated + Losses.

**First Law of Thermodynamics applied to a food process unit:**

$$\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \sum \dot{m}_{out} \cdot h_{out} - \sum \dot{m}_{in} \cdot h_{in}$$

Where:
- $\dot{Q}$ = heat transfer rate (W)
- $\dot{W}$ = shaft work (W)
- $h$ = specific enthalpy (J/kg)

For **sensible heating** of a food stream (no phase change):

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$$

For **latent heating** (e.g., boiling, evaporation, drying):

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \lambda$$

For combined sensible + latent:

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \left[ C_p \cdot \Delta T + \lambda \right]$$

### Pillar 3 — Unit Operations Sequencing
A food manufacturing process is a **network of unit operations** linked by material streams. The sequence must be optimized for:
- **Product quality** (minimal thermal damage to nutrients)
- **Energy efficiency** (counter-current heat exchange)
- **Capital cost** (number and size of equipment)
- **Food safety** (no cross-contamination)

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Food Industry Unit Operations:**
> Cleaning → Sorting/Grading → Size Reduction → Mixing/Blending → Heat Treatment (Pasteurization/Sterilization) → Concentration/Evaporation → Drying → Packaging → Storage

### Pillar 4 — Process Flow Diagram (PFD)
The PFD is the **graphical blueprint** showing:
- All major equipment (symbolized as per ISO 10628)
- All process streams with flow rates, temperatures, pressures, and compositions
- Material and energy balances
- Utility streams (steam, cooling water, compressed air)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** A PFD is **NOT** the same as a P&ID (Piping & Instrumentation Diagram). P&IDs include **every valve, instrument, and control loop**. PFDs show only the major flow lines.

### Pillar 5 — Equipment Selection & Sizing
Selection criteria include:
- **Throughput capacity** (kg/h or L/h)
- **Operating temperature and pressure**
- **Product rheology** (Newtonian vs. non-Newtonian — affects pump and mixer selection)
- **Hygienic standards** (3-A, EHEDG)
- **Material of construction** (SS304 vs. SS316 vs. SS316L)
- **Capital and operating cost**

---

## 2.2 Equipment Design: Categories & Engineering Principles

### 2.2.1 Heat Exchangers
Used for pasteurization, sterilization, cooling, and evaporation.

**Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)** — fundamental for sizing:

$$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}$$

**Overall heat transfer equation:**

$$\dot{Q} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$$

Where:
- $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $A$ = heat transfer area (m²)
- Typical $U$ values in food industry:
  - **Plate heat exchanger (PHE):** **3000–7000 W/m²·K**
  - **Scraped surface heat exchanger (SSHE):** **1500–3000 W/m²·K**
  - **Shell-and-tube:** **1000–3000 W/m²·K**

### 2.2.2 Mixers and Agitators

**Power number correlation** (Reynolds-number-based, for scale-up):

$$P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5$$

Where:
- $P$ = power consumption (W)
- $N_p$ = power number (dimensionless, function of Reynolds number and impeller type)
- $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m³)
- $N$ = impeller rotational speed (rev/s)
- $D$ = impeller diameter (m)

**Reynolds number for mixing:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho \cdot N \cdot D^2}{\mu}$$

Scale-up principle (constant $P/V$ — power per unit volume):

$$\frac{P_1}{V_1} = \frac{P_2}{V_2}$$

### 2.2.3 Pumps and Piping
**Hagen-Poiseuille equation** for laminar flow in pipes:

$$\Delta P = \frac{32 \cdot \mu \cdot L \cdot v}{D^2}$$

**Darcy-Weisbach equation** for turbulent flow:

$$\Delta P = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho \cdot v^2}{2}$$

Where $f$ = Darcy friction factor (Moody chart or Colebrook equation).

### 2.2.4 Dryers
**Moisture content definition (wet basis):**

$$X_{wb} = \frac{m_{water}}{m_{total}} \times 100\%$$

**Moisture content (dry basis):**

$$X_{db} = \frac{m_{water}}{m_{dry\,solid}}$$

Conversion: $X_{db} = \dfrac{X_{wb}}{1 - X_{wb}}$

---

## 2.3 Hygienic Design Principles (EHEDG / 3-A Standards)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 12 Golden Rules of Hygienic Equipment Design (EHEDG Doc. 8):**
> 1. Surfaces in contact with food must be **smooth** (Ra ≤ 0.8 μm), **non-toxic**, and **corrosion-resistant**.
> 2. All product contact surfaces must be **self-draining** (slope ≥ 3°).
> 3. **No dead legs, crevices, or pockets** where food can accumulate.
> 4. All joints and seals must be **food-grade** (EPDM, silicone, Viton).
> 5. Equipment must be **designed for CIP** (Clean-in-Place) or be easily disassembled for manual cleaning (COP).
> 6. **Inside radii of bends** must be ≥ 1.5 × pipe diameter.
> 7. Use **sanitary fittings** (tri-clamp, DIN 11851) — never threaded joints in product zones.
> 8. **Materials of construction:** SS304 acceptable for non-corrosive products; **SS316/316L mandatory** for acidic, salty, or chloride-containing foods.
> 9. Avoid **cold spots** that promote condensation and microbial growth.
> 10. Provide **adequate access** for inspection and maintenance.
> 11. Equipment must withstand **thermal cycling** of CIP (typically 85 °C).
> 12. **No lubricants** in product zones — use food-grade NSF H1 lubricants.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Boundary / Typical Values |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out}$ | Steady-state total mass balance | kg/s | Holds when no reaction & no accumulation |
| 2 | $\dot{m}_{A,in} = \dot{m}_{A,out}$ | Component mass balance (no reaction) | kg/s | Used for mixing, blending, dilution |
| 3 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat transfer | W | $C_p$ varies with water content |
| 4 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \lambda$ | Latent heat transfer | W | $\lambda_{water,\,100°C}$ = 2257 kJ/kg |
| 5 | $\dot{Q} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Overall heat transfer | W | $U$ depends on equipment type |
| 6 | $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log mean temperature difference | K | $\Delta T_1 \neq \Delta T_2$ |
| 7 | $P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5$ | Mixer power consumption | W | $N_p$ from standard curves |
| 8 | $Re_{mix} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot N \cdot D^2}{\mu}$ | Mixing Reynolds number | dimensionless | Laminar: < 10; Turbulent: > 10,000 |
| 9 | $X_{db} = \dfrac{X_{wb}}{1 - X_{wb}}$ | Moisture conversion | dimensionless | Wet basis ranges 0–100% |
| 10 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{32 \mu L v}{D^2}$ | Laminar pipe flow | Pa | Hagen-Poiseuille |
| 11 | $\Delta P = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Turbulent pipe flow | Pa | Darcy-Weisbach |
| 12 | $\eta_{thermal} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{useful}}{\dot{Q}_{input}}$ | Thermal efficiency | dimensionless | 0 < $\eta$ ≤ 1 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always **state assumptions** (steady-state, no reaction, negligible heat loss) before applying any mass or energy balance. Marks are awarded for **assumption statements** before the calculation begins.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - In **dairy industry**, plate heat exchangers (PHEs) are designed using LMTD to achieve HTST pasteurization (72 °C × 15 s).
> - In **brewing**, mash tuns are sized using material balance + residence time of **60–90 minutes**.
> - In **edible oil refining**, scraped surface heat exchangers (SSHEs) handle viscous, fouling fluids using $U \approx$ **1500–2500 W/m²·K**.
> - In **beverage carbonation**, in-line static mixers are designed using Reynolds number ($Re > 4000$) for turbulent dispersion of CO₂.

Process and equipment design is therefore not an academic exercise — it is the **daily language** spoken by process engineers in every food factory in the world.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: General Mass Balance on a Food Mixing Tank

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A fruit juice blending tank receives two streams:
> - **Stream A (Apple juice concentrate):** $\dot{m}_A = 500$ kg/h, $Brix_A = 70\%$
> - **Stream B (Water):** $\dot{m}_B = 1000$ kg/h, $Brix_B = 0\%$
> - **Output Stream C (Reconstituted juice):** $\dot{m}_C = ?$, $Brix_C = ?$
> - Steady-state, perfect mixing, no reaction, no evaporation.

---

### Step 1 — State Assumptions Explicitly

> **Assumptions:**
> 1. Steady-state operation → $\frac{dm_{sys}}{dt} = 0$
> 2. Perfect mixing → outlet composition uniform
> 3. No chemical reaction → no generation/consumption terms
> 4. No evaporation → all water and solids conserved
> 5. Density variations neglected

---

### Step 2 — Write Total Mass Balance Equation

For a control volume at steady state:

$$\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}$$

Substituting the streams:

$$\dot{m}_A + \dot{m}_B = \dot{m}_C$$

---

### Step 3 — Solve for Outlet Mass Flow Rate

$$\dot{m}_C = 500 + 1000 = 1500 \; \text{kg/h}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_C = 1500 \; \text{kg/h}}$$

---

### Step 4 — Write Component Mass Balance for Soluble Solids (Brix)

Total soluble solids (TSS) is conserved (no reaction):

$$\dot{m}_A \cdot Brix_A + \dot{m}_B \cdot Brix_B = \dot{m}_C \cdot Brix_C$$

---

### Step 5 — Substitute Known Values

$$500 \times 0.70 + 1000 \times 0.00 = 1500 \times Brix_C$$

$$350 + 0 = 1500 \cdot Brix_C$$

$$Brix_C = \frac{350}{1500}$$

$$\boxed{Brix_C = 0.2333 = 23.33\%}$$

---

### Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** To produce **1500 kg/h** of apple juice at **23.33 °Brix**, the plant must meter **500 kg/h of concentrate (70 °Brix)** and **1000 kg/h of water** precisely. A deviation of ±5% in concentrate flow changes the Brix by ±1.17 °Brix — affecting sweetness, label declaration, and regulatory compliance with FSSAI/BIS standards.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Heat Exchanger Sizing for Milk Pasteurization

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** An HTST pasteurizer must heat milk from **4 °C to 72 °C** using hot water at **85 °C entering** and **78 °C leaving** the heat exchanger. Milk flow rate = **10,000 kg/h**. $C_{p,milk}$ = **3.9 kJ/(kg·K)**. Assume $U = 5000$ W/m²·K (typical for plate heat exchanger).

---

### Step 1 — Compute Heat Duty (Sensible Heating)

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$$

$$\dot{Q} = 10000 \times 3.9 \times (72 - 4)$$

$$\dot{Q} = 10000 \times 3.9 \times 68$$

$$\dot{Q} = 2{,}652{,}000 \; \text{kJ/h}$$

Convert to kW (1 kW = 3600 kJ/h = 1 kJ/s):

$$\dot{Q} = \frac{2{,}652{,}000}{3600}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{Q} = 736.67 \; \text{kW}}$$

---

### Step 2 — Identify Temperature Differences at Both Ends of Exchanger

Counter-current flow assumption:
- **Hot end:** $\Delta T_1 = 85 - 72 = 13 \; °C$ (hot water in vs. milk out)
- **Cold end:** $\Delta T_2 = 78 - 4 = 74 \; °C$ (hot water out vs. milk in)

---

### Step 3 — Apply LMTD Formula

$$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}$$

$$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{13 - 74}{\ln\left(\dfrac{13}{74}\right)}$$

$$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{-61}{\ln(0.1757)}$$

Compute logarithm:

$$\ln(0.1757) = -1.738$$

$$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{-61}{-1.738}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{LMTD} \approx 35.10 \; °C}$$

---

### Step 4 — Solve for Heat Transfer Area

From $\dot{Q} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$:

$$A = \frac{\dot{Q}}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}}$$

Convert $\dot{Q}$ to W: $\dot{Q} = 736{,}670 \; \text{W}$

$$A = \frac{736670}{5000 \times 35.10}$$

$$A = \frac{736670}{175500}$$

$$\boxed{A \approx 4.20 \; \text{m}^2}$$

---

### Step 5 — Apply Correction Factor (Real World)

For plate heat exchangers, the LMTD method is corrected by a factor $F$ (typically 0.85–0.95):

$$A_{actual} = \frac{A_{ideal}}{F}$$

$$A_{actual} = \frac{4.20}{0.90}$$

$$\boxed{A_{actual} \approx 4.67 \; \text{m}^2}$$

---

### Step 6 — Select Plate Configuration

> [!NOTE]
> Standard plate area per plate in commercial PHE = **0.15–0.30 m²**. Required number of plates:

$$N_{plates} = \frac{4.67}{0.25} \approx 19 \; \text{plates}$$

> **Final Design:** A plate heat exchanger with **~19 plates (0.25 m² each)**, counter-current flow, delivering **736.7 kW** of heat duty.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Scale-Up of a Mixing Tank Using Constant P/V Rule

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A laboratory mixer (5 L) operates at $N_1 = 500$ rpm with $D_1 = 0.08$ m. Power consumption $P_1 = 12$ W. Scale-up to a production tank (5000 L) using the **constant power-per-volume** criterion. Find $N_2$, $D_2$, and $P_2$.

---

### Step 1 — Determine Volume and Diameter Ratio

For geometric similarity, all linear dimensions scale with the cube root of volume:

$$\frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{5000}{5} = 1000$$

$$\frac{D_2}{D_1} = \left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^{1/3} = 1000^{1/3} = 10$$

$$D_2 = 0.08 \times 10 = 0.80 \; \text{m}$$

---

### Step 2 — Apply Constant P/V Criterion

$$\frac{P_1}{V_1} = \frac{P_2}{V_2}$$

$$\frac{12}{0.005} = \frac{P_2}{5}$$

$$2400 \; \text{W/m}^3 = \frac{P_2}{5}$$

$$P_2 = 12000 \; \text{W} = 12 \; \text{kW}$$

---

### Step 3 — Find New Rotational Speed

Using $P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5$, with $N_p$ and $\rho$ assumed constant:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 \cdot \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^5$$

$$\frac{12000}{12} = 1000 = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 \cdot 10^5$$

$$\left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 = \frac{1000}{100000} = 0.01$$

$$\frac{N_2}{N_1} = (0.01)^{1/3} = 0.2154$$

$$N_2 = 500 \times 0.2154 \approx 107.7 \; \text{rpm}$$

---

### Step 4 — Verify Reynolds Number Regime

$$Re_1 = \frac{1000 \times (500/60) \times 0.08^2}{0.001} \approx 53{,}333 \; (\text{turbulent})$$

$$Re_2 = \frac{1000 \times (107.7/60) \times 0.80^2}{0.001} \approx 1{,}148{,}800 \; (\text{turbulent})$$

> **Conclusion:** Both tanks operate in the turbulent regime, validating the $N_p = $ const assumption. Production mixer requires **12 kW** motor running at **~108 rpm** with a **0.80 m** impeller.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Equipment Selection for Liquids

| Product Property | Recommended Pump Type | Recommended Mixer Type | Recommended Heat Exchanger |
|---|---|---|---|
| Low viscosity Newtonian (milk, juice) | Centrifugal (sanitary) | Centrifugal (high-shear) | Plate (PHE) |
| High viscosity Newtonian (oil, syrup) | Positive displacement (lobe) | Anchor / Helical | Scraped surface (SSHE) |
| Non-Newtonian shear-thinning (yogurt) | Positive displacement | Helical / Screw | SSHE |
| Particulate-containing (soup, fruit chunks) | Progressive cavity | Low-shear paddle | Tubular |
| Heat-sensitive (enzymes, probiotics) | Peristaltic | Gentle paddle | PHE with regeneration |

---

## 3.5 Mass & Energy Balance — Integrated Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A tomato ketchup batch process concentrates tomato paste from **12% total solids (TS)** to **32% TS** by evaporating water. Initial mass = **10,000 kg**. Determine water removed and energy required.

### Step 1 — Initial Water and Solids Mass

$$m_{solids} = 10000 \times 0.12 = 1200 \; \text{kg}$$

$$m_{water,\,initial} = 10000 - 1200 = 8800 \; \text{kg}$$

### Step 2 — Final Total Mass at 32% TS

Let $m_f$ = final mass:

$$\frac{1200}{m_f} = 0.32 \implies m_f = 3750 \; \text{kg}$$

### Step 3 — Water Removed

$$m_{water,\,removed} = 8800 - (3750 - 1200) = 8800 - 2550 = 6250 \; \text{kg}$$

$$\boxed{m_{water,\,removed} = 6250 \; \text{kg}}$$

### Step 4 — Energy Required (latent heat of vaporization at ~100 °C)

$$\dot{Q} = m_{water} \cdot \lambda = 6250 \times 2257$$

$$\boxed{\dot{Q} = 14{,}106{,}250 \; \text{kJ} \approx 14{,}106 \; \text{MJ}}$$

> **Interpretation:** To concentrate one batch (10 tonnes → 3.75 tonnes), **6.25 tonnes of water** must be evaporated, requiring approximately **3,918 kWh** of thermal energy. This justifies the use of **multi-effect evaporators** (3-effect reduces steam consumption by ~65%).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CIP_T[CIP Tank - Caustic 2 percent 80 C] --> P_CIP[CIP Pump]
    P_CIP --> R[Rinse Station]

    RV --> CIP_T

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard CIP Sequence:**
> 1. **Pre-rinse** with warm water (40–50 °C) → remove loose soil.
> 2. **Caustic wash** (1–3% NaOH, 75–85 °C, 20–30 min) → hydrolyze proteins/fats.
> 3. **Intermediate rinse** with water → remove caustic residues.
> 4. **Acid wash** (1–2% HNO₃ or H₃PO₄, 60–70 °C) → remove mineral scale.
> 5. **Final rinse** with deionized water (75–85 °C) → remove acid + sanitize.
> 6. **Optional sanitization** with 100–200 ppm chlorine or peracetic acid.
