# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define "Log Mean Temperature Difference" (LMTD). Why is it preferred over arithmetic mean temperature difference for heat exchanger design?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> LMTD is defined as:
> $$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}$$
> where $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ are the temperature differences between hot and cold streams at the two ends of the heat exchanger. **(1 mark)**
> The arithmetic mean is mathematically incorrect because heat transfer rate is proportional to the *driving force*, which decays exponentially along the exchanger length, not linearly. **(1 mark)**
> LMTD correctly represents the **logarithmic mean** of this exponential decay profile, giving the true average driving force for the **$\dot{Q} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$** equation. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any SIX hygienic design principles for food processing equipment as per EHEDG guidelines.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks — any 6):**
> 1. Product contact surfaces must be **smooth** (Ra ≤ 0.8 μm), non-toxic, corrosion-resistant. **(0.5 mark)**
> 2. All surfaces must be **self-draining** (slope ≥ 3°). **(0.5 mark)**
> 3. **No dead legs, crevices, or pockets** in product zone. **(0.5 mark)**
> 4. Use **sanitary fittings** (tri-clamp) — avoid threaded joints. **(0.5 mark)**
> 5. Material of construction: **SS316/316L** for acidic/ chloride foods. **(0.5 mark)**
> 6. Equipment must be **CIP-compatible** or easily disassembled (COP). **(0.5 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**A dairy plant must produce 8000 kg/h of standardized milk (3.5% fat, 8.5% SNF). It receives raw milk (4.2% fat, 8.7% SNF) and cream (40% fat, 5.8% SNF). It also removes skim milk (0.05% fat, 8.9% SNF) as a by-product. The standardization requires blending raw milk with skim milk to achieve 3.5% fat. Assume steady-state and no reaction.**

**(a)** Set up the **overall and component mass balance equations** for the standardization process. Determine the **flow rates of raw milk and skim milk** required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a **plate heat exchanger** to pasteurize the standardized milk from **6 °C to 72 °C** using hot water entering at **88 °C** and leaving at **76 °C**. Take $U = 5500 \; W/m^2 \cdot K$, $C_p = 3.9 \; kJ/(kg \cdot K)$, plate area = 0.25 m², and correction factor $F = 0.92$. State all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — State Assumptions (1 mark):**
- Steady-state → accumulation = 0
- Perfect mixing → uniform compositions
- No chemical reaction → no generation/consumption
- Only fat content is the controlled variable; SNF assumed satisfied

**Step 2 — Define Variables (1 mark):**
Let:
- $\dot{m}_R$ = raw milk flow rate (kg/h)
- $\dot{m}_{Sk}$ = skim milk flow rate (kg/h)
- $\dot{m}_P$ = product standardized milk flow rate (kg/h)
- $\dot{m}_{Cr}$ = cream flow rate (kg/h) [not used in blending step, but flows out]

Given:
- $\dot{m}_P = 8000 \; kg/h$
- Standardized milk fat = 3.5%

**Step 3 — Total Mass Balance (1 mark):**

$$\dot{m}_R + \dot{m}_{Sk} = 8000 \; kg/h$$

**Step 4 — Fat Mass Balance (2 marks):**

$$\dot{m}_R \times 0.042 + \dot{m}_{Sk} \times 0.0005 = 8000 \times 0.035$$

**Step 5 — Solve the System (2 marks):**

From Equation 1: $\dot{m}_{Sk} = 8000 - \dot{m}_R$

Substitute:

$$\dot{m}_R \times 0.042 + (8000 - \dot{m}_R) \times 0.0005 = 280$$

$$0.042 \dot{m}_R + 4 - 0.0005 \dot{m}_R = 280$$

$$0.0415 \cdot \dot{m}_R = 276$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_R = 6650.6 \; kg/h}$$

$$\dot{m}_{Sk} = 8000 - 6650.6 = 1349.4 \; kg/h$$

**Step 6 — Verification (Bonus 0 mark):**
- Fat in = $6650.6 \times 0.042 + 1349.4 \times 0.0005 = 279.33 + 0.67 = 280.0$ kg/h ✓
- Fat out = $8000 \times 0.035 = 280$ kg/h ✓

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — State Assumptions (1 mark):**
- Steady-state, counter-current flow, no phase change, negligible heat loss to surroundings, constant $C_p$

**Step 2 — Compute Heat Duty (1.5 marks):**

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$$

$$\dot{Q} = 8000 \times 3.9 \times (72 - 6)$$

$$\dot{Q} = 8000 \times 3.9 \times 66 = 2{,}059{,}200 \; kJ/h$$

Convert to W:

$$\dot{Q} = \frac{2{,}059{,}200}{3600} = 571{,}867 \; W \approx 571.9 \; kW$$

**Step 3 — Determine Temperature Differences (1 mark):**
Counter-current:
- $\Delta T_1 = 88 - 72 = 16 \; °C$ (hot water in vs. milk out)
- $\Delta T_2 = 76 - 6 = 70 \; °C$ (hot water out vs. milk in)

**Step 4 — Compute LMTD (1 mark):**

$$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{16 - 70}{\ln(16/70)} = \frac{-54}{\ln(0.2286)} = \frac{-54}{-1.476}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{LMTD} = 36.59 \; °C}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Ideal Area (1 mark):**

$$A_{ideal} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}} = \frac{571{,}867}{5500 \times 36.59}$$

$$A_{ideal} = \frac{571{,}867}{201{,}245} = 2.842 \; m^2$$

**Step 6 — Apply Correction Factor and Select Plates (1 mark):**

$$A_{actual} = \frac{A_{ideal}}{F} = \frac{2.842}{0.92} = 3.089 \; m^2$$

Number of plates (0.25 m² each):

$$N = \frac{3.089}{0.25} = 12.36 \approx 13 \; \text{plates}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 13 \; \text{plates, each } 0.25 \; m^2}$$

**Step 7 — Design Summary (0.5 mark):**
A **13-plate PHE** with counter-current hot water/milk flow delivers **571.9 kW** heat duty, achieving HTST pasteurization from 6 °C to 72 °C.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**A fruit juice concentrate plant processes mango pulp from 14% TS to 28% TS using a triple-effect falling film evaporator. The feed flow rate is 20,000 kg/h at 30 °C. Steam is supplied at 120 °C (saturated) to the first effect. Each effect operates at progressively lower pressure (Effect 1: 101 kPa, Effect 2: 50 kPa, Effect 3: 20 kPa).**

**(a)** Draw a **block diagram of the triple-effect evaporator** showing feed, steam, vapor, and condensate streams. Compute the **total water to be evaporated** and the **theoretical minimum steam consumption** (assume equal water distribution: ~3333 kg/h per effect, and $\lambda_{steam,120°C} \approx 2200 \; kJ/kg$). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **selection criteria for choosing a falling film evaporator over a forced circulation evaporator** for heat-sensitive mango pulp. Include hygienic design considerations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Calculate Initial and Final Masses (2 marks):**

Initial solids:
$$m_s = 20000 \times 0.14 = 2800 \; kg/h$$

Let $m_f$ = final mass at 28% TS:

$$\frac{2800}{m_f} = 0.28 \implies m_f = 10000 \; kg/h$$

**Step 2 — Total Water to be Evaporated (1 mark):**

$$m_{water} = 20000 - 10000 = 10000 \; kg/h$$

**Step 3 — Distribute Water Across Three Effects (1 mark):**
Equal distribution assumption:

$$\dot{m}_{evap,1} = \dot{m}_{evap,2} = \dot{m}_{evap,3} = \frac{10000}{3} \approx 3333 \; kg/h$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Heat Duty per Effect (1 mark):**

$$Q_1 = 3333 \times 2200 = 7{,}333{,}333 \; kJ/h \approx 2037 \; kW$$

**Step 5 — Total Steam Consumption (Theoretical Minimum) (1 mark):**

$$\dot{m}_{steam} = \frac{10000 \times 2200}{2200} = 10000 \; kg/h \; (\text{without vapor bleeding})$$

With vapor bleeding from Effect 1 to Effect 2 to Effect 3 (efficient operation):

$$\dot{m}_{steam,min} \approx \frac{10000}{3} \approx 3333 \; kg/h \; (\text{3-effect savings})$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{steam,\,min} = 3333 \; kg/h}$$

**Steam Economy** = $10000 / 3333 = 3.0 \; kg \; water/kg \; steam$

**Step 6 — Block Diagram (1 mark):**

```
[Feed: 20000 kg/h, 30°C, 14% TS]
        |
        v
   [Pump]
        |
        +--> [Effect 1: 101 kPa] --vapor--> [Effect 2: 50 kPa] --vapor--> [Effect 3: 20 kPa]
        |       |                              |                              |
        |       v                              v                              v
        |    [Concentrate                  [Concentrate                  [Final Product
        |     recycle]                      recycle]                      10000 kg/h, 28% TS]
        |
   [Steam: 120°C saturated, 3333 kg/h]
        |
        v
   [Effect 1: 101 kPa] --vapor to Effect 2--...
```

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Selection Criteria for Falling Film Evaporator for Mango Pulp (7 marks):**

| Criterion | Falling Film Evaporator (FFE) | Forced Circulation Evaporator (FCE) |
|---|---|---|
| **Residence time** | Very short (5–30 seconds) → minimal thermal damage **(1 mark)** | Longer (1–5 minutes) → higher nutrient loss |
| **Product viscosity** | Handles up to 500 cP (suitable for 28% TS mango pulp) **(0.5 mark)** | Better for highly viscous (>1000 cP) |
| **Heat sensitivity** | Ideal for heat-sensitive products (vitamins, color, flavor) **(1 mark)** | Risk of caramelization, Maillard browning |
| **Fouling tendency** | Lower (thin film, high velocity) **(0.5 mark)** | Higher (recirculation concentrates solids at heat surface) |
| **Energy efficiency** | Suitable for multi-effect (3-effect economy = 3.0) **(0.5 mark)** | Similar |
| **Capital cost** | Higher initial investment **(0.5 mark)** | Lower |
| **Hygienic design** | Easy CIP, smooth tubes (Ra ≤ 0.8 μm), SS316L **(1 mark)** | More complex internal geometry |

**Hygienic Design Considerations (1 mark):**
- Tubes must be **SS316L** with internal surface finish **Ra ≤ 0.8 μm**.
- All product-contact welds must be **orbital TIG welds**, ground and polished.
- Top vapor separator must be **self-draining** with slope ≥ 3°.
- Equipment must support **CIP** with 2% NaOH at 80 °C.
- Use **sanitary tri-clamp** connections, **EPDM gaskets**, **no threaded joints**.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Falling film evaporators are the **gold standard** for heat-sensitive fruit juice concentration due to short residence time, low fouling, and excellent hygienic compatibility.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Process & Equipment Design Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state assumptions** (steady-state, no reaction, no heat loss) → **loses 1–2 marks** automatically.
> 2. **Using arithmetic mean temperature difference** instead of LMTD → **loses 1 mark**.
> 3. **Forgetting the correction factor F** in real PHE design → **loses 0.5 mark**.
> 4. **Mixing up wet-basis and dry-basis moisture content** in drying problems → **loses 1 mark**.
> 5. **Not converting units consistently** (e.g., kg/h vs. kg/s) → **loses 0.5 mark** and may lead to a wrong answer by factor of 3600.
> 6. **Confusing PFD with P&ID** in diagrams → **loses 0.5 mark**.
> 7. **Omitting material of construction** (SS304 vs. SS316L) in equipment selection → **loses 0.5 mark**.
> 8. **Not mentioning CIP, hygienic slope, or surface finish** when designing food equipment → **loses 1 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY SUMMARY — Read This the Night Before the Exam**

### Core Concepts to Memorize

- [x] **Mass Balance:** $\sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out}$ (steady-state, no reaction)
- [x] **Energy Balance:** $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ (sensible), $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot \lambda$ (latent)
- [x] **LMTD:** $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$
- [x] **Overall Heat Transfer:** $\dot{Q} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$
- [x] **Mixer Power:** $P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5$
- [x] **Mixing Reynolds:** $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot N \cdot D^2}{\mu}$
- [x] **Scale-up rule (constant P/V):** $\dfrac{P_1}{V_1} = \dfrac{P_2}{V_2}$
- [x] **Moisture conversion:** $X_{db} = \dfrac{X_{wb}}{1 - X_{wb}}$
- [x] **Hagen-Poiseuille:** $\Delta P = \dfrac{32 \mu L v}{D^2}$
- [x] **Darcy-Weisbach:** $\Delta P = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$

### Hygiene & Equipment Standards

- [x] Surface finish: **Ra ≤ 0.8 μm**
- [x] Self-draining slope: **≥ 3°**
- [x] Material: **SS316L for acidic/salty foods**
- [x] Joints: **Tri-clamp sanitary fittings**
- [x] Bend radius: **≥ 1.5 × pipe diameter**
- [x] CIP sequence: **Pre-rinse → Caustic → Rinse → Acid → Final rinse → Sanitize**

### Equipment by Application

- [x] **Milk pasteurization** → Plate Heat Exchanger (HTST: 72 °C × 15 s)
- [x] **Heat-sensitive juice** → Falling Film Evaporator (short residence time)
- [x] **High-viscosity products** → Positive Displacement Pump + Helical Mixer
- [x] **Particulate foods** → Progressive Cavity Pump + Paddle Mixer
- [x] **Spray drying** → Atomizer + Cyclone Separator
- [x] **Fermentation** → Stirred Tank Reactor (STR) with CIP

### Common Exam Question Types

- [x] Mixing/dilution problems (mass balance + Brix calculation)
- [x] Heat exchanger sizing (LMTD + area + plate count)
- [x] Evaporator water removal and steam economy
- [x] Scale-up of mixers (constant P/V rule)
- [x] Selection of equipment based on product properties
- [x] PFD and CIP loop diagrams

### Units to Watch (Conversion Pitfalls)

- [x] **kg/h → kg/s:** Divide by **3600**
- [x] **kJ/h → kW:** Divide by **3600**
- [x] **°C difference = K difference** (only for differences, not absolute)
- [x] **1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Strategy Tip:** Always begin your answer with **(1) the givens, (2) the assumptions, (3) the governing equation, (4) the substitution, and (5) the boxed final answer**. This 5-step structure is the universal template that KTU examiners reward with full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Manufacturing Protocol, Establishing Process Parameters for Optimum Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT834 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Manufacturing Protocol, Establishing Process Parameters for Optimum Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Manufacturing Protocol** is a comprehensive, validated, and documented set of Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) that precisely defines every unit operation, raw material specification, equipment setting, in-process check, and quality attribute required to consistently reproduce a food product at industrial scale.

**Process Parameters** are the independent, controllable variables (temperature, time, pH, moisture, shear, pressure, ingredient concentration, etc.) that govern a unit operation. When these are deliberately set, monitored, and statistically validated to deliver maximum **Optimum Quality** — the simultaneous satisfaction of sensory, nutritional, microbiological, functional, and shelf-life specifications at minimum cost — the procedure is called **Establishing Process Parameters for Optimum Quality**.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, this topic bridges **Product Design (Module 1–2)** with **Scale-up and Commercialization (Module 4)**. It is the engineering "bridge" that converts a bench-top recipe into a bankable industrial process.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — Brewing the Perfect Cup of Filter Coffee** ☕
>
> Imagine you have invented a new coffee blend in your kitchen. To sell 10,000 cups a day in a factory, you cannot just say "boil, filter, serve." You must write a **Manufacturing Protocol** — a recipe-card that specifies:
> - **Raw material specs** (coffee particle size = **600 µm**, water TDS = **< 50 ppm**)
> - **Equipment** (piston espresso machine, 9 bar)
> - **Process parameters** (water temperature = **92 ± 1 °C**, brew time = **27 ± 2 s**, dose = **18 g**)
> - **Quality check** (cupping score ≥ **80/100**, crema thickness ≥ **3 mm**)
>
> The **Process Parameters** are the *dials* you turn; **Optimum Quality** is the *taste on the palate*. **Establishing Parameters** is the disciplined, statistically designed trial-and-error of turning those dials until the perfect taste is locked in, then sealing them inside an SOP.

## 1.3 Key Vocabulary for KTU Board Examinations

| Term | One-Line Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **CPP** | Critical Process Parameter — a variable whose deviation impacts safety/quality and *must* be controlled. |
| **CQA** | Critical Quality Attribute — a measurable product property (e.g., moisture, texture, colour) linked to consumer acceptance. |
| **DoE** | Design of Experiments — statistically structured trials to study parameter effects. |
| **RSM** | Response Surface Methodology — a DoE technique that builds a mathematical model and finds the optimum. |
| **OFAT** | One-Factor-At-A-Time — the *anti-pattern* of changing only one variable, which is replaced by DoE. |
| **Cpk** | Process Capability Index — quantifies how well a parameter stays inside specification limits. |
| **Validation** | Documented evidence that the process *consistently* produces a product meeting pre-defined specs. |
| **Verification** | Periodic re-confirmation that the validated process is still under control. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Trigger Words** to memorize: "CPP", "CQA", "DoE", "RSM", "Validation", "Control limits", "Specifications". Using these terms in the right context scores extra marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Pillars of a Manufacturing Protocol (KGU/Engineering Logic)

A robust protocol is built on **six sequential pillars**. Each pillar is mandatory for a 14-mark KTU answer.

1. **Raw Material & Ingredient Specification** — identity, purity, supplier, microbial load, particle size, water activity (a<sub>w</sub>).
2. **Equipment & Facility Description** — capacity, material of construction (SS-304/316), CIP/SIP capability.
3. **Process Flow & Unit Operations** — sequenced block diagram from receiving → processing → packaging → storage.
4. **Process Parameters (CPPs) and Their Limits** — for *each* unit operation, with target ± tolerance.
5. **In-Process Controls (IPCs) & Finished Product Specifications** — sampling plan, frequency, and acceptance criteria.
6. **Documentation, Traceability & Corrective Actions** — batch records, lot coding, deviation handling, recall mechanism.

## 2.2 Classification of Process Parameters

Process parameters are classified on a **risk-and-impact pyramid**:

| Tier | Type | Example in Baking | Effect on Quality |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1 (Top)** | **Critical** (CPP) | Oven temperature = **180 ± 2 °C** | Direct safety/legal/sensory impact |
| **2** | **Major** | Dough hydration = **60 ± 1 %** | Significant quality impact |
| **3** | **Minor** | Mixing bowl RPM = **120 ± 10** | Minor, compensated downstream |

## 2.3 Why the "Why" Matters (Engineering Rationale)

- **Why classify?** — Resources (sensors, time, money) are limited. *All* parameters cannot be tightly controlled; the CPPs are the ones to invest in.
- **Why use DoE/RSM?** — Variables interact. Raising temperature *and* time simultaneously may over-bake, even though each alone is safe. OFAT misses this; RSM captures it via **interaction terms**.
- **Why Cpk ≥ 1.33?** — It guarantees less than **63 ppm** defects — the industry benchmark for food quality.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table verbatim. It is the single most-tested mathematical content of Module 3.

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Name / Meaning | Typical Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $Y = \beta_{0} + \sum_{i=1}^{k}\beta_{i}x_{i} + \sum_{i=1}^{k}\beta_{ii}x_{i}^{2} + \sum_{i<j}\beta_{ij}x_{i}x_{j}$ | Second-order RSM polynomial | dimensionless (coded) |
| 2 | $\dfrac{\partial Y}{\partial x_{1}} = \beta_{1} + 2\beta_{11}x_{1} + \beta_{12}x_{2} = 0$ | Stationary-point condition (∂Y/∂x = 0) | — |
| 3 | $x_{1}^{*} = \dfrac{2\beta_{22}\beta_{1} - \beta_{2}\beta_{12}}{\beta_{12}^{2} - 4\beta_{11}\beta_{22}}$ | Optimum coordinate of factor 1 | coded units |
| 4 | $C_{p} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Process Capability (spread only) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left[\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\;\dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right]$ | Process Capability (centred) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}\right) = -\dfrac{E_{a}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{2}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{1}}\right)$ | Arrhenius shelf-life acceleration | dimensionless |
| 7 | $Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}\right)^{\!\tfrac{10}{T_{2}-T_{1}}}$ | Temperature acceleration factor (often ≈ **2**) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{SS}_{\text{Total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_{i} - \bar{y})^{2}$ | Total Sum of Squares (ANOVA) | (response)² |
| 9 | $R^{2} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{SS}_{\text{Res}}}{\text{SS}_{\text{Total}}}$ | Coefficient of determination (R² ≥ 0.80) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{Yield}\;\eta = \dfrac{\text{Useful product mass}}{\text{Input raw mass}} \times 100$ | Process yield (mass balance) | **%** |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE**: In the table above, absolute-value bars are written as `\vert` inside LaTeX so the markdown pipe `|` does not break the column structure. Copy this trick in your exam script.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of the System

- **Dairy Industry**: Optimizing pasteurization (72 °C × 15 s) and UHT (140 °C × 4 s) using RSM balances *safety* (log-reduction of *Listeria*) against *quality* (vitamin retention, cooked flavour).
- **Snack Extrusion**: RSM optimizes barrel temperature, screw speed, and feed moisture to control **expansion ratio** and **crispness**.
- **Baking**: Time–Temperature–Humidity (TTH) surfaces are RSM-mapped to lock in a **golden-brown crust** and **moist crumb** simultaneously.
- **Beverage Clarification**: Centrifugation R²-speed, time, and temperature are tuned to achieve **clarity ≥ 95 % T** at **600 nm**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stationary Point of a Second-Order RSM Model (Two-Factor Case)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the **most-tested 14-mark derivation** in Module 3. Learn every line.

### Step 1 — Write the second-order model for *k = 2* factors

For two independent process parameters (e.g., temperature $x_{1}$ and time $x_{2}$), the second-order RSM polynomial reduces to:

$$
Y = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1}x_{1} + \beta_{2}x_{2} + \beta_{11}x_{1}^{2} + \beta_{22}x_{2}^{2} + \beta_{12}x_{1}x_{2}
$$

**Conversion logic:** $Y$ is the predicted response (e.g., sensory score); $\beta_{0}$ is the intercept; $\beta_{1}, \beta_{2}$ capture **linear** effects; $\beta_{11}, \beta_{22}$ capture **quadratic (curvature)** effects; $\beta_{12}$ captures the **interaction** between $x_{1}$ and $x_{2}$.

### Step 2 — Apply the Stationary-Point Condition

At the optimum (maximum or minimum of $Y$), the partial derivatives vanish:

$$
\frac{\partial Y}{\partial x_{1}} = \beta_{1} + 2\beta_{11}x_{1} + \beta_{12}x_{2} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial Y}{\partial x_{2}} = \beta_{2} + 2\beta_{22}x_{2} + \beta_{12}x_{1} = 0
$$

**Conversion logic:** A *flat* surface has zero slope in every direction; this is the definition of a stationary (extrema or saddle) point.

### Step 3 — Express the System in Matrix Form

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} 2\beta_{11} & \beta_{12} \\ \beta_{12} & 2\beta_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_{1} \\ x_{2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -\beta_{1} \\ -\beta_{2} \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** A set of two linear equations in two unknowns is most cleanly written in matrix notation, where the Hessian matrix $\mathbf{H}$ is the $2 \times 2$ matrix on the left.

### Step 4 — Solve Using Cramer's Rule

The determinant of the Hessian is:

$$
D = (2\beta_{11})(2\beta_{22}) - (\beta_{12})^{2} = 4\beta_{11}\beta_{22} - \beta_{12}^{2}
$$

By Cramer's rule:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{1}^{*} = \frac{\begin{vmatrix} -\beta_{1} & \beta_{12} \\ -\beta_{2} & 2\beta_{22} \end{vmatrix}}{D} = \frac{-2\beta_{22}\beta_{1} + \beta_{12}\beta_{2}}{4\beta_{11}\beta_{22} - \beta_{12}^{2}} = \frac{2\beta_{22}\beta_{1} - \beta_{2}\beta_{12}}{\beta_{12}^{2} - 4\beta_{11}\beta_{22}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{2}^{*} = \frac{\begin{vmatrix} 2\beta_{11} & -\beta_{1} \\ \beta_{12} & -\beta_{2} \end{vmatrix}}{D} = \frac{-2\beta_{11}\beta_{2} + \beta_{1}\beta_{12}}{4\beta_{11}\beta_{22} - \beta_{12}^{2}} = \frac{2\beta_{11}\beta_{2} - \beta_{1}\beta_{12}}{\beta_{12}^{2} - 4\beta_{11}\beta_{22}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Cramer's rule replaces a column of the Hessian by the right-hand-side vector $-\boldsymbol{\beta}$ and divides by $D$. The signs flip because the original equation was $\mathbf{H}\mathbf{x} = -\boldsymbol{\beta}$.

### Step 5 — Classify the Stationary Point (Second-Derivative Test)

Inspect the eigenvalues of the Hessian. Equivalently, for the $2 \times 2$ case, evaluate:

- If $\beta_{11} < 0$ **and** $\beta_{22} < 0$ **and** $D > 0$ → **Maximum** (typical for quality optimization — quality peaks then declines).
- If $\beta_{11} > 0$ **and** $\beta_{22} > 0$ **and** $D > 0$ → **Minimum** (typical for cost or defect optimization).
- If $D < 0$ → **Saddle Point** (no practical optimum).

**Conversion logic:** The sign of curvature (the second derivative) determines whether we are on top of a hill, in a valley, or on a saddle. Negative curvature in every direction = a maximum.

### Step 6 — Canonical Form (for completeness)

Rotate the coordinate system by an angle $\theta$ given by:

$$
\tan(2\theta) = \frac{\beta_{12}}{\beta_{11} - \beta_{22}}
$$

This yields the **canonical form** $Y = Y_{s} + \lambda_{1}w_{1}^{2} + \lambda_{2}w_{2}^{2}$, which makes the nature of the stationary point obvious.

**Conversion logic:** A 2-D quadratic surface is geometrically a *paraboloid* or *hyperboloid*; rotating the axes aligns the surface with its principal axes.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Process Capability Index $C_{pk}$

A parameter is **in statistical control** with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$. The spec window is **[LSL, USL]**.

- The natural process spread is $\mu \pm 3\sigma$, i.e., a width of $6\sigma$.
- The spec width is $\text{USL} - \text{LSL}$.

**One-sided capability** (above LSL only): $C_{pl} = \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}$

**One-sided capability** (below USL only): $C_{pu} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}$

**Combined capability** (the worst of the two governs): $C_{pk} = \min(C_{pl}, C_{cu}})$

$$
C_{pk} = \min\!\left[\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right]
$$

**Conversion logic:** A process can drift off-centre even if its spread is fine. $C_{p}$ ignores centring; $C_{pk}$ penalises drift. The KTU benchmark $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ corresponds to ≤ 63 defects per million opportunities (DPMO).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic for Optimum Quality

| Stage | Question to Ask | Statistical Tool | Pass Criterion |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. Screening | Which 5–6 parameters matter most? | **Plackett–Burman / Fractional Factorial** | $p < 0.05$ for the main effects |
| 2. Modelling | What is the mathematical relationship? | **Full Factorial / CCD / Box–Behnken** | $R^{2} \geq 0.80$, $R^{2}_{\text{adj}} \geq 0.70$ |
| 3. Optimisation | Where is the optimum? | **RSM** (∂Y/∂x = 0, Stationary Point) | Desirability $\mathcal{D} \geq 0.8$ |
| 4. Confirmation | Do predicted optima hold experimentally? | **Triplicate confirmatory runs** | $\vert Y_{\text{pred}} - Y_{\text{obs}} \vert \leq 5\,\%$ |
| 5. Validation | Does the process hold at production scale? | **Cpk / Control charts** | $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for viva**: *S-M-O-C-V = "Samosa, Masala, Onion, Chutney, Vinegar"* — Screening, Modelling, Optimisation, Confirmation, Validation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Manufacturing Protocol Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Raw Material Specification] --> B1[Equipment Selection]
    B1 --> C1[Unit Operation Sequence]
    C1 --> D1[Process Parameter Identification]
    D1 --> E1[Design of Experiments]
    E1 --> F1[RSM Modeling]
    F1 --> G1[Statistical Validation]
    G1 --> H1[Optimum Parameter Lock]
    H1 --> I1[Pilot Scale Trials]
    I1 --> J1[Industrial Scale Validation]
    J1 --> K1[SOP Documentation]
    K1 --> L1[Continuous Improvement]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: Each block is a *gate*. A new product cannot pass from `D1` to `E1` without a parameter listing; cannot pass from `G1` to `H1` without a statistically significant model ($p < 0.05$, $R^{2} \geq 0.80$).

## 4.2 Block Diagram: RSM Optimisation Sub-System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[Factor Selection] --> B2[Experimental Design CCD or BBD]
    B2 --> C2[Run Experiments in Random Order]
    C2 --> D2[Fit Second Order Model]
    D2 --> E2[ANOVA and R2 Check]
    E2 --> F2{Model Adequate}
    F2 -- No --> B2
    F2 -- Yes --> G2[Generate Response Surface]
    G2 --> H2[Solve Stationary Point]
    H2 --> I2[Canonical Analysis]
    I2 --> J2[Confirmatory Trials]
    J2 --> K2[Lock Optimum]
```

## 4.3 Process-Parameter Decision Tree (Risk-Based Selection)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Identify all parameters] --> P2{Does deviation affect safety?}
    P2 -- Yes --> P3[Mark as Critical CPP - tight control]
    P2 -- No --> P4{Does deviation affect sensory quality?}
    P4 -- Yes --> P5[Mark as Major - statistical control]
    P4 -- No --> P6[Mark as Minor - monitor only]
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Quality Attribute vs Process Parameter Relationship (Cause-Effect Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Moisture Content] --- R1[Oven Temperature]
    Q1 --- R2[Drying Time]
    Q1 --- R3[Air Velocity]
    Q2[Texture Crispness] --- R4[Baking Time]
    Q2 --- R5[Initial Moisture]
    Q3[Color L Value] --- R6[Oven Temperature]
    Q3 --- R7[pH of Dough]
    R1 --> Q1
    R2 --> Q1
    R3 --> Q1
    R4 --> Q2
    R5 --> Q2
    R6 --> Q3
    R7 --> Q3
```

> [!TIP]
> The **double-edges** (---) represent *influences*; the **single-arrows** (-->) represent *causal direction*. In a viva, point to a specific edge and explain the chemistry: e.g., higher oven temperature → Maillard reaction → lower L\* value → darker crust.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

### Question 1. Define *manufacturing protocol*. List its **six essential components**.
**[3 Marks — 2024 Scheme, CO3, Bloom: Remember/Understand]**

**Model Answer:**

A **manufacturing protocol** is a validated, written set of instructions that defines every step, parameter, and quality check required to consistently produce a food product at industrial scale.

Its six essential components are:

1. **Raw material and ingredient specifications** (identity, purity, supplier).
2. **Equipment and facility description** (capacity, material of construction, CIP/SIP).
3. **Process flow and unit operations** (sequenced block diagram).
4. **Process parameters with critical limits** (target ± tolerance for each).
5. **In-process controls and finished product specifications** (sampling, frequency, criteria).
6. **Documentation, traceability, and corrective action** (batch records, lot coding, recall).

---

### Question 2. What is **Response Surface Methodology (RSM)**? State its **two main advantages** over the One-Factor-At-A-Time (OFAT) approach.
**[3 Marks — 2024 Scheme, CO3, Bloom: Understand]**

**Model Answer:**

**RSM** is a collection of mathematical and statistical techniques useful for developing, improving, and optimizing processes in which a *response of interest* is influenced by several independent variables. The objective is to **simultaneously** locate the optimum operating conditions.

**Two advantages over OFAT:**

1. **Captures interaction effects** between parameters (e.g., temperature × time), which OFAT completely misses.
2. **Reduces the number of experiments**, saving time, raw materials, and money while providing a full mathematical model for prediction.

*(Optional third point for extra credit: Generates 3-D response surfaces that visually guide the formulator to the optimum.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — ESE Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> Students frequently lose 2–3 marks by (a) confusing **Validation** with **Verification**, (b) omitting the *mathematical* stationary-point derivation in RSM questions, and (c) forgetting to state the **Cpk ≥ 1.33** benchmark. Read the model answers carefully to avoid these traps.

---

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) — *Option 1*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model, CO3, Bloom: Apply/Analyse]**

#### (a) Discuss, with a suitable flow diagram, the **step-by-step procedure for establishing a manufacturing protocol** for a newly developed ready-to-drink (RTD) fruit beverage. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The step-by-step procedure is as follows:

**Step 1 — Define the Product & Quality Target**
Finalize the **CQA** (Critical Quality Attributes): Brix = 12 ± 0.5 °, pH = 3.5 ± 0.2, TSS = 11.5 %, colour L\* = 35 ± 2, microbial count < 10 CFU/mL, shelf-life = 6 months at ambient.

**Step 2 — Raw Material Specifications**
- Fruit pulp: TSS 10 °Brix, acidity 0.8 %, pesticide residue below FSSAI limits.
- Sugar, acidulants, water (TDS < 50 ppm), permitted preservatives.

**Step 3 — Identify Unit Operations**
Reception → Pulping → Filtration → Standardization → Pasteurization (CPP) → Cooling → Aseptic filling → Storage.

**Step 4 — Identify Critical Process Parameters (CPPs)**
- Pasteurization: temperature 90 ± 1 °C, holding time 30 ± 2 s.
- Brix adjustment: 12 ± 0.5 °Brix.
- pH adjustment: 3.5 ± 0.2.

**Step 5 — Design of Experiments (DoE)**
A Central Composite Design (CCD) with three factors (Temperature, Time, Brix) at five levels is run. Responses (colour, microbial count, sensory score) are recorded.

**Step 6 — Model Fitting & RSM**
Fit a second-order polynomial. Validate with ANOVA ($p < 0.05$, $R^{2} \geq 0.90$).

**Step 7 — Optimisation & Confirmation**
Solve ∂Y/∂x = 0 to find the stationary point. Run triplicate confirmatory trials.

**Step 8 — Scale-up & Validation**
Pilot (50 L) → Plant (5 000 L) batches. Compute $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ for each CPP. Lock the SOP.

**Step 9 — Documentation & Continuous Improvement**
Prepare batch manufacturing records; institute HACCP and a corrective-action system.

#### (b) Explain the **classification of process parameters** with examples. How is the **Process Capability Index (Cpk)** used to judge whether the process is *capable*? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Classification of process parameters:**

Process parameters are divided into three tiers based on their impact on product quality and safety:

| Tier | Class | Example (Biscuit) | Consequence of Deviation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1** | **Critical (CPP)** | Oven temperature = 180 ± 2 °C | Safety / legality / major quality |
| **2** | **Major** | Dough moisture = 14 ± 1 % | Significant quality impact |
| **3** | **Minor** | Conveyer belt speed = 5 ± 0.5 m/min | Compensated downstream |

**Process Capability Index (Cpk):**

The **Cpk** quantifies how well the *centred* process fits inside the specification window:

$$
C_{pk} = \min\!\left[\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right]
$$

**Decision rule (industry & KTU benchmark):**

| Cpk Value | Interpretation | Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $C_{pk} < 1.00$ | Process is **not capable** | Stop production, investigate |
| $1.00 \leq C_{pk} < 1.33$ | **Marginally capable** | Tighten control, re-train |
| $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ | **Capable** (≤ 63 ppm defects) | Acceptable for food quality |
| $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ | **Highly capable** (six-sigma) | World-class benchmark |

**Conversion logic:** $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ corresponds to less than 63 defects per million opportunities — a level statistically safe for food products where even tiny microbial or allergen slips are unacceptable.

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — *Option 2 (Internal Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model, CO3, Bloom: Apply/Analyse/Evaluate]**

#### (a) Explain the **mathematical basis of Response Surface Methodology (RSM)** with a labelled second-order polynomial model for *k = 2* factors. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Mathematical basis:**

RSM uses a low-order polynomial — typically second order — to approximate the *true* functional relationship between the independent variables $\mathbf{x} = (x_{1}, x_{2}, \dots, x_{k})$ and the response $Y$ within a *region of operability*.

For $k$ factors, the **second-order model** is:

$$
Y = \beta_{0} + \sum_{i=1}^{k}\beta_{i}x_{i} + \sum_{i=1}^{k}\beta_{ii}x_{i}^{2} + \sum_{i<j}\beta_{ij}x_{i}x_{j} + \varepsilon
$$

For *k = 2* factors (say $x_{1}$ = temperature, $x_{2}$ = time), the polynomial reduces to:

$$
Y = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1}x_{1} + \beta_{2}x_{2} + \beta_{11}x_{1}^{2} + \beta_{22}x_{2}^{2} + \beta_{12}x_{1}x_{2} + \varepsilon
$$

**Meaning of each term:**

| Coefficient | Geometric Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $\beta_{0}$ | Intercept (response at centre point) |
| $\beta_{1}, \beta_{2}$ | **Linear** effects — slope along each axis |
| $\beta_{11}, \beta_{22}$ | **Pure quadratic** effects — curvature |
| $\beta_{12}$ | **Two-factor interaction** effect (cross-product) |
| $\varepsilon$ | Random experimental error, $\varepsilon \sim N(0, \sigma^{2})$ |

**Why second order?** — It is the *lowest-order* polynomial that can capture *curvature* (essential for finding an interior optimum) and *interaction* (essential when one variable's effect depends on another's level).

**Conversion logic:** First-order models yield flat planes — they cannot produce a maximum or minimum. Adding squared terms makes the surface *bowed*, allowing the calculus of stationary points to find the optimum.

#### (b) Derive the **equations for the stationary point** of the two-factor RSM model. Discuss **how the nature of the stationary point is determined** using the canonical form. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation:**

Starting from the two-factor second-order model:

$$
Y = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1}x_{1} + \beta_{2}x_{2} + \beta_{11}x_{1}^{2} + \beta_{22}x_{2}^{2} + \beta_{12}x_{1}x_{2}
$$

Set the partial derivatives to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial Y}{\partial x_{1}} = \beta_{1} + 2\beta_{11}x_{1} + \beta_{12}x_{2} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial Y}{\partial x_{2}} = \beta_{2} + 2\beta_{22}x_{2} + \beta_{12}x_{1} = 0
$$

Write in matrix form (as derived in Section 3.1):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} 2\beta_{11} & \beta_{12} \\ \beta_{12} & 2\beta_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_{1} \\ x_{2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -\beta_{1} \\ -\beta_{2} \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying Cramer's rule with $D = 4\beta_{11}\beta_{22} - \beta_{12}^{2}$:

$$
x_{1}^{*} = \frac{2\beta_{22}\beta_{1} - \beta_{2}\beta_{12}}{D}
$$

$$
x_{2}^{*} = \frac{2\beta_{11}\beta_{2} - \beta_{1}\beta_{12}}{D}
$$

**Nature of the stationary point — Canonical Analysis:**

Rotate the $(x_{1}, x_{2})$ axes by an angle $\theta$:

$$
\tan(2\theta) = \frac{\beta_{12}}{\beta_{11} - \beta_{22}}
$$

The model becomes:

$$
Y = Y_{s} + \lambda_{1}w_{1}^{2} + \lambda_{2}w_{2}^{2}
$$

where $\lambda_{1}, \lambda_{2}$ are the **eigenvalues** of the Hessian matrix.

| Signs of $\lambda_{1}, \lambda_{2}$ | Stationary-Point Type | Practical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Both **negative** | **Maximum** | Quality peaks at this point (typical) |
| Both **positive** | **Minimum** | Cost / defect minimisation |
| **Opposite signs** | **Saddle point** | No practical optimum — redesign region |

**Conversion logic:** Eigenvalues are the principal curvatures of the surface. If every curvature bends *downward*, the surface is dome-shaped — a maximum. The KTU question often asks only the *sign rule*, not the rotation angle.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density revision checklist — read once, retain forever.**

- **Manufacturing Protocol** = written, validated, *complete* recipe for industrial production.
- **Six pillars**: Raw specs, Equipment, Process flow, CPPs, IPCs, Documentation.
- **Process Parameters** are independent variables; **Quality Attributes** are dependent responses.
- **Parameters are tiered**: Critical > Major > Minor — based on *risk to safety and quality*.
- **OFAT is obsolete**; **DoE + RSM** is the KTU-recommended modern approach.
- **RSM model** = second-order polynomial with linear, quadratic, and interaction terms.
- **Stationary point** = where all $\partial Y / \partial x_{i} = 0$; solve via matrix inversion or Cramer's rule.
- **Canonical form** decides max / min / saddle via **eigenvalue signs**.
- **Process Capability** = $C_{p}$ (spread) and $C_{pk}$ (spread *and* centring).
- **KTU benchmark** = $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ for an acceptable food process.
- **Validation ≠ Verification**: Validation is one-time proof; verification is ongoing re-confirmation.
- **ANOVA must pass**: $p < 0.05$ and $R^{2} \geq 0.80$ before any model is trusted.
- **Confirm the optimum** with triplicate runs: $\vert Y_{\text{pred}} - Y_{\text{obs}} \vert \leq 5\,\%$.
- **HACCP integration** is mandatory once the protocol is locked.
- **Documentation** is the *legal* backbone — every deviation must be recorded with corrective action.

> **End of Notes — Module 3, Topic: Manufacturing Protocol, Establishing Process Parameters for Optimum Quality.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensory Evaluation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT834 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sensory Evaluation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Sensory Evaluation** is a scientific discipline used to evoke, measure, analyze, and interpret human responses to food products as perceived through the five senses — **sight (vision), smell (olfaction), taste (gustation), touch (somesthesis/tactile), and sound (audition)**. It is the most direct and authentic method of measuring consumer acceptability and product quality, since humans are the ultimate consumers of food.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-aligned):**
> *Sensory Evaluation is a scientific method employed to evaluate the quality of a food product using human senses, employing controlled test conditions, statistical design, and trained/untrained panels to generate reproducible data about a product's sensory attributes and consumer acceptability.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are at a coffee shop trying two different cappuccinos. You take a sip of each and instinctively say, "This one tastes richer and smoother." That instinctive human judgment — when **standardized, controlled, and statistically analyzed** — becomes **Sensory Evaluation**.

Think of it like this:
- A **spectrometer** measures light absorption → that is *instrumental* analysis.
- A **human panel** measures flavor perception → that is *sensory* analysis.
- Both produce **quantitative data**, but sensory evaluation captures what no machine can fully replicate — **the holistic human experience**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Human Sensory Panel is the "Instrument":** Unlike a pH meter or colorimeter, the sensory panel must be carefully selected, trained, calibrated, and maintained — because the panel itself is the measurement device.

### 1.3 The Five Senses Used in Sensory Evaluation

| Sense | Stimulus Type | Examples in Food |
|-------|--------------|------------------|
| **Vision (Sight)** | Light waves | Color, size, shape, glossiness, surface texture |
| **Olfaction (Smell)** | Volatile compounds | Aroma, fragrance, off-odors |
| **Gustation (Taste)** | Chemical (water-soluble) | Sweet, sour, salty, bitter, umami |
| **Tactile/Texture** | Physical/mechanical | Crispness, creaminess, chewiness, hardness |
| **Audition (Sound)** | Vibrations | Crunchiness, sizzle, fizz, crackle |

### 1.4 Why Sensory Evaluation is Critical

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Sensory evaluation bridges the gap between **objective instrumental measurements** and **subjective consumer preferences**. A product may have perfect pH, color, and texture — yet fail in the marketplace if it doesn't taste acceptable.

Key reasons:
1. **Consumer Acceptance:** No instrument can fully substitute human perception.
2. **Quality Control:** Detects subtle changes missed by instruments.
3. **Product Development:** Guides formulation decisions.
4. **Shelf-life Studies:** Tracks sensory changes during storage.
5. **Cost Optimization:** Validates ingredient substitutions.
6. **Marketing Claims:** Validates sensory descriptors on labels.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Sensory Tests (KTU High-Yield Framework)

Sensory tests are broadly classified into **three major categories**, and this classification is a **favourite KTU exam question**.

#### 2.1.1 Analytical / Discriminative Tests

**Purpose:** Determine whether a perceptible difference exists between two or more products.

**Examples:**
- Triangle Test
- Duo-Trio Test
- Paired Comparison (Directional)
- 2-AFC (Two-Alternative Forced Choice)
- 3-AFC (Three-Alternative Forced Choice)

**Panel:** Trained or semi-trained (typically **20–50 panelists**).

#### 2.1.2 Descriptive Tests

**Purpose:** Identify and quantify specific sensory attributes and their intensities.

**Examples:**
- Flavor Profile Method (FPM)
- Texture Profile Method (TPM)
- Quantitative Descriptive Analysis (QDA™)
- Spectrum™ Descriptive Analysis
- Free-Choice Profiling

**Panel:** Highly trained (typically **8–12 panelists**, often >100 hours training).

#### 2.1.3 Affective / Hedonic Tests (Consumer Tests)

**Purpose:** Measure preference, liking, or acceptance among consumers.

**Examples:**
- Hedonic Rating (9-point scale)
- Paired Preference Test
- Preference Ranking
- Just-About-Right (JAR) Scale
- Acceptance Test

**Panel:** Untrained consumers (typically **75–150+ panelists**, representing target demographic).

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Sensory Test Selection

The following decision logic is critical for KTU product design questions:

1. **Step 1 — Define the objective:** Are you looking for a difference, describing attributes, or measuring acceptance?
2. **Step 2 — Select test category:** Difference → Discriminative; Description → Descriptive; Acceptance → Affective.
3. **Step 3 — Select panel:** Trained, semi-trained, or consumer panel.
4. **Step 4 — Choose test method:** Specific protocol (triangle, ranking, QDA, etc.).
5. **Step 5 — Conduct under controlled conditions:** Standardized booth, lighting, temperature.
6. **Step 6 — Statistical analysis:** ANOVA, chi-square, t-test, Mann-Whitney.
7. **Step 7 — Interpret and conclude.**

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Test / Parameter | Formula / Critical Value | Description |
|------------------|--------------------------|-------------|
| **Triangle Test — Critical Number** | $n_c$ from table (Roessler table) | Minimum correct responses for significance at $\alpha = 0.05$ or $\alpha = 0.01$ |
| **Chi-Square Statistic** | $\chi^2 = \sum \frac{(O - E)^2}{E}$ | Used for binary sensory responses |
| **Duo-Trio — Critical Number** | $n_c$ from Roessler table | Same logic as triangle |
| **Paired Preference — Binomial** | $P(X \geq x \mid n, 0.5) = \sum_{k=x}^{n} \binom{n}{k}(0.5)^n$ | Used when no ties allowed |
| **Hedonic Scale Mean** | $\bar{X} = \frac{\sum X_i}{n}$ | Average liking score |
| **Standard Deviation of Liking** | $s = \sqrt{\frac{\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2}{n-1}}$ | Variability in consumer liking |
| **Coefficient of Variation** | $CV = \frac{s}{\bar{X}} \times 100\%$ | Relative precision |
| **Power of Test (n)** | $n = \frac{(Z_{\alpha} + Z_{\beta})^2 \cdot 2 \sigma^2}{\Delta^2}$ | Sample size for specified power |
| **Kappa Coefficient** | $\kappa = \frac{P_o - P_e}{1 - P_e}$ | Panel agreement measure |
| **Significance Level (KTU standard)** | $\alpha = 0.05$ | Default unless otherwise stated |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Numbers** for triangle tests are tabulated by Roessler et al. For example, with **30 panelists** and triangle test at $\alpha = 0.05$, you need a minimum of **12 correct responses** to declare a significant difference. This is a **frequent KTU numerical question**.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

Sensory evaluation is indispensable in:
- **New Product Development (NPD):** Validating prototypes.
- **Quality Assurance:** Detecting batch-to-batch variation.
- **Shelf-life Studies:** Tracking flavor/texture deterioration.
- **Competitive Benchmarking:** Comparing products against market leaders.
- **Ingredient Substitution:** Validating cost-saving replacements.
- **Cost Reduction Programs:** Reformulating without losing acceptance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Industry Linkage:** Companies like Nestlé, Mondelez, and ITC use descriptive analysis to maintain brand consistency across global production facilities — the trained panel acts as a "human quality sensor."
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Triangle Test Critical Number

The triangle test presents three coded samples — **two identical and one different** — and asks the panelist to identify the odd sample. We must determine the minimum number of correct responses needed to conclude a significant difference at a given significance level.

**Step 1 — Set up the null hypothesis.**

Under the null hypothesis $H_0$, there is **no difference** between samples. Each panelist has a **1/3 probability** of guessing correctly by chance.

$$P(\text{correct by chance}) = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.3333$$

**Step 2 — Binomial distribution under $H_0$.**

The number of correct responses $X$ follows a binomial distribution:

$$X \sim B(n, p) \quad \text{where} \quad p = \frac{1}{3}, \quad n = \text{number of panelists}$$

**Step 3 — Probability mass function.**

The probability of exactly $k$ correct responses out of $n$ panelists is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X = k \mid n, p) &= \binom{n}{k} p^k (1-p)^{n-k} \\
&= \binom{n}{k} \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^k \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{n-k}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Cumulative probability for decision rule.**

We reject $H_0$ if the number of correct responses $x$ exceeds a critical value $c$. The critical number $n_c$ is the smallest integer such that:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X \geq c \mid n, p = 1/3) &\leq \alpha \\
\sum_{k=c}^{n} \binom{n}{k} \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^k \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{n-k} &\leq \alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Example computation for $n = 30$ and $\alpha = 0.05$.**

For $n = 30$, $p = 1/3$, the cumulative probability from $k = 12$ onwards:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X \geq 12) &= \sum_{k=12}^{30} \binom{30}{k} \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^k \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{30-k} \\
&\approx 0.0431 \quad (\text{below } 0.05, \text{ so } 12 \text{ is significant}) \\
P(X \geq 11) &\approx 0.0849 \quad (\text{above } 0.05, \text{ so } 11 \text{ is NOT significant})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** For $n = 30$ panelists at $\alpha = 0.05$, the critical number is **$n_c = 12$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Step-by-Step Style:** Always show (i) hypothesis, (ii) probability under $H_0$, (iii) cumulative computation, (iv) critical number, (v) decision. This 5-step format consistently scores full marks.

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Chi-Square for Paired Preference Test

In a paired preference test, two samples (A and B) are presented, and each panelist selects the one they prefer. We test whether there is a significant preference.

**Step 1 — Hypotheses.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_0 &: P(A) = P(B) = 0.5 \quad \text{(no preference)} \\
H_1 &: P(A) \neq P(B) \quad \text{(preference exists)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Expected frequencies under $H_0$.**

If $n$ panelists complete the test, expected preference count for each sample:

$$E_A = E_B = \frac{n}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Observed frequencies.**

Let $O_A$ = number preferring A, $O_B$ = number preferring B, $O_T$ = no-preference (tied) responses.

**Step 4 — Chi-square statistic.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\chi^2 &= \sum \frac{(O - E)^2}{E} \\
&= \frac{(O_A - n/2)^2}{n/2} + \frac{(O_B - n/2)^2}{n/2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Degrees of freedom.**

$$df = (\text{number of categories}) - 1 = 2 - 1 = 1$$

**Step 6 — Decision rule.**

Compare $\chi^2_{\text{computed}}$ with $\chi^2_{0.05, 1} = 3.841$. If $\chi^2_{\text{computed}} > 3.841$, reject $H_0$.

**Step 7 — Worked numerical example.**

Suppose **60 panelists** preferred A, **40 preferred B**, with **no ties**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\chi^2 &= \frac{(60 - 50)^2}{50} + \frac{(40 - 50)^2}{50} \\
&= \frac{100}{50} + \frac{100}{50} \\
&= 2.0 + 2.0 \\
&= 4.0
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $4.0 > 3.841$, **reject $H_0$** — there is a significant preference for sample A at $\alpha = 0.05$.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Test to Use?

| Objective | Best Test | Panel Size | Statistic | Marks Weight (KTU typical) |
|-----------|-----------|------------|-----------|---------------------------|
| Detect any difference | Triangle | 20–50 | Roessler table | High |
| Attribute a difference to one sample | Paired Comparison | 30–60 | Chi-square | High |
| Describe sensory profile | QDA / Spectrum | 8–12 | ANOVA | High |
| Measure liking | 9-point Hedonic | 75–150 | Mean, ANOVA | Very High |
| Rank multiple samples | Ranking | 30–60 | Friedman | Medium |
| Penalize specific deviations | Penalty Analysis | 100+ | Penalty scores | Medium |
| Quantify intensity | Just-About-Right | 75+ | Penalty analysis | Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *discriminative* tests with *affective* tests. **Discriminative = is there a difference?**, **Affective = which one do you like?** These are fundamentally different objectives.

### 3.4 Hedonic Scale Interpretation (9-Point)

The 9-Point Hedonic Scale is the **gold standard** for acceptance testing in KTU and industry.

| Score | Label |
|-------|-------|
| **9** | Like Extremely |
| **8** | Like Very Much |
| **7** | Like Moderately |
| **6** | Like Slightly |
| **5** | Neither Like nor Dislike |
| **4** | Dislike Slightly |
| **3** | Dislike Moderately |
| **2** | Dislike Very Much |
| **1** | Dislike Extremely |

A product is generally considered **acceptable** if its mean hedonic score is **$\geq 6.5$** (between "Like Slightly" and "Like Moderately").

---

### 3.5 Step-by-Step Calculation of Consumer Acceptability Index

**Definition:** The Consumer Acceptability Index (CAI) is sometimes used to summarize consumer data:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CAI} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i}{n \times 9} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $X_i$ = hedonic score of consumer $i$
- $n$ = number of consumers
- $9$ = maximum possible score

**Example:** 100 consumers evaluate a product. Sum of hedonic scores = 720.

$$
\text{CAI} = \frac{720}{100 \times 9} \times 100\% = \frac{720}{900} \times 100\% = 80\%
$$

This indicates **80% consumer acceptance** — generally a strong product.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Sensory Evaluation Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Define Objective"] --> nodeB["Select Test Type"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Discriminative"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Descriptive"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["Affective"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["Select Panel"]
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG["Trained Panel"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["Consumer Panel"]
    nodeG --> nodeI["Conduct Test in Controlled Booth"]
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Statistical Analysis"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Interpretation and Decision"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["Product Reformulation"]
    nodeK --> nodeM["Market Launch"]
    nodeK --> nodeN["Reject Concept"]
```

### 4.2 Sensory Test Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA["Sensory Evaluation Tests"] --> rootB["Analytical Discriminative"]
    rootA --> rootC["Descriptive"]
    rootA --> rootD["Affective Hedonic"]
    rootB --> b1["Triangle Test"]
    rootB --> b2["Duo-Trio Test"]
    rootB --> b3["Paired Comparison"]
    rootB --> b4["Two AFC Test"]
    rootC --> c1["Flavor Profile Method"]
    rootC --> c2["Texture Profile Method"]
    rootC --> c3["Quantitative Descriptive Analysis QDA"]
    rootC --> c4["Spectrum Method"]
    rootD --> d1["Hedonic Rating 9 Point"]
    rootD --> d2["Preference Ranking"]
    rootD --> d3["Paired Preference"]
    rootD --> d4["Just About Right JAR Scale"]
```

### 4.3 Sensory Laboratory Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    labA["Sample Preparation Area"] --> labB["Sensory Booths Individual"]
    labB --> labC["Panelists Seated"]
    labC --> labD["Data Entry via Computer"]

```

flowchart TD

    booth2 --> booth3["Sample Pass Through Hatch"]
