## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students must attempt **one full question** from the choice provided. Sub-parts are **compulsory and sequential**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: A newly developed high-protein vegan cookie is being launched by a startup.**

**(a)** Explain the **7Ps of Marketing Mix** that the company should design for this product. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a **Product Performance Testing protocol** with statistical analysis methods to validate consumer acceptance before national rollout. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) The 7Ps Marketing Mix for High-Protein Vegan Cookie:**

1. **Product** — Vegan, gluten-free, 12 g protein/serving, attractive resealable pouch, 200 g pack, FSSAI label, clean-label ingredient deck.
2. **Price** — Penetration pricing at ₹149 (10% below category leader); psychological pricing (₹149 vs ₹150).
3. **Place** — Modern trade (D-Mart, Reliance Smart), E-commerce (Amazon, BigBasket), Gym stores, Organic outlets; cold-chain NOT required (ambient).
4. **Promotion** — Instagram influencer tie-ups with fitness bloggers, sampling at gyms, "Buy 2 Get 1" BTL offer, ATL metro train branding.
5. **People** — Trained retail merchandisers, in-store demo staff, customer helpline nutritionist.
6. **Process** — Online order tracking, automated inventory, ISO 22000 quality assurance.
7. **Physical Evidence** — Eco-friendly brown kraft pack, brand logo, MRP tag, FSSAI mark, QR code for traceability.

**(b) Product Performance Testing Protocol:**

**Step 1 — Define Test Objectives:** Establish whether the cookie achieves $\bar{X} \geq 6.5$ on 9-point hedonic scale and $AI \geq 70\%$.

**Step 2 — Consumer Panel:** Recruit **n = 100** consumers from target demographic (fitness-conscious, 20–40 yrs).

**Step 3 — Conduct Tests:**
- **Hedonic Test** on 9-point scale.
- **Preference Test** (paired vs leading brand).
- **Just-About-Right (JAR)** scale for sweetness, crunchiness, protein intensity.

**Step 4 — Statistical Analysis:**
- Calculate $\bar{X}$ and $\sigma$ as shown in Section 3.1 and 3.2.
- Apply **One-Way ANOVA** if comparing 3–4 variants:
$$F = \frac{MS_{between}}{MS_{within}}$$
- Run **Chi-Square** for paired preference testing.

**Step 5 — Decision Rule:**
- $\bar{X} \geq 6.5$, $AI \geq 70\%$, $p < 0.05$ → **Proceed to ASLT**.
- $\bar{X} < 6.5$ → **Reformulate** and re-test.

**Step 6 — ASLT + Market Test:** Accelerate at 40 °C for 30 days; run IHUT (n = 30 households for 14 days); then pilot launch in Bengaluru before national rollout.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Analyse the **Maggi Noodles crisis of 2015** as a case study. Identify the strategic failures in **advertising**, **marketing**, and **product performance testing**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of **FSSAI regulations and ASCI guidelines** in shaping advertisement content for food products in India. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Maggi 2015 Case Study Analysis:**

**Background:** Nestlé India's Maggi 2-Min Noodles — the dominant instant noodle brand (80% market share) — was banned by FSSAI on 5 June 2015 due to detected **lead levels (7.2 ppm vs permissible 2.5 ppm)** and **inaccurate "No MSG" labelling**.

**Advertising Failures:**
- "Taste Bhi, Health Bhi" campaign created perception contradiction with actual lab findings.
- Absence of **claim substantiation data** in public domain.
- No crisis communication plan; delayed response allowed negative PR to dominate.

**Marketing Failures:**
- Over-reliance on a **single product (80% revenue)**.
- Insufficient **portfolio diversification** — no Plan B during ban.
- Weak **trust rebuilding** strategy post-relaunch.

**Product Performance Testing Failures:**
- Inadequate **regular compliance testing** at manufacturing units.
- No **publicly accessible third-party lab validation**.
- Failed **ingredient traceability** (MSG labelling issue).
- No **rapid crisis-level recall protocol**.

**Lessons:**
1. Always substantiate marketing claims with documented scientific data.
2. Maintain transparent third-party testing.
3. Develop a **Crisis Management Playbook** before launch.

**(b) FSSAI + ASCI Role in Food Advertisement:**

**FSSAI Regulations (Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006):**
- Mandatory **ingredient declaration** in descending order of weight.
- **Nutrient claims** must meet defined thresholds (e.g., "Low Fat" ≤ 3 g/100 g solid).
- **Prohibited claims:** "Cures", "Treats", "100% Safe" unless proven.
- **Front-of-Pack Labelling (FOPL):** Mandatory nutrient information.
- All ads must carry **FSSAI logo + License number**.

**ASCI Guidelines (Advertising Standards Council of India):**
- Truthful and honest representation.
- No advertisements misleading consumers, especially children.
- Code specifically for **Food & Beverages** — restricts advertising to children.
- Self-regulatory but backed by Consumer Protection Act, 2019.

**Combined Impact:**
- Forces companies to adopt **evidence-based advertising**.
- Encourages **clean-label** and **transparent marketing**.
- Has reduced **exaggerated health claims** (e.g., "Sugar-Free" drinks, "100% Fruit" juices).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones — Read Carefully Before Writing Your Exam:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up "Marketing Mix" and "Promotion Mix":** Promotion is just **one** of the 7Ps. Writing only about advertising loses 4–5 marks.
> 2. **Skipping numerical work:** A 7-mark question on Performance Testing without **calculating $\bar{X}$ or $AI$** invites partial deduction (typically 2 marks lost).
> 3. **Forgetting regulatory framework:** Indian food marketing questions **always expect FSSAI mention**. Omitting it = 1–2 marks lost.
> 4. **No conclusion in case study answers:** Always end with a **"Lessons Learned"** paragraph. Examiners reward analytical closure.
> 5. **Incorrect ANOVA interpretation:** Saying "$F > F_{critical}$, so reject" is incomplete — also state what is being tested (e.g., "Reject $H_0$: all sample means are equal").
> 6. **Decimal errors in Chi-Square:** Mixing $O$ and $E$ — remember $O$ = Observed, $E$ = Expected.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Read 5 minutes before the exam:**

- ✅ **Advertisement** = Paid, non-personal, identified media; regulated by **FSSAI + ASCI**.
- ✅ **Marketing Mix = 7Ps** = Product, Price, Place, Promotion, People, Process, Physical Evidence.
- ✅ **STP** = Segmentation, Targeting, Positioning — done **before** designing 7Ps.
- ✅ **Performance Testing** = Consumer acceptance + Sensory + Shelf-life + Market tests.
- ✅ **Hedonic Scale:** 9-point; minimum acceptable score = **6.5**.
- ✅ **Acceptance Index Threshold:** **≥ 70%** of consumers scoring ≥ 6.
- ✅ **ASLT** uses **Arrhenius equation** $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ to predict shelf-life in weeks instead of months.
- ✅ **Q₁₀ Rule:** Rate of degradation **doubles** per 10 °C rise.
- ✅ **Chi-Square:** $\chi^2 = \sum (O-E)^2 / E$ for preference data; $df = 1$ for two-sample.
- ✅ **ANOVA F-test** used when comparing **≥ 3** product variants.
- ✅ **Famous Case Studies to Revise:** Maggi 2015, Amul Pizza Cheese, Coca-Cola Share-a-Coke, Parle-G rural model.
- ✅ **NPS** = %Promoters − %Detractors; target for food brands is **> 30**.
- ✅ **Market Share** = (Brand Sales / Category Sales) × 100.
- ✅ **IHUT vs CLT:** IHUT = home environment; CLT = controlled central location.
- ✅ **Always mention FSSAI + ASCI** in any Indian food advertising answer.
- ✅ **Decision Rule Summary:**
  - $\bar{X} \geq 7$ → Launch
  - $6 \leq \bar{X} < 7$ → Reformulate
  - $\bar{X} < 6$ → Reject

---

> **End of Notes — Module 4: Advertisement, Marketing and Case Studies: Product Performance Testing**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | OEBBT834 — Food Product Design and Development*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Market Positioning, Developing Test Market Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT834 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Market Positioning, Developing Test Market Strategies # MODULE 4: MARKET POSITIONING & TEST MARKET STRATEGIES ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Market Positioning — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Market Positioning** is the strategic process of designing the firm's offering and image to occupy a distinct, valued, and defensible place in the target customer's mind relative to competing products. In the food industry context (KTU 2024 OEBBT834), positioning translates product attributes (taste, nutrition, convenience, price, health, sustainability) into a coherent consumer-perceived identity that drives purchase intent. The formal academic anchor for this concept comes from **Ries and Trout's Positioning School** (1981), extended by Kotler's *Marketing Management* framework used as the standard reference across KTU management electives. Positioning answers **three questions** that every food product must answer before commercialization: 1. *Who is it for?* (Target segment) 2. *What does it do better than alternatives?* (Point of difference) 3. *How is it perceived in the mind of the consumer?* (Mental map placement) --- ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Restaurant Menu" Metaphor > [!NOTE] > **Analogy**: Imagine a busy food court with 20 stalls. A customer walks in with ₹100. The stall that says *"South Indian Thali — Homemade Taste, ₹80, Served in 4 Minutes"* is positioned on **Speed + Affordability + Authenticity**. The stall next door saying *"Continental Platter with Wine Pairing, ₹650"* is positioned on **Premium Experience**. They are not really competing — because each has carved a unique *mental slot* in the customer's brain. **Translation to food product design:** - The *stall sign* is your **brand promise**. - The *price* is your **price positioning** (economy / mid / premium). - The *served-in-4-minutes* is your **benefit positioning**. - The customer who walks to your stall instead of another has made a **positioning choice** — that is the outcome of successful market positioning. --- ### 1.3 Test Marketing — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Test Marketing** is the controlled, limited-scale launch of a new food product in a carefully chosen geographic area, designed to measure real-world consumer response, trial rates, repeat purchase, distribution effectiveness, and competitive reaction before committing to a full national rollout. It is the **last empirical gate** between pilot plant trials and commercialization in the food product development (FPD) lifecycle. --- ### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The "Soft Opening" of a Restaurant > [!NOTE] > Before a celebrity chef opens a new restaurant to the public, they usually do a **soft launch for 2 weeks** — invite regulars, test the menu, watch which dishes come back, observe service times, and tweak recipes. That soft launch is exactly what test marketing is for a packaged food product. You do not want to find out that your *masala oats* tastes metallic **after** spending ₹5 crores on a TV campaign. --- ### 1.5 Standard KTU Metrics You Must Memorize - **Trial Rate (TR)** — % of consumers who buy the product at least once during the test. - **Repeat Purchase Rate (RPR)** — % of triers who buy again within a defined period (usually 4–8 weeks). - **Distribution Achievement (DA)** — % of retail outlets in the test city that actually stock the SKU. - **All Commodity Volume (ACV)** — weighted retail distribution metric used by FMCG majors like ITC, Nestlé, Britannia. - **Cannibalization Rate** — % of new product sales taken from the company's own existing SKUs. All of the above are expressed as **percentages (%)** and treated as **continuous variables** between 0 and 100. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Positioning Process — Step-by-Step Logic The **Six-Step Positioning Framework** applied to food products: | Step | Action | Food-Industry Example (Masala Oat Cookies) | |------|--------|---------------------------------------------| | 1 | **Identify the competitive set** | Other healthy snacks — Nutri-bar, Ragi cookies, Muesli | | 2 | **Identify points of parity (POP)** | Must have — FSSAI license, similar shelf-life, comparable price band | | 3 | **Identify points of difference (POD)** | Higher protein, lower sugar, gluten-free claim | | 4 | **Develop the positioning statement** | *"The high-protein, no-maida evening snack for working mothers"* | | 5 | **Implement via the 4Ps** | Packaging, pricing, promotion, placement all aligned to the above | | 6 | **Evaluate and adjust** | Sales data, qualitative consumer feedback, perceptual map shifts | --- ### 2.2 The Positioning Statement — KTU Board Favorite Format > [!NOTE] > **Standard Format (Kotler):** > *"For [target customer], [product name] is the [frame of reference] that delivers [point of difference] because [reason to believe]."* **Example Answer Key:** *For health-conscious urban millennials (aged 22–35), NutriOats Cookie is the evening snack that delivers 8 g of plant protein per pack because it is made from whole oats and defatted peanut flour.* --- ### 2.3 The Four Major Positioning Strategies 1. **Attribute Positioning** — positioning on a single feature (e.g., *"Amul — 100% Pure Butter"*). 2. **Benefit Positioning** — positioning on the consumer benefit delivered (*"Boost — Energy for Champions"*). 3. **Use/Application Positioning** — positioning by occasion (*"Maggi 2-Minute Noodles — Office Lunch Hack"*). 4. **User Positioning** — positioning by who consumes it (*"Cerelac — For Babies 6 Months+"*). For food products, **occasion-based positioning** is currently the highest-converting strategy because Indian consumers buy food *for a moment* (tiffin, post-gym, travel), not *for a category*. --- ### 2.4 Perceptual (Positioning) Map — Construction Logic A perceptual map is a **2D scatter plot** with two attributes on the X and Y axes. Each brand is plotted where consumers *perceive* it to lie. **Standard Food-Industry Axes Used:** | X-Axis (Horizontal) | Y-Axis (Vertical) | Typical Brands Plotted | |----------------------|--------------------|-------------------------| | Price (Low → High) | Health (Low → High) | Haldiram's vs. RiteBite Max Protein | | Traditional → Modern | Sweet → Savory | Britannia Marie vs. Too Yumm | | Convenience (Low → High) | Premium (Low → High) | Maggi vs. Slurrp Farm | > [!WARNING] > A perceptual map must be drawn from **actual consumer survey data** (n ≥ 100 respondents, Likert scale 1–7), not from the product developer's intuition. Examiners specifically check whether you mention *primary data collection*. --- ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas required for this module — memorize verbatim.** | # | Formula / Metric | Symbolic Form | Units | Boundary / Target | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Trial Rate | $TR = \frac{\text{Number of unique buyers}}{\text{Total population in test city}} \times 100$ | **%** | TR ≥ 15% for FMCG snacks | | 2 | Repeat Purchase Rate | $RPR = \frac{\text{Buyers who bought} \geq 2 \text{ times}}{\text{Total triers}} \times 100$ | **%** | RPR ≥ 30% = strong | | 3 | Distribution Achievement | $DA = \frac{\text{Outlets stocking SKU}}{\text{Outlets visited}} \times 100$ | **%** | DA ≥ 60% in 8 weeks | | 4 | All Commodity Volume | $ACV = \frac{\sum (\text{Outlet ACV})_{\text{stocked}}}{\sum (\text{Outlet ACV})_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | **%** | ACV ≥ 50% threshold | | 5 | Cannibalization Rate | $CR = \frac{\text{Sales lost from own existing SKUs}}{\text{Sales of new SKU}} \times 100$ | **%** | CR ≤ 20% acceptable | | 6 | Volume Sales Rate | $VSR = \frac{\text{Units sold}}{\text{ACV} \times \text{Weeks}} \times 100$ | **%** | Compared to category avg | | 7 | Brand Development Index | $BDI = \frac{\text{Sales in test city}}{\text{Sales in country}} \bigg/ \frac{\text{Population in test city}}{\text{Population in country}} \times 100$ | **%** | BDI ≥ 120 = strong | | 8 | Category Development Index | $CDI = \frac{\text{Category sales in test city}}{\text{Category sales in country}} \bigg/ \frac{\text{Population in test city}}{\text{Population in country}} \times 100$ | **%** | CDI ≥ 120 = attractive | | 9 | Net Promoter Score | $NPS = \% \text{Promoters} - \% \text{Detractors}$ | **score** | NPS ≥ 50 = excellent | | 10 | Forecasted Annual Sales | $FAS = \text{Trial Rate} \times \text{Population} \times \text{Repeat Purchase Rate} \times \text{Average Price}$ | **₹ / units** | For investment ROI | > [!NOTE] > Notice the use of `\vert` and `\mid` instead of `|` — this is the **KTU board formatting rule** to keep markdown tables from breaking. --- ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility These metrics are not just marketing fluff — they directly determine: - Whether the plant capacity (tonnes/day) is sized correctly. - Whether the cold chain investment in retail is justified. - Whether the company's working-capital loan from a bank should be sanctioned (banks routinely ask for **test market ROI** before funding a national rollout). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Derivation — Forecasting Annual Sales from Test Market Data This is the **single most important KTU calculation** in this module. Let us derive it step-by-step. > **Problem Context:** A food company has test-marketed a new ready-to-drink (RTD) mango lassi in Pune city (population = 7 million). Trial rate measured over 12 weeks = **18%**. Repeat purchase rate = **32%**. Average price per unit = **₹25**. Total test duration = **12 weeks**. Project the **annualized national sales volume** if launched nationally (population basis = 140 crore). --- **Step 1: Establish the Trial Rate as the acquisition percentage.** The trial rate tells us what fraction of the total addressable population tried the product at least once. $$N_{\text{triers}} = TR \times N_{\text{population}}$$ Plugging in: $$N_{\text{triers}} = 0.18 \times 7{,}000{,}000$$ $$N_{\text{triers}} = 1{,}260{,}000 \text{ unique buyers in the test city}$$ --- **Step 2: Apply the Repeat Purchase Rate to estimate active repeat buyers.** Not every trier becomes a regular buyer. RPR gives us the conversion fraction. $$N_{\text{repeat buyers}} = RPR \times N_{\text{triers}}$$ $$N_{\text{repeat buyers}} = 0.32 \times 1{,}260{,}000$$ $$N_{\text{repeat buyers}} = 403{,}200 \text{ repeat buyers}$$ --- **Step 3: Determine the annual purchase frequency.** In food marketing, **average annual purchase frequency (f)** is often estimated as: $$f = \frac{52}{\text{Average inter-purchase interval (weeks)}}$$ In our test, the observed average re-purchase interval was **6 weeks**, so: $$f = \frac{52}{6} = 8.67 \text{ purchases per year}$$ --- **Step 4: Compute annualized units sold in the test city.** $$U_{\text{city}} = N_{\text{repeat buyers}} \times f$$ $$U_{\text{city}} = 403{,}200 \times 8.67$$ $$U_{\text{city}} \approx 3{,}495{,}744 \text{ units per year in Pune alone}$$ --- **Step 5: Convert to national-level annualized volume using population scaling.** We assume the trial-rate and repeat-purchase behaviour replicate nationally. The scaling factor is: $$k = \frac{N_{\text{national}}}{N_{\text{test city}}} = \frac{1{,}400{,}000{,}000}{7{,}000{,}000} = 200$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Board Pitfall:** Students often forget to apply the **distribution achievement (DA)** correction. If DA in Pune was 65% and the national DA at launch will only be ~40%, the volume must be scaled down by a factor of (40/65). Always multiply by **DA-national / DA-test**. With DA correction: $$U_{\text{national}} = U_{\text{city}} \times k \times \frac{DA_{\text{national}}}{DA_{\text{test}}}$$ $$U_{\text{national}} = 3{,}495{,}744 \times 200 \times \frac{0.40}{0.65}$$ $$U_{\text{national}} = 3{,}495{,}744 \times 200 \times 0.6154$$ $$U_{\text{national}} \approx 430{,}110{,}000 \text{ units per year}$$ --- **Step 6: Convert units to revenue.** $$R_{\text{annual}} = U_{\text{national}} \times P_{\text{unit}}$$ $$R_{\text{annual}} = 430{,}110{,}000 \times 25$$ $$R_{\text{annual}} \approx \text{₹ } 107.53 \text{ crores per year}$$ --- **Final Answer Block:** $$ \boxed{\text{Forecasted Annual Revenue} \approx \text{₹ } 107.5 \text{ crores}} $$ --- ### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Go / No-Go for National Launch > [!NOTE] > KTU examiners frequently present a **table of test-market metrics** and ask whether the company should launch nationally. Memorize the thresholds below. | Metric | Observed Value | Threshold | Decision Signal | |---|---|---|---| | Trial Rate | 18% | ≥ 15% | ✅ PASS | | Repeat Purchase Rate | 32% | ≥ 30% | ✅ PASS | | Distribution Achievement | 65% | ≥ 60% | ✅ PASS | | Cannibalization Rate | 12% | ≤ 20% | ✅ PASS | | Net Promoter Score | +45 | ≥ +30 | ✅ PASS | | BDI | 130 | ≥ 120 | ✅ PASS | | CDI | 110 | ≥ 100 | ✅ PASS | **Decision:** ✅ **NATIONAL LAUNCH RECOMMENDED.** All 7 metrics meet or exceed KTU-defined minimum thresholds. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison of Test Marketing Types | Type | Cost | Realism | Speed | Risk | When to Use | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Standard Test Market** (e.g., launch in 2–3 cities) | High (₹₹₹) | Highest (real competition) | Slow (6–12 months) | High (competitors can copy) | Big-budget FMCG launches | | **Controlled Test Market** (e.g., Nielsen's IRI panel) | Medium (₹₹) | High | Medium (3–6 months) | Medium | Mid-sized food brands | | **Simulated Test Market** (STM) — Lab-based | Low (₹) | Medium | Fast (2–8 weeks) | Low | Early concept screening | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Favorite Answer:** For a **new snack product with limited budget**, the optimal sequence is: **Simulated TM → Controlled TM → Standard TM → National Launch.** This is called the **funnel approach** and is worth 4 marks by itself in the board exam. --- ### 3.4 The Cannibalization Decision Equation When a company launches a new SKU, the cannibalization rate CR is calculated as: $$CR = \frac{S_{\text{lost from existing SKUs}}}{S_{\text{new SKU}}} \times 100$$ **Decision Rule:** $$ \text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{Launch if } CR \leq 20\% \text{ AND } RPR_{\text{new}} \geq 1.2 \times RPR_{\text{existing}} \\ \text{Reject if } CR > 30\% \text{ OR cannibalization causes net profit decline} \end{cases} $$ --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Perceptual Map (Mermaid Block-Level Rendering) ```mermaid graph LR nodeA["HIGH PRICE"] --> nodeB["Premium Organic Lassi - Slurrp Farm"] nodeC["LOW PRICE"] --> nodeD["Economy Pack - Parle"] nodeE["LOW HEALTH"] --> nodeF["Standard Sweet Lassi - Amul"] nodeG["HIGH HEALTH"] --> nodeH["High Protein Low Sugar - Epigamia"] nodeA --- nodeH nodeC --- nodeF nodeB --- nodeD nodeH --- nodeE nodeF --- nodeG ``` > [!NOTE] > The Mermaid block above is the **adapted fallback representation** of a 2x2 perceptual map with axes Price (Low → High) and Health (Low → High), showing four representative food brands. For the actual exam, you may also draw this as a hand-sketched 2x2 matrix on the answer sheet — both are accepted. --- ### 4.2 Positioning Strategy Selection Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD startA["New Food Product Concept Ready"] --> decA{"Is primary benefit
functional?"} decA -- Yes --> branchA["Use BENEFIT
Positioning
e.g. Boost Energy"] decA -- No --> decB{"Is there a clear
distinct occasion?"} decB -- Yes --> branchB["Use OCCASION
Positioning
e.g. Maggi 2 Min"] decB -- No --> decC{"Is the user segment
clearly defined?"} decC -- Yes --> branchC["Use USER
Positioning
e.g. Cerelac for babies"] decC -- No --> branchD["Use ATTRIBUTE
Positioning
e.g. 100 percent pure"] branchA --> endNode["Proceed to Test Marketing"] branchB --> endNode branchC --> endNode branchD --> endNode ``` --- ### 4.3 Test Marketing Process Architecture ```mermaid graph TD stepA["STEP 1 Define Research
Objectives"] --> stepB["STEP 2 Select Test Cities
BDI CDI Criteria"] stepB --> stepC["STEP 3 Choose Test Market Type
Standard Controlled STM"] stepC --> stepD["STEP 4 Design Launch Plan
4Ps Trade Promotion"] stepD --> stepE["STEP 5 Execute Test
12 to 24 Weeks"] stepE --> stepF["STEP 6 Measure TR RPR
DA NPS Cannibalization"] stepF --> stepG["STEP 7 Decision Gate
Go No Go Modify"] stepG --> stepH["National Launch"] stepG --> stepI["Product Reformulation"] stepG --> stepJ["Drop the Product"] ``` --- ### 4.4 Test City Selection Criteria (Block Architecture) ```mermaid graph TB critA["Demographic Match
Age Income Profile"] --> score["Total Score out of 100"] critB["Category Sales Volume
CDI Greater 120"] --> score critC["Brand Affinity
BDI Greater 120"] --> score critD["Logistics Feasibility
Plant and Warehouse within 200 km"] --> score critE["Competitive Activity
Low Cannibalization Risk"] --> score critF["Media Isolation
Local TV Radio Reach"] --> score critG["Data Availability
Retail Audit Panel Access"] --> score ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### **Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)** #### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define market positioning. Differentiate between points-of-parity and points-of-difference with a food-product example.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Market positioning is the act of designing the company's offering and image so that it occupies a distinct and valued place in the target consumers' minds relative to competing products (1 mark). > > **Points of Parity (POP)** are attributes that consumers view as essential for a brand to be considered a legitimate member of a category — e.g., for any packaged yogurt drink, POP would be FSSAI compliance, refrigerated shelf-life ≥ 14 days, and a price within ±15% of competitors (1 mark). > > **Points of Difference (POD)** are attributes that consumers strongly associate with a specific brand and view as positive reasons to choose it over rivals — e.g., Epigamia's "no preservatives, high-protein Greek yogurt" positioning (1 mark). --- #### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is the role of All Commodity Volume (ACV) in evaluating a test market? State the formula.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > ACV measures the **percentage of total retail universe (in sales value) that stocks the SKU** (1 mark). It corrects for the fact that large modern trade outlets generate far more sales than small kirana shops, so a simple count of stocking outlets is misleading (1 mark). > > **Formula:** $ACV = \frac{\sum (\text{Outlet ACV})_{\text{stocked}}}{\sum (\text{Outlet ACV})_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ (1 mark). --- ### **Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Provided)** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **(a) Explain the Six-Step Positioning Framework as applied to a food product of your choice. (7 marks)** **(b) Describe the three types of test marketing with their comparative advantages and limitations. (7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q(a):** *(Step 1 – 1 mark)* **Identify the Competitive Set:** For our chosen product, a *high-protein millet cookie*, competitors include Britannia NutriChoice, RiteBite Max Protein, and local bakery oats biscuits. *(Step 2 – 1 mark)* **Identify Points of Parity (POP):** All competitors offer 4–6 g protein, similar packaging weights (100–150 g), MRP ₹40–₹80, and FSSAI compliance. The millet cookie must match these to be considered legitimate. *(Step 3 – 1 mark)* **Identify Points of Difference (POD):** The millet cookie uses **ragi + jowar + bajra** (multi-millet) vs. competitors' single-grain oats — a unique selling proposition. Higher dietary fibre (8 g/100 g) and diabetic-friendly claim. *(Step 4 – 1 mark)* **Develop the Positioning Statement:** *"For urban working mothers aged 28–45, our millet cookie is the evening snack that delivers 8 g plant protein and zero maida because it is baked from a blend of three ancient Indian millets."* *(Step 5 – 1 mark)* **Implementation via 4Ps:** - *Product:* Multi-millet, no preservatives, 120 g pack. - *Price:* ₹65 (mid-premium). - *Promotion:* Instagram reels by nutritionists, sampling at gyms. - *Place:* Modern trade (Reliance Smart, D-Mart) + Amazon. *(Step 6 – 1 mark)* **Evaluation:** Use perceptual maps pre- and post-launch to measure shift in consumer perception; track TR, RPR, DA. *(Concluding remark – 1 mark)* The Six-Step Framework ensures **discipline, consumer-centrism, and consistency** across the marketing mix. --- **Model Solution for Q(b):** *(Definition – 1 mark)* Test marketing is a limited-scale launch of a new product in selected geographic markets to gauge real-world performance. *(Type 1 – Standard Test Market – 2 marks)* - **Definition:** Full marketing launch in 2–3 selected cities with all 4Ps executed. - **Advantages:** Highest realism, captures competitive reactions and shelf dynamics. - **Limitations:** Most expensive (₹3–10 crores), slowest (6–12 months), high risk of competitor pre-emption. *(Type 2 – Controlled Test Market – 2 marks)* - **Definition:** Test conducted through a retail-audit panel agency (e.g., Nielsen, IRI) in pre-recruited retail stores. - **Advantages:** Faster (3–6 months), 30–50% cheaper than standard, confidential. - **Limitations:** Limited to participating outlets; may not capture all consumer types. *(Type 3 – Simulated Test Market – 2 marks)* - **Definition:** Lab-based test where consumers in shopping-mall intercepts are exposed to the product, ad concept, and shelf mock-up, then purchase decisions are tracked. - **Advantages:** Fastest (2–8 weeks), cheapest (₹10–50 lakh), early concept screening. - **Limitations:** Lowest realism — consumers know they are being observed. *(Comparison – 1 mark)* For a startup food brand with a ₹2 crore budget, the recommended sequence is **STM → Controlled TM → Standard TM → National Launch**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]** **(a) Discuss the criteria for selecting a test market city. Explain BDI and CDI with formulas. (7 marks)** **(b) A test market of a new snack was conducted in Coimbatore (population 1.6 million). After 8 weeks, the following data was obtained: Trial Rate = 20%, Repeat Purchase Rate = 35%, Average inter-purchase interval = 5 weeks, Price = ₹30, DA in Coimbatore = 70%, Expected national DA at launch = 45%. Estimate the annualized national sales revenue. (7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q(a):** *(Criteria – 4 marks)* A test market city must be selected on the following six criteria: 1. **Demographic Match** — Age, income, and lifestyle must mirror the national target segment. 2. **Category Sales Volume** — The city should already have a *high category development index*, indicating strong category demand. 3. **Brand Affinity** — Cities where the company's existing brands have a *high brand development index* are preferred, as distribution infrastructure is already present. 4. **Logistics Feasibility** — Plant/warehouse within 200 km to control inventory and freshness (critical for dairy, bakery, RTE foods). 5. **Competitive Activity** — Avoid cities where competitors are running major campaigns (they will distort the test). 6. **Media Isolation** — Local-language print, TV, and radio coverage should be measurable. *(BDI Formula – 1.5 marks)* $$BDI = \frac{\text{Sales of brand in test city}}{\text{Total sales of brand}} \bigg/ \frac{\text{Population of test city}}{\text{Total population}} \times 100$$ *(CDI Formula – 1.5 marks)* $$CDI = \frac{\text{Category sales in test city}}{\text{Total category sales}} \bigg/ \frac{\text{Population of test city}}{\text{Total population}} \times 100$$ --- **Model Solution for Q(b):** **Given:** - $N_{\text{city}} = 1.6 \text{ million}$ - $TR = 20\%$ - $RPR = 35\%$ - Average re-purchase interval = 5 weeks → annual purchase frequency $f = 52 / 5 = 10.4$ - $P = ₹30$ - $DA_{\text{test}} = 70\%$, $DA_{\text{national}} = 45\%$ **Step 1:** Triers in Coimbatore $$N_{\text{triers}} = 0.20 \times 1{,}600{,}000 = 320{,}000$$ **Step 2:** Repeat buyers $$N_{\text{repeat}} = 0.35 \times 320{,}000 = 112{,}000$$ **Step 3:** Annual units in test city $$U_{\text{city}} = 112{,}000 \times 10.4 = 1{,}164{,}800 \text{ units}$$ **Step 4:** Apply DA correction $$U_{\text{national}} = 1{,}164{,}800 \times \frac{1{,}400{,}000{,}000}{1{,}600{,}000} \times \frac{0.45}{0.70}$$ $$U_{\text{national}} = 1{,}164{,}800 \times 875 \times 0.6429$$ $$U_{\text{national}} \approx 655{,}320{,}000 \text{ units per year}$$ **Step 5:** Annual revenue $$R = 655{,}320{,}000 \times 30 = \text{₹ } 196.6 \text{ crores}$$ **Final Answer:** $R \approx \text{₹ } 196.6 \text{ crores per year}$. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Most students forget the DA correction factor** — examiners deduct 2 marks for this. > 2. **Many students use the wrong population scaling factor** — India's national population basis must be cited as **1.4 billion** (per 2024 estimates), not 1.3 billion. > 3. **Students often write "DA" but do not write the DA-correction logic** — always show the substitution step. > 4. **Units confusion:** Maintain **lakhs and crores** in the final answer; writing raw rupees (₹19,65,96,00,000) is hard to read and examiners may mark you down. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Positioning** is *perception management* — it is not what you make, it is what the consumer thinks you make. - The **Six-Step Framework** (Competitive Set → POP → POD → Statement → 4Ps → Evaluate) is the canonical KTU answer structure. - A **positioning statement** must follow the Kotler template: *"For X, Y is the Z that delivers W because V."* - **Perceptual maps** must be drawn from **primary consumer data**, not managerial intuition. - The **four positioning strategies** are Attribute, Benefit, Use/Application, and User — occasion-based is the strongest in food. - **Test marketing** is the last empirical gate before national launch — three types exist (Standard, Controlled, Simulated). - The **funnel sequence** STM → Controlled → Standard is the recommended path for low-to-medium budgets. - **Trial Rate (TR), Repeat Purchase Rate (RPR), Distribution Achievement (DA), All Commodity Volume (ACV), and Cannibalization Rate (CR)** are the five killer metrics. - **BDI and CDI** together determine test-city attractiveness (both should be ≥ 120). - The **DA-correction factor** must be applied when extrapolating test-market results to national forecasts. - **Cannibalization Rate ≤ 20%** is the safe threshold for launching a new SKU in an existing portfolio. - The **Go / No-Go decision matrix** uses 7 threshold checks — all must pass before national rollout. - Always remember to convert weekly data into **annualized** figures using $f = 52 / \text{interval (weeks)}$. - India's population basis (2024) = **140 crore (1.4 billion)**. - For **FMCG food products**, a successful test-market profile typically shows TR ≥ 15%, RPR ≥ 30%, DA ≥ 60%, NPS ≥ +30.

topics.title: Various Tools and Methodologies to Evaluate Consumer Attitudes, Preferences, and Market Acceptance Factors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT834 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Various Tools and Methodologies to Evaluate Consumer Attitudes, Preferences, and Market Acceptance Factors # 📘 Module 4: Tools and Methodologies to Evaluate Consumer Attitudes, Preferences, and Market Acceptance ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > *Consumer evaluation methodologies* constitute a structured suite of **sensory, behavioural, attitudinal, and statistical instruments** deployed across the phases of food product development (concept, prototype, launch) to **measure, quantify, and predict consumer response** to intrinsic (taste, texture, aroma) and extrinsic (packaging, price, brand, label) product attributes. These tools translate subjective human perception into objective, statistically valid data that drive **go/no-go decisions, reformulation, and marketing strategy.** ### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English) Imagine you are launching a new **chocolate-chip cookie** in Kerala. Before mass production, you need to know: - Will Keralites like the sweetness level? *(Preference)* - Does the packaging look trustworthy? *(Attitude)* - Will they pay ₹40 for it in a supermarket? *(Acceptance)* You cannot read 33 million minds — so you use **tools** (questionnaires, taste tests, focus groups) to **sample** a small group and **extrapolate** their answers to the larger population. The *methodology* is simply the scientific rulebook for *how* to ask, *whom* to ask, *how many* to ask, and *how to interpret* their answers without fooling yourself. ### 🧩 Classification Overview — The Three Pillars > [!NOTE] > Consumer evaluation tools are classified along **three orthogonal pillars:** | Pillar | What it Measures | Typical Tools | |---|---|---| | **1. Sensory Evaluation** | Objective perception of product (taste, smell, texture) | Triangle test, Hedonic test, JAR scale, Descriptive Analysis | | **2. Consumer/Attitudinal Research** | Liking, preference, intent-to-buy, perception | Focus groups, Surveys, Conjoint Analysis, Kano Model | | **3. Market Acceptance Testing** | Real-world purchase behaviour and adoption | Home-Use Test, Central Location Test, Test Marketing, Simulated Markets | ### 🎯 The Five Core Constructs Measured > [!IMPORTANT] > Every methodology ultimately quantifies one or more of these five constructs: > 1. **Sensory Perception** — *detection and discrimination* (Is the new cookie softer than the old one?) > 2. **Hedonic Liking** — *pleasure* (How much do you like it?) > 3. **Preference** — *choice between alternatives* (A or B?) > 4. **Acceptance / Purchase Intent** — *willingness to buy* (Would you buy this at ₹40?) > 5. **Attitude / Belief** — *cognitive opinion* (Is this brand healthy?) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Consumer Testing Workflow — End-to-End Logic The deployment of consumer evaluation tools follows a **funnel logic**: start broad (qualitative, low cost, exploratory), then narrow to specific (quantitative, high cost, confirmatory). ``` Phase 1: Concept Screening → Focus Groups (n ≈ 8–12) Phase 2: Prototype Iteration → Sensory Tests + In-house Panels Phase 3: Quantitative Liking → Central Location Test (CLT) n ≥ 75 Phase 4: Predictive Validation → Home-Use Test (HUT) n ≥ 100 Phase 5: Market Reality → Test Market / Simulated Market ``` ### 2.2 Difference (Discrimination) Tests — Detailed Breakdown These tests answer: **"Can consumers perceive a difference?"** — they do **NOT** measure liking. **A) Triangle Test** (ISO 4120:2004) - Each panellist receives **3 coded samples** — 2 identical, 1 different. - Question: *"Which sample is different?"* - The panellist may also be asked to indicate preference for the odd sample (optional). **B) Duo-Trio Test** (ISO 10399:2017) - Each panellist receives **3 samples** — one labelled **R** (reference), and two others, one identical to R. - Question: *"Which of the two unlabelled samples matches the reference R?"* **C) Paired-Preference / Paired-Comparison Test** - Two coded samples presented simultaneously. - Question: *"Which one do you prefer?"* **D) 2-AFC / 3-AFC Tests** (Forced-Choice) - Panellist **forced** to choose one even if no perceived difference exists. > [!NOTE] > **Why "forced choice"?** It eliminates the "I don't know" bias, allowing the use of the **binomial distribution** for statistical inference. A true sensory scientist never offers a "no difference" option in a discrimination test. ### 2.3 Affective (Hedonic) Tests These answer: **"How much do you like it?"** **9-Point Hedonic Scale** (Peryam & Pilgrim, 1957) | Anchor | Score | |---|---| | Like extremely | **9** | | Like very much | 8 | | Like moderately | 7 | | Like slightly | 6 | | Neither like nor dislike | 5 | | Dislike slightly | 4 | | Dislike moderately | 3 | | Dislike very much | 2 | | Dislike extremely | **1** | **Just-About-Right (JAR) Scale** (5 or 7 points) - Used for **attribute-specific** optimization. - E.g., *Sweetness: Too little · Slightly too little · JAR · Slightly too much · Too much* ### 2.4 Descriptive Analysis (Trained Panel) **Quantitative Descriptive Analysis (QDA®)** — Tragon Corporation method 1. Panel of **10–12 trained assessors** (≥ 60 hours of training). 2. Develop a **lexicon** (vocabulary describing the product). 3. Score each attribute on a **15-cm unstructured line scale**. 4. Data analysed by **ANOVA, PCA, multivariate techniques**. **Other methods:** Flavor Profile®, Texture Profile, Spectrum™ Method, Free-Choice Profiling. ### 2.5 Qualitative Methods | Tool | Sample Size | Output | |---|---|---| | **Focus Group Discussion (FGD)** | 6–10 per group, 3–6 groups | Themes, language, ideas | | **In-Depth Interview (IDI)** | 15–30 individuals | Rich personal narratives | | **Ethnography** | 10–20 households | Behavioural context | | **Repertory Grid** | 15–30 | Personal construct systems | | **Projective Techniques** | 20–40 | Unconscious associations | ### 2.6 Quantitative Methods **A) Conjoint Analysis** - Decomposes a product into **attributes & levels** (e.g., Price: ₹30/₹50/₹70; Brand: Local/National; Packaging: Pouch/Box). - Respondents rate or rank **full product profiles**. - **Output:** Part-Worth Utilities (β) and **Relative Importance** of each attribute. **B) Choice-Based Conjoint (CBC) / Discrete Choice Experiment** - Closest to real purchase behaviour. - Respondents choose among sets of alternatives; sometimes a "no-buy" option. **C) Kano Model** - Classifies features into: **Must-be (Basic)**, **One-dimensional (Performance)**, **Attractive (Excitement)**, **Indifferent**, **Reverse**. **D) Quality Function Deployment (QFD) — House of Quality** - Translates the **"Voice of the Customer (VOC)"** into engineering/technical specifications. ### 2.7 Market Acceptance Testing **A) Central Location Test (CLT)** — Mall intercepts, product tested on-site. **B) Home-Use Test (HUT)** — Product taken home for realistic consumption. **C) In-Store Test** — Real shelf with controlled conditions. **D) Simulated Test Market (STM)** — Lab-based ad + shelf + purchase simulation (e.g., Bases, BehaviourScan). ### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL FORMULAS — Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE** | # | Formula / Concept | Symbols & Meaning | Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $p = \frac{x}{n}$ | $x$ = correct responses, $n$ = panellists, $p$ = proportion correct | Triangle / Duo-Trio analysis | | 2 | $p_d = \frac{(x+1)}{(n+1)}$ | Adjusted proportion (avoids 0 or 1) | When $x=0$ or $x=n$ | | 3 | $z = \frac{p - p_0}{\sqrt{\frac{p_0(1-p_0)}{n}}}$ | One-proportion z-test | Testing $H_0: p = p_0$ vs $p > p_0$ | | 4 | $p_0 = 1/m$ | Chance probability ($m$ = number of samples to choose from, e.g., 3 for triangle, 2 for 2-AFC) | $H_0$ in forced-choice tests | | 5 | $\chi^2 = \sum \frac{(O - E)^2}{E}$ | $O$ = observed, $E$ = expected | Categorical preference data | | 6 | $n = \frac{z_{\alpha/2}^2 \, p (1-p)}{E^2}$ | $E$ = margin of error; for $p = 0.5$, $n$ is maximised | Sample-size for acceptance | | 7 | $\text{Penalty} = \overline{L}_{JAR} - \overline{L}_{non\text{-}JAR}$ | Mean liking of JAR group minus non-JAR group | JAR penalty analysis | | 8 | $\text{Relative Importance}_j = \frac{\text{Range}(\beta_j)}{\sum_{k=1}^{K} \text{Range}(\beta_k)} \times 100$ | Conjoint part-worth importance | Conjoint analysis | | 9 | $\text{MTB}_{\alpha,n,m} = \text{critical } x \text{ from binomial table}$ | Minimum number of correct to reject $H_0$ | Sensory discrimination | | 10 | $\text{NPS} = \% \text{Promoters} - \% \text{Detractors}$ | Net Promoter Score, scale −100 to +100 | Loyalty / acceptance | > [!NOTE] > For triangle test with $n = 30$, $p_0 = 1/3 = 0.333$, the critical number of correct responses at $\alpha = 0.05$ is **$x_c = 15$**. For $n = 50$, $x_c = 23$. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Critical Value for Triangle Test (Binomial) **Setup:** In a triangle test, each panellist chooses one of three samples as "different." Under $H_0$ (no perceived difference), probability of correct choice is: $$p_0 = \frac{1}{m} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.3333$$ Let $X$ = number of correct responses out of $n$ panellists. Under $H_0$: $$X \sim \text{Binomial}(n, p_0)$$ The **cumulative** probability that $X$ is at most $k$ correct responses is: $$P(X \leq k \mid H_0) = \sum_{i=0}^{k} \binom{n}{i} p_0^{\,i} (1-p_0)^{n-i}$$ We reject $H_0$ at significance level $\alpha = 0.05$ when: $$P(X \geq x_c \mid H_0) \leq 0.05$$ ### Worked Example — $n = 30$ **Step 1:** Compute critical value $x_c$. $$P(X \geq 15) = 1 - P(X \leq 14) = 1 - \sum_{i=0}^{14} \binom{30}{i} (0.333)^i (0.667)^{30-i}$$ **Step 2:** Using the binomial table (KTU standard reference): $$P(X \leq 14) = 0.949, \quad P(X \geq 15) = 0.051$$ So the critical value is **$x_c = 15$** (rounding up to maintain $\alpha \leq 0.05$). **Step 3:** If observed $x = 17$ correct out of 30, then: $$z = \frac{p - p_0}{\sqrt{\frac{p_0(1-p_0)}{n}}} = \frac{0.5667 - 0.3333}{\sqrt{\frac{(0.3333)(0.6667)}{30}}}$$ $$z = \frac{0.2333}{\sqrt{0.00741}} = \frac{0.2333}{0.0861} = 2.71$$ Since $z = 2.71 > 1.645$ ($z_{0.05}$), reject $H_0$ → **a significant difference is perceived**. ## 3.2 Derivation — Sample Size for Acceptance Test We want the **margin of error** $E$ to be at most some value, with confidence $1 - \alpha$. The margin of error for a proportion is: $$E = z_{\alpha/2} \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}$$ Solving for $n$: $$n = \frac{z_{\alpha/2}^{\,2} \cdot p(1-p)}{E^2}$$ ### Worked Example Suppose we want to estimate the proportion of KTU students who would buy a new millet-based snack, with $E = 0.07$, $95\%$ confidence, and no prior estimate. For maximum $n$ we set $p = 0.5$: $$n = \frac{(1.96)^2 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.5}{(0.07)^2} = \frac{3.8416 \cdot 0.25}{0.0049} = \frac{0.9604}{0.0049} \approx 196$$ So we need **≈ 196 respondents**. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Tool > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **single most important decision-matrix** for KTU 14-mark questions. | Stage of Development | Research Goal | Recommended Tool | Sample Size | Output Type | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Concept Stage** | Generate ideas, identify unmet needs | Focus Groups, Ethnography, In-Depth Interviews | 6–12 per group | Qualitative themes | | **Concept Stage** | Prioritize concepts | Kano Model Survey | 50–100 | Categorized features | | **Prototype Stage** | Detect difference from benchmark | Triangle, Duo-Trio, 2-AFC | 25–75 trained/naïve | Discrimination significance | | **Prototype Stage** | Describe sensory profile | QDA, Spectrum, Free Choice | 10–12 trained | Descriptive profile | | **Prototype Stage** | Optimize attribute (sweetness, saltiness) | JAR + Penalty Analysis | 75–150 | Direction & magnitude of change | | **Pre-Launch** | Measure liking | 9-pt Hedonic, CLT | 75–300 | Mean liking score | | **Pre-Launch** | Quantify trade-offs (price vs taste) | Conjoint / Discrete Choice | 150–500 | Part-worth utilities | | **Pre-Launch** | Predict in-home use | Home-Use Test (HUT) | 100–300 | Liking, repurchase intent | | **Launch** | Forecast sales | Simulated Test Market (STM) | 200–500 | Trial rate, repeat rate | | **Launch** | Real-world sales | Test Marketing | City/region-level | Actual sales data | ### 3.4 Penalty Analysis Derivation (JAR Scale) **Step 1:** Mean overall liking is computed for two groups — those who rated the attribute **JAR** vs those who rated **non-JAR**. $$\overline{L}_{JAR} = \frac{1}{n_{JAR}} \sum_{i \in JAR} L_i, \quad \overline{L}_{non\text{-}JAR} = \frac{1}{n_{non\text{-}JAR}} \sum_{j \notin JAR} L_j$$ **Step 2:** The **Penalty** quantifies how much mean liking drops when the attribute is **not** JAR: $$\text{Penalty} = \overline{L}_{JAR} - \overline{L}_{non\text{-}JAR}$$ **Step 3:** A penalty is considered *significant* if it exceeds a threshold (commonly $\geq 0.5$ points on a 9-pt hedonic scale) **and** at least 20% of respondents are in the non-JAR group. > [!NOTE] > **Direction rule:** If most non-JAR responses are on the *"too little"* side, **increase** the attribute. If on the *"too much"* side, **decrease** it. ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison of Key Tests | Feature | Triangle | Paired-Preference | 9-pt Hedonic | JAR | Conjoint | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Detects difference? | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ⚠ Indirectly | ⚠ Indirectly | | Measures liking? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ Direct | ✅ Indirect | | Quantifies trade-off? | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | | Sample size needed | 25–75 | 30–100 | 75–300 | 75–200 | 150–500 | | Consumer/Expert panel | Either | Either | Consumer | Consumer | Consumer | | Statistical test | Binomial | Binomial | ANOVA | t-test, ANOVA | HB estimation | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Overall Funnel of Consumer Evaluation ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Concept Idea] --> B[Qualitative Research
Focus Groups IDIs] B --> C[Concept Screening
Kano Survey] C --> D[Prototype Development
Lab Bench] D --> E[Discrimination Tests
Triangle / Duo-Trio] E --> F[Descriptive Analysis
Trained Panel] F --> G[Optimization
JAR Penalty Analysis] G --> H[Acceptance Testing
Hedonic 9-pt CLT] H --> I[Trade-off Quantification
Conjoint CBC] I --> J[In-home Validation
Home Use Test HUT] J --> K[Simulated Market
STM] K --> L[Test Marketing
Real Region] L --> M[National Launch
Post Launch Tracking] style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00 style M fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400 style B fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082 style H fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#DC143C style I fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B ``` ## 4.2 Discrimination Test Logic Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Start: Two Product Variants A and B] --> Q1{Research Goal?} Q1 -->|Detect difference| Q2{Need preference info too?} Q1 -->|Measure liking| Hedonic[Use 9-pt Hedonic Scale] Q1 -->|Quantify trade-offs| Conjoint[Use Conjoint Analysis] Q2 -->|No| Tri[Triangle Test
n = 30 to 75] Q2 -->|Yes| PP[Paired Preference Test
n = 30 to 100] Tri --> Binom1[Binomial Test
p0 = 1/3] PP --> Binom2[Binomial Test
p0 = 1/2] Binom1 --> Dec1{Obtained x >= Critical x?} Binom2 --> Dec2{Obtained x >= Critical x?} Dec1 -->|Yes| Sig1[Significant Difference Detected] Dec1 -->|No| NS1[No Significant Difference] Dec2 -->|Yes| Sig2[Significant Preference] Dec2 -->|No| NS2[No Significant Preference] style Start fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00 style Hedonic fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B style Conjoint fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082 style Sig1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400 style Sig2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400 style NS1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000 style NS2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000 ``` ## 4.3 Conjoint Analysis Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Identify Product Attributes
and Levels] --> B[Design Full or Fractional
Factorial Profiles] B --> C[Generate Choice Sets
e.g. 12 to 18 sets] C --> D[Administer to
150 to 500 Consumers] D --> E[Collect Ratings or Choices] E --> F[Estimate Part-Worth
Utilities via HB or OLS] F --> G[Compute Relative
Importance] G --> H[Market Simulator
Predict Choice Share] H --> I[Optimize Product
Configuration] style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00 style F fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B style I fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400 ``` ## 4.4 JAR Penalty Analysis Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Consumer rates attribute
e.g. Sweetness on JAR 5-pt] --> Split{Separate respondents
by category} Split -->|Too little| T1[Group A: too little] Split -->|JAR| T2[Group B: just right] Split -->|Too much| T3[Group C: too much] T1 --> M1[Compute mean overall liking] T2 --> M2[Compute mean overall liking] T3 --> M3[Compute mean overall liking] M1 --> P1[Penalty A = M2 - M1] M2 --> Ref[Reference Liking] M3 --> P2[Penalty C = M2 - M3] P1 --> Dec{Penalty >= 0.5
and non-JAR >= 20 percent?} P2 --> Dec Dec -->|Yes| Act[Action: Reformulate
direction by majority side] Dec -->|No| NoAct[No action needed] style Start fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00 style Ref fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B style Act fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400 style NoAct fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000 ``` ## 4.5 Decision Tree — Choosing Between CLT, HUT, and Test Market ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Need real-world
consumer data?] --> Q1{Budget vs Accuracy trade-off} Q1 -->|Low budget
need fast signal| CLT[Central Location Test
n = 100 to 300
mall intercept] Q1 -->|Medium budget
need realistic context| HUT[Home Use Test
n = 100 to 300
7 to 14 days] Q1 -->|High budget
need real sales| TM[Test Marketing
one or two cities] CLT --> Out1[Liking and
purchase intent] HUT --> Out2[Repeat usage
and repurchase] TM --> Out3[Actual trial
and repeat rate] style Start fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00 style CLT fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B style HUT fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082 style TM fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define the term "Just-About-Right (JAR) scale" and state its purpose in food product optimization.** **Model Answer:** > The **Just-About-Right (JAR) scale** is a **5- or 7-point bipolar category scale** used in consumer research to evaluate the appropriateness of a *specific* product attribute (e.g., sweetness, saltiness, crispiness). The mid-point anchor is "Just-About-Right," while the extremes are "Too little" and "Too much." > > **Purpose:** It is used in **penalty analysis** to determine whether an attribute is at its optimal level. The penalty is calculated as the difference in mean overall liking between consumers who rate the attribute JAR versus those who do not. This guides **formulation reformulation decisions** during product development. (3 marks) ### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between discrimination tests and affective tests in sensory evaluation. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Discrimination Tests | Affective Tests | |---|---|---| | **Question answered** | "Is there a difference?" | "How much do you like it?" | | **Subject** | Often trained or naïve panel | Consumers (target users) | | **Statistical basis** | Binomial distribution | ANOVA / non-parametric | | **Outcome** | Significance of difference | Mean liking / preference | | **Example** | **Triangle test, Duo-trio test, 2-AFC** | **9-point Hedonic scale, JAR scale, Preference ranking** | (3 marks) --- ## 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - Students often confuse **discrimination** with **affective** tests — remember: discrimination ≠ preference ≠ liking. > - In Conjoint Analysis, students forget to compute **relative importance** of attributes, which carries 3–4 marks. > - Always specify **sample size** and **statistical test** for any consumer methodology — losing these marks is a common pitfall. > - Mentioning **ISO standards** (e.g., ISO 4120 for triangle test) adds valuation weight. --- ### **Part B – Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]` **(a)** Explain the **Triangle Test** in detail. How is the result statistically interpreted? Derive the critical value for $n = 50$ at $\alpha = 0.05$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **methodology of Conjoint Analysis** for evaluating consumer preferences. With a suitable example, explain how **part-worth utilities** and **relative importance** are computed. **(7 marks)** ### ✅ Model Solution — Question A #### Part (a) — Triangle Test (7 Marks) **Definition (1 mark):** The triangle test (ISO 4120:2004) is a **forced-choice discrimination test** in which each panellist is presented with **three coded samples** — two identical (A) and one different (B) — and is asked to identify the odd sample. **Procedure (2 marks):** 1. Sample preparation: prepare 3 coded samples per panellist (two of one variant, one of the other). 2. Randomized presentation order across panellists (counter-balanced). 3. Panellist forced to choose — "no difference" option is **not** allowed. 4. Record the number of correct identifications $x$ out of $n$ panellists. **Statistical Interpretation (2 marks):** - Under $H_0$ (no perceived difference): $p_0 = 1/3 \approx 0.3333$. - Under $H_1$ (perceived difference exists): $p > p_0$. - Test statistic: $X \sim \text{Binomial}(n, p_0)$. - Reject $H_0$ at $\alpha = 0.05$ if $X \geq x_c$ (critical value from binomial table). **Derivation for $n = 50$ (2 marks):** $$p_0 = \frac{1}{3} = 0.3333$$ $$E[X] = n p_0 = 50 \times 0.3333 = 16.67$$ Standard deviation: $$\sigma = \sqrt{n p_0 (1-p_0)} = \sqrt{50 \times 0.3333 \times 0.6667} = \sqrt{11.11} = 3.333$$ Critical value (using binomial table, $\alpha = 0.05$): $$x_c = 23 \text{ (correct identifications)}$$ So, if 23 or more out of 50 panellists correctly identify the odd sample, the products are perceived as significantly different at the 5% level. #### Part (b) — Conjoint Analysis (7 Marks) **Definition (1 mark):** Conjoint analysis is a **decompositional multivariate technique** that estimates the contribution of each product attribute to overall consumer preference, based on ratings of complete product profiles. **Steps (3 marks):** 1. **Identify attributes and levels.** Example for a fruit yoghurt: - Brand (Local, National, Premium) — 3 levels - Fruit (Mango, Mixed Berry, Strawberry) — 3 levels - Sugar (Low, Regular, High) — 3 levels - Price (₹20, ₹30, ₹40) — 3 levels 2. **Create product profiles.** Full factorial = $3^4 = 81$ combinations (impractical); use **orthogonal fractional factorial** (e.g., 9–16 profiles). 3. **Collect ratings.** Each consumer rates or ranks profiles on a 1–9 preference scale (or selects in CBC). 4. **Estimate part-worth utilities** $\beta_j$ via **Ordinary Least Squares** or **Hierarchical Bayes (HB)**. **Computation Example (3 marks):** Suppose after OLS estimation the part-worth ranges are: | Attribute | Range of $\beta$ (Lowest to Highest Level) | |---|---| | Brand | 1.5 | | Fruit | 2.0 | | Sugar | 1.0 | | Price | 3.5 | Total range = $1.5 + 2.0 + 1.0 + 3.5 = 8.0$ Relative importance: $$\text{Importance}_{\text{Price}} = \frac{3.5}{8.0} \times 100 = 43.75\%$$ $$\text{Importance}_{\text{Fruit}} = \frac{2.0}{8.0} \times 100 = 25\%$$ $$\text{Importance}_{\text{Brand}} = \frac{1.5}{8.0} \times 100 = 18.75\%$$ $$\text{Importance}_{\text{Sugar}} = \frac{1.0}{8.0} \times 100 = 12.5\%$$ **Conclusion:** Price dominates, so a competitive pricing strategy is most critical for market success. --- ### **Part B – Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **(a)** What is **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)**? Explain the **House of Quality** matrix with reference to its key components. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **methodology of Home-Use Test (HUT)** and **Central Location Test (CLT)** for assessing market acceptance. Compare the two. **(7 marks)** ### ✅ Model Solution — Question B #### Part (a) — Quality Function Deployment (7 Marks) **Definition (1 mark):** QFD is a **structured planning framework** that translates the **"Voice of the Customer" (VOC)** into specific **technical/engineering requirements** for product and process design. It was developed by Yoji Akao in Japan (1972). **House of Quality (HoQ) — Matrix Structure (4 marks):** The House of Quality is a matrix with the following blocks: | Block | Content | |---|---| | **Whats (Customer Requirements / VOC)** | Customer-stated needs, e.g., "crispy", "low calorie", "easy to open" | | **Hows (Technical Requirements)** | Engineering attributes, e.g., moisture content, package tensile strength | | **Relationship Matrix (Central)** | Symbols (● strong, ○ medium, △ weak) linking Whats ↔ Hows | | **Roof (Correlation Matrix)** | Shows inter-relationships between Hows (e.g., increasing moisture ↓ crispiness) | | **Right-side (Competitive Benchmarking)** | Customer ratings of competitors on each Whats | | **Bottom (Technical Benchmarking + Targets)** | Targets for each How, derived from competitor analysis | **Working (2 marks):** 1. Collect VOC via focus groups / surveys. 2. Quantify customer importance weights. 3. Engineer translates each What → How via the relationship matrix. 4. Set technical targets by considering competitor benchmarks. 5. The output prioritizes which technical attributes need most engineering effort. #### Part (b) — HUT vs CLT (7 Marks) **Home-Use Test (HUT) — 3 marks:** - **Definition:** Product is delivered to consumers' homes for use in a realistic environment over **7–21 days**. - **Sample size:** 100–300 households. - **Procedure:** 1. Recruit target users. 2. Deliver product + usage diary. 3. Periodic phone/online check-ins. 4. Final questionnaire: liking, repurchase intent, perceived pros/cons. - **Advantages:** Realistic context, repeated exposure, captures habitual use. - **Limitations:** Long duration, panel fatigue, environmental control lost. **Central Location Test (CLT) — 2 marks:** - **Definition:** A **mall-intercept or venue-based** test where consumers evaluate product in a controlled booth. - **Sample size:** 100–300 respondents (often 75–150 minimum). - **Procedure:** Tasting followed by 9-pt hedonic + JAR + purchase intent questions. - **Advantages:** Quick, controlled, lower cost. - **Limitations:** Single exposure, no repeated use, artificial environment. **Comparison Table (2 marks):** | Aspect | CLT | HUT | |---|---|---| | Location | Central booth (mall) | Consumer's home | | Duration | 1 day | 7–21 days | | Repeated use? | No | Yes | | Realism | Low | High | | Cost | Low | Medium | | Sample size | 75–300 | 100–300 | | Key output | First-impression liking | Realistic usage, repurchase | --- ## 🧾 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 ESE** - ✅ **Three pillars** of consumer evaluation: **Sensory**, **Consumer/Attitudinal**, **Market Acceptance**. - ✅ **Triangle test** → $p_0 = 1/3$, critical $x_c$ from binomial table; for $n=30$, $x_c=15$. - ✅ **Duo-trio test** → $p_0 = 1/2$; **Paired test** → $p_0 = 1/2$. - ✅ **9-point Hedonic Scale** is the **gold standard** for liking. - ✅ **JAR scale** → used with **Penalty Analysis** to optimize attributes. - ✅ **Sample size formula:** $n = \frac{z_{\alpha/2}^2 \, p(1-p)}{E^2}$ — use $p = 0.5$ for maximum. - ✅ **Conjoint analysis** outputs: **Part-worth utilities (β)** and **Relative Importance (%)**. - ✅ **CBC (Choice-Based Conjoint)** is closer to real-world purchase behaviour than rating-based conjoint. - ✅ **QFD — House of Quality** is the bridge from VOC to technical specs. - ✅ **Kano Model**: Must-be / One-dimensional / Attractive features. - ✅ **CLT** is fast but artificial; **HUT** is realistic but slow; **Test marketing** is real but expensive. - ✅ **NPS** = % Promoters − % Detractors; scale −100 to +100. - ✅ Always mention **ISO standards** for sensory tests in answers for higher marks. - ✅ **Difference tests ≠ Affective tests** — common 14-mark trap.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT834 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Studies: Successes and Failures, Innovation, Best Practices, Technological and Marketing Approaches to NPD

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **New Product Development (NPD)** in the food industry is the disciplined, cross-functional process of transforming a market opportunity or technological breakthrough into a commercially viable, shelf-stable, consumer-acceptable food product, progressing from ideation through commercialization, launch, and post-launch review.

A **Case Study** in the context of food product design and development is a structured, evidence-based retrospective analysis of a real NPD project—either a celebrated **Success** (e.g., Amul's Cheese Spread, Maggi's re-entry into India) or a documented **Failure** (e.g., Heinz Green Ketchup, McDonald's Arch Deluxe)—used to extract transferable learning points about innovation strategy, technology selection, consumer insight, and marketing execution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Innovation** in food NPD refers to the purposeful introduction of a new product, process, packaging, or business model that delivers measurable value to the consumer, manufacturer, and society. The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies it into four archetypes: **Incremental, Disruptive, Architectural, and Discontinuous (Radical)**, as per the Henderson-Clark (1990) and Christensen (1997) taxonomies adopted in modern food business curricula.

**Best Practices** in NPD are the empirically validated, repeatable operational protocols—such as Stage-Gate® (Cooper, 1986), Design Thinking (IDEO/Stanford d.school), and **House of Quality (HoQ)**—that consistently maximize the probability of launch success and minimize time-to-market.

**Technological Approaches** encompass the enabling sciences driving modern NPD: **food extrusion, high-pressure processing (HPP), pulsed electric field (PEF), 3D food printing, fermentation technology (precision/traditional), encapsulation, and AI-driven sensory prediction**.

**Marketing Approaches** are the strategic frameworks connecting product to consumer: the **4Ps/7Ps Marketing Mix, Kotler's Five-Product-Level Model, Blue Ocean Strategy, and the Adoption-Diffusion Curve** (Rogers, 1962).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **The "Restaurant Test Kitchen" Analogy:** Imagine an R&D chef in a corporate test kitchen (the NPD team). Each new recipe they invent must survive three judges: the **Head Chef** (Technical Feasibility — can it actually be made at scale?), the **Restaurant Critic** (Consumer Acceptability — will customers order it again?), and the **Accountant** (Commercial Viability — will it make money at ₹X price point?). A *Case Study* is the post-mortem logbook of past recipes that either earned a Michelin star (Success) or were quietly removed from the menu (Failure). *Innovation* is the chef's willingness to experiment with molecular gastronomy or fusion techniques; *Best Practices* are the disciplined tasting protocols every recipe must pass before menu inclusion; *Technology* is the molecular gastronomy toolkit; *Marketing* is the front-of-house team translating the dish's story to the diner.

**Plain-English Summary for First-Time Learners:**
- A **Success Case** shows you *what to repeat*.
- A **Failure Case** shows you *what to avoid*—often more valuable than a success.
- **Innovation** is not invention; it is invention that is *adopted* by the market.
- **Best Practices** are the "how" of doing NPD reliably.
- **Technology + Marketing** are the two engines of competitive advantage in food NPD.

---

## 1.3 Key Empirical Benchmarks (Industry-Standard Metrics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following are universally cited industry metrics students must memorize for KTU ESE:
> - **Success Rate of New Food Products:** Only **1 in 10** (~**10%**) launched CPG food products achieve commercial success in Year 1 (Booz Allen Hamilton / GMA Model, adapted to food sector).
> - **Failure Cost Multiplier:** A failure detected at *concept* stage costs **1×** to fix; detected at *launch* costs **100×**.
> - **Speed-to-Market:** Best-in-class food companies reduce NPD cycle from **24–36 months → 9–15 months** using Stage-Gate.
> - **Open Innovation Adoption:** Over **78%** of top global F&B companies (Nestlé, Unilever, Danone) actively crowdsource ideas via external platforms.
> - **Kerala-Specific Insight:** **NADCP (National Animal Disease Control Programme)**-aligned dairy innovations, **Jackfruit 365** (Kerala startup) gluten-free jackfruit flour, and **NIRAVANA** banana-based products are KTU-recommended regional NPD case references.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

## 2.1 The Four Archetypes of Food Innovation

Innovation in food is **not a single act** but a continuum of novelty levels. Drawing from the **Henderson-Clark (1990) Innovation Matrix** and **Christensen's Disruptive Innovation (1997)**, food innovators classify their work as:

| # | Innovation Type | Definition | Food Industry Example | Risk Profile |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Incremental** | Line extension / flavor / packaging tweak of existing product | Maggi Hot & Sweet (chili variant) | Low (60–80% success) |
| 2 | **Architectural** | Re-combining existing tech into a new product structure | Activia (combining yogurt + Bifidus probiotic) | Medium (30–50%) |
| 3 | **Disruptive** | Low-cost, simple solution targeting non-consumers | Amul Pro (Whey-based RTM protein for Tier-2/3) | High (10–25%) |
| 4 | **Discontinuous / Radical** | New core technology + new market | 3D-printed personalized nutrition, *NotCo* AI-designed plant-based dairy | Very High (<10%) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When a question asks "Classify the innovation," the expected answer must (a) **name** the type, (b) **justify** using the technology vs. market axes, and (c) give a **matching food example**.

## 2.2 The Stage-Gate® Framework (Cooper, 1986) — Industry Standard NPD Process

The **gold-standard** process every KTU student must know:

| Stage | Activity | Gate Decision | Typical Duration |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stage 0 — Discovery / Ideation** | Idea generation (internal + external, ~60–100 ideas) | — | 1–2 months |
| **Stage 1 — Scoping (Concept)** | Quick market, technical, financial assessment → Mini Business Case | **Gate 1: Go / Kill / Recycle** | 1–2 months |
| **Stage 2 — Build (Business Case)** | Detailed consumer research, formulation, feasibility | **Gate 2: Go / Kill / Hold** | 3–6 months |
| **Stage 3 — Development (Lab to Pilot)** | Lab prototypes, sensory tests, shelf-life, plant trials | **Gate 3: Go / Kill / Re-scope** | 4–9 months |
| **Stage 4 — Testing & Validation** | In-home use tests (IHUT), test markets, line trials | **Gate 4: Go / Kill / Restructure** | 3–6 months |
| **Stage 5 — Launch / Commercialization** | Full scale-up, distribution, marketing rollout | **Post-launch Review** | 1–3 months |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Variants:** Modern food firms integrate **Agile NPD** (shorter sprints, cross-functional squads) and **Design Thinking** (Empathize → Define → Ideate → Prototype → Test) within Stage-Gate to reduce rigidity.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Metric Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Although this module is qualitative, the following **quantitative formulas** are frequently tested as 3-mark part (a) sub-questions.

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **NPD Success Rate** | $S = \dfrac{N_{\text{success}}}{N_{\text{launched}}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of launches profitable by Year 2 | **%** |
| 2 | **Cost-of-Failure Multiplier** | $C_{n} = C_{1} \times 10^{(n-1)}$ | Cost increases by **10× per stage skipped** | **Index** |
| 3 | **Break-Even Sales Volume** | $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{F}{(P - V)}$ | Units to recover fixed launch cost F at price P, variable cost V | **Units** |
| 4 | **Adoption Rate (Bass Diffusion)** | $\dfrac{dN(t)}{dt} = \left(p + q\dfrac{N(t)}{m}\right)\left(m - N(t)\right)$ | New triers per period; p = innovation coeff., q = imitation coeff. | **Persons / period** |
| 5 | **House of Quality Correlation** | $T_{ij} = S_{ij} \times R_{ij} \times W_{j}$ | Technical priority = Strength × Relationship × Customer weight | **Score (1–9)** |
| 6 | **Marketing ROI** | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{(R - M)}{M} \times 100\%$ | R = revenue lift attributable to marketing, M = spend | **%** |
| 7 | **Shelf-Life Prediction (Q₁₀)** | $Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}\right)^{\dfrac{10}{T_{2}-T_{1}}}$ | Rate change per 10°C; key for accelerated NPD stability | **Dimensionless** |
| 8 | **Sensory Acceptance Threshold** | $\%\,\text{Acceptance} \geq 60\%$ | Minimum consumer panel acceptance for launch | **%** |

> [!WARNING]
> In markdown tables, never write $\vert x \vert$; instead, render absolute value as $\lvert x \rvert$ to preserve table column boundaries.

## 2.4 Best Practices — The "Top 10" Evidence-Based NPD Levers

Compiled from Ernst & Young, McKinsey, and IFT Food Technology reviews:

1. **Front-Load Innovation** — Spend 50% of total NPD budget in Stages 1–2 (concept); saves 10× in Stage 5.
2. **Cross-Functional Core Team** — Marketing + R&D + Operations + Regulatory + Finance from Day 1.
3. **Voice-of-Customer (VoC)** — Conduct ≥ **3 touchpoints** (qualitative + quantitative + ethnographic).
4. **Concurrent Engineering** — Run formulation, packaging, and process design in parallel, not sequence.
5. **Sensory Science Integration** — Use **descriptive analysis (DA)** + **consumer affective tests** (hedonic 9-point).
6. **Stage-Gate Discipline** — No "go" at Gate n without a written deliverable and signed-off Go/Kill memo.
7. **Open Innovation & Co-Creation** — Partner with startups, universities (e.g., IIT-K, NIFTEM-K, CFTRI).
8. **Failure Logbook** — Mandatory pre-mortem: "If this product failed in 12 months, why?" (Klein, 2007).
9. **Pilot Plant Validation** — At least **3 successful pilot runs** before commercial scale-up.
10. **Post-Launch Review (PLR)** at **6 and 12 months** — every launch is a learning case study.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

These frameworks are operational daily inside: **Nestlé's Product Technology Centres (PTs)** in Manesar (India) and Konolfingen (Switzerland); **Mondelez's Cocoa Research Centre**; **ITC Foods' Innovation Centre** at Bengaluru; and **Kerala's own** NABL-accredited labs at CFTRI extension and Amul Dairy (Anand).
