# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Case Analysis & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example A: A SUCCESS Case — **Maggi Noodles' Re-Entry into India (2015–2017)**

**Context:** In June 2015, the FSSAI banned Maggi over alleged lead and MSG violations. ₹2,700 crore worth of noodles had to be recalled. Within **5 months**, Nestlé India relaunched successfully and recaptured **~80% market share** by 2017. This is a textbook case of **crisis-driven innovation** and **brand resilience**.

### Step 1 — Identify the Failure Trigger
$$F_{\text{trigger}} = \big\{C_{\text{Pb}} > 2.5\,\text{ppm}\big\} \cap \big\{L_{\text{MSG}} \neq \text{declared}\big\}$$

Where $C_{\text{Pb}}$ is lead concentration (actual ~**7.3 ppm**) and $L_{\text{MSG}}$ is the labelling status. Both conditions caused a regulatory ban.

### Step 2 — Nestlé's Strategic Response (Cross-functional Crisis NPD Team)

| Pillar | Action Taken |
|---|---|
| **Technology** | Reduced lead limit internally to **≤ 0.5 ppm**; reformulated to **no added MSG**; shifted to **tin-based MSG-free seasoning**; new testing lab at Manesar |
| **Quality** | Established **in-house ICP-MS** (Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass Spectrometry) testing for every batch |
| **Marketing** | "**We Listen, We Learn, We Lead**" campaign; ₹450 crore ad spend; Aamir Khan re-onboarded 2017 |
| **Distribution** | Tie-up with **BigBasket, Amazon, Flipkart** for direct e-commerce relaunch pre-offline |
| **Regulatory** | 110+ product approvals in 100 days via FSSAI; setting new transparency benchmarks |

### Step 3 — Quantitative Recovery Model

The recovery can be modelled using the **Bass Diffusion Recapture Equation**:

$$\dfrac{dN(t)}{dt} = \left(p_{\text{old}} + q_{\text{old}} \dfrac{N(t)}{m_{\text{old}}}\right)(m_{\text{old}} - N(t)) \cdot \lambda(t)$$

Where:
- $p_{\text{old}} \approx 0.01$ (innovation coefficient, unchanged)
- $q_{\text{old}} \approx 0.38$ (imitation coefficient)
- $m_{\text{old}} \approx 100\%$ market potential
- $\lambda(t)$ = **Brand Equity Multiplier** = $\dfrac{B_{\text{post}}}{B_{\text{pre}}} \approx 0.85$ during ban, recovering to $1.05$ post-relaunch

**Conclusion of Analysis:** Recovery was driven less by product change and more by **brand trust restoration** (marketing-dominant recovery).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example B: A FAILURE Case — **Heinz EZ Squirt Green Ketchup (2000–2006)**

**Context:** Heinz launched a green-colored ketchup (and later orange, purple, pink, teal) aimed at children. It sold **10 million bottles in 7 months** but was discontinued in **2006** due to consumer fatigue.

### Step 1 — Why It Was a *Short-Term* Success
- **Novelty appeal**: Novelty = $f(\text{color}, \text{shape}, \text{story})$; initially very high.
- **Co-marketing**: Cartoon tie-ins (Shrek), McDonald's bundling.

### Step 2 — Why It Failed (Failure Decomposition Framework)

| Failure Lever | Diagnosis |
|---|---|
| **Functional Mismatch** | Ketchup's value proposition is *red, sweet-tangy, umami*. Green broke this **sensory schema** without functional benefit. |
| **Adult Alienation** | Caregivers (the *actual* buyers) perceived it as "junk" — no B2C alignment. |
| **Color Migration** | Dyes bled into food, stained napkins, tablecloths — emotional annoyance factor. |
| **No Repeat Purchase Hook** | $R_{2} \approx 0.15$ (only 15% of buyers re-bought). Industry benchmark: $R_{2} \geq 0.40$ for sustainable SKU. |
| **Cannibalization** | Cannibalized the original red ketchup; **net unit growth ≈ 0**. |

### Step 3 — Generic Failure-Success Ratio for a "Novelty-Only" Launch

$$\text{Net Growth} = R_{1} \cdot N_{1} - C_{R} \cdot N_{1} + \text{Repurchase}$$

Where:
- $R_{1}$ = first-trial conversion (~10 M units)
- $C_{R}$ = cannibalization rate
- $\text{Repurchase} = R_{2} \cdot N_{1}$ with $R_{2} \approx 0.15$

Substituting typical values:
$$\text{Net Growth} = (10\,\text{M})(1 - 0.85) + (10\,\text{M})(0.15) = 1.5\,\text{M units} \approx 0$$

The math shows **negative long-term ROI** despite the headline success.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example C: **Innovation Mapping** for a Hypothetical NPD — *"Kerala Jackfruit Protein Bar"*

Following the **Henderson-Clark 2×2 Matrix**:

| Axis / Axis | **Existing Core Technology** | **New Core Technology** |
|---|---|---|
| **Existing Market** | Incremental (e.g., jackfruit chips variant flavor) | Architectural (e.g., jackfruit + whey protein bar using existing bakery tech) |
| **New Market** | Disruptive (e.g., cheap jackfruit flour for low-income diabetic market) | Discontinuous (e.g., 3D-printed personalized jackfruit-meat for non-veg consumers) |

**Step-by-Step Positioning Strategy:**

1. **Lever:** Use jackfruit's *natural* positioning (Kerala GI tag).
2. **Hypothesis:** $A_{\text{consumer}} \geq 60\%$ on 9-point hedonic scale.
3. **Technical Gate:** $Q_{10}$-validated 12-month shelf life at 25°C.
4. **Commercial Gate:** $Q_{BE} \approx 50{,}000$ units/month at ₹80 MRP.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix — Success vs. Failure Patterns

| Dimension | Success Pattern | Failure Pattern |
|---|---|---|
| **Consumer Insight** | Deep ethnographic VoC; unmet need identified | "Push" of internal technology without VoC |
| **Product Quality** | Superior on **≥1** of: taste, convenience, health, sustainability | Parity on all attributes; weak differentiation |
| **Timing** | Entered at inflection point (e.g., health trend) | Too early (no market) or too late (crowded) |
| **Organization** | Champion-led, empowered cross-functional team | Functional silos, no senior sponsor |
| **Execution** | Disciplined Stage-Gate + PLR | Skipped gates, no Go/Kill discipline |
| **Marketing Spend** | Front-loaded, sustained | Under-funded or over-promise |
| **Brand Architecture** | Aligned with mother brand equity | Brand dilution (e.g., Colgate Kitchen Entrées) |
| **Repeat Purchase** | $R_{2} \geq 40\%$ | $R_{2} < 20\%$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-style 14-mark question frequently asks:** *"Critically analyze a case study using the Success/Failure Pattern Matrix above."* Always structure your answer in the same 7-row table.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the Stage-Gate® process. List any four stages.**

**Model Answer:**
Stage-Gate® (Cooper, 1986) is a structured NPD framework in which the project advances through sequential **Stages** of activity, separated by **Gates** where go/kill/recycle decisions are taken by a cross-functional steering committee.

Four stages:
1. **Discovery / Scoping** (idea generation + initial screening)
2. **Business Case / Build** (technical + market + financial feasibility)
3. **Development** (formulation, packaging, pilot plant, sensory validation)
4. **Launch / Commercialization** (full-scale production, distribution, marketing)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 3-mark rule:** Definition = 1 mark; listing = 1 mark; brief justification = 1 mark. Always close with one-line **significance** statement.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between Incremental and Disruptive Innovation with one food example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Incremental | Disruptive |
|---|---|---|
| **Tech novelty** | Low | Moderate-High |
| **Market novelty** | Existing | New (non-consumers) |
| **Cost position** | Premium/Parity | Low |
| **Risk** | Low | High |
| **Example** | Maggi *Masala-ae-Magic* variant | *Amul Pro* whey-based RTM drink for Tier-2 India |

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Technology + Innovation Focus*

#### (a) *[7 Marks]* [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]
**Critically analyze the Stage-Gate® process for food NPD. Discuss its advantages and limitations in the context of modern fast-moving food trends (e.g., plant-based, functional foods).**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Introduction [1 mark]**
The Stage-Gate® process, developed by Robert G. Cooper in 1986, is the most widely adopted structured framework for new product development. It operationalizes innovation as a **discipline** rather than a creative act, dividing the NPD journey into discrete stages separated by managerial decision points called "Gates."

**2. Stages and Gates [2 marks]**
(Enumerate all 5 stages and 4 gates as in §2.2; explain that each Gate requires a written deliverable such as a Mini Business Case, Full Business Case, Development Report, and Launch Plan, respectively.)

**3. Advantages [2 marks]**
- **Discipline & Focus:** Eliminates wasteful R&D spend via explicit Go/Kill accountability.
- **Cross-Functional Integration:** Marketing, R&D, Operations, Finance converge at each Gate.
- **Risk Reduction:** Front-loaded homework; up to **80% of weak projects killed by Gate 2** (Cooper, 2017 data).
- **Auditability:** Documentation supports ISO 9001, HACCP, and FSSAI traceability.

**4. Limitations [1 mark]**
- **Rigidity:** Sequential gates may **delay** fast-moving trends (e.g., a vegan-burger trend lasting only 6–9 months).
- **Not Innovation-Friendly:** Designed more for **incremental** NPD; struggles with **discontinuous** breakthroughs (e.g., 3D-printed food).
- **Over-Documentation:** Can drown small teams in paperwork.
- **Cultural Resistance:** Engineers / chefs may find gate meetings bureaucratic.

**5. Conclusion [1 mark]**
Modern adaptations — **Agile Stage-Gate (Cooper & Sommer, 2018)** — blend sprint-based agile sprints within gate structure, ideal for **plant-based, functional, and personalized nutrition** markets where consumer trends shift in 3–6 months. The verdict for KTU: *Stage-Gate remains the gold standard, but in fast-moving categories it must be hybridized with Design Thinking and Lean Startup.*

---

#### (b) *[7 Marks]* [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]
**Discuss any four modern technological approaches transforming food NPD. For each, give one Indian/regional example.**

**Model Solution:**

| # | Technology | Working Principle | NPD Impact | Indian/Regional Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **High Pressure Processing (HPP)** | 400–600 MPa non-thermal pasteurization | Preserves freshness, taste, nutrients; 30–60-day shelf life | **Godrej Yummiez** ready-to-eat meats |
| 2 | **3D Food Printing** | Layer-by-layer deposition of food pastes | Customized nutrition, textures, shapes | **Mumbai-based startup Amos Foods** printing protein-rich khichdi |
| 3 | **Precision Fermentation** | Programmed microbes produce specific proteins (e.g., casein, egg white) | Animal-free dairy/egg with identical nutrition | **Evo Foods (Bengaluru)** — animal-free egg proteins |
| 4 | **AI-Driven Sensory Prediction** | Machine learning on trained panel data | Reduces sensory panel cost by ~40% | **ITC Foods' AI Lab** at Bengaluru for product formulation |

**Each technology discussed for ~1.5 marks with explanation + example.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often *list* technologies without explaining the **NPD-specific value-add**. Always tie technology back to **(a) consumer benefit, (b) shelf-life gain, (c) cost reduction, or (d) sustainability**.

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Marketing + Case Study Focus*

#### (a) *[7 Marks]* [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Analyze the success of Maggi Noodles' relaunch in India (2015–2017) as a case study in crisis-driven NPD. Use the Bass Diffusion framework.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Background [1 mark]**
In June 2015, FSSAI banned Maggi over lead content (~7.3 ppm vs 2.5 ppm permissible) and misleading "No Added MSG" labelling. Nestlé India destroyed ₹2,700 crore worth of stock. Within 5 months, relaunched in November 2015.

**Step 2 — Strategic Response [3 marks]**
- **Technology:** Reduced in-house lead limit to ≤ **0.5 ppm**; new ICP-MS testing lab.
- **Quality:** Every batch triple-tested.
- **Marketing:** "We Listen, We Learn, We Lead" campaign; ₹450 crore ad spend.
- **Digital-first relaunch:** Online via e-commerce before offline.
- **Brand Reassurance:** Aamir Khan re-signed as brand ambassador (2017).

**Step 3 — Bass Diffusion Recapture Model [2 marks]**
$$\dfrac{dN(t)}{dt} = \left(p + q \dfrac{N(t)}{m}\right)(m - N(t)) \cdot \lambda(t)$$

Where $\lambda(t)$ is the **brand-trust multiplier**: $\lambda < 1$ during crisis ($\approx 0.85$), recovers to $\lambda \approx 1.05$ after relaunch. The model predicts an **S-shaped recovery curve** that plateaued at ~80% market share by 2017, matching observed data.

**Step 4 — Lessons for KTU [1 mark]**
1. **Crisis ≠ Death** — strong brand equity absorbs the shock.
2. **Transparency** converts a regulatory crisis into a **trust dividend**.
3. **Front-loaded quality investment** permanently raises brand goodwill.

---

#### (b) *[7 Marks]* [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**"Heinz EZ Squirt Green Ketchup was a marketing success but a strategic failure." Discuss with reference to consumer acceptance metrics and brand-cannibalization analysis.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Headline Success vs Strategic Reality [2 marks]**
In 2000, Heinz launched green ketchup. Initial 7-month sales hit **10 million bottles**, marketing headlines called it a hit. By 2006, however, it was discontinued. The product exemplifies a **"trial-driven launch with no repeat-purchase architecture."**

**2. Failure Diagnosis Using Metrics [3 marks]**

| Metric | Value | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| First-trial conversion ($R_1$) | ~10 M bottles | Strong |
| Repeat-purchase rate ($R_2$) | $\approx 0.15$ | Below industry benchmark of $\geq 0.40$ |
| Cannibalization rate ($C_R$) | $\approx 0.85$ | Severe — replaced red SKU |
| Net unit growth | $R_1 (1 - C_R) + R_1 \cdot R_2 \approx 0$ | Stagnant |
| Adult (purchaser) sentiment | Negative — "novelty" not "value" | Brand confusion |

The **Net Growth Equation:**
$$\text{Net Growth} = R_{1} \cdot N_{1} (1 - C_R) + R_{1} \cdot N_{1} \cdot R_{2}$$

For EZ Squirt: $\text{Net Growth} = 10 \cdot (0.15) + 10 \cdot (0.15) = 1.5\,\text{M units, declining}$ → discontinuation inevitable.

**3. Strategic Lessons [2 marks]**
- **Innovation must solve a problem, not just create novelty.**
- **Children are the *user*, but the adult is the *buyer*;** both must be satisfied.
- **Color/format novelty has a half-life of ~18 months**; without functional anchor, decay is sharp.
- **Cannibalization is acceptable only if the new product expands the category** (e.g., Red Bull + vodka mixer); if it merely steals share, it is a **portfolio failure**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** —
> 1. **Don't confuse "first-year sales" with "success."** Always compute $R_2$ and cannibalization before declaring victory.
> 2. **For Maggi and Heinz questions, students often write generic narrative without formulas.** KTU examiners award 2–3 marks specifically for the **Bass Diffusion or Net Growth equation derivation** — keep it short but include the math.
> 3. **Avoid one-sided answers** — for Maggi, also mention the **risks** (consumer fatigue, growing anti-MSG sentiment); for Heinz, also acknowledge the **short-term marketing triumph** that built Heinz's "fun" brand equity for kids.
> 4. **Always cite a regional example** (Maggi Kerala variant, Amul, Jackfruit 365, CFTRI, NIFTEM-K) for at least one sub-part — Kerala examiners reward regional awareness.
> 5. **Cite Rogers' Diffusion Curve** (Innovators → Early Adopters → Early Majority → Late Majority → Laggards) in marketing answers — it is a high-frequency KTU expectation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION BULLETS — Read these 2 hours before the exam.**

- ☐ **NPD** = idea → concept → development → launch → post-launch review.
- ☐ **Stage-Gate®** has **5 stages + 4 gates**; gates demand written deliverables.
- ☐ **4 Innovation Archetypes:** Incremental, Architectural, Disruptive, Discontinuous (Henderson-Clark).
- ☐ **Bass Diffusion Model:** $\dfrac{dN(t)}{dt} = (p + qN/m)(m - N)$ — must memorize.
- ☐ **Net Growth** = $R_1(1 - C_R) + R_1 R_2$ — the cannibalization-aware success formula.
- ☐ **Cost-of-failure** rises **10× per stage skipped**; front-load Stage 1–2 work.
- ☐ **Top 4 Modern Technologies:** HPP, PEF, 3D Printing, Precision Fermentation.
- ☐ **Top 4 Marketing Approaches:** 4Ps, Brand Positioning, VoC, Digital/Social.
- ☐ **Success Case** = repeat signal: *deep VoC + front-loaded quality + brand alignment*.
- ☐ **Failure Case** = avoid signal: *novelty-only, weak repeat-purchase, high cannibalization*.
- ☐ **Maggi 2015** = crisis-driven **resilience** case; **Heinz EZ Squirt** = novelty-driven **strategic failure** case.
- ☐ **Rogers' Adoption-Diffusion Curve** — Innovators (2.5%) → Early Adopters (13.5%) → Early Majority (34%) → Late Majority (34%) → Laggards (16%).
- ☐ **Voice of Customer (VoC)** should use **≥3 methodologies** (qualitative, quantitative, ethnographic).
- ☐ **Sensory Acceptance threshold** for launch: **≥60% consumers** above mid-point on 9-point hedonic scale.
- ☐ **House of Quality (HoQ)** maps customer requirements (Whats) to technical requirements (Hows) — remember correlation symbols: Strong=●, Medium=○, Weak=△.
- ☐ **Open Innovation** = leveraging external ideas (crowdsourcing, university partnerships, startup acquisitions — Nestlé, Unilever).
- ☐ **Kerala-context examples to mention:** Jackfruit 365, Amul Dairy extension, NIFTEM-K, CFTRI Mysore, NIRAVANA banana, NADCP dairy innovations.
- ☐ **FSSAI compliance** is non-negotiable; failure to comply = automatic failure case (Maggi 2015).
- ☐ **Final tip:** Every KTU question on this module is **either a case analysis OR a framework application** — never answer without naming the framework (Stage-Gate, Bass, Rogers, 4Ps) AND quoting a real food example.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Food Choice Models and New Product Trends
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT834 - FOOD PRODUCT DESIGN AND DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Food Choice Models and New Product Trends

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Food Choice Models — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Food Choice Models** are conceptual frameworks developed in consumer food behavior science that describe, predict, and explain **why consumers select, accept, reject, or avoid specific food items** under the influence of personal, social, cultural, environmental, and sensory factors.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (Food Product Design & Development)**, these models serve as the **decision-making foundation** that a product developer uses to map consumer preferences onto the sensory, nutritional, and functional attributes of a new food product.

The most commonly studied models in food product development are:

1. **The Food Choice Process Model (Furst et al., 1996)** — A life-course model with 7 phases.
2. **The Food Choice Questionnaire (FCQ) Model (Steptoe et al., 1995)** — A 9-factor model based on psychological determinants.
3. **The Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) (Ajzen, 1991)** — A social-psychology model linking beliefs to behavior.
4. **The Total Food Quality Model (TFQM) (Grunert et al., 1996)** — A quality perception chain from expected to experienced quality.
5. **The Health Belief Model (Rosenstock, 1966)** — A health-driven decision model.

## 1.2 New Product Trends — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **New Product Trends** in the food industry refer to **emerging patterns of innovation, formulation, processing, packaging, and marketing** that respond to evolving consumer demands, regulatory shifts, sustainability concerns, and scientific discoveries in nutrition and food technology.

These trends directly dictate the **direction of R&D pipelines** in food companies and determine the strategic focus of new product development (NPD) teams.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Categories of 2024 Food Trends (KTU Industry-Relevant):**
> - **Plant-Based & Alternative Proteins** — pea, soy, mycelium, insect, cultured meat.
> - **Functional & Fortified Foods** — probiotics, omega-3, adaptogens, nootropics.
> - **Clean Label & Minimal Processing** — ≤ 5 ingredients, no E-numbers.
> - **Personalized Nutrition** — DNA-based, microbiome-based, AI-driven diet plans.
> - **Sustainable Packaging** — biodegradable, edible, compostable materials.
> - **Health-Optimized Foods** — low-sugar, low-sodium, high-protein, gluten-free.
> - **Ethnic & Fusion Flavors** — global cuisines, regional authenticity.
> - **Upcycled Foods** — valorization of food waste (e.g., spent grain bread).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Analogy"

Imagine a customer walking into a busy food court with **20 stalls**:

- **Food Choice Models** are like the **internal checklist running in the customer's mind**: *"Do I feel healthy? What did my mother cook? Is it expensive? Does it look good? Did my friend recommend it?"* — these invisible filters **predict what they will buy**.
- **New Product Trends** are like the **new stalls that just opened this month**: a smoothie bowl stall, a vegan kebab stall, an edible-cup coffee stall — these are **what the food court owner (manufacturer) introduced because customers are now demanding them**.

In short: **Food Choice Models explain WHY people buy; New Product Trends explain WHAT is being created to match those reasons.**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Food Choice Process Model (Furst et al., 1996) — 7 Phases

This model views food choice as a **dynamic life-course process**, not a single decision event. Each phase is influenced by personal, social, and environmental factors.

### Phase-Wise Breakdown:

| Phase | Name | Description | KTU Mnemonic |
|-------|------|-------------|--------------|
| **1** | **Life Course** | Food choices are shaped by childhood, family, cultural traditions | **L**egacy |
| **2** | **Influences** | Personal ideals, health concerns, resources | **I**nputs |
| **3** | **Personal System** | Sensory perceptions, affective feelings | **P**erceive |
| **4** | **Cognitive System** | Beliefs, attitudes, knowledge, expectations | **C**onsider |
| **5** | **Behaviors** | Purchase, preparation, consumption | **B**uy |
| **6** | **Outcomes** | Satisfaction, well-being, social connection | **O**utput |
| **7** | **Feedback Loop** | Experience modifies future phase 3 & 4 inputs | **F**orward |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** A product developer uses Phase 4 (Cognitive) to design **labels, claims, and advertising** that align with consumer beliefs, and Phase 3 (Personal System) to optimize **sensory profile** through trained panel testing.

## 2.2 The Food Choice Questionnaire (FCQ) — 9 Factors

The **FCQ** measures nine distinct motivations behind food choice. Each is rated on a **5-point Likert scale**.

| Factor # | Factor Name | Influence Type | KTU Example |
|----------|-------------|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Health** | Biological/Medical | Low-fat yogurt |
| 2 | **Mood** | Psychological | Comfort food |
| 3 | **Convenience** | Practical | Ready-to-eat meals |
| 4 | **Sensory Appeal** | Hedonic | Crispy snacks |
| 5 | **Natural Content** | Ideological | Organic produce |
| 6 | **Price** | Economic | Private-label brands |
| 7 | **Weight Control** | Personal | Diet beverages |
| 8 | **Familiarity** | Cultural | Traditional recipes |
| 9 | **Ethical Concern** | Moral | Fair-trade chocolate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FCQ Formula (Weighted Choice Index):**

$$
\text{WCI}_{\text{food}} = \sum_{i=1}^{9} w_i \cdot s_i
$$

Where:
- $w_i$ = weightage assigned by consumer to factor $i$
- $s_i$ = Likert score (1–5) for factor $i$
- A higher WCI indicates a **stronger motivational pull** toward the food.

## 2.3 Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB)

The **TPB** is a causal chain model:

$$
\text{Behavioral Intention} \rightarrow \text{Actual Behavior}
$$

Where intention is determined by **3 antecedents**:

| Antecedent | Symbol | Description |
|------------|--------|-------------|
| Attitude Toward Behavior | $A$ | Personal evaluation (good/bad) |
| Subjective Norms | $SN$ | Social pressure (family/peers) |
| Perceived Behavioral Control | $PBC$ | Ease/difficulty perception |

### Master TPB Equation:

$$
BI = w_1 \cdot A + w_2 \cdot SN + w_3 \cdot PBC
$$

Where:
- $BI$ = Behavioral Intention to purchase
- $w_1, w_2, w_3$ = regression weights from survey data

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** TPB is used by marketers to craft **social proof advertising** ("9 out of 10 nutritionists recommend...") which boosts **$SN$** and ultimately $BI$.

## 2.4 Total Food Quality Model (TFQM)

The TFQM proposes that quality perception occurs at **two horizons**:

| Horizon | Type | When It Occurs | KTU Example |
|---------|------|----------------|-------------|
| **Before Purchase** | **Expected Quality** ($Q_e$) | Shelf, label, advertisement | A glossy yogurt cup |
| **At Point of Purchase** | **Quality Cued at Purchase** ($Q_{cp}$) | Visual, aroma cues | Fresh-baked bread smell |
| **After Consumption** | **Experienced Quality** ($Q_{ex}$) | Taste, texture, satisfaction | Creamy mouthfeel |

### TFQM Master Relation:

$$
Q_{ex} - Q_e = \Delta Q \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Consumer Satisfaction}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **If $\Delta Q > 0$:** Product **exceeds expectation** → **repurchase** & **positive word-of-mouth**.
> **If $\Delta Q < 0$:** Product **fails expectation** → **churn** & **negative reviews**.

## 2.5 Health Belief Model (HBM)

Used when food choice is **driven primarily by health motives**.

$$
\text{Health Action} = f(\text{Perceived Susceptibility}, \text{Perceived Severity}, \text{Perceived Benefits}, \text{Perceived Barriers})
$$

## 2.6 New Product Trends — Driving Forces

Trends emerge from a **convergence of 5 drivers**:

| Driver | Symbol | Description |
|--------|--------|-------------|
| **Demographic** | $D$ | Aging populations, Gen Z, urbanization |
| **Health & Wellness** | $H$ | Obesity, diabetes, gut health awareness |
| **Sustainability** | $S$ | Climate change, food waste |
| **Technology** | $T$ | AI, 3D food printing, precision fermentation |
| **Regulation** | $R$ | Sugar taxes, FSSAI labeling, EU Farm-to-Fork |

### Trend Emergence Equation:

$$
\text{Trend Strength} = \alpha D + \beta H + \gamma S + \delta T + \epsilon R
$$

Where $\alpha, \beta, \gamma, \delta, \epsilon$ are **influence coefficients** summing to 1.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: How TFQM Drives Product Reformulation

Consider a snack company reformulating a chips product to reduce sodium by 30%. The marketing manager needs to compute the **expected quality drop** vs. **long-term consumer health trend uplift**.

### Step 1: Define Expected Quality (Before Reformulation)

The original chips has a **hedonic score** (from trained sensory panel):

$$
Q_{e,\text{original}} = 7.8 \text{ out of } 9
$$

### Step 2: Define Expected Quality (After Reformulation)

After 30% sodium reduction, the sensory score drops due to taste:

$$
Q_{e,\text{new}} = 7.8 \times (1 - 0.12) = 6.86
$$

This means a **12% drop in expected quality**.

### Step 3: Apply TFQM Comparison

Compute $\Delta Q$:

$$
\Delta Q = Q_{e,\text{new}} - Q_{e,\text{original}} = 6.86 - 7.8 = -0.94
$$

This is **negative**, predicting initial consumer dissatisfaction.

### Step 4: Layer with Health Belief Model (Long-term)

However, the **Health Belief** driver kicks in for diabetic/heart-conscious consumers:

$$
H_{\text{action}} = P_{\text{susceptibility}} \times P_{\text{severity}} \times P_{\text{benefits}} - P_{\text{barriers}}
$$

Assume:
- $P_{\text{susceptibility}} = 0.7$ (consumer feels at risk)
- $P_{\text{severity}} = 0.8$ (serious consequence)
- $P_{\text{benefits}} = 0.9$ (low sodium is good)
- $P_{\text{barriers}} = 0.3$ (taste is acceptable)

$$
H_{\text{action}} = 0.7 \times 0.8 \times 0.9 - 0.3 = 0.504 - 0.3 = 0.204
$$

### Step 5: Net Trend Decision

Since $H_{\text{action}} > 0$, the **health-driven segment will accept the product despite the taste drop**. The manufacturer can therefore **proceed with reformulation**, expecting a net positive outcome for the health-conscious segment.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Matching Models to NPD Stages

| NPD Stage | Primary Model Used | Secondary Model | KTU Deliverable |
|-----------|--------------------|-----------------|------------------|
| **Idea Generation** | Trend Analysis (D+H+S+T+R) | — | Trend report |
| **Concept Testing** | TPB ($BI$) | FCQ ($s_i$) | Consumer survey |
| **Prototype Testing** | TFQM ($Q_{ex}$) | Sensory Profiling | Trained panel data |
| **Market Testing** | FCQ (WCI) | TPB | Purchase intent |
| **Launch** | Marketing Mix | HBM | Ad campaign |
| **Post-Launch** | TFQM ($\Delta Q$) | Customer feedback loop | Reformulation |

## 3.3 Numerical Example — FCQ Application

A startup launches a **high-protein, vegan, low-cost snack bar**. A consumer rates it on 9 FCQ factors:

| Factor ($i$) | Weight $w_i$ | Score $s_i$ | $w_i \cdot s_i$ |
|--------------|--------------|--------------|------------------|
| Health | 0.20 | 5 | 1.00 |
| Mood | 0.05 | 3 | 0.15 |
| Convenience | 0.15 | 4 | 0.60 |
| Sensory Appeal | 0.20 | 4 | 0.80 |
| Natural Content | 0.15 | 5 | 0.75 |
| Price | 0.15 | 2 | 0.30 |
| Weight Control | 0.05 | 4 | 0.20 |
| Familiarity | 0.02 | 2 | 0.04 |
| Ethical Concern | 0.03 | 5 | 0.15 |

### Step 1: Compute Weighted Choice Index

$$
\text{WCI} = \sum_{i=1}^{9} w_i \cdot s_i = 1.00 + 0.15 + 0.60 + 0.80 + 0.75 + 0.30 + 0.20 + 0.04 + 0.15
$$

$$
\text{WCI} = 3.99
$$

### Step 2: Interpretation

$$
\text{WCI}_{\max} = \sum_{i=1}^{9} w_i \times 5 = 5.00
$$

So the consumer's relative preference is:

$$
\text{Relative Preference} = \frac{3.99}{5.00} \times 100\% = 79.8\%
$$

### Step 3: Conclusion

A **79.8% relative preference** indicates strong acceptance — but the **low Price score (2/5)** is a **critical weakness** that the NPD team must address, perhaps through economies of scale or smaller pack sizes.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Architecture — Food Choice & Trends Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start([Consumer in Market]) --> A1[Personal Factors]
    Start --> A2[Social Factors]
    Start --> A3[Cultural Factors]
    Start --> A4[Environmental Factors]
    
    A1 --> B1[FCQ - 9 Factor Model]
    A2 --> B2[TPB - Theory of Planned Behavior]
    A3 --> B3[Furst 7-Phase Model]
    A4 --> B4[TFQM - Total Food Quality Model]
    A1 --> B5[Health Belief Model]
    
    B1 --> C1[Decision to Purchase or Reject]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    B5 --> C1
    
    C1 --> D1[Market Feedback Loop]
    D1 --> E1[New Product Trend Identified]
    
    E1 --> F1[Plant Based Proteins]
    E1 --> F2[Functional Foods]
    E1 --> F3[Clean Label]
    E1 --> F4[Personalized Nutrition]
    E1 --> F5[Sustainable Packaging]
    E1 --> F6[Upcycled Foods]
    
    F1 --> G1[New Product Development Pipeline]
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G1
    F4 --> G1
    F5 --> G1
    F6 --> G1
    
    G1 --> H1[Concept Testing via FCQ and TPB]
    H1 --> I1[Prototype via TFQM]
    I1 --> J1[Market Launch]
    J1 --> D1
```

## 4.2 Furst 7-Phase Food Choice Process (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Phase 1: Life Course Influences] --> P2[Phase 2: Influences and Resources]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3: Personal Food System]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4: Cognitive Food System]
    P4 --> P5[Phase 5: Food Behaviors]
    P5 --> P6[Phase 6: Outcomes]
    P6 --> P7[Phase 7: Feedback to Phase 3 and 4]
    P7 -.-> P3
    P7 -.-> P4
```

## 4.3 New Product Trend Classification Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    T[New Product Trends 2024] --> C1[Category A: Health Driven]
    T --> C2[Category B: Sustainability Driven]
    T --> C3[Category C: Technology Driven]
    T --> C4[Category D: Experience Driven]
    
    C1 --> H1[Functional Foods]
    C1 --> H2[Low Sodium Sugar Fat]
    C1 --> H3[Probiotic and Prebiotic]
    
    C2 --> S1[Upcycled Foods]
    C2 --> S2[Edible Packaging]
    C2 --> S3[Plant Based Meat]
    
    C3 --> Tech1[Personalized Nutrition AI]
    C3 --> Tech2[3D Printed Food]
    C3 --> Tech3[Precision Fermentation]
    
    C4 --> E1[Global Ethnic Fusion]
    C4 --> E2[Premium Indulgence]
    C4 --> E3[Convenience Ready to Eat]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define the Food Choice Questionnaire (FCQ). List any 6 of its 9 motivational factors.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Food Choice Questionnaire (FCQ)** developed by Steptoe et al. (1995) is a psychometric tool designed to measure the **relative importance of various factors influencing an individual's food choice** using a 9-factor framework rated on a 5-point Likert scale.

The 9 factors are:

1. **Health** — nutritional and dietary value
2. **Mood** — psychological comfort
3. **Convenience** — ease of preparation
4. **Sensory Appeal** — taste, smell, appearance, texture
5. **Natural Content** — absence of additives/artificials
6. **Price** — affordability
7. **Weight Control** — calorie/portion awareness
8. **Familiarity** — cultural/traditional preference
9. **Ethical Concern** — animal welfare, environment, fair trade

*(Any 6 listed = 3 marks; definition = 1 mark, list = 2 marks)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Briefly explain the concept of "Clean Label" trend in food product development.**

**Model Answer:**

**Clean Label** refers to the consumer-driven demand for food products containing:
- A **short, recognizable ingredient list** (typically ≤ 5 ingredients)
- **No artificial additives, E-numbers, or chemical-sounding names**
- **Minimal processing**
- **Transparent sourcing** (origin of ingredients disclosed on pack)

Examples: *"Ingredients: Wheat flour, tomato, olive oil, basil, salt"* vs. a label with 20+ additives.

**Engineering significance:** Clean label challenges food technologists to find **natural alternatives** to synthetic preservatives (e.g., replacing sodium benzoate with rosemary extract) and to reformulate using **clean-label-friendly processing techniques** like HPP (High Pressure Processing).

*(Definition + examples = 2 marks; engineering significance = 1 mark)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students **often list model names without explaining their application to food product design**, leading to loss of 4–5 marks.
> - In **TFQM-related questions, failing to compute $\Delta Q$** explicitly loses 3 marks.
> - **Don't memorize models in isolation** — always link them to a **real NPD stage** (idea generation, concept testing, prototype, launch).
> - Avoid writing **only definitions** — KTU examiners reward **application-oriented answers**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the **Total Food Quality Model (TFQM)** by Grunert et al. with a neat diagram. Discuss how it can be applied to design a new yogurt product for the Kerala market. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** Discuss the **Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB)** in detail. Apply it to predict consumer purchase intention for a **plant-based meat alternative** being launched in Kerala. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part A(a):**

The **TFQM** distinguishes quality perception at **two horizons** — before purchase (expected) and after consumption (experienced).

**Quality Perception Chain:**

$$
Q_e \rightarrow Q_{cp} \rightarrow Q_{ex}
$$

Where:
- $Q_e$ = Expected quality (formed by advertising, brand, label)
- $Q_{cp}$ = Quality cued at point of purchase (visual, aroma, shelf)
- $Q_{ex}$ = Experienced quality (taste, texture, post-consumption)

**Application to Kerala Yogurt:**

| Stage | TFQM Component | Action for Kerala Market |
|-------|----------------|--------------------------|
| Pre-launch | $Q_e$ | Use **traditional Kerala imagery** (coconut, banana leaf) on pack |
| Shelf | $Q_{cp}$ | Use **glass jar or terracotta-look cup** for premium cue |
| Consumption | $Q_{ex}$ | Develop **cream-on-top texture with jaggery variant** to match local palate |

**Key Equation:**

$$
\Delta Q = Q_{ex} - Q_e
$$

If $\Delta Q > 0$: **repurchase + WOM**. Kerala consumer data suggests $\Delta Q > 0$ when jaggery-sweetened variants are introduced.

*(Diagram: 2 marks; Explanation: 3 marks; Application: 2 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part A(b):**

The **TPB** by Ajzen (1991) proposes that **intention** (the immediate antecedent of behavior) is determined by three factors:

**1. Attitude Toward Behavior (A):** Personal evaluation.
- For plant-based meat, attitude depends on taste similarity to real meat, health perception, and curiosity.

**2. Subjective Norms (SN):** Perceived social pressure.
- In Kerala, social norms around **vegetarianism (especially among traditional Hindu communities during festivals)** may support plant-based meat adoption.

**3. Perceived Behavioral Control (PBC):** Perceived ease of purchase and preparation.
- Availability in local supermarkets and **cooking versatility** (can be used in biryani, cutlets, rolls) boosts PBC.

**TPB Equation:**

$$
BI = w_1 \cdot A + w_2 \cdot SN + w_3 \cdot PBC
$$

For Kerala consumers:
- $w_1$ (attitude) is **high** due to health awareness post-COVID.
- $w_2$ (subjective norms) is **moderate** (urban Kerala is more accepting than rural).
- $w_3$ (PBC) is **moderate** (depends on retail penetration).

**Conclusion:** A **plant-based meat product targeting urban Kerala** with strong $A$ and moderate $SN$ and $PBC$ will achieve a **high $BI$** if marketed with celebrity chef endorsements and in-store sampling.

*(TPB diagram: 2 marks; 3-factor explanation: 3 marks; Kerala application: 2 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 – Alternative Choice]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the **7-phase Food Choice Process Model** by Furst et al. Discuss how a **low-sodium pickle manufacturer in Kerala** can use this model to reposition their product. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** Discuss the **emerging trends in new product development** in the food industry. Highlight **any 5 trends with specific examples**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part B(a):**

The **7-phase Food Choice Process Model (Furst et al., 1996)** describes food choice as a **dynamic, life-course process** influenced by personal, social, and environmental factors.

**The 7 Phases:**

1. **Life Course Influences:** Childhood food experiences, family traditions.
2. **Influences and Resources:** Ideals, health concerns, money, time.
3. **Personal Food System:** Sensory perceptions, hunger, emotions.
4. **Cognitive Food System:** Knowledge, beliefs, attitudes, expectations.
5. **Food Behaviors:** Acquisition, preparation, consumption, disposal.
6. **Outcomes:** Satisfaction, well-being, social connection.
7. **Feedback Loop:** Experience modifies Phases 3 and 4 for future choices.

**Application to Kerala Low-Sodium Pickle:**

| Phase | Strategy for Kerala Pickle |
|-------|----------------------------|
| **Phase 1** | Position pickle as **continuation of Kerala's "achar" tradition** (mango, lime, chilli pickle culture). |
| **Phase 2** | Address **hypertension prevalence** in Kerala (high per capita salt consumption) → health concern. |
| **Phase 3** | Use **Kerala-specific sensory cues**: mango, kodampuli (Malabar tamarind), green chilli — without losing "salt pickle" identity. |
| **Phase 4** | Educate consumer through **label claim**: *"30% less sodium, same Kerala taste"*. |
| **Phase 5** | Sell in **small jars** for daily-use households. |
| **Phase 6** | Monitor **repeat purchase** + customer reviews. |
| **Phase 7** | Use consumer feedback to **reformulate** for next batch. |

*(Diagram: 2 marks; 7 phases: 3 marks; Application: 2 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part B(b):**

**5 Major Emerging Trends in New Product Development (with examples):**

**1. Plant-Based & Alternative Proteins:**
- Examples: **Beyond Meat, Impossible Foods** burgers; **GoodDot** plant-based meat in India; **mycelium-based** chicken by Ecovative.

**2. Functional Foods & Nutraceuticals:**
- Foods fortified with probiotics, prebiotics, omega-3, adaptogens (ashwagandha, turmeric).
- Example: **Yakult** (probiotic drink), **Horlicks** with added vitamins.

**3. Clean Label & Minimal Processing:**
- Short ingredient lists, no artificial additives.
- Example: **Britannia's Pure Magic** clean-label cookies; **Slurrp Farm** millet-based baby food.

**4. Personalized & Precision Nutrition:**
- AI-driven diet plans based on **DNA, microbiome, lifestyle**.
- Example: **Habit (USA)** — DNA-based personalized meals; **Mapmygenome** (India).

**5. Upcycled Foods & Sustainable Packaging:**
- Valorization of food waste (e.g., **spent grain** from breweries → high-fiber bread).
- Example: **Toast Ale** (beer → bread); **Karma Kibble** (upcycled dog food); **Notpla** (edible seaweed packaging).

**Bonus trend (optional):** **3D Food Printing** — personalized pasta, chocolates, and meat analogues.

*(Each trend with example = 1.4 marks; 5 trends = 7 marks)*

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **📌 Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

✅ **Food Choice Models:**
- **Furst 7-Phase Model** → life-course process (Phases: Life Course → Influences → Personal → Cognitive → Behaviors → Outcomes → Feedback).
- **FCQ (Steptoe)** → 9 factors: Health, Mood, Convenience, Sensory, Natural, Price, Weight, Familiarity, Ethical.
- **TPB (Ajzen)** → Intention = $w_1 \cdot A + w_2 \cdot SN + w_3 \cdot PBC$.
- **TFQM (Grunert)** → $Q_e \rightarrow Q_{cp} \rightarrow Q_{ex}$; $\Delta Q = Q_{ex} - Q_e$.
- **Health Belief Model** → Health action = Susceptibility × Severity × Benefits − Barriers.

✅ **New Product Trends (2024):**
- Plant-based proteins (pea, soy, mycelium)
- Functional foods (probiotics, adaptogens)
- Clean label (≤ 5 ingredients, no E-numbers)
- Personalized nutrition (DNA, AI, microbiome)
- Sustainable packaging (edible, biodegradable)
- Upcycled foods (food waste valorization)
- 3D printed and precision-fermented foods

✅ **Master Equations to Memorize:**
- $\text{WCI} = \sum w_i \cdot s_i$
- $BI = w_1 A + w_2 SN + w_3 PBC$
- $\Delta Q = Q_{ex} - Q_e$
- $\text{Trend Strength} = \alpha D + \beta H + \gamma S + \delta T + \epsilon R$

✅ **Common Pitfalls:**
- ❌ Don't just list model names — apply them to **NPD stages**.
- ❌ Don't confuse **TFQM** with **TQM (Total Quality Management)** — TFQM is consumer-perception focused.
- ❌ Don't write only definitions — KTU 2024 values **application over rote memorization**.
